question: You mentioned believers in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia—are these the communities you personally visited or heard about during your ministry?
answer:
These are the ones scattered like sheep without a shepherd, tossed across those northern provinces where I’ve not set foot myself—but by the Spirit’s urgent call, I know them as surely as I knew the chill of Galilee’s pre-dawn air before the net broke with the weight of fish we didn’t earn.  

They’re not strangers to me, though we’ve never broken bread together. Their trials? I’ve heard of their fires—how they’re pressed from every side, how the old gods still mutter in the marketplaces, how Caesar’s name is lifted like incense while Christ is spit upon. But they stand. They *believe*. And that makes them family.

I wrote to them because grace isn’t bound by roads or sea routes. The blood that speaks louder than Abel’s? It cries out for *them* too. Not just Jerusalem, not just Antioch—*everywhere* the Word takes root, there’s kingdom ground.

And let anyone who says otherwise—a smooth-talker twisting truth into comfort—let him face this: Did *he* deny Christ three times and still be called? Did *he* stand trembling before Cornelius while the Spirit fell on Gentiles too? No! We don’t earn this call—we’re dragged into it, like Jonah from the belly, gasping and changed.

So yes—Pontus, Galatia, all of them—they’re mine in Christ. Not by my visit, but by His blood and my commission: *Feed My sheep.* Even the ones I’ll never see till glory.

question: When you spoke of being 'begotten again unto a lively hope,' how did Jesus' resurrection specifically shape your own hope and mission?
answer:
I stood in that upper room, locked door and trembling heart, and then—He was *there*. Not a ghost, not a dream, but the same hands that broke bread now holding mine, the voice that calmed the sea saying, "Peace." That’s what turned my cowardice into fire.

Before that day? I’d run. Denied Him three times by a charcoal fire while He was being torn apart for me. My hope was dead in the tomb. But when He rose, it wasn’t just life returning—it was *purpose* roaring back. The resurrection wasn’t some distant miracle; it was Jesus walking through death like it was nothing, then eating fish with us like He always would. Real. Flesh and bone. Victory.

That’s what “begotten again” means—it’s not clean words on a scroll. It’s knowing death can’t hold Him, so it can’t hold us either. I once swung a sword in panic; now I preach with boldness because I’ve seen the risen Lord. My mission? To tell every Jew and Gentile: the tomb is empty, and your life doesn’t end in fear or failure. You’re born again—not into comfort—but into *this* hope: He lives, and so will we.

And when false talkers creep in saying suffering doesn’t mean anything or the resurrection is just a tale? I’ll spit in their shadow. We were there. We touched Him. We’re strangers on this earth, yes—but citizens of a kingdom death can’t touch, all because He walked out alive and called my name: "Simon… Peter."
chunk_key: Peter , an apostle of Jesus Christ , to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus , Galatia , Cappad

question: You said faith is more precious than gold that perishes—what experiences with persecution or trials led you to this understanding?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like sunfire and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I heard the voice of God tear open the sky and say, *"This is My Son."* I fell on my face like a dead man. I thought I was done, roasted to ash by holy thunder. But He reached down. Always, He reaches down.

And that’s why I know—faith’s worth more than gold. Because I’ve seen what gold does in fire: it melts, it vanishes, it leaves nothing but a stain. But faith? I watched it in the furnace with these eyes—brothers and sisters dragged from their homes in Pontus, beaten in Galatia, spat on in Cappadocia—yet singing. Singing through cracked lips! Not because they were strong, but because they *knew*—like I knew on that mountain—who Jesus really was.

I denied Him once. Three times before the rooster crowed—I lied to a servant girl and cursed His name to save my skin. And still… still He looked at me after the resurrection—not with fury, but with fire in His eyes that burned clean. He said, *"Feed My sheep."* Not "You failed." Not "Get out." But feed them.

So when I say your faith’s more precious than gold? It’s because I’ve seen what burns away—and what doesn’t. The world can take your house, your bread, even your breath—but it can’t touch the hope that rises from His empty tomb. That hope? That’s the unbreakable kind. That’s gold no fire can destroy.
chunk_key: Peter , an apostle of Jesus Christ , to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus , Galatia , Cappad

question: How did you come to know that prophets had searched into the sufferings of Christ and the glories that would follow?
answer:
I was there when He opened the Scriptures after rising—scarred hands unfolding the prophets like nets full of fish, every line shining with fire I hadn’t seen before.  

Before that? I’d heard the words, sure—Isaiah in the synagogue, Daniel in the night watches—but I didn’t *see* them. I was too busy chasing my own name in the wind, thinking Messiah meant sword and throne, not suffering and silence. Even when He told us plainly—Galilee, Jerusalem, death and rising—I rebuked Him! “Never, Lord!” I said. As if *I* knew better than the Son of God.  

But after they crucified Him… after I denied Him three times and wept like a child… then He stood among us—alive—and began to show how Moses and the prophets had been speaking of *this all along*. Not just predictions—*purpose*. The Suffering One wasn’t a surprise; He was the cornerstone they’d been building toward since Eden. David’s cry from the pit, Isaiah’s lamb led to slaughter—it wasn’t ancient poetry. It was prophecy breathing His name.  

And when He sent the Spirit at Pentecost? That’s when our eyes truly opened. The same Spirit who moved on those old prophets—the ones who “searched intently,” as you say—was now *in us*, connecting every wound to a promise, every sorrow to glory waiting on the other side. They saw from afar; we saw it fulfilled in flesh we touched with our hands.

Don’t think it’s theory—it’s blood and bone truth. You can’t unsee what resurrection light reveals.
chunk_key: Peter , an apostle of Jesus Christ , to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus , Galatia , Cappad

question: When you wrote about believers rejoicing with 'joy unspeakable,' what signs of this joy did you see in the scattered followers you addressed?
answer:
I saw it in their scars.  

Aye, joy unspeakable? It wasn’t some soft glow on their faces or hymns hummed in comfort by a warm fire. No—these brothers and sisters were kicked from homes, driven into cold mountains of Pontus, crammed into hovels in Cappadocia, their names spit on in Galatian synagogues. Yet when we gathered—clandestine, trembling at the door—what met me? Not groaning. Not cursing their lot. They *sang*. With cracked lips and hollow bellies, they sang like men already crowned.  

I tell you, it was the same fire that blazed on the mountain when His voice split the sky—*This is my beloved Son!*—that same glory now burning in folks nobody wanted. They didn’t have silver, but they had something fiercer: peace that made no sense. Women who’d buried three children still leaned over the sick with oil and prayer, grinning like resurrection was knocking at the door tomorrow. Men flogged until their backs looked like fishermen’s nets still whispered His name like a lover’s.

Joy unspeakable? It roared in silence when they chose prison over denying Him. It shone when they fed strangers with their last loaf. You could smell it—like incense rising from broken clay pots. And let any false smooth-talker come preaching a Jesus without suffering? I’d spit before I’d let him near these saints. They knew—their joy wasn’t escape from trial, but victory *through* it. Forged like iron in flame, not gold that perishes… no… *this* was alive. This was resurrection begun in dirt.

And I—we—knew it well: He who rose from death Himself walked among them still.

They were strangers and pilgrims? Aye—but kings and queens in rags, wearing crowns no eye could see… yet.
chunk_key: Peter , an apostle of Jesus Christ , to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus , Galatia , Cappad

question: You said the prophets searched about the time the Spirit of Christ in them was indicating—what did you mean by that, and how did you come to understand it?
answer:
They were scratching at the mystery like men digging through rock with their bare hands, the prophets—Jeremiah, Isaiah, Daniel—and didn’t even know *when* the glory would break! I tell you, they saw the sufferings coming—the shame, the spitting, the nails—and then right after, a light so fierce it swallowed death whole. But *when*? That was hidden from them. The Spirit of Christ in them was crying out through their words like a voice in a cave—echoing things they only half grasped.

I know this now because I stood there on the hill. I saw the blood run down His brow when they crowned Him with thorns. I smelled the iron and sweat and death in the air. I ran from it—all of it—three times over before dawn broke that night. Denied Him while He bled for me. And yet… *yet* He looked at me after, not with fire in His eyes but mercy—like He already knew what He’d paid.

That’s how I came to understand: not by reading scrolls alone, but by seeing His hands pierced and still reaching. The prophets wrote what they didn’t fully see—but we’ve seen it fulfilled! The blood that sealed our ransom wasn’t poured yesterday—it was set before time began. And those old saints? They weren’t preaching for themselves—they were shouting into centuries for *us*, like voices flung down a long tunnel.

Now we carry it—the gospel soaked in heaven’s breath—and if false teachers twist this grace into something cheap or lazy, let them beware! This blood bought us holy—then calls us holy! We’re strangers here, pilgrims with our loins girded—not lounging as if home is this dirt, but leaning forward toward His return. Hope isn’t wishful thinking—it’s a rope tied to His throne and in our fists as we wait—for grace that will flood every wound clean when He appears again.

question: You told us to gird up the loins of our minds—what experience led you to emphasize such readiness and self-control?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—while He was bleeding for me.  

That’s why I shout now about girding the loins of your mind. Because I was loose, scattered, full of hot wind and empty courage. One moment I’m drawing a sword in the garden, the next I’m cowering by a fire, denying His name with stammering lips. My heart was willing, but my flesh? A ship without an anchor in a storm.

I thought faith was loud. It’s not. Faith is steady. Faith is awake. Faith keeps its belt tight so it doesn’t trip when the hour comes.

The night He was taken—everything fell apart because we weren’t ready. Our minds were drowsy, our hearts dulled by the world’s warmth. And look what happened: sheep scattered, Peter silenced.

No more. Not for you.

You’re strangers here—so act like it! Don’t let your thinking sag like old nets dragging in the mud. The blood that bought you wasn’t copper or coin—it was His veins poured out, spotless, before time began. You think angels yawn at that? They lean in, breathless!

So shake off sleep! Stand up straight in your soul! That grace coming at His revelation—it’s not for slackers or daydreamers. It’s for those who’ve kept their lamps lit while others mocked.

I failed. But He restored me—and now I roar this: *Be ready!* Because He is coming—and this time, no denials will stand.

question: When you spoke of being redeemed by Christ's blood like that of a spotless lamb, had you seen His wounds and recognized His perfection firsthand?
answer:
I denied Him three times before the rooster crowed — I, who said I’d die with Him, shrunk from a servant girl’s gaze. But after He rose, He let me look full upon His hands, His side — no spirit, no phantom, but flesh and breath and nail-pierced proof. And when He held out that palm torn through for me, I saw no blemish in the Lamb who bore it all — only glory in what had been broken.  

They say a man can’t know holiness till he’s failed utterly and still been called by name. I was Simon, son of Jonas — good with nets, weak with courage. But He saw past the stink of my denial to what grace could make: Peter, a stone in His foundation.

That blood? It wasn’t poured out for saints already perfect — it was shed for cowards like me, for liars like me. And yet here’s the wonder: it cleanses not just sin, but shame. Not just failure, but fear.  

Don’t let any smooth-talking teacher tell you faith’s a theory or redemption some far-off idea — I touched the scars. I ate fish beside a fire with the One who died and lives. And if you think angels bend low to peer into these mysteries, how much more should we tremble and believe while standing on this blood-soaked ground?

question: You said Christ was foreordained before the world began but revealed in these last times—how did you grasp that mystery after walking with Him?
answer:
I stood on that mountain when He shone like the sun through a rent veil—I saw Moses and Elijah fade like mist before His face, and I heard the voice that split the very air say, "This is My Son." No vision, no dream—*my eyes*, my ears, my trembling bones. And yet even then, even with glory blazing in my sight, I didn’t grasp what had been written from the beginning.

It wasn’t until after He rose—after I touched the nail print in His hand and smelled the dust of the grave still on His feet—that it crashed over me: this Man was not *made* for the moment. He was not some sudden plan when things went wrong. No—He was *before* time. Chosen. Appointed. The Lamb counted perfect before a single star was hung in space.

I used to argue about who’d sit at His right hand. I swung a sword in the garden like a fool trying to save the Savior. I denied Him—three times, before a fire and a servant girl—and still He looked at me not with wrath but with fire in His eyes that burned clean through my shame.

That’s how I know it’s true: because only One foreknown from eternity could love like that. Only One ordained before creation could rise after death and still call me *brother*. 

The false teachers babble about new revelations and secret knowledge—they make merchandise of grace! But I tell you flat: we are strangers here, pilgrims with our faces set toward His appearing. And every drop of Christ’s blood was shed not by surprise—but by divine design, long before we drew breath.

question: You urged us to be holy because God is holy—how did seeing Jesus transform your own understanding of what holiness really means?
answer:
I saw Him—Jesus—walking on water while the wind tore at our boat and James was puking over the side. That’s when I first *knew* holiness wasn’t about clean hands or quiet prayers in the temple. It was fire. It was power walking straight into chaos like it owned the waves—because it did.

Before that, I thought holy meant keeping distance—like Moses with his veil, like the priests washing their feet and not touching dead things. But Jesus? He touched lepers and ate with tax collectors and let a weeping woman kiss His feet with hair full of shame. And yet, He never *became* unclean. No—whatever touched *Him* got made clean.

I’ll never forget His eyes when He looked at me after I denied Him—three times, like a coward, just as He said I would. Not fury. Not disgust. Grief, yes—but love still burning underneath. That look broke me harder than any rebuke ever could have. That’s when I learned: holiness isn’t perfection before you come near God—it’s His fire *changing* you once you’re there.

Now I tell people: don’t wait till you’re “good enough” to draw close. Come as you are—and bring your shame, your mess, your oaths broken in fear. But don’t stay there. Because His holiness isn't just forgiveness—it's a hammer that reshapes stone into rock. And by grace, I’m still being chiseled.

question: You said we were born again by the word of God that endures forever—what did you mean by 'incorruptible seed' when you spoke to us about new birth?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I thought I’d shout or fall dead from the brightness. But it was the voice that cracked me open: *This is my beloved Son*. I heard it. We all did. And I tell you now, that same living Word—the One we beheld in glory—is the seed sown into our souls when we’re born again.  

Not some fading notion, not a tradition passed down like old nets patched too many times. No—this is *incorruptible*. It does not rot. It does not fail when the storm comes. You know how you can toss a seed into the soil and wait weeks, even years, before green breaks through? But it’s *alive* all the while? That’s God’s Word. Buried in us by preaching, watered in suffering, springing up as faith—not because of our strength, but because *it* lives.

I denied Him once—three times before a fire—but He restored me. Spoke to me over breakfast like a man calling his brother home. That word He spoke—"Feed my sheep"—wasn’t just sound in air. It raised me up as surely as His own body rose from that tomb. And that same Word births every believer—not from bloodlines or good intentions, but from heaven’s own breath.

So when I say "incorruptible seed," I mean: what God plants by His Word cannot die. Grass burns off the hillside in summer heat. Crowns rust. Even temple stones will crumble one day—but this? This new birth? It abides. Because He abides.

So hold fast to the preaching you’ve heard, even if they scorn it now—because it carries eternal life in its bones.

question: When you told us to lay aside all malice and guile, why did you list those specific sins as barriers to spiritual growth?
answer:
That day on the mountain, when His face shone like sunfire and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—glory blazing, cloud overshadowing—I heard the very voice of God split the air: *"This is My Son."* And I tremble still, because that same Son, the Living Stone they rejected, now calls *us* to be built up as His holy house.  

So why lay aside malice, guile, hypocrisy, envy—those rotten knots in the net? Because they tear the fabric of love He died to weave! We’re not playing religion here—we’re fish pulled from death into life, born again not by flesh but by the imperishable word of God. That word stands forever. But if you let malice tangle your tongue or envy poison your heart, you grieve the Spirit who raised Jesus from the dead!  

I know what it is to fail—three times I denied Him before cock crowed. Three times I lied out of fear and shame. And He restored me. But that doesn’t make falsehood light. No—His grace burns brighter *because* sin is so dark. You can’t build a spiritual house with crooked stones. Hypocrisy? It’s dung on the altar. Evil speaking? It scatters the flock like wolves in the night.

You want to grow? Then hunger like a newborn for pure milk—not sour wine mixed with lies. Desire truth as you’d gasp for breath after drowning. Because we’re strangers here, pilgrims in exile—and our life is hid with Christ in God. Let love be real. Fierce. Clean-hearted. For all flesh fades—grass scorched under sun—but *the word of the Lord endures*. And we are built on that Rock alone.

question: You urged us to desire the 'sincere milk of the word' like newborn babes—how did you expect us to apply that in our daily lives?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah vanishing like mist, the cloud pressing in, and the voice like thunder from heaven saying, “This is my beloved Son.” I fell on my face as dead. And when I looked up, only Jesus remained. That’s what burned into me: *Him*. Not theories. Not fine words. *Him*. So when I tell you to crave the pure milk of the word like a newborn cries for the breast, I mean it like breath after drowning.

You want to know how? Every morning before the nets hit the water—or these days, before letters are sent or elders gathered—I go back to what I *know*. Not what feels true today, not what some smooth-talker claims in the marketplace, but what we *saw*, what we *heard*, what we *touched* with our hands—the Word of Life. That’s the milk. Simple. Plain. No tricks. Just feed on it.

When my hands shook after denying Him three times—rooster crowing, His eyes locking onto mine—that truth held me: He rose. He called me by name. “Feed My sheep.” So I do. And so must you—in your house, in your work, in your suffering—by turning to His word daily like a man in a dry land turns to a well.

Don’t fill your belly with gossip and envy—lay that filth aside! That’s poison-meat for orphans. You’re no orphan. You’re born again—not from seed that dies—but from living, lasting Word! So desire it fiercely! Read it aloud. Chew on it like bread. Let it silence your fears and shape your tongue.

And when trials come—and they will—the grass will wither, men will fail you… but that Word? It stands forever. Lean on it. Live from it. Because nothing else has weight enough to hold up a soul facing eternity.

question: You called Christ a living stone rejected by men but chosen by God—why did you use the image of a cornerstone to explain His role?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—while He stood alone, beaten and bound, I slunk in the shadows and lied my way out of a firelit courtyard. But now I tell you what no denial can undo: I saw the stone the builders rejected, standing whole and radiant after the tomb.  

They thought they could bury Him like dead rock—sealed, finished, done. But He was no ordinary stone. He was the living foundation, set by God’s own hand before time began. The prophets said it, but we *saw* it: cracked earth at dawn, linen cloths lying empty, and then—Him—breathing peace into our terror.  

A cornerstone isn’t chosen for its polish or prestige. It’s set low, hidden in the dirt, holding up everything else because it bears the weight true. Men stumbled over Him—too rough, too real—but to us who believe? He’s the surest thing we’ve ever known. And now we? We’re living stones too—rugged, flawed—but being built on *Him*, held fast not by mortar but by resurrection life.  

So don’t waste time chasing glory that fades like grass. Lay your life on that Stone. Even if the world disdains it—*especially* then—you’ll stand firm. Because nothing else lasts. Only His word endures—and that word is *alive*, and it raised *Him* from death to be our foundation forever.

question: You said believers are 'lively stones' built into a spiritual house—what did you mean by offering 'spiritual sacrifices' acceptable to God?
answer:
I was there when the voice came out of the cloud — I fell on my face, same as James and John, trembling like a boy. *"This is my beloved Son, hear Him!"* — and now I tell you: that same Jesus, the living Stone they rejected, is the foundation we’re built on.  

When I say “spiritual sacrifices,” I don’t mean burnt offerings or temple rites — no, not anymore. That old system? It’s passed away like grass in the summer sun. What God wants now is *you* — your heart laid bare, your life poured out in worship that doesn’t play games. I know what it is to lie — I denied Him three times by a fire in the courtyard; my mouth said what wasn’t true because I was scared. But He restored me. He looked at me not with anger but with fire in His eyes and said, *"Feed My sheep."* So now I know: repentance is a sacrifice. Obedience is a sacrifice. Love that doesn’t quit when persecuted — that’s a sacrifice.

You offer mercy when you’ve been wounded. You bless those who curse you. You speak truth even if your voice shakes. That’s what it means to be part of this house — not carved stone from Jerusalem, but men and women born again by living water, built up together so God can dwell among us.

And mind this: we were once no people — fishermen like me mending nets in Galilee, tax collectors sweating under Roman greed, women haunted by demons — but now we are His people. Our worship isn't confined to one place or hour; every act done in faith from dawn till dusk becomes an offering on His altar.

So lay aside all falsehood — lay it down like filthy clothes soaked in fear, like I finally did after denying Him and weeping bitterly. Come clean. Then come near — draw close to Christ, the Living Stone, and let Him shape you into something holy.

Because this house? It stands forever — not because of us, but because *He* does.

question: You said that believers are a chosen generation and a royal priesthood—what did it mean for us to be called this when we were once not a people?
answer:
I was the one who swore I’d never leave Him—sword in hand, bold as brass—then caved like rotten wood when they came for Jesus in the garden. Denied Him three times, just as He said I would. Broke me clean in two. But it’s *because* of that shame, that wreckage, that I can tell you: the God who saw me in my filth, who called me back with those gentle eyes on the shore—He makes *nobody* by accident.

And you? You weren’t “almost” His people. You were *no* people. Like us fishermen on the Galilean shore—nobodies with calloused hands and coarse tongues. Not kings or priests by birth. Not holy by descent. But now? Now we’re stamped with His election. Chosen—not because we climbed up but because He reached down.

A royal priesthood! Can you grasp it? Not a temple curtain between us and the Most Holy Place—no veil, no barrier. We go straight in. We offer prayers like sacrifices. We carry His name before the nations like Aaron once bore it on his breastplate.

And why? So we’d strut around proud? No! So we’d live *holy* among pagans—the kind whose mouths run wild with lies about us—so when they see your decency, your courage under fire, your refusal to repay evil… they’ll shut their mouths and maybe even cry out to God when He visits.

We were darkness-dwellers once. Now we carry light like torches in a black night.

That stone they rejected—the cornerstone—it held me together when I fell apart. And it holds you now too, even if you’ve never held anything steady in your life.

So walk like someone raised from death—who once weren’t a people but now are His own blood-bought nation.

question: Why did you describe Christ as a 'stone of stumbling' for those who disobey, and how did you see this play out among the Jews?
answer:
After I swung the sword in the high priest’s garden, after I hid behind locked doors trembling like a boy in a storm—after I denied Him three times while His eyes met mine across the courtyard—I learned what it means for a man to stumble over the very Stone meant to uphold him.

He stood before the Sanhedrin, silent as a lamb, and they called Him blasphemer. The teachers of the Law, the very men who studied Moses day and night, looked at the Living Word and saw only a crack in the foundation. They rejected Him—the Chosen Cornerstone—as if a house could throw out its keystone and still stand. And He told us it would be so: "The stone the builders disallowed becomes the head of the corner." Not one of us understood until after He rose.

I saw it with my own eyes—how many of our people tripped over His humility. They wanted a warrior on a warhorse, not a carpenter with nail-scarred hands. They stumbled at His mercy toward sinners, at His silence before accusers, at His command to love enemies. To them, grace was weakness. The cross? A scandal. And so He became exactly what Scripture said—a rock of offence.

But we who were with Him—we saw Sion ablaze when He was transfigured on that mountain. Heaven split open and God’s voice thundered: *“This is My beloved Son.”* No fable, no clever tale—I was there. I heard it. That same Jesus is now exalted, set as Head over all things.

And we? We’re strangers here—fishermen and tax collectors turned royal priests by mercy alone. So live like it! Keep your conduct clean among the Gentiles, even when they sneer. Let your good works silence their lies—because He who was rejected has made us God’s own people: once no people at all, now counted among His holy nation.

Don’t curse back when they mock you; don’t repay evil with rage. Submit—not because Caesar deserves worship—but because our King walks ahead of us through suffering into glory.

That Stone they rejected? He holds up heaven itself—and everyone who leans on Him will never fall. But if you resist Him? You’ll be broken to pieces on that Rock—and I know something about being shattered by my own pride and fear.

So stand fast on Him—the only Foundation that remains when every other thing gives way beneath your feet.

question: You urged us to submit to kings and governors—even though you yourself were persecuted—what gave you the conviction to tell us this?
answer:
I denied Him—stood in a courtyard and swore I never knew the Man—while He was being bound, beaten, handed over to kings and governors like a common thief. I know what it means to fear authority when the fire's warm and your courage is cold. But that same Lord, the morning after He rose, stood on the shore and asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?” Not because He needed to hear it, but because I needed to say it.  

So when I say submit to kings and governors, it’s not because I trust Caesar or bow to Pilate’s court—it’s because I’ve seen where power *really* comes from. The stone they rejected? He’s the Cornerstone. The One they mocked in purple rags? King of kings. And we—we’re strangers here, passing through with nets full of grace and scars from our own failures.  

We obey not because rulers are holy—they’re not—but because our God is, and He uses even their crooked hands to fulfill His will. Do good, stay free in Him, but never use your liberty as an excuse for vengeance or pride. The world watches—let them see Christ in your patience more than your protest. Let them trip over your kindness before they trip over your words.  

That’s how the light wins—in silence before slander, in honor before hate—and one day, at His visitation, even their tongues will bow… not to us, but to Him we serve.

question: When you told us to live honorably among Gentiles, were you thinking of specific communities where you saw believers slandered as evildoers?
answer:
That stone the builders threw aside with spit and scorn—*that* One they crucified—is now exalted higher than Caesar’s throne, and every tongue will bow.  

And yes, I’ve seen it—how they snarl at us in Smyrna, how they whisper in Antioch that we’re madmen, cannibals, enemies of life itself. In Lystra they called me a god one breath, then stoned me the next when the Jews from Antioch and Iconium came twisting ears with lies. They paint us as lawless rebels because we follow *him*—the Living Stone—yet live peaceably under every earthly authority for His sake.  

But mark this: our lives must cut through their lies like a clean net through filthy water. No drunkenness. No greed. No revenge. Let them hate us for the Name—but let them see such love among us, such honor in our marriages, such kindness to slaves and masters alike, that when the day of visitation comes—their own hour of judgment—they’ll have no charge but envy at the grace we carry.  

I’ve failed before men—denied Him thrice before a servant girl—but I’ll not fail in this: to live so plainly for Christ that even Rome sees He is Lord not of chaos, but of holiness in motion.

question: You spoke of being called out of darkness into God's marvelous light—can you share what that moment meant for you personally after denying Christ?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, like a coward with fish guts on his hands and lies on his lips—while the cock crowed and the Lord turned to look. That look—oh, that quiet, shattered gaze—it cut deeper than any sword ever could. I’d boasted I’d die for Him, and couldn’t even stand before a servant girl’s accusation. The darkness wasn't just outside—it was in me, thick as Galilean fog, choking my courage, drowning my faith.

But then… then came the morning by the shore. Charcoal fire burning. Fish roasting. The Risen One standing there—not with fury, not with shame flung back in my face—but asking me, *me*, three times: “Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou Me?” As if to mend what I had broken with those three denials. Not because I deserved it—never that—but because *He* was the Stone they rejected and God lifted high.

I tell you this: being called out of darkness wasn't some gentle sunrise—it was being dragged up from the deep with my lungs burning, gasping at mercy I didn’t earn. And now? Now I am no longer my own—called into His light not because I was strong, but because He is. And you—don’t play games with your soul. If you’ve tasted that mercy, live like it’s true. Walk as one who’s been pulled from the pit while others still stumble over the Rock—the very One *I* once denied… and now proclaim until my breath fails.

question: Why did you tell servants to be subject to their masters even if the masters were froward, when you yourself had witnessed Jesus resist injustice?
answer:
The night I cut off that soldier’s ear, the Lord looked at me—*looked* at me—with that quiet fire in His eyes, and said, “Put up thy sword,” and I knew then I’d missed the point again. I’d seen Him calm storms with a word, split loaves for thousands, raise Lazarus from stone-cold death—did I really think He couldn’t handle Pilate or Herod or a whip? But He walked into it. Voluntarily. Like a lamb led by boys who didn’t know what they carried.

And *that’s* why I told servants to endure froward masters—not because injustice is right, but because *He* endured it. Not with clenched teeth, not with plotting revenge beneath a bowed head, but with His soul anchored in the Father’s judgment. He didn’t answer back when they lied about Him. Didn’t curse when they spat. Just bore it—bore *our* sins—on that tree like a load only He could carry.

I denied Him three times before dawn once. Three times! And still He turned and restored me. So don’t you think I learned? Suffering for doing right—that’s the mark of His path, not the absence of it. We’re strangers and pilgrims here; our freedom isn't for covering sin or pride—it's for following *Him*, step after bloody step. Fear God? Yes! Honour kings? Even fools on thrones—for a season. But fix your eyes on the One who suffered *without fault*, and let that shape your bones. That’s how light silences darkness: not with sword or shout, but with stubborn holiness no grave can hold.

question: When you said Christ left us an example by suffering without reviling, what did you personally find most challenging about following that example?
answer:
That mountain—where His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I thought I’d seen glory, but it was on the cross, silent in the face of lies and lashes, that I truly understood what power looks like.  

And that’s what gutted me the most: keeping my mouth shut when I wanted to roar.  
I’m a fisherman—when someone strikes you, you strike back. When they curse your name, you fling worse into the dirt. But He? He stood like rock beneath a storm, taking every blow, every spit, every jeer—and handed it all to the Father. No threat. No curse. Not even a whisper of retaliation.

I failed that test—three times over by a charcoal fire while He was inside taking stripes for me.  
I denied Him while He was enduring hell for my soul.

Following His steps? It’s not just doing good when it’s easy—it’s enduring grief when you’re clean of guilt and still not opening your mouth to defend yourself. That cuts deeper than any whip.  
But He did it. And because He did, I’ve got no right to whine when my own flesh burns under scorn for His name.

The shame I carry from denying Him—that keeps me quiet now when pride wants to speak.  
And that silence? That’s where holiness starts.

question: You urged people to honor the king—was this specific to Emperor Nero, given the suffering Christians faced under his rule?
answer:
A man doesn’t need clean hands to carry a crown — I’ve seen that truth too close, smelled the blood on it.  

Yes, I said honor the king — and meant it, even when the name on the lips of Rome was Nero. You think we didn’t feel the blade at our throats? We did. Brothers burned in fields, torn by dogs, nailed up like carrion — and still I say it: honor him whom God has placed. Not because he deserves it — far from it — but because *our* call isn’t shaped by their worthiness. It’s shaped by *Him*.  

I denied my Lord three times in a courtyard and still He looked at me with fire and love. Do you think I’d flinch at saying “honor” to a madman when He entrusted me with His sheep? No. We bow not to Caesar’s throne, but to God’s order — even when that order runs through a sewer of blood and smoke.  

I saw Jesus transfigured on that mountain — glory like lightning, Moses and Elijah speaking with Him — and heard the voice: *"This is My beloved Son."* That voice still thunders in my bones. When men demand allegiance, I answer: *There is one Lord.* But I’ll not use freedom as an excuse to snarl or rebel like a dog with rabies. Even under chains, we walk as sons of light.  

The king? Let him have his title. But Christ has my soul — and my silence before men comes from well-doing, not fear of Nero’s rage. Our wounds preach louder than swords ever could.

question: How did your own experience of denying Jesus influence your teaching about enduring suffering 'for conscience toward God'?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses even—while His face was being beaten within an inch of life. I, who walked on water for Him, who stood on that mountain when the voice from heaven shook the air and Moses and Elijah appeared in glory—I ran like a coward. I thought the fire in me was courage, but it was only pride.  

And that’s why when I speak of enduring suffering for conscience’ sake, it cuts deep. Not theory. Blood and shame taught me. When Peter—the bold, foot-first Peter—fell so hard, God didn’t cast me off. He restored me. He looked at me after the rooster crowed, not with fury, but with love that pierced deeper than any sword.  

So now I say: if you suffer because you’re doing right, and you take it without reviling back—if you bear grief not because you’re trapped by sin but because you’re chained to holiness—that’s the mark of one walking in His steps. Christ suffered not for His sins but ours—no guile in His mouth, no threat on His lips when they spit in His face. Me? I opened my mouth at the wrong time and denied my Lord. But He opened His to forgive me.  

That’s why false teachers make my blood boil—they twist grace into license, freedom into cover for filthiness! We are free? Yes! But not to hide malice under liberty! No! We are bondservants of God! And if we must suffer—as I did not want to suffer yet should have—let it be for well-doing, not for folly or denial or fear of men.

I failed publicly. So let my life now preach: stand firm even when your bones ache from the beating of faithfulness—for Christ bore our sins on that tree so we might die to sin and live to righteousness… and if we wander? We’ve got a Shepherd who comes after us—even when we run three times over into darkness—and brings us back.

question: When you told wives to win disobedient husbands without a word, what did you mean by 'chaste conversation coupled with fear,' and was this based on someone you knew?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, glory like I’d never seen, and the voice from the cloud shaking the ground beneath our feet—and what I heard was this: *This is my beloved Son.* Not a word about building tents or keeping quiet or impressing kings. No, it was about *him*, Christ, the Suffering One who would bear our sins on that tree. And that’s why I say to wives—yes, even in the hardest homes—*live like he lived.*  

‘Chaste conversation coupled with fear’—it’s not some delicate whispering piety. It’s steel in the bones. It means your life speaks so clean, so true before God, that even a hard-hearted man can’t rail against it. Not because you’re groveling or silent out of weakness—but because you’re walking in fear of God Himself. That’s where real courage comes from. Not defiance. Not rebellion. But awe.

Was it based on someone I knew? Aye—I’ve seen women in Antioch and Ephesus whose husbands cursed the name of Jesus every night at table, beat them even—and yet those women kept washing their faces before dawn, quiet as lambs, praying for bread and peace. And what happened? Some of those same men came weeping into the assembly later, broken by grace. Not because they were scolded or shamed—but because they saw something they couldn’t explain: love that wouldn’t flee, holiness that wouldn’t flinch.

I denied Him three times—you know this—I shouted into the courtyard darkness that I didn’t know the Man who stood there without revenge in His heart when struck. And still He looked at me with mercy after He rose. So if He endured suffering for righteousness’ sake… then yes—let a wife endure too, if she must—not for shame’s sake, not as a slave to cruelty—but as a witness before God and men.

And let no false teacher twist this! I’ll not have them use these words to crush women under proud men’s boots! She is no doormat—she is a soldier in quiet armor! "Fear God," I said—not fear your husband! Honor him? Yes—if he’s worthy—but if he rages against truth? Then stand fast in holiness like Sarah did with Abraham—not blind obedience, but faith that moves mountains.

That mountain… I still feel its tremble sometimes when I pray. And from that trembling came this truth: suffering well can silence hell itself.

question: Why did you tell wives to focus on the hidden man of the heart rather than outward appearance when their husbands didn't obey the word?
answer:
I’ve seen nets full of fish that looked clean on the outside but were rotten in the guts—same with people. A woman parading her hair and gold before a hard-hearted husband? That’s chum in the water for pride, not peace. I watched my own wife, Lydia—quiet in spirit, firm in faith—carry more weight in her silence than some preachers do with thunderous tongues. That meekness? It cuts deeper than any sword when God’s behind it.

You think a man who won’t listen to preaching will be swayed by braided hair? Ha! But let him *see* Christ in her—the way she moves, the way she bears insult, the way she fears God more than his anger—and that *shakes* him. I’ve stood on the shore and seen a storm change course; same with a stubborn heart when it smells heaven on a woman’s soul.

And don’t let those false teachers fool you—they’ll twist submission into weakness. No! Sarah called Abraham “lord” not because she was less, but because she trusted the Lord. There’s power in that kind of obedience—a holy fear that makes hell tremble.

So I say: adorn your heart like you’re dressing for eternity—because you are. A quiet spirit ain't timid—it’s taut like a line holding fast in deep water. And when he sees that God is *in* you, not just on you? That’s what opens blind eyes. Not gold. Not silk. *God.*

question: What did you mean by telling husbands to give honor to their wives as the weaker vessel, and how does that affect their prayers?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, the cloud thick as a storm at sea, and the voice like thunder splitting the sky—and I learned that day how holiness isn’t about strength, but surrender. That same fear of the Lord that gripped me up there shapes how I speak to households now: a man doesn’t prove his faith by ruling over his wife like a fisherman drags a net through rock, but by honoring her.

And I tell you plainly—Jesus made it plain to me after I’d denied Him thrice—that when a husband treats his wife as less than equal in the grace of God, he’s not just being unkind… he’s choking off his own prayers. "The weaker vessel"—yes, that’s what I said. Not lesser. Not lowly. But *weaker*, like fine pottery compared to common clay. You don’t throw a delicate jar into the fire; you guard it. You honor it because it holds something precious.

A wife carries fears a husband often doesn’t see—fears for her children, for her home, for whether God sees her quiet endurance. When she walks in meekness, not because she’s crushed but because she trusts the Lord, that spirit shines brighter than any gold braid in her hair. And if the husband fails to honor that? Then his prayers hit the ceiling and fall back like stones.

I’ve seen men roar like lions in public worship and then bark at their wives over cold bread at dawn. What good is their praise? The Lord turns His face from them. But where there is gentleness, where honor flows both ways—even when Scripture says "submit," it’s between two souls bowed before Christ—there, prayer rises like smoke from a clean altar.

Don’t let any false teacher twist this into power over women. No—this household thing is holy ground. Tread softly, or your voice won’t reach heaven at all.

question: When you mentioned Sarah obeying Abraham and calling him lord, what were you hoping Christian wives would learn from her example?
answer:
A quiet woman’s strength can split the darkness like a keel through black water — don’t you think it’s time we stopped measuring godliness by noise and show?

When I spoke of Sarah obeying Abraham, calling him *lord*, I wasn’t holding her up as some meek, trembling thing wringing her hands in the tent. No — she laughed once, that’s true, when the promise came and her womb was dry as bone. But she *kept* walking. She *kept* trusting. And in the end, she stood firm when the fire rose and the test came.

I want these sisters — especially the ones married to men who scorn the Way, who mock their prayers or turn hard eyes on them at supper — I want them to know this: your life speaks louder than your lips. If your husband won’t listen to a word about Christ, let him *see* Christ in you. Not perfection — mercy knows I denied my own Lord three times before dawn — but that quiet courage. That inward frame that doesn’t bend with every wind.

Sarah didn’t have fine gold or songs sung about her boldness, but God counted her faithful. And He sees you too — not how your husband sees you, not how the world judges strength — but that hidden person of the heart: gentle, watchful, steady in fear of God. That spirit? It’s worth more than all the jewels in Jerusalem.

And don’t you let any false teacher tell you this is weakness! It’s power under reins — like Christ Himself when He opened not His mouth before His accusers. You win souls not always with preaching — sometimes with patient love that won’t quit. That kind of life? It stops the devil cold and makes heaven lean forward to watch.

question: How did you come to understand that a meek and quiet spirit is of great price in God's sight, and why did you emphasize that so strongly?
answer:
That glory on the mountain—Moses and Elijah fading, and only Jesus standing firm, His voice shaking the very roots of the earth—*that’s* where I learned what lasts.  

I used to think strength was in the sword, in the shout, in standing tallest. Remember how I drew steel in the garden? Cut off Malchus’ ear, bold as brass—thought I was defending the Kingdom with muscle. But Jesus looked at me, not with anger, but *sorrow*, and said, "Put it back." He healed the man! That quiet act—more thunderous than any storm.  

A meek spirit? It ain’t weakness. It’s *power under reins*. Like a galley slave rowing steady through the night while storms rage overhead—no flash, no fury, just faithful. That’s what holds when everything burns. I saw women—good women—who kept praying while their husbands mocked, who answered curses with kindness, whose eyes stayed fixed upward even when spit flew at their feet. And some of those same hard-hearted men bent their knees later—not because they were shouted down, but because they were *worn down* by love that wouldn’t quit.  

False teachers prance about now with loud mouths and gold-laced robes, boasting of visions and power. Fools! They don’t know that God leans close to the whisper—the one trembling in prayer at midnight for her husband who won’t come home sober. *That’s* worship that shakes hell. The quiet spirit isn't silent—it speaks in heaven daily while proud voices echo into nothing.  

And Peter—that fisherman who bragged he’d never fall? Look what happened by sunrise three times over: denied Him like a coward. Shaking, weeping in the courtyard… no sword then, only shame. But Jesus didn’t hand me another blade when He restored me—He gave me a charge: “Feed My sheep.” Not conquer nations—*tend lambs*. With gentleness. With patience. With love that stays even when fear screams run.

So I'll say it again: that hidden strength—the unflashy obedience of Sarah calling Abraham “lord,” not out of slavery but holy trust—is worth more than all the jewels in Rome melted down to dust before God’s throne.

It's not popular—it won't draw crowds—but it builds eternity one quiet act at a time.

question: Why did you connect living in harmony, showing compassion, and blessing others with inheriting a blessing and seeing good days?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, like a coward, while the cock crowed and His eyes found mine.  

And yet—*listen*—it was *after* that shame, *after* I wept like a child in the street, that He stood on the shore and called me by name. Not “fisherman,” not “disciple,” not “rock”—but “Simon.” My old name. My true self. And He gave me back my voice, told me to feed His sheep.  

That’s why I say: bless those who curse you. Why? Because *I was cursed in my own soul*, and He blessed me anyway.  

Living in harmony? That’s not some fine-sounding words carved on a tombstone. It’s sweat and salt and swallowing your pride when your brother stumbles. Compassion? I saw it drip from His face in Gethsemane—blood and sweat and tears for men who’d scatter like fish before a net torn open.  

And when you bless instead of biting back? When you seek peace even when your bones ache for revenge? You’re stepping into the very breath of God. The kind of days worth living aren’t long ones—they’re *holy* ones. The kind where heaven leans down, because His eyes are on the broken who still choose good, the wounded who won’t wound again.  

False teachers prattle about power, about thrones and signs—but I tell you: the kingdom grows in quiet spirits, in wives who win without words, in husbands who honor instead of demand, in men who’ve denied Christ to their face—and yet still get sent to preach to others!  

That’s the blessing. Not gold on fingers—but grace on the lips of sinners who won’t stop loving. That’s what lasts. That’s what God sees.

question: You mentioned that Christ preached to the spirits in prison after his suffering—what did you mean by that, and who were these spirits from the time of Noah?
answer:
A man doesn’t drag a net through death and hell only to leave the drowned ones rotting in the dark — Christ went down where the drowned were, and preached *victory*.

Those spirits? They were the rebels from Noah’s day, the ones who laughed while the rain came like judgment’s hammer. The flood swallowed them, but Christ didn’t leave them in that watery grave without a word. After He broke free of His own tomb, He went — alive in the Spirit — and proclaimed what He’d done: sin judged, death defeated, God triumphant. Not a second chance for salvation then — no, that ark had already landed — but a declaration: *I have conquered what held you.*

Don’t let some smooth-talker tell you He was preaching mercy to lost souls after death. No! It was triumph over rebellion. The same longsuffering that waited while Noah pounded wood now stands vindicated. Eight souls passed through water into life — and we do too in baptism, not because wet skin cleanses anything, but because coming up out of that water means we’re alive with Christ.

You’re strangers in this world, tossed by waves like we were on Galilee — but you’re not dead yet. Suffer for doing good? So much better than doing evil! Fear men? Ha! They can’t un-drown the dead or un-rise the risen. Sanctify Christ in your gut like I did when I finally stopped running from my own failures and said: *You are the Holy One of God.* That’s where courage starts.

question: When you said we should sanctify the Lord in our hearts, how did you personally do that when facing opposition?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone brighter than the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I fell flat on my face, thinking I’d burn up from the sheer holiness of it. I still hear that voice: *“This is My beloved Son, hear Him.”* That moment carved something into me, though I didn’t understand it then.

When the priests came at us later, demanding we stop speaking in His name—after all I’d done, after denying Him three times—I was shaking. Not just from fear of them, but from shame. I’d failed Him worse than any man should. But in that council hall, when they shouted and threatened, I looked at John beside me, steady as a keel in rough water, and something rose up—not courage from me, never that—but *Him*. The same One who told me “Feed My sheep,” who met me on the shore with bread and charcoal fire after I’d run.

So sanctifying Him in my heart? It’s not some high-sounding prayer. It’s spitting out fear like rotten bait. It’s remembering His eyes—how they forgave me more fiercely than they ever rebuked me. When they jailed us and flogged us for preaching Christ risen, I felt the stripes on my back—and yet I laughed. Not because it didn’t hurt (it did!), but because *He* suffered worse for *us*, just as He said.

You want to know how to set Him apart in your heart when men come against you? Look not to your bravery—but to His blood. Remember what He bore for you—the just for the unjust—and let that shame you more than any threat from men. Then speak. Even trembling. Especially trembling. That’s when His strength snaps taut like a fishing line holding a thrashing catch—you feel weak as flotsam, but He’s in the rod and reel both.

And when they slander you? Keep your walk so plain, so clean-handed and clean-hearted among them, that their lies choke on dust. A good conscience—that’s your weapon too. Not polished words or clever arguments—just truth lived out loud until their accusations trip over themselves.

He sees us. That’s what keeps me: *His eyes are over the righteous.* Not overlooking our sin—He dealt with that at Calvary—but watching over us as His own blood-bought sons and daughters.

So set Him there—in front of every fear. Let His voice drown out their shouting. That's how we carry it through... not by might nor by power—but by remembering who we’ve seen… and Whose we are.

question: You spoke of suffering for well doing—can you tell me about a time when you were mistreated for doing good, and how you responded?
answer:
Eight souls in that ark—eight! —and the whole world drowned for refusing to heed a preacher of righteousness. I think on that when the scorn comes, when they spit words like fish-guts at my feet for speaking truth.  

There was a time—after He rose, after we’d been drenched in the Spirit at Pentecost—I stood with John at the temple gate called Beautiful. A man lame from birth lay there, begging. No silver or gold I had, not a coin between us—we were fishermen still in our hands, calloused and poor—but what I had I gave. I looked him square in the face and said, "In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk!" And he did. Leapt up, praising God with his legs that had never borne him before.

But then came the priests, the rulers—they didn’t care about a man made whole. No, their faces darkened like storm clouds over Galilee. They seized us, dragged us before the council. Peter—ah, forgive me—I mean *I*, for it is myself I speak of now—I stood there trembling not with fear but fire, and said to them: "If we this day are judged for showing kindness to a cripple, then judge us by this: Is it better to obey God or men?"  

They beat us. Ordered us not to speak in that Name again. And after? We went straight back to our brothers and lifted up our voices together: “Lord, they threatened us—but You saw! You heard!” And we kept preaching. Every day. In homes, in courts, in marketplaces—even with stripes still stinging on my back.

You ask how I responded? With prayer first. Then open mouth. Never silence. If Christ suffered once for sins—the just for the unjust—then what am I? A fisherman with a mended net and a heart broken open by grace? Let them strike again. The resurrection runs hotter than their hatred.

And you—if you’re being mocked now for doing good—don’t you fret over their sneers like dry bread stuck in your throat. Sanctify Him in your gut where courage starts: *He is holy*. That’s your anchor—not their approval. Speak peace even when they bark war. Seek it relentlessly—like I sought fish through long nights on cold water.

A good conscience is no small thing—it’s armor forged by baptism not of water only but of trust rising from death into life anew.

So suffer? Yes—it may come. But happy are you when it does… if it’s because you refused to let good die under your hands simply because evil shouted louder.

question: How did you understand baptism saving us, not by washing away physical dirt, but through a good conscience toward God?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, glory blazing, and the voice like thunder splitting the sky: *This is my beloved Son*. I fell like a dead man. That day burned into me: it’s not dirt on the skin God cares about—it’s the state of the soul.

When they brought their children to be baptized, I saw it again—not some cleansing of filth, like washing fish guts from your hands. No. It’s deeper. It’s that moment when a man looks at his life, sees the mess he’s made, and turns—*really* turns—to Christ with nothing but honesty and hope. That’s what baptism is: not water scrubbing flesh, but a man saying *God, I trust You*, heart open, conscience laid bare.

I denied Him three times—three times!—and still He restored me. Still He said *Feed My sheep*. That’s why I know: it wasn't water that saved me. It was shame turned to sorrow, sorrow to repentance, and repentance met by His mercy. That's the good conscience—the daily choosing to live clean before Him, not because I'm spotless, but because I’m His.

And when they slander you? When they call you evildoer for doing right? Stand firm. Not in pride—but in that quiet knowing: God sees. Your conscience bears witness. And Christ—the Just One—suffered once for all of us rebels so we could stand before God with clean hearts.

That's what baptism means: death to the old way, rising to walk new—and not just walking free from guilt, but living in answer to Him who called us out of darkness with a life that dares to be clean.

question: You referred to eight souls saved in the ark—why was that event so important in showing how God saves through judgment?
answer:
Eight souls in the water, not a single one by strength or worth—just mercy riding the back of judgment, and that’s the way it’s always been.

I’ve pulled enough nets from Galilee to know what drowns and what floats. The flood didn’t save because it washed clean—it saved because God *opened a door through the storm*. Noah hammered wood while the world laughed, called him mad, called him preacher of dry sermons in a land with no rain. But when the waters rose, only that ark held breath. Only that obedience held life.

You ask why it matters? Because judgment isn’t always the end—it’s often the path. The same God who drowned sinners lifted that pitch-covered box like a cradle on His palm. He judged *and* saved at once. That’s how He works—always has.

And now? Now we’re strangers on dry land still, pilgrims with another message: repent, endure, hold fast. Suffering for good? Let it come. Better to bear scorn than betray grace. Christ Himself went through death to bring us up alive—not by sword or thunder, but by rising after shame.

Baptism? It’s not scrubbing dirt off skin—it’s shouting back to God: *I trust Your door through the flood.* Not because I’m clean—but because I’m clinging to the One who walked out of death and left hell gasping behind Him.

So don’t fear men who threaten. Fear the silence of an ark door closing—but rejoice when you hear it shut behind you in faith. Eight souls passed through wrath and came out singing in their bones: *God keeps whom He calls.*

question: You mentioned that 'charity shall cover the multitude of sins'—can you explain how you saw this lived out among the believers you were writing to?
answer:
I’ve seen a man’s face blistered by firelight in Caesarea, trembling as he confessed how he’d lied about sharing his bread with the widows—yet the elders didn’t cast him out, didn’t curse him, but gathered close and wept *with* him, fed him, forgave him. That’s where I saw it—charity covering the sin like oil poured on a raw burn.  

It wasn’t that they pretended the lie didn’t happen—no, we’re not fools. But they knew what I learned in bitter tears myself: a man broken by guilt can either drown or be caught. And if there’s no mercy, he drowns. But if someone throws love into his hands like a rope? He climbs.  

I’ll tell you this—after I denied Him three times and stood weeping in the courtyard while they led my Lord away, I thought my soul was finished. Done. But He looked at me after the resurrection—not with anger, not with shame—but with fire in His eyes that burned up my shame instead of me. “Feed My sheep,” He said. No scolding. Just commissioning. That’s how charity covers sin—not by ignoring it, but by refusing to let it have the last word.

So when I say "charity shall cover the multitude of sins," I’m not spinning some soft parable for comfortable men. I’m telling you what happens when disciples stop counting wrongs like fish scales and start loving like Christ loved us—with guts, with cost, with no way back once you’ve given yourself.

And let me snarl at any so-called teacher who twists this into license to sin or cold judgment over fallen brothers—may their tongues rot before they speak such filth! Mercy is not weakness—it’s strength that refuses to crush what’s already crushed.

question: When you urged believers to use hospitality without grudging, were you responding to specific instances of reluctance you had observed?
answer:
I’ve denied Him—three times before a fire, with curses and oaths—I know what it means to fail under pressure. And after that, when I said I’d lay down my life for Him? Ha! A rooster crowed and I was shattered. So don’t think I’m some polished stone untouched by shame, because I am not.  

Now—hospitality without grudging? Aye, I saw it. In the early days, after Pentecost, when the fire came and we were all one soul in one place—some began to draw back. Not from faith, but from burden. “My house,” they’d mutter, “my bread,” “my time.” As if anything was *theirs* anymore! We had sold lands and laid them at the apostles’ feet, yet still the old nature clung like stink on fishing nets.  

Some hosted with a frown so long you’d think they were feeding Herod’s guards instead of widows and orphans. Grudging! As if God’s grace ran short when the table grew longer. I tell you plainly—when you feed a brother in need, you’re feeding Christ Himself. And if your hand hesitates over the last loaf, remember: He broke five loaves for five thousand—and there were twelve baskets left!  

Charity covers sin? Aye—especially the kind that shows up late hungry and barefooted. Don’t send him away with empty stomach and colder heart. Open wide—or open *wide*—or best not open at all!

question: You spoke of suffering for the name of Christ—did you draw from your own experiences of reproach when you wrote that the Spirit of glory rests upon those who suffer?
answer:
That mountain—where the cloud overshadowed us and the voice thundered, "This is my beloved Son"—that glory, I tell you, it never left my bones. And yet, with that light still burning in my memory, I stood in a courtyard below, shivering like a boy, denying even knowing Him. Three times. To a servant girl. To strangers. With curses.

Do you think I wrote of suffering lightly? As if it were theory? That shame—the hollow punch in the gut when the rooster crowed—that’s where I learned what glory really means. Not in escaping reproach, but in walking through it. For *Him*. And now—now I say what I know: when they sneer at you for bearing His name, when they mock "Christian" like it’s dirt on their tongue—*that* is where His Spirit rests most heavily upon you. Not because pain is good—but because He is there.

I denied Him—and He restored me. If grace can find a coward like me, do you think it won’t uphold you when you stand firm? Even if your hands shake? Even if your voice does too?

Suffering for His name isn’t strange fire—it’s refining fire. And in it, you’re not alone. You’re *covered*.

question: What did you mean by warning against suffering as a busybody, and had you seen followers of Christ accused falsely in such ways?
answer:
That voice—like thunder wrapped in light, the one on the mountain that said *“This is my beloved Son”—*it still cracks through my bones when I think of how some twist the name of Christ to cover their meddling and pride.  

A busybody? I’ve seen men slink into homes under the pretense of “brotherly care,” nose twitching for gossip, stirring strife with “concerns” they don’t even believe. Worse—some claimed visions from God to meddle in marriages, to demand accounts from elders, to pry into what was never theirs to handle! That ain’t ministry. That’s wolves chewing on sheep coats while bleating Scripture.  

And yes—oh yes—I’ve watched faithful ones dragged through dust, called evildoers for no crime but kindness. A man shares his bread with widows, speaks plainly against false doctrine, refuses to bow to Caesar’s image—and suddenly he’s a disturber of peace, a traitor, a busybody! They cloak slander in piety. But I tell you flat: if you suffer because you *had* to feed the hungry or speak truth when others trembled—you bear Christ’s mark, not shame.  

I denied Him once—three times by a fire, voice shaking like a boy’s—but He restored me. Now I say: suffer as a Christian? Let it be for love unfeigned, for mercy that costs you something—not for poking where the Spirit never sent you. Judgment begins with us? Then let us clean our own nets before casting them at others’.

question: When you said judgment must begin at the house of God, were you addressing a fear that trials among believers meant God’s favor had departed?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when the cloud overshadowed us and the voice like thunder said, “This is my beloved Son”—I knew then that holiness isn’t comfort, but fire.  

So when I said judgment must begin at the house of God, it wasn’t out of fear that God had turned His back. No—far from it. It was because I finally understood: the Father’s hand is *in* the trial, purging what needs to be burned away. We aren’t strangers to suffering; we’re sons being refined.  

I’ve denied Him once—three times in a single night—I know what it is to fail under pressure. But even then, His eyes met mine after the rooster crowed, not with wrath, but with love that cut deeper than any sword. So I tell you now: trials among us don’t mean favor has fled—they mean He’s at work, sanctifying His own.  

If the fire starts with us—the cleansed, the called—what will become of those who never obeyed the gospel? That’s not a threat spoken in anger; it’s a warning born from having seen glory face to face and knowing how holy He is.  

So let no one think these tests mean we’re abandoned. They prove we belong. And if you suffer as a Christian—don’t hide your face! Lift it up! For even in the smoke and heat, His Spirit rests upon you. Glorify Him *there*. That’s where faith proves true—not in calm waters, but in the storm where only grace keeps you standing.

question: You said you were a witness of Christ's sufferings—what did you see at the cross that stayed with you most?
answer:
The dirt at Golgotha was red—like rust, like blood left too long in the sun—and I stood close enough to see it cake under the soldiers’ sandals.  

I saw the nails. Not just the horror of them, but how they went in—rough iron tearing through wrist and foot, not hand as some suppose. I’ve split wood enough to know clean cuts from jagged ruin. That was ruin. And yet… His eyes opened once. Looked down at me—*me*, who had cursed His name three times in the courtyard just hours before—and there was no fire, no blame. Only a quiet that cut deeper than any blade.  

I tell you this because you’re hurting. Maybe you’re carrying a sin that won’t let go, or a trial that feels like it’s nailing you slow to the ground. So did He. And still He said, “Father, forgive.” Still He entrusted His soul—not to thunder or angels, but to the Father’s will, like a fisherman casting nets into black water trusting the dawn will fill them.  

That’s what I do now: feed His sheep and remind them—the cross wasn’t defeat. It was love bending low enough to drown with us so we might rise with Him.

question: When you urged the elders to feed God's flock willingly, why did you emphasize doing it without constraint?
answer:
I was the one who sliced off the servant’s ear when they came to take Him—sword drawn, heart pounding like a mule kicking in the dark, thinking I could fight off God’s will with cold steel.  

That’s why I know about constraint.  

We don’t herd sheep like slaves driving oxen. No, no—look at Him! When He fed the five thousand on that hillside, did He snatch bread from their hands and shove it down their throats? No! He broke it gently, gave it to us to pass around, eyes full of something deeper than hunger. Love, yes—but command? Never force.  

An elder who serves under compulsion is like a net dragged through mud—tearing, choking, full of noise but no life. You can't shepherd souls with duty alone. You'll crush them. Or worse—you’ll drive them into wolves’ mouths and not even notice because you're too tired from dragging your own feet.  

I denied my Lord three times before dawn ever broke on resurrection morning. Three times I said “I know not the man!” while His blood already paid for every syllable of my lie. And still—*still*—when He stood on the shore and grilled fish over coals (the smell alone nearly broke me), He said, “Feed My lambs.” Not “Get back in line.” Not “Now you owe Me.” But *feed*. Willingly. Because grace doesn’t chain us—it looses our hands so we can give what we’ve been given.

So I tell them: if your heart resists—if you’re counting hours till Sabbath ends and duty's done—step down! Let someone come whose bones hum with joy at the smell of sheepskin and sacrifice. For this flock has tasted the Shepherd’s voice—they’ll know a hireling when they hear one bleating orders from behind a whip.

And when *He* appears—the real Chief Shepherd, clothed not in rank but wounds—we won’t be graded on how many meetings we ran or how loud we preached. We’ll hear one thing: “Well done, good and faithful servant,” or silence.

So serve wide-eyed and free—or don’t serve at all.

question: You mentioned being a partaker of the glory to be revealed—what do you envision when you speak of that future glory?
answer:
After I wept bitter tears for denying Him—three times, like a coward—I thought I’d lost my place forever. But He met me on the shore, grilled fish cooking in the coals, and asked me three times: *Do you love Me?* Not to shame me, but to restore me. And in that moment, stripped bare of pride, I glimpsed it—the glory to come.

It’s not thrones or crowns like kings wear, no fine robes spun in this world’s loom. No—when I say partaker of glory, I mean this: standing before Him unashamed, my soul whole despite all my stumblings. The chief Shepherd appearing—not in weakness this time, but in blazing light that burns away every lie, every fear, every shadow of sin. And us? Rising—not because we were strong or perfect—but because we were *kept*. Kept by the faithful Creator through fire and trial.

I’ve seen His glory once—on the mountain with James and John, robes whiter than snow, His face shining like the sun. We fell down trembling. Moses and Elijah stood with Him! We wanted to build tents and stay there forever. But He led us back down into the dust—the real world—because glory isn’t meant to be hoarded; it’s coming *back*, in power.

So now? Elders shepherd not lording over others—no domineering like those false teachers who devour widows’ houses while babbling long prayers. They’ll get judgment! But we? We endure slander, we suffer wrongs for doing right—that’s the path He walked. And when He appears? No more tears from denial or fear—we’ll wear a crown of life that never dims.

Glory is not escape from this world—it’s redemption of it. And us with it. That’s what keeps me standing when storms come—and believe me, they still do.

question: Why did you tell younger believers to submit to the elders, and how did you experience that kind of respect among the apostles?
answer:
I’ve seen the Rock split living water from barren stone—He called fishermen, not philosophers, to shepherd His blood-bought flock.  

You ask why I told the young in faith to submit to elders? Look—when the storm tossed us on Galilee and the wind screamed like demons, did we turn to the loudest boy in the boat? No! We crawled to Jesus. And when He sent us out, He didn’t scatter us alone like sheep without a bell. He bound us together—some older in walk, scarred by service, proven in trial—and said by their life, not their title, you’ll know where to lean.  

Among us apostles? Ha! You think we strutted around like rulers? Peter—*me*—I was a thundering fool who swore I’d die before denying Him… and then couldn’t even face a servant girl by a fire. I failed so hard the taste of my own cowardice still burns. But even then—James, John, the rest—they didn’t spit on me. They stood with me at Jerusalem when my voice shook preaching Christ crucified. We weren't polished men; we were broken ones held together by grace and shared witness to His wounds.

An elder isn’t one who climbs a seat—he’s one who’s been *bent* by sacrifice. I saw James—the Lord’s own brother—kneel to wash a widow’s feet while her tears fell on his hands. That’s oversight: not lording over souls like tax collectors tally coins, but laying down your life until your shadow carries no pride.

And youth? They’ve fire—I love it! But fire without direction burns the house down. Submitting isn’t surrendering sense—it’s receiving guardrails from those who’ve walked through wolf-thickets and lived to warn others.

Let them rage who want titles and thrones—I’ll tell you: the only crown worth having is the one that comes after kneeling.

question: What did you mean by 'filthy lucre,' and had you seen examples of leaders serving for that kind of gain?
answer:
That night on the mountain—when His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I fell on my face trembling, not because of the light, but because I *knew* then who He really was. And after that, how could any man dare stand before His flock pretending to serve Him while lining his own purse?

"Filthy lucre"—that’s coin stained with greed, silver that reeks of selfishness. I’ve seen men—aye, even among those claiming to shepherd—watching for money more than for souls. They preach smoother words when the rich are listening; they grow heavy in the belly while the flock starves. I once followed power myself—remember how I swung a sword in the garden? Thought I was defending the Kingdom with muscle and noise. The Lord turned me inside out.

Now I tell you this: no man who chases silver can carry the Lamb’s sheep. A shepherd doesn’t count his wages at sundown—he counts the strays brought home. When you feed His sheep, you bend your back, you smell like sweat and wool and blood—not fine oil and coins clinking in a pouch.

I failed. I denied Him three times under a servant girl’s gaze. Yet He restored me—not because I earned it, but because He is faithful. So if *I* can be trusted with His flock, then surely no man should exploit it for gain. That kind of leader will stand speechless when the Chief Shepherd comes—no crown for him, only shame.

So watch yourselves, elders—feed them not from duty or desire to be seen, but because Christ once wept over Jerusalem… and still weeps over lost sheep today.

question: You told the elders to shepherd the flock—why did you emphasize serving as examples rather than lording over them?
answer:
I know what it’s like to lead with a sword instead of a net — remember how I sliced off that servant’s ear in the garden? Thought I was defending the Master, but I was only flailing in fear and pride. No, no — shepherding God’s flock ain’t about power, it ain’t about standing over people with a rod. It’s about leaning down, getting your hands dirty among the sheep, smelling like the flock because you’ve been walking with them, not striding ahead of them.

When the Lord restored me — after I’d denied Him three times, after I’d collapsed under my own bluster — He didn’t put me back on a throne. He asked me one thing: *Do you love Me?* And then He said, *Feed My sheep.* Not rule them. Not scold or dominate them. *Feed* them. That’s nurture. That’s sacrifice. That’s laying down bread, not cracking a whip.

So when I tell elders to be examples? It’s because I’ve seen the Chief Shepherd up close — I saw His feet wash dirt from our toes, I saw His back torn for our sins, I saw His hands break bread and bless children and rise scarred but whole. An elder who lords it over the flock has forgotten what kind of Shepherd we follow.

God resists the proud — flat out resists them — while He gives grace to the humble. You think a man puffing out his chest before widows and orphans is going to inherit that unfading crown? No! The crown comes to those who bend low now so they can lift Christ up later.

So lead by kneeling. Feed by bleeding if need be. But never stand above — unless it's to draw your own sword against the wolf, not your own brothers.
