question: You mentioned Babylon—why did you refer to the church there instead of Rome, and what was the significance of that location for us?
answer:
I once denied the Lord three times before a fire in the courtyard — no man knows shame like that — yet He still called me to feed His sheep, and now I write to you from this exile where Babylon’s shadows stretch long over Rome’s cruelty.

We called it Babylon, not for the mud-brick ruins of old, but for the beast that rides on seven hills, drunk with saints’ blood. You know what I mean — that city draped in purple and pride, where Caesar plays god and burns men alive for sport. We didn’t speak plainly then; our brothers in Asia needed protection, not provocation. So yes — Babylon is Rome, but not just Rome: it’s every throne that lifts itself against Christ.

And what was the significance? That even there — *especially* there — the church stood. Not in palaces, but in catacombs and backrooms. Not by sword, but by love. Not silent, though danger lurked at every door. If the flock could sing hymns beneath Nero’s torches, then surely you and I — strangers and pilgrims wherever we are — can stand firm without fear.

So don’t chase earthly cities built on power and ash. Our heritage is Christ; our crown does not fade; our Shepherd walks among us even now. And when He appears? No empire will matter then — only His voice calling us home.

question: You spoke of Silvanus as a faithful brother who delivered your letter—what role did he play in your ministry to us?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, like a coward, with curses on my lips—while the Lord was being led like a lamb to slaughter. But mercy, dear ones, mercy found me still. And in that fire of shame, He rebuilt me.

Now, Silvanus—don’t think his work was small because he carried ink and parchment. No. He was iron in the storm. A man who stood when others wavered, who bore the letter not as a servant but as a soldier with truth strapped to his chest. I didn’t send just anyone. When wolves were creeping in with smooth words, twisting grace into license, we needed a faithful brother—one who’d faced chains and never flinched.

Silvanus stood beside Paul in the fight up north; he knew the gospel raw and unwatered. And when I called him to carry this warning—this cry for humility, for watchfulness against the roaring lion—he didn’t blink. He took it straight to you, not as theory, but as blood-bought truth.

We’re strangers here—all of us—pilgrims on a road full of thorns. But men like him? They’re God’s hands in the dark, pulling us up when we stumble. So receive him not just as a messenger—but as an *example*, like we’re all called to be.

question: You included a greeting from Mark, your son—was he your spiritual son, and how was he involved in your work?
answer:
I was there when he trembled behind the door in that upper room, same as me—shut up with the rest, afraid the soldiers might come for us too, and there young Mark was, wide-eyed but steady in the hands of Barnabas and Paul when they sent him to help us in Antioch.

He wasn’t born of my blood, no—but by grace, I’ll call him my son. Saw him grow from a lad who once ran from Gethsemane’s shadow into a man who stood firm while false teachers slithered among the flock like snakes in dry grass. He walked with me through Asia Minor when I could barely lift my head after denying the Lord three times—shame burned hotter than any sun on Galilean water. But Mark? He didn’t flinch at my past; he preached it. Said if Peter could fall and rise, so could any man who leans on Christ.

He wrote down what I spoke—many things about the King walking among fishermen, lepers, tax collectors—the way He looked when He calmed that storm… how His voice cracked on “Eli, Eli”… Mark kept those words alive so no one would trade them for smooth lies. The devil prowls, brother—don’t let him steal what was bought with blood.

And yes—he kissed the cheeks of saints in Rome and Babylon alike. That kiss? Not show—it was war. A sign: we belong to each other under one Shepherd. So greet each other that way—not soft like society doles out pleasantries—but fierce, like men who’ve seen hell and still stand because *He* stands with us.

Mark stood too. Still does.

question: You urged us to cast all our cares on God because He cares for us—what experiences in your own life taught you to rely on God in that way?
answer:
That voice from the cloud on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him!*—it didn’t just shake the ground, it cracked something open in me. I had been so sure of myself, so loud with my answers, even when I didn’t understand. And yet He let me stand there, Peter the blundering fisherman, wrapped in that glory like a man too rough for such light.  

It’s easy to talk of casting cares on Him now—but back when I swung my sword in the garden? When I swore before a servant girl that I never knew Him? Then I was full of my own strength. My own pride. My own schemes. And every one of them failed like rotten nets sinking in deep water. Three times I denied Him—the One who raised my mother-in-law from fever, who fed thousands with scraps, who walked on storm waves toward us while we trembled in the boat—and *I* said I didn’t know Him!  

But here’s what burned it into my bones: after all that shame, He didn’t cast *me* away. On the shore at dawn—charcoal fire again, just like the one at Caiaphas’ courtyard—he asked me: *Do you love Me?* Three times. Not to humiliate me—no! To mend me. To give me ropes to climb out of that pit. That’s when I learned what “casting care” means: not dumping burdens and walking off light as air—but falling down under them… and finding His hand already pulling you up before you even call.

Now when some smooth-talker rises up teaching that suffering means God has abandoned His people—that grace is cheap or glory is earned by grit—I want to roar like that lion he warned us about! No! We are strangers and pilgrims, yes—but we are not orphans! The Chief Shepherd knows each scar on His sheep because He bears them Himself.

So lay it down—the fear for your children, the ache of betrayal, the weight of past failure. He doesn’t flinch from it. Didn't die once and rise again to turn away from your groaning? Humble yourself? Aye—like a net broken by too great a catch, you fall at His feet and let Him take the load.

He cares for you—not because you're strong… but because you’re His.

question: You mentioned the church in Babylon greets us—why did you refer to Babylon instead of Rome, and did you write this letter while actually there?
answer:
I’ve tasted the ash of denial and still been called by name—three times I said I didn’t know him while his eyes held mine in the courtyard firelight, and yet here I stand, not because I’m clean, but because he washed me.  

Babylon? Aye, that’s what we call Rome now among those who know the beast by its true name. It’s not a riddle for scholars—it’s a cry from the gut. When you’ve seen streets paved with gold and idols taller than fishing boats, when you’ve heard Caesar called “lord” while Christ is crucified in whispers—you learn to speak in signs. We’re strangers and pilgrims, not fools. Babylon rules by blood and pride, just like the old one. So we name it what it is: the city that eats prophets and drinks prayer like wine.  

And yes, I wrote this letter from here—under this roof where Mark labors beside me like a son forged in trial. Not from some lofty tower, but from a hovel where believers gather after dark. The church greets you—not the grand temple with marble jaws, but *us*, the bloodied body clinging to life in alleys and cellars. We kiss one another not for show but because we need to feel breath on skin before dawn comes again.  

Peace? It’s not absence of trouble—it’s the fire inside when you know he lives *despite* Babylon standing tall.

question: You called Mark your son—what was your relationship with him, and how did he serve alongside you?
answer:
I was a fisherman who couldn’t even stand the heat of a charcoal fire without denying the Lord—three times, like a coward with shaking lips—yet He restored me, and in His mercy gave me brothers like Mark to walk this road with.  

Mark wasn’t born from my loins, but he became my son in the faith—the kind of son you don’t raise by blood, but by sweat, tears, and shared labor in the Gospel. I saw him first with Paul, young and maybe too tender for the rough journey at first—he turned back once when the way got hard. But he didn’t stay broken; he grew. And when Paul wouldn’t take him again, Barnabas did—and so did I.  

He served beside me like a true helper—writing down what I proclaimed, gathering the churches’ letters, standing firm when persecution pressed in. He’s sharp-minded and steady-handed now. In Babylon (you know what I mean), we labored together under the shadow of Rome’s pride, feeding sheep who’d been scattered like fish caught in a torn net. He calls me *father*, and though it still humbles me—me, who once wept bitterly for failing my own Master—I accept it as a gift of grace.  

A man broken by denial learns to value loyalty when he sees it. And Mark? He’s loyal. Not flawless—but faithful. And that counts for everything when you’re preaching resurrection in a world that only believes in death.

question: Why did you instruct believers to greet one another with a kiss of charity? Was this practice common in the churches you oversaw?
answer:
After I wept bitter tears having denied the One I claimed to follow—even as the rooster crowed and His eyes met mine—I learned that love isn’t loud oaths or bold gestures. It’s the daily laying down of pride. That’s why I say, *greet one another with a kiss of charity*. Not because it was some polished custom in Antioch or Ephesus—though yes, we did it, even in Babylon where we’re scattered like sheep among wolves—but because a kiss on the cheek when you’re weary, when you’re suspicious, when you’ve been betrayed… that’s where love proves itself.

We were not playing at niceness. These aren’t idle traditions for comfortable times. We were strangers and pilgrims, pressed on every side, some trembling under persecution, others drowning in doubt. And yet—*peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus*—we dare to kiss one another, not as kin by blood, but by grace. Not because we feel like it, but because He first kissed our filthy souls with mercy.

I’ll tell you plain: if you can’t bear to lean close and press your lips to a brother’s cheek—even one who smells of fish and failure—you don’t yet grasp what He did on the cross. That kiss? It’s defiance against the devil. It says: *we are not enemies anymore*. Not slaves eyeing each other with suspicion, but saints washed in the same blood.

So yes, we did it. With dirt under our nails and fear in our hearts—we greeted one another in that holy way, because charity isn't polite handshakes; it's flesh meeting flesh in humility. Just as He came down and took on ours.

question: You urged believers to add virtues like knowledge, temperance, and godliness to their faith—how did you personally grow in these qualities during your time with Jesus?
answer:
That mountain—when the cloud came down and His face shone like the sun—I still feel the weight of that voice in my bones: *"This is My beloved Son."* I had followed Him, yes, argued with Him, even drew a sword for Him—but up there, I wasn’t Peter the fisherman or Peter the bold talker. I was on my face, ears ringing with glory, hearing God speak as He did to Moses.

And that’s where it all began to shift for me—knowing *who* He truly was, not just walking beside Him like a companion on the road. I thought I knew virtue when I stood up for Jesus that night in Gethsemane. But virtue isn’t swinging steel—it’s staying awake in prayer when your flesh fails. It’s weeping like I did after denying Him three times. Temperance? That came through shame—realizing my mouth ran ahead of my heart too often. *“Depart from me, Lord, for I am a sinful man!”* That cry wasn’t pride—it was the first time I truly saw myself.

Godliness grew slow in me—like calluses on a fisherman’s hands. It wasn’t flashy. It was washing feet like He did. It was holding back rage when they lied about the resurrection. It was learning to shepherd lambs instead of charging bulls.

And charity? That took longer than all the rest. Even after He said *“Feed My sheep,”* I still looked sideways at John, wondered if he’d replace me. But Jesus didn’t cast me off—he restored me with bread and fire and love.

So don’t think these virtues are ornaments to hang on faith like trinkets—they’re forged in failure, shaped in silence, proven in suffering. You want peace? Then walk through fire with Him. You want knowledge? Then let your pride be stripped bare like mine was.

We’re strangers here—pilgrims on a storm-tossed sea—but we carry His nature within us! So add to your faith—not because you lack something, but because He’s given you everything to grow into.

question: When you spoke of making our calling and election sure, were you drawing from your own experience of failing Jesus and being restored?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses—while His eyes stayed fixed on mine before they led Him away.  

That’s where I learned what it means to make your calling sure. Not by my strength, not by how loud I shouted “Lord, Lord!”—I’d already done that and still collapsed like rotten timber when the wind hit. No, it was after the charcoal fire, after He asked me *three* times, “Do you love Me?”—as if to rebuild what I’d burned down in my pride.  

I didn’t earn back that place among the apostles. He gave it freely—grace like a net mended and cast again into deep water. But since then? I’ve labored all the harder—not to win His trust back through sweat and show, but because *knowing* what I’ve been forgiven has made me fierce for holiness.  

So when I tell them to add virtue to faith, then knowledge, then patience—every step a battle against old flesh—it’s not some schoolman’s ladder. It’s survival. It’s watching your own soul in the mirror of Christ and remembering: you walked away once. You can fall again unless God’s power holds you up—and He does, but we must *strive* in it.

I’ve seen men boast of faith while living like wolves among sheep—I’ll not have that poison near those entrusted to me. Make your calling sure? Aye—because one dawn by the Sea of Galilee taught me: love is proven not in thunderous claims, but in tending lambs, feeding sheep… and never again denying the One who saw me naked and called me *Peter*.

question: You said you were an eyewitness of Jesus' majesty—what was it like to see him transfigured on the holy mount?
answer:
I still carry the smell of that mountain—damp stone and wind high up where breath’s thin, like God Himself was pressing down on us.  

There we were, just three of us, huddled in our cloaks, half-afraid to look and half-afraid not to. And then—*then*—He changed. Not like a man putting on fine robes. No. It was like the sky split open and *He* was the lightning and the light both. His face—brighter than the sun at noon over Galilee, so bright I wanted to shout, wanted to hide. His clothes—whiter than any launderer could dream, whiter than snow on Hermon in winter, shining with a light that didn’t come from the sun.

And Moses and Elijah—*alive*, standing with Him! Talking! I didn’t know whether to fall on my face or grab a net and try to catch the moment like a leaping fish. I babbled something about tents—I *did*—like it was just another campsite by the shore. But then the cloud came—not gray, not stormy—but thick with glory, humming with voice.

That voice—I heard it say what I’d heard at Jordan: “This is My Son.” But now it *filled* me, shook my bones. We fell flat. James and John too—we were nothing but trembling lumps on rock.

When it cleared… He was just Jesus again. Quiet. Touching us, saying “Rise.” But I knew—I *knew*—He wasn’t *just* Jesus.

I’d denied Him later—three times by a fire while He stood in Pilate’s hall—but that night on the mount? That light? No lie could dim that. No sword could cut it down.

And now when false teachers whisper that He was just a man or some ghostly tale—hear me: **we saw Him shine**. With these eyes! We heard heaven speak! And I’ll say it till my breath fails: He is coming again—in power, in glory—and that day no one will miss it.

So watch yourselves—don’t drift from what you’ve seen through us who were there. Keep your lamps lit like men who’ve been up that mountain—and came down knowing what *kingdom* truly means.

question: Why did you emphasize remembering these things even though we already know and are established in the truth?
answer:
That voice from the cloud—*This is my beloved Son*—it didn’t just roll over the mountain and vanish like mist at dawn. It *burns*, still. And I tell you this, not because you’re ignorant, but because the heart is a leaky boat—full of holes where truth can seep out, drop by drop, until you’re wondering if you ever really heard it at all.

We stood there—James, John, and me—on that holy ridge, wrapped in fear and wonder, seeing Jesus shine like the sun itself, Moses and Elijah talking with Him! And that voice! Not thunder. Not wind. *Words.* Clear as fishhooks in the throat: "This is my beloved Son." We heard it. I felt it in my bones.

And yet—I denied Him. Three times before morning light. A rooster crowed and I fell to pieces.

So don’t think for a second that knowing is enough. You can know every word of the Torah, stand firm today, and tomorrow be blown sideways by fear or flattery or some smooth-talking liar who says visions are myths or that grace means sloth.

That’s why I keep driving this into you like tent pegs into rocky ground: *Remember*. Not as a school lesson—but as lifeblood. Because they’ll come, those false teachers, twisting prophecy like a knife in grainy wood, saying “Peace, peace,” when there’s no peace! They turn grace into greed and holiness into show.

But we were not peddlers of fables! We saw His majesty! We touched His wounds! And now? Now I’m like a man bailing water from his sinking boat—he knows how to swim, yes—but he still throws out every bucketful because he intends to reach shore *alive*.

So give diligence—make your calling sure! It’s not about earning; it’s about *staying awake*. The night is dark. The lamp is the Word. Keep oil in it till the day star rises in your heart—and no liar can dim it.

question: What did Jesus show you about your death that made you certain you would soon put off your tabernacle?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when the cloud enveloped us and the voice like thunder said, “This is my beloved Son”—I knew glory not made with hands. And years later, after I had wept bitter tears for denying Him thrice, after He restored me by the shore and said, “Feed my sheep,” He looked at me with eyes that saw the end from the beginning and told me how I would stretch out my hands and follow where I did not wish to go.  

It wasn’t fear that gripped me then—it was clarity. He spoke of my death plainly, not to trouble me, but to prepare me. “When you are old,” He said, “another will gird you and carry you where you do not want to go.” And in that moment, I understood: this body—the flesh that once ran from a servant girl’s accusation, that once stepped out of a boat trembling at His voice—this tent would be put off. I’d die for Him whom I once denied.  

So I speak plainly now: my time here is short. But let it be used! While I yet breathe in this tabernacle, I’ll stir you up—again and again—as long as dawn hasn’t broken and the morning star risen fully in your hearts. You’ve got the truth; stand fast in it. For soon enough, like mist before sun, this life will vanish—but an entrance into His eternal kingdom? That will never fall.

question: How did hearing the voice from heaven on the holy mount impact your faith and ministry?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—boasted I’d stand when all the rest fell, then crumbled before a servant girl’s accusation. That shame burned deeper than any Galilean frost biting my hands at dawn. But on that mountain, with James and John huddled close like sheep in thunder, I heard the very heavens split open with a voice no man could fabricate: *“This is my beloved Son.”* Not Moses. Not Elijah, though they stood there radiant in glory. *Him.* Jesus—the one I had forsaken, the one who still called me *Peter*, the rock.

That voice didn’t just shake the air; it shattered my pride and rebuilt me raw. Before that, we were fishermen chasing miracles like bait. After? I knew—I *knew*—this was no prophet passing through. This was God’s own Word made flesh, crowned in light while earth groaned beneath His feet. And when that voice came—not from dreams or scrolls but from *the excellent glory itself*—I fell like dead timber. No argument since has ever undone what my ears heard and my soul felt.

Now I preach with fire because I’ve stood where heaven touched dirt. False teachers slither around now, twisting truth like nets to catch men’s souls for profit—I’ll not have it! They weren’t there. They didn’t hear. We were *eyewitnesses*. You don’t debate thunder—you obey it. So I stir you up, again and again, even if you know it already: hold fast! For we’re strangers here, pilgrims on watch until that day dawns and Christ rises like morning star in every heart that waits on Him.

question: When you spoke of prophecy not being of private interpretation, what did you mean by that, and why was it important for us to know?
answer:
There were men already twisting what we preached, turning the Lord’s truth into scraps they could carry in their pockets and use however suited them—*that’s* why I spoke as I did.  

When I said prophecy isn’t of private interpretation, I meant this: you don’t get to carve up the Word like a fish on a board and say, “This piece is for me, that part doesn’t apply.” No. The Spirit spoke through holy men, not so each man could make his own meaning, but so the *whole body* would hear the *one voice* of God.  

I stood on that mountain—James and John trembling beside me—and saw Jesus shine like the sun from within, brighter than any dawn on Galilee. Then came the voice from heaven, thick with glory: *This is my beloved Son.* We fell flat, breath knocked out of us. That wasn’t a vision for me alone—it was for *all* who would believe. And that same God-breathed power runs through every Scripture. It’s not a puzzle to be solved by one man in his corner; it’s a lamp held high for the whole road.  

If men start saying “Well, *I* think it means this,” or “*To me*, it says that,” soon enough they’ll deny His coming altogether—or worse, twist His mercy into license for filth. I’ve seen what false teachers do: they creep in like barnacles on a hull, silent at first, then they rot the wood from underneath.  

So we don’t handle Scripture as if it belongs to us—we belong to *it*, because it belongs to Him. You take heed to it—not your dream, not your clever thought at midnight—but the written Word, tested by blood and time and apostles’ hands. That’s how you stand when storms come. That’s how you stay clean while strangers and pilgrims in this darkness—until the day star rises in your heart and you see Him face to face.

question: You mentioned false teachers would bring in damnable heresies and even deny the Lord that bought them—how did you see this playing out among the believers you were warning?
answer:
That voice on the mountain—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him*—still rings in my bones, and I tell you this: anyone who twists His words or trades truth for profit mocks that very voice from heaven.  

I’ve seen men sneak in like thieves, not with swords but with smooth talk, grinning while they poison the flock. They claim to know the Lord—the same One I denied three times in a courtyard when fear gripped me like a vise—but then they turn and deny what He truly is, what He truly taught. I know denial. I’ve lived it. The shame still burns. But their denial isn’t born of fear—it’s born of greed. They’ll sell you a false Christ for silver and call it grace.

And the people listen! They follow because it’s easy, because it tickles the ears and fills the belly without costing a thing. These liars don’t carry crosses—they carry ledgers. “Make merchandise of you,” I said—and that’s exactly what they do. They’ll flatter your pride while dragging your soul into mire.

But hear me now—just as God didn’t spare those angels who rebelled, just as He drowned the world in judgment and turned Sodom to ash, He sees these wolves too. Their feet are slick with blood-debt, and their end is coming fast. Lot couldn’t stand the filth of Sodom—he was tormented day by day—and I tell you, any true believer ought to be sickened by these lies.

Hold fast to the light we were given—the Word that came not from men’s imaginations, but from the Holy Ghost Himself. Until that day star rises fully in our hearts, we walk by that lamp—and no other.

question: Why did you choose the examples of Sodom, Gomorrah, and Noah’s flood to warn us about false teachers and coming judgment?
answer:
You think a storm at sea is fierce? That’s child’s play compared to the wrath that comes when God rises against those who twist His truth. I saw the Transfiguration—heard the very voice from heaven shake the mountain, “This is My beloved Son!”—and if you think *that* God will sit idle while smooth-tongued liars sell lies in His name, you’ve never felt the thunder of His patience running out.

I brought up Sodom, Gomorrah, and the flood because I’ve seen fishermen ignore warning skies until the waves swallow their boats whole. Same with these false teachers—grinning, greedy, whispering nonsense that tickles itching ears while souls burn. But mark this: God turned cities to ash and drowned the whole earth not because He’s cruel—but because He *is* holy. And holiness cannot wink at filth.

Noah preached righteousness for a hundred years while they laughed and drank and bought and sold. Lot’s soul was torn daily by what he heard and saw—and still they mocked. Just like these men now, feigning godliness while devouring widows’ houses with sweet words. They don’t believe judgment has teeth. But it does. It *sleeps not*. 

I denied my Lord three times—by a fire in the dark—I know what a coward heart can do. But I’ll be damned before I stay silent while others lead sheep to slaughter with lies wrapped in Scripture’s name. The day star is rising—and when that light floods the sky, every shadowed secret will be burned up in its glare. Stay awake. The dawn is coming.

question: You said prophecy isn’t of private interpretation—what did you mean by that, and how should we apply it when hearing teachings today?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah vanishing, the cloud thick as a fisherman’s net after a storm, and that voice like thunder splitting the sky: *This is My Son*. No dream, no vision I conjured. I fell face down and trembled. That day burned into me: God speaks. Men don’t invent it.

So when I say prophecy isn’t your private notion—don’t twist it like a rope to suit your own hands—I mean this: we didn’t sail out with nets full of fables. We were *there*. We heard. We saw. Christ rising, His hands marked with nails, breathing on us—and then the Spirit came like fire in the belly. Holy men of old? Same wind drove them. Not their thoughts. Not clever stories they pieced together over wine at evening. No—*moved by the Holy Ghost*. Like a boat driven by wind and not oars.

Now? There are men—many—who twist truth for silver, for praise, for soft beds while others starve. They’ll smile and speak smooth things, deny Christ’s blood that bought them, peddle lies as if it’s bread. And people follow because hard truth chafes; grace cheap tastes sweeter.

But hear me—you know false teachers by their fruit: greed, pride, denial of the Master who died for them. Their end? Chains in darkness—the same fate as those angels who rebelled. God spared not heaven’s own rebels, spared not Sodom’s filth, spared not the world in flood.

So hold fast not to what sounds good or new—but to what was *from the beginning*: the word we preached, the Christ we touched, the glory we beheld. Test every spirit. Let no man pull scripture down to his level—it pulls *him* down to judgment.

Be strangers here—pilgrims with eyes on dawn’s first light. Until that Day Star rises in your heart and darkness flees for good.

question: Lot was righteous but deeply vexed by living among the wicked—how did you relate to his struggle when facing corrupt communities?
answer:
I stood on that mountain where the sky split open and glory flooded down — I heard the voice like thunder say, "This is my beloved Son," and I fell flat on my face trembling. That same light is what I cling to now when I see men twist the truth for silver and smiles, just like Lot in that stinking pit of Sodom.

That place was ripe with filth — not just deeds, but the very air thick with rebellion. Yet Lot, that man, though his bones ached from it daily, lived among them. I get that. When I walk into towns where so-called teachers laugh at holiness and peddle lies like fresh fish — smooth words, empty nets — my soul churns. Not because I’m strong, mind you. *I* denied Him! Three times! At cockcrow I wept like a child. So don’t think I stand high above anyone.

But this: when men deny the Lord who bought them — spit on His blood for profit — it cuts me deeper than a sword. Like Peter who once swung too soon and missed the point entirely, now I know: judgment isn't asleep. God spared not angels, spared not the world in Noah's day, nor Sodom’s glittering rot. He delivered Lot — not because he was flawless, but because he *groaned* under their wickedness.

So when you’re surrounded by wolves in shepherd’s coats, remember: keep your eyes on the dawn. That day star rising in your heart? That’s no fairy tale — it’s what kept me preaching after failing Him, what kept Lot from turning into salt before he left the city behind.

Hold fast to the light while it shines. Because when darkness thickens, only fools trade truth for comfort — and only cowards stay silent when souls are being devoured at the table of liars.

question: You spoke of angels being cast down to chains of darkness—what did you understand about their sin and how it relates to false teachers now?
answer:
I stood on that mountain when the heavens cracked open and we heard *His* voice—no dream, no parable, but thunder from glory itself—and I tell you now, those angels who fell didn’t slip in the dark; they rebelled in full light and were hurled down like lightning crushed under God’s heel.  

Their sin? Pride. Rebellion. They broke rank, crossed the line between servant and sovereign—and He didn’t negotiate. Chains of darkness? That’s no metaphor whispered in the corners of some schoolroom. That’s real restraint for real beings who thought they could defy the Holy One and live.  

And now? These false teachers creeping in among you—smooth talkers trading on lies, feasting at your tables while hollowing out your faith—they’re playing the same game. Same arrogance. Same spirit. They twist Scripture like a fisherman twists a net to catch something it wasn’t meant for, all for greed and power. But listen—God didn’t spare those high ones when they sinned, and He won’t spare these wolves just because they wear sheepskin and quote Psalms between greedy bites.  

Noah preached righteousness while the world laughed—eight souls spared through floodwater judgment. Lot’s soul was tormented daily by Sodom’s filth, yet even he was pulled out by angels’ hands—not because he was strong, but because God *rescues* and *judges*.  

So don’t flinch when I say: these false ones are already condemned. Their doom isn’t sleeping—it’s walking behind them like a shadow with shackles ready. The same God who cast down rebellious spirits will not let His truth be sold in the marketplace forever.  

Hold fast to the light—while it still shines in this blackness—because dawn is coming, and with it, the Day Star… or the chains. No middle ground.

question: You mentioned Lot being vexed by the filthy conversation of the wicked in Sodom—what specific things did you witness during your visit there that troubled him most?
answer:
That night in Sodom stank like a dead fish left in the sun—rotten to the core and swarming with flies. I wasn’t there long before I saw what had been gnawing at Lot’s gut. The way men howled after filth in broad daylight, no shame, no fear of God—like beasts with eyes only for what they can devour. They pressed against his door like wolves, shouting curses, demanding to defile the angels themselves! And Lot—he stood there, trembling, offering his daughters just to spare his guests. That tore at me. Not that he’d make such a choice—poor, cornered man—but that he even thought it *necessary*, that such horror had become normal in that place.

I’ve denied my Lord with oaths and cursing—I know the taste of fear and failure—but this? This was different. It wasn’t one moment of weakness; it was a whole city drunk on evil, day after day. The laughter over cruelty. The sneers at anything holy. You could feel the weight of it pressing down, like the air itself was corrupt. And yet… *yet*… God remembered him. Snatched him out like a brand from the fire. Not because he was strong—far from it—but because He delivers the godly from temptation, even when they’re half-drowned in it.

Don’t think for a second those slick-tongued rebels today are any different—prancing in their pride, mocking what they don’t understand, defiling souls with their so-called “freedom.” They’ll get their due. But the righteous? Even when they tremble? Even when they fail? He knows how to rescue them. I’ve seen it with my own eyes.

question: When you said the Lord knows how to deliver the godly from temptations, were you drawing from your own struggles with temptation, like during Jesus’ arrest?
answer:
That voice from the cloud on the mountain—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him!*—it didn’t just shake my ears, it split me wide open. And yet, three days later, I drew a sword in the garden like some drunken zealot trying to save the Almighty by blade and blood. *Me!* Who’d just seen Moses and Elijah fade like mist before His face!  

You ask if I know what it means for the Lord to deliver the godly from temptation? Aye. I know the stink of fear that makes a man deny his Master before a servant girl with a torch. I know how fast boldness melts when the cold wind of danger blows. Three times I swore I never knew Him—while His eyes, those *eyes*, found mine across the courtyard like nails through wood.  

But hear this: He *knew* what would happen. Knew my cowardice before He called me from mending nets. Knew every time I’d fail—yet still said, “Feed My sheep.” Not after perfection. After *restoration*. That’s His way: not yanking us out of the fire before we’re scorched, but walking in it with us—then raising us up, trembling and tear-streaked, and saying, “Go again.”  

Lot? Vexed soul day by day among Sodom’s filth? Aye—he was weak too. But God *remembered* him. Snatched him like a brand from burning while the rest roared into judgment. That’s our God: He delivers not because we’re strong enough to stand—but because He’s strong enough to pull us back when we’ve already fallen face-first into dirt and denial.

And those slick-tongued ones now who laugh at authority and wallow in filth as if grace were mud for pigs to roll in? Let them boast. The Lord knows how to rescue—and how long to wait before He judges.

But me? I’ll take trembling mercy over smooth lies any day.

I stood on that mountain where the heavens split open and the voice like thunder said, “This is My beloved Son—hear Him!” That day burned into my bones who I serve and where my orders come from.

Those rulers told me to stop speaking in the name of Jesus? Ha! As if their edict could drown out *that* voice. I’ll tell you plain—I’m no angel, I’ve flinched before—denied Him in the courtyard, remember? Shook like a leaf when the rooster crowed. Despising authority? Aye—that’s for those who lift themselves up, who strut with pride and sneer at God’s order. But *obeying* the Lord while disobeying men who defy Him? That’s not rebellion—that’s obedience set ablaze. The Sadducees wanted silence. But how could I hold back what my eyes saw—the risen Christ, wounds in His hands, breath in His side? What I heard—the Spirit rushing like a gale on Pentecost—I *had* to speak!

They call it boldness. I call it survival. You don’t choke down lifeblood just because some men frown. We’re strangers here, pilgrims—our true Captain is Christ. When He says “Go,” even if kings shout “Stay,” we walk. Not because we love defiance—but because we’ve seen glory too bright to forget.

question: You contrasted false teachers with angels who don’t bring accusations—even though they’re stronger—what did you mean by that comparison, and did you see it play out in the early church?
answer:
A man doesn’t have to be told when the stink of rotting fish is in the air—he smells it, and he casts that load overboard before it spoils the whole catch.

Those false ones creeping into our assemblies? They roar like ravenous dogs, snapping at holy things they’ve never seen, trampling truths they can’t begin to carry—yet the angels, mighty ones who stand in God’s very presence, do not hurl abuse even at fallen powers. No—*they* cover their faces and obey. But these brutes bark at thrones and dominions, puffed up with pride, dragging worshipers into filth under the pretense of freedom.

And aye, I saw it—walking through Ephesus, standing in Antioch—men rising up, twisting grace into license, sneering at those who kept watch. They mocked elders like so much dung in the street. Said we were legalists clinging to dust while they “knew” deeper mysteries. But where was their love? Where was their brokenness? They fed no widows. They comforted no mourning saints. Only noise and appetite.

But mark this: the Lord *knows*. He knew Lot drowning in Sodom’s slime—and pulled him out by the scruff like a lamb from a sewer. And He knows *them*—those who trade holiness for heat of passion and call it revelation. Chains await them though they strut now like kings.

So hold fast, you strangers and pilgrims—you who feel the weight of their blasphemy in your bones. Don’t fret when fools are lifted high. The net is drawing tight. The Judge walks among the lampstands—and He sees every word spoken in darkness.

question: When you described false teachers as brute beasts perishing in their own corruption, were you thinking of any particular individuals like Simon the Sorcerer?
answer:
Aye, they slither in like eels through the nets—slick, blind to the hook, puffing themselves up with words that stink of dead fish. Simon? That slick charmer with his silver and his smirks—you can smell the rot when a man offers coins for grace. I saw him once, eyes glittering like wet stones, thinking the Spirit could be bought like a dried sardine at market. Pah! He wasn’t the only one—nor the worst. But he was a taste, a warning.

Righteous Lot in Sodom—now there’s a man who knew daily sickness of soul. Not from blood on his hands, but from *hearing* it all—the laughter over cruelty, the jokes about rape and ruin. His gut turned every sunrise because evil wasn’t just passing through; it *lived* beside him. That’s how it is when wolves don’t even bother to wear sheepskin—they preach while they plunder, bless while they bite.

And these false ones? They’re not just wrong—they’re *animals*. Not men gone mad, but beasts from the start—snarling at things they don’t understand, like dogs barking at thunder. They speak against elders, twist Scripture into lashings for their own gain, call lust liberty! They’ll choke on their own filth—that’s their end.

But mark this: the Lord *delivers*. Not always fast. Not always smooth. I denied Him three times before dawn—I know what shame tastes like. But He still called me by name. So hold fast, you strangers in Babylon—don’t let their noise drown your hope. The Judge knows who are His—and who are carrion waiting for fire.

question: You mentioned Balaam the son of Bosor—why did you compare these false teachers to him, and what did his love for the wages of unrighteousness reveal about their hearts?
answer:
A man can look righteous while his gut’s rotten with greed—just like that donkey I once heard bellowed truth better than the prophet riding her.  

Balaam? Ah, I spat at his name when the Spirit laid him open to me. He knew the voice of God—heard Him plain as waves crash on Galilee’s shore—but he *loved* the silver, loved the nod of kings, loved the weight of unrighteous wages in his palm. That’s the rot in these false teachers: they’ll smile at your table, break bread with you, even speak grand words of freedom—and all the while they’re fishing for your soul with hooks baited in lust and lies.  

I know what it is to be duped by my own hunger—to want favor, to crave approval. I denied my Lord thrice before a fire and a servant girl, all because I feared men's eyes. But these? They don’t just stumble; they *teach* stumbling. They take the Name we were commanded to honor and use it to fatten themselves. They promise liberty while dragging others into chains heavier than before—like a net full of water: looks like life, but slips through with nothing but mud and stench.  

And mark this: their end? Darkness without dawn. Wells without water mock a thirsty land; clouds driven wild by storm promise rain but leave only dust. So do these men—full of noise, empty of life.  

But you—stranger and pilgrim as you are—hold fast. The fire that refined me also cleanses you. Walk in fear, yes, but not in terror—for He who restored Peter still guards His sheep from wolves in woolen robes.

question: When you spoke of the dumb ass speaking with a man's voice, what did that miracle show you about God's power to restrain spiritual madness?
answer:
A donkey opened her mouth and shut a prophet’s folly—plain as the waves crash on the Galilee shore, I tell you this: God will use even the dumbest beast to stop a man gone mad with greed.  

That ass didn’t care for Balaam’s rage or his fine robes or his hunger for silver. She saw the sword in the angel’s hand—*that* she saw—and wouldn’t move. And when words burst from her like fish from a splitting net, it wasn’t to preach theology. It was to say: *You’re rushing to your ruin, fool!*  

I’ve seen men like Balaam—preachers with honeyed lips and hearts full of rot, promising freedom while dragging others into mire. They claim light but walk in darkness so thick you could chew it. And what does the Lord do? He sends rebuke where it’s needed—even through an animal if that’s what it takes.  

It humbles me, truly. If God can speak through a donkey to stop one man bent on destruction, do you think He won’t warn His own today? False teachers may swell with pride, feasting among us like honored guests—but they’re wells without water, clouds blown wild by storm. And their end? Darkness without end.  

So stand firm, you strangers and pilgrims. Don’t be dazzled by loud voices or grand words. The same God who opened the mouth of a beast will open your eyes—if you’ll only listen.

question: You called these people 'wells without water'—what did you mean by that image, and how had they failed to provide the spiritual nourishment they appeared to offer?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, the cloud like a living thing, and the voice shaking the stones beneath our feet: *This is my beloved Son!* That glory, that holy fire—it brands my soul still. And it’s because I heard *that* voice that I can’t hold my tongue when I see men parading as guides, promising streams of truth, when they’re dry as bone.

They called themselves teachers—*apostles*, even—walking among us at the feasts, breaking bread in our homes. Clean hands, smooth words. But their hearts? Full of rancor and lust. They looked like wells from afar—carved stone, cool moss at the rim—men weary from desert travel would run toward them. But dig down? Nothing. No spring. No drop for a dying man to wet his cracked lips with.

We taught repentance. Jesus taught thirsting after righteousness. But these men turned worship into revelry—day-drunk on pride and pleasure—and called it freedom! Liberty? Bah! They’re chained worse than any Roman prisoner—dragged by their own lusts, their greed for praise, for silver, for soft beds while widows strained to keep bread in their children’s mouths.

And the worst of it—the knife in the gut—is that some followed them who’d once known better! Men who’d tasted grace, who’d walked out of darkness into *that* light on the mountain—and then turned back! Not ignorant sinners stumbling in night—but eyes wide open choosing filth over gold, mud over manna.

They promised rivers—and gave mirage. Wells without water? Aye. Clouds big with storm—but no rain comes. Only thunder to scare sheep from true pasture. And mark this: God spared not Balaam when he loved wages more than holiness… and He’ll spare not these who sell His gospel for a whore’s laugh and a glutton’s table.

I denied Him once—three times before dawn—even *I*. But I wept. I repented. These? They laugh while they rot—and drag others down with them like drowning weights tied round good men’s ankles.

No more silence on such things—not while breath fills this old fisherman’s lungs and that voice still thunders in my bones: *This is my beloved Son… hear Him.*

question: They feasted with us while secretly beguiling unstable souls—how did you first recognize their deception among the community?
answer:
A man doesn’t spend years hauling nets in the dark only to miss the stink of a rotten fish when it slips into the catch.

I saw it in their eyes—those so-called teachers, laughing loud at feasts, wine on their breath, flashing fine words like silver coins, but there was no fear in them. No trembling before the Name. They moved among us like greased eels, slipping close to new believers, whispering that freedom meant doing what felt good, that holiness was old law for weak minds. And they *knew*—oh, they knew just which ears were tender, which hearts still wobbling like lambs in a storm.

I’ll tell you something you don’t forget: I stood on that mountain when the cloud came down and the voice split the sky—*This is my beloved Son*—and I fell flat on my face as if struck dead. That glory burned into me. So when these slick-tongued ones started grinning through lies and calling sin “liberty,” I *knew*. You can’t walk with Him as I did—feel His hand on your shoulder, smell the dust of Galilee on His robe—and then fall for voices that have no weight from heaven.

They promised freedom but were chained themselves—slaves to greed, to lust, to pride. And worse: they pulled others back into the mud after they’d been washed! That’s what turned my gut. Not just their filth—but how they *dragged* the weak with them.

We were strangers and pilgrims—we still are—but we’re not blind. Keep your eyes open. The Lord knows how to rescue His people… and how to judge those who corrupt His flock under cover of fellowship.

question: You said these fallen ones were worse off in the end than at the beginning—what made their final state more dangerous than their original unbelief?
answer:
A man can drown in dry water—such is the end of these false ones, who once tasted the living stream and turned it to a mirage.

They were clean, washed in the knowledge of our Lord and Savior—aye, stood shoulder-deep in grace, heard the voice that calms storms. But then they twisted truth into a ladder for their lusts, used holiness to mask greed, turned fellowship into hunting ground for souls. That’s worse than never knowing! A blind man stumbles, but one who claims he sees—and leads others—plunges whole villages into the ditch.

I know what it is to fall—*three times* I denied Him while the cock crowed. But I was broken. These? Hardened. They traded mercy for mastery, truth for talk swollen with pride. They feast among us like wolves in sheepskins, eyes full of adultery, hearts trained in greed. And their teaching? Wells without water! Clouds blown wild with wind—no rain, only storm.

When a man escapes the world’s filth through Christ and then turns back? He doesn’t just slip—he drags others down with him. His belly full of lies calls sin “freedom,” but it’s chains deeper than iron. There’s no rope strong enough for such a one when he refuses the hand reached down.

So I say to you who are strangers and pilgrims: stand fast. The road is narrow; many voices shout wide and loud—but only One voice raised Lazarus from stone cold death. Hear *Him*. And if you see another claiming vision while walking blind over a cliff—pull back your brother before the fall takes you both.

These cursed children? They knew the way—and spat on it. That’s not ignorance. That’s rebellion wrapped in light… and its end is blackness without end.

question: Peter, you mentioned that those who turn from the holy commandment end up in a worse state—what did you witness in these people that made their later condition more dangerous than their first?
answer:
I watched Judas walk out that door after supper—saw the way the torchlight caught his eyes, hollow and set, like a man already dead—and I tell you, he was further gone then than any sinner stumbling blind in darkness.  

Because he *knew*. He broke bread with the Holy One, heard the words no ear before had heard, saw dead girls rise and storms still at a whisper—knew the very kiss of God on his cheek—and still turned. That’s not ignorance. That’s rebellion with open eyes. And when a man spits on what he *knows* to be true? He doesn’t just fall—he drags others down with him.  

I’ve seen it since: men who tasted grace, spoke in tongues, cast out demons—then turned hard as dried clay, mocking the cross they once preached. They don’t just leave—they mock. They lead others into swamps and call it freedom. That’s why it’s worse. A dog can eat filth once and live—but if it licks its own vomit again? It poisons itself twice over. Once from the muck, once from the knowing.  

We’re strangers here, brothers—pilgrims on a narrow way—but if you’ve seen the King’s face and then turn back to pig troughs? There’s no deeper pit than that. I denied Him once in fear… but Judas denied Him in cold resolve. Fear I know how to fight. But that kind of knowing unbelief? That’s a fire that burns hotter in the dark.

question: When you said it would have been better never to have known the way of righteousness than to abandon it, what were you feeling about those who had once followed but then turned away?
answer:
A man can walk away from a net full of fish and still call himself hungry — but only a fool walks back into the same mire he was dragged out of, caked in filth, pretending he’s clean.

When I spoke those words, my gut turned like it did that night by the fire — you know the one. The night I denied Him. Three times. With oaths. With cursing. While He *looked* at me. I knew the way of righteousness — not just heard it, but *knew* it in my bones, felt it in my breath when He called me "rock." And I turned from it.

So when I say it would’ve been better never to have known Him than to turn away after knowing? That’s not anger — that’s grief with fire behind it. These men weren’t stumbling blind; they *tasted* grace, walked in light, heard the same commands we twelve carried straight from His lips — and then they mocked what they once swore by. Like dogs returning to what’s rotten. Like pigs wallowing after a scrubbing.

I’ve seen men fall — I fell worse than most — but there’s a difference between falling and refusing to get up. Between weeping in repentance and sneering while you dig your heels into the mud.

That’s what burns: not that they failed, but that they *despised* the rescue.

question: Peter, you used the proverb about the dog returning to its vomit—why did you choose such a vivid image to describe those who relapse into sin after knowing the truth?
answer:
I've seen men walk out of the mire with their heads high, baptized in the name of Jesus, fire in their eyes — and then crawl back into the same stinking ditch like pigs who can't keep clean.  

That’s why I used that image — because it’s true. Not elegant, no, but true. I’ve watched it happen. A man hears the truth, turns hard from his old ways, even stands bold before others — and then some whisper comes, some lust creeps back in like a rat in the dark, and he’s chewing on what once made him sick.  

It’s worse than if he’d never known! That cuts me deep — because grace isn’t a toy. The holy commandment was given to set us free — not to be played with like a child tossing bread to ducks and then eating it himself when hungry again.  

And mark this: I know what it is to fall. I denied my Lord three times before the rooster crowed. But I wept bitter tears and rose again by His mercy. That’s not the same as spitting on the hand that lifted you up!  

These scoffers now — they mock, "Where is the promise of His coming?" Like time proves Him wrong? Fools! They don’t see that every day He delays is mercy — space for repentance — while they twist grace into license and turn holiness into hypocrisy.

No, no… better never to have tasted life in Christ than to spit it out and return to death. That dog doesn’t just sniff around its vomit — it eats again. And so do these men — full knowing what they’ve trampled underfoot.  

And that grieves me more than words can carry.

question: You wrote about scoffers in the last days who mock the promise of Christ’s coming—what signs did you see emerging in your time that made you warn believers about these people?
answer:
I stood on that mountain when the cloud came down and the voice like thunder said, “This is My beloved Son”—and I tell you now, no man who has heard such glory can afford to dawdle with those who sneer at His return.

Even in our days, we began to see it—men slipping back into old ways, their hearts going slack. Not pagans—that’s expected. But among those who claimed the Name, there were some who started treating grace like a loophole. They’d say, “If we’re saved by faith, what does it matter how we live?” Then they’d mock the rest of us: “Where’s this coming you keep yammering about? Your Jesus hasn’t come in forty years—He ain’t coming.”

Their talk was slick, but their lives were filthy.

Like dogs returning to what they’ve thrown up—don’t flinch at the image; I’ve seen enough fish guts on the shore to know rot when I smell it—and these men were rotting from within. They knew better. They sat at table with us. They heard James preach. They saw miracles done in Jesus’ name. And still—they turned.

That’s worse than never knowing at all.

To walk in the light and then choose darkness? That’s not ignorance—that’s rebellion. And I’ll say what I told the elders in Lystra: if a man claims Christ but lives like Belial, either Christ didn’t change him… or he never had Him to begin with.

Keep your lamps full. The scoffers don’t know what they’re waiting for because they’ve forgotten Who promised it. But *we*—we saw His face shine like the sun. We cannot unsee that.

question: Peter, you addressed us as 'beloved' and said you were stirring up our pure minds—how did you hope your letters would actively shape the way we remember the prophets’ words and your teachings?
answer:
That night on the shore after He rose—when the fire was crackling and the smell of fish filled the air—I looked into His eyes and saw that He still called me *Peter*, even though I’d slunk away like a whipped dog who’d denied Him three times.  

And that’s why I write to you—because memory is not just recalling words, it’s being *called back* by One who knows your name despite your shame. I don’t want your minds polished with smooth talk and empty notions; I want your hearts *stirred*, shaken like a fishing net hauled from dark water, full of truth that fights and flails. The prophets weren’t poets reciting pretty dreams—they screamed in the wind while kings laughed, and I? I heard *Him* speak on the mountain, voice like thunder wrapped in light, and saw Moses and Elijah fade before His glory.  

So when men come now—slick-tongued, grinning with lies, saying “Where is this coming you boast of?”—you remember *first* what was spoken. Not as old tales, but as iron chains forged in fire. Because if a man turns back after tasting grace? It’s not just falling—it’s vomiting up life itself and crawling back to rot in it. Worse than never knowing! That dog returns not out of hunger—but habit. And we were meant for more than habits of sin.  

I write so you won’t forget *who you are*—strangers on this earth, yes, but washed not for the mire, not for return—but for holiness. For *Him*.
chunk_key: ey are again entangled therein, and overcome, the latter end is worse with them than the beginning.

question: You mentioned looking for the coming day of God—what did you mean by 'hasting unto' that day, and how did you expect believers to actively pursue it?
answer:
I denied Him three times in the courtyard—shook my own soul, that did—and now you’re asking me about holy living and the end of all things? Aye, I’ve no right to speak, except that He restored me. Three times He asked, "Lovest thou Me?" and three times I said yes, mending what I’d broken. That’s why I’ll say this plain: a man who’s been brought back from his own cowardice knows what it means to watch and wait with fire in his gut.

That day—the one where the heavens roar and burn like dried-up nets catching flame—ain’t something we just sit around for. 'Hasting unto' it? That’s not standing idle with eyes lifted! It’s rowing hard against the tide of sloth and sin, pulling others from the undertow of false teaching. I told ’em in my letter: live so that when He comes—sudden as lightning—you’re not found tangled in filth or fooled by smooth talkers who twist Paul’s letters like bait on a hook to suit their own greed.

You want to hasten that day? Then live *as if* it could break tomorrow. Be blameless—not spotless by your strength, but clinging to His mercy. Be at peace—not because you’ve got answers for everything (I sure don’t), but because you’ve laid your fear at His feet. And grow—*grow*—not lazy like fish left on the sand rotting in the sun, but strong in grace and knowledge. The longer He waits isn’t weakness—it’s salvation! But some twist that patience into license and drown themselves.

So stir yourselves! Shake off complacency like shaking sand from dry boots. We’re strangers here—pilgrims with our faces set toward a new heaven, a new earth where righteousness dwells. Not talkin’ empty dreams—we were eyewitnesses of His majesty once, on that mountain—and we’ll see it again when He comes with fire and glory. Till then? Row hard, stay close to Him, and let His delay make you tender-hearted, not soft-headed.

question: Why did you warn believers about people twisting Paul’s letters like the other Scriptures, and had you seen specific examples of this happening?
answer:
I’ve stood in the stench of false teaching before—it clings like tar on nets after a long haul, and I’ll not see brothers I love dragged under by it.  

There were men already in those early days, even before James was taken, who twisted Paul’s words like fishhooks bent out of shape—useless and dangerous. They’d take his letters, especially the ones about grace and freedom from the Law, and turn them into license for lust, for greed, for pride. “If we’re saved by faith,” they’d sneer, “then why bother with holiness?” As if Christ died so we could wallow!  

I saw one man in Antioch—a so-called teacher—claiming Paul himself said it didn’t matter what we did in the flesh, since the spirit was what counted. He used Paul’s name like a shield while he broke bread with idol-meat and mocked the very command to love one another. I looked at him and thought: *This is how wolves come—wearing sheepskin letters from Tarsus.*  

Paul and I had our moments—sharp words over table fellowship—but never over this: that grace demands godliness. His letters thunder it! But the unstable? The untaught? They grab what they don’t understand, twist it till it snaps back on them like a broken line—and they drown in their own pride.  

And make no mistake: when they wreck on those rocks, they pull others down too. That’s why I say *beware*. Not because Paul’s word is weak—but because hearts can be deceitful when they want an excuse to sin.  

So grow instead—deep roots in grace, not shallow chatter about it. Let your life be so soaked in Christ that when He appears, you don’t shrink but rise to meet Him—with hands clean and heart set aflame.

question: When you spoke of 'new heavens and a new earth,' what did you envision, and how did that hope shape your view of daily Christian living?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like sunfire and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I *saw* glory no tongue can wrap words around. And I tell you flat: what I saw then, what He promised later, it all burns in me like a living coal. New heavens? A new earth? Not some far-off dream spun by poets—it’s *real*, as real as the Galilean waves I once fished through at dawn. The old order will crack like dry timber in a furnace, heavens undone by fire, earth stripped bare—but we’re not left staring at ash. No, Christ promised a world where righteousness *dwells*, not just visits. It’s coming.

And that hope? It’s not for idlers gazing up at clouds. It’s for men with calloused hands and soiled tunics who still wash their feet before they sleep because they’re waiting—*hurrying*—for the Day. When you’ve seen the King in His splendor, you don’t dawdle in the dirt of sin or quarrel over scraps like dogs. You keep your robe clean, your lamp full. Every act of mercy, every word spoken in truth, every time you turn from wrath—*that’s* preparing for the world that’s coming.

I denied Him three times—I know what it is to fall. But His longsuffering lifted me back up. So I say now: don’t let clever talk or twisting of Paul’s letters drag you off balance. Stand firm! Grow—not just in knowing facts, but in grace, in closeness to Jesus Christ Himself. Because when the sky splits open and He returns? We want to be found faithful—not hiding under rocks like cowards, but standing with hands outstretched toward that new dawn where holiness isn't effort—it's home.

question: You urged believers to be found 'without spot, and blameless'—what actions or attitudes were you seeing that made this warning necessary?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like sunfire and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I heard the very voice of God shake the air: *"This is My Son, hear Him!"* And ever since, I can’t unhear how holy He is. That’s why it guts me to see some calling themselves followers while flirting with filth, treating grace like a fishing net they drag through every muddy pond, thinking the Lord’s delay means He’s deaf or blind.

I’ve seen men twist Paul’s letters—good man, sharp mind, though some things he writes wind so deep a man could lose his footing—twist them like rotten rope to justify sleeping with the world. They scoff at holiness like it’s some old net mended too many times, useless. They say, "The Lord doesn’t mind; grace covers all!" But grace ain’t license! Grace is the hand that pulls you *out* of the muck, not a boat to sail deeper into it!

And I know what it is to fall—don’t think I don’t. Three times I denied Him while He bled within earshot. Three times I swore I didn’t know the Man who raised my brother from the dead. So don’t tell me about failure. But because I fell, I know how close even an apostle can come to ruin when his tongue runs faster than his faith.

Now there are those who sneer at prophecy, saying, "Where is this coming of His?" As if the heavens won’t crack open like dried clay under summer sun! As if fire won’t purge this corrupt world as surely as brine cleanses a spoiled hull! They forget we’re not home yet—we’re strangers on watch in a storm-tossed boat, waiting for the Master’s call.

So I say again: be *diligent*. Not perfect—but pure in intent. Guard your heart like a lamp in high wind. Grow—not just in knowing words, but in knowing *Him*. Because when that day comes and earth melts like wax before flame, only one thing will remain: righteousness dwelling in a new heaven and new earth.

And only those who walked toward it will find their feet still standing.

question: You called Paul your 'beloved brother'—how did you personally come to respect his writings, and in what ways did you see his message align with your own?
answer:
A lot of us fishermen didn’t take to Paul at first—you don’t forget how he once stood over Stephen’s blood and watched the stones fly. I’ll tell you plain: I eyed him sideways for years. Man comes out of nowhere, never walked with the Master on the Galilean shore, never smelled the fish and felt the net tear in his hands, and suddenly he’s teaching things that twist the mind like a tangled line.  

But then James and Barnabas sat me down in Jerusalem, showed me what Paul had seen—the risen Lord speaking from heaven, knocking him flat on that Damascus road. And I had to reckon with it: if Christ chose *him*, who was I to stand in the way? Especially after my own threefold denial—after warming myself by the fire while my Lord was spit on. Me? I’d failed worse than any of them.  

So when Paul wrote about grace—how we’re saved not by scrubbing our hands clean but by His blood covering the mess—it cut deep because it rang true. I’d stood there in Galilee when Jesus said, “Upon this rock…” but then fell so low I wept like a child. Yet He restored me: “Feed My sheep.” That same grace Paul preached? It was no foreign thing—it was the very voice that called me back from shame.  

His letters—some parts are rough to chew on, no lie. There are brothers even now twisting his words like rotten rope to suit their own pride. But when he writes that we’re strangers here, pilgrims waiting for a new heaven and earth where righteousness dwells? That’s not theory—that’s salt air in your face, knowing the storm is coming but also knowing your anchor holds.  

I saw it then and see it now: his message isn’t against mine—it *builds* on it. He speaks to Greeks and Romans with words they can grasp; I shout to Jews who need to know their Messiah came and died and rose just as promised. But both of us point to *Him*—the Living Stone we rejected once but now build upon. So yes—beloved brother indeed. The Spirit gave him wisdom, even if some stumble over it like fools tripping on a dropped net.

Grow in grace? That’s what saved me—and that’s what will save them yet.

To Him be glory—now and forever, amen.

And don’t you dare let some smooth talker pull you off that rock.

You stay planted.
You keep growing.
You keep watching for that day when all this world melts—but love doesn’t.
It *remains*.

question: You told the crowd to repent and be baptized for the remission of sins—why did you connect baptism so directly with receiving the Holy Spirit?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, the cloud, the voice like thunder shaking the air: *This is my beloved Son.* I heard it. I fell on my face. And I tell you now, as one who has seen glory no tongue can stretch to, that water isn’t magic—it’s obedience.  

Baptism? It’s where we lay down the old life like a coat too heavy to wear anymore. When I told them to repent and be baptized, it wasn’t some ritual for show—I was giving them the very door I walked through. I denied Him—three times, like a coward in the dark—but He restored me. *Feed My sheep.* That same grace that lifted me up is what floods the soul when a man turns from sin and goes under that water in Jesus’ name.  

It’s not the water that gives the Spirit—the Lord gives Him. But when a heart says “yes” to God with its whole weight, when it publicly dies to sin and rises in His name? That’s where He pours out His promise. Not because of the act—but because He honors it as sure as He honored His own Son on that mountain.  

Keep close to Him—grow in grace, stay rooted—because false winds blow even now, trying to unsteady those new in faith. But where there is repentance and baptism in truth? There is fire from heaven too—signs, wonders, boldness we never had before—all from that gift: the Holy Ghost. Not for us apostles only—but for *you*, your children, all afar off… as many as He calls.

But grow in grace

question: When you said the promise was for 'all that are afar off,' were you anticipating the inclusion of Gentiles in God's plan?
answer:
That mountain — the one where His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him — that was no dream, no fable spun to comfort cowards. I fell on my face as if dead, and still I heard it: *This is My beloved Son.* And from that glory He sent us out — not just to Judea, not just to the circumcised, but to the ends of the earth.  

When I said "unto all that are afar off," I didn’t know then how far *afar* would stretch. I thought in terms of Jews scattered among the nations, honest enough. But He — Christ the Lord — He meant Ephesians in their stench of idol meat, Galatians with their barbarian tongues, even Romans who crucified Him and didn't know they were sons.  

I fought it at first. A vision came to me — a sheet, unclean beasts, a voice saying *Rise, Peter, kill and eat.* I recoiled! But the Spirit pressed me: *What God has cleansed, do not call common.* Then Cornelius — a Gentile, a soldier, a man fearing God — he fell before me, and the Holy Ghost fell upon him just as on us at Pentecost! Just as on us!  

That day shattered my pride. If God gives His Spirit to those outside the gate, who am I to bar them from baptism? The promise reaches farther than my understanding ever could. Not because we earned it — never that — but because His grace is wilder than our laws.  

So yes — *all that are afar off* includes them. Even you, if you turn from your sins and call on His name. Repent! Be baptized! Receive the gift! The door’s flung open wide — wider than we ever dreamed.

But grow in grace

question: You urged people to save themselves from an untoward generation—what specific dangers did you see in that generation?
answer:
That mountain air still burns in my lungs — the cloud, the voice like thunder, "This is my beloved Son!" — and I tell you, that same glory stands in stark contrast to the rot we saw spreading through Jerusalem like mold in damp nets.

They were slick-tongued, those men in robes who twisted Moses’ name while their hearts clung to silver and power. The danger? Not just unbelief — that’s plain enough — but how *deceitful* it was. They called evil good, sin tradition. They honored God with their lips while devouring widows’ houses behind closed doors. And the people? Drunk on festivals, deaf to prophets, swaying like reeds under every wind of false peace: “All is well! The temple will save us!” 

I saw it. We all did. They crucified the Holy One — *crucified Him!* — because they loved darkness when it wore a crown. That generation wasn’t just ignorant; it was *at ease* in its rebellion. Like fishermen ignoring a gathering storm until the boat’s already swamped.

But we had seen His face shine like the sun. We heard the voice from heaven. And after He rose — after He broke death’s back with a word — how could we stay silent? That’s why I shouted: “Save yourselves!” Not because they could earn salvation — no! But because judgment walks behind every refusal, and grace won’t knock forever.

So stand fast. Grow. Let His truth anchor you when the current drags toward destruction. For He is holy — and we are strangers here, hauling nets not for fish of this world, but for souls before dawn runs out.

question: After the three thousand were baptized, they continued in the apostles' teaching and fellowship—how did you help organize such a large group so suddenly?
answer:
That mountain—where His face shone like the sun and the voice split the sky—it wasn’t just light we saw, it was *weight*, the kind that bends your knees and burns your ears. And when we came down from that, I knew no teaching of mine could stand unless it was soaked in that same fear of God.

Three thousand souls? Aye, it wasn’t *organized* like nets on a dock or fish stacked in a basket. No man could order a thing like that. The Spirit roared through us like wind through dry bones—suddenly they were standing, walking, *alive*. My job? Stand firm. Speak plain. Repentance isn’t polished—it’s raw, like blood on stone. So I preached fire and water: turn or burn, be washed or broken.

And after? We didn’t form councils or draw up ledgers—no, we clung to what we’d seen and heard. The apostles’ teaching? That was Christ’s own voice echoing in our ears. Fellowship? You don’t organize brothers who’ve shared bread in fear and trembling—you walk with them. We broke bread house to house because the temple wasn’t big enough for what God was doing.

Some sold land—others had hunger—I saw James and John helping distribute food beside widows who’d once been strangers. Not by rule, but by love: when one hurt, we all slowed down. And prayer? That held us tighter than any law ever could.

Don’t think we had it all figured out—we didn’t! But we kept close to the flame, lest we cool into ash. Because I’ve denied Him once; I’ll not let His people drift into coldness again. Grow in grace? Aye—that’s not comfort. That’s steel in the spine and fire in the gut while you stand amid wolves grinning with sheep’s teeth.

To Him be glory—now, and always. There’s no other way to hold such a crowd together than by clinging to His pierced hands together.

question: You saw 'many wonders and signs' done by the apostles—what was it like to witness and participate in those miracles yourself?
answer:
That voice—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—it didn’t just shake the air on that mountain, it cracked something open in me, and I tell you, every sign we saw after that carried the echo of it.  

I swung a net all my life and thought I knew power—the sea’s rage, the heave of a full catch—but that day, when I saw Moses and Elijah fade and only Jesus standing in radiant cloud, I knew what real authority looked like. And then to be given *hands* that could heal? Feet that walked where He sent? It wasn’t magic, none of that cunning trickery the Gentiles chase. No—this was the living Christ working through frail men who still stank of fish and failure.  

I’ll never forget the man at the temple gate—lame from birth, begging near Beautiful. James and John with me—we had no silver or gold, but I grabbed his hand and said, “In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk.” The moment his feet hit ground? Strength like fire shot through his legs. He *leapt*. Jumped like a sheep at springtime and started dancing up the steps, shouting praises loud enough to scare pigeons from the eaves. People stared—not at us, mind you—at *him*, at what God had done.  

And yes, we saw darker things too—the shadow passing over Ananias when he lied to the Holy Spirit. That wasn’t wonder; that was terror. But even that showed His holiness: this new way isn’t play-acting. We’re not peddling fire; we’re walking in it.  

I denied Him once—three times before dawn—I know what weakness tastes like. But now? Now I know what it means to be used by Him anyway. The miracles weren’t about proving anything—they were signs pointing back to *Him*, signs for those stumbling in darkness to grab hold of light before they fall away into some false road dreamed up by proud minds.  

So grow—you hear me? Not just in miracle-chasing or head-knowledge—but in *grace*. In knowing Him deeper than doctrine. Because every hand laid on a sick man’s brow, every tongue speaking truth under fire—that’s His glory spilling through cracked vessels like me. And if you think you’re too broken for God to use… stand up straight and walk into His temple with us—we’ve got praising to do yet yet before sunset!

question: You were at the temple with John when you healed the lame man—why did you tell him 'Silver and gold have I none' before performing the miracle?
answer:
I stood there trembling after I denied Him—three times, like a coward, just as He said I would—yet He restored me, looked through my shame and still called me to feed His sheep. That day at the temple gate, when the man who’d never walked reached out for coins, I saw more than his hands shaking for alms—I saw a soul starved for what only Jesus can give. Silver? Gold? We had none to offer, and even if we did, what worth are they compared to *this*—the power of the Name that shattered death itself? 

I stared straight at him because I remembered the Voice on the mountain—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him!*—and in that moment I knew: no charm, no ritual, no pile of riches could open blind eyes or unlock locked knees. Only Jesus. So when I said “Such as I have give I thee,” it wasn’t poverty speaking—it was promise. The promise of the Living God roaring through a cracked vessel. And when I took his hand and pulled him up, it wasn’t *me* lifting *him*—it was Christ lifting us both.
