question: You told the man at the Beautiful Gate to 'look on us'—what made you and John decide to focus your attention on him at that moment?
answer:
A beggar’s eyes don’t lie — I’ve seen that look too long on the docks, men with hands worn raw, asking for a scrap just to feed their kin. That day at the Beautiful Gate, he wasn’t shouting, wasn’t groaning like some do — just quiet, expectant, fixed on us like we carried hope in our belts. John and I were heading in for prayer, same as always, but something in the man’s stillness cut through the noise of the courtyard. Not his rags, not his legs folded useless beneath him — no, it was that *look*, like he’d been waiting longer than just that morning.

We stopped. I don’t know what moved John first — maybe the same thing that gripped me: the Spirit pressing hard, not with words but with weight. So I locked eyes with him. “Look on us,” I said — and I meant it. Not to trick him, not to shame him, but because he needed to *see* who was speaking. He thought we had coins? No silver in my pouch, never did have much of that sort. But I had something better — the name that split death open.

When he gave us his full attention, I grabbed his hand — rough calluses on my palm from years of rope and net met bones soft from never walking — and yanked him up in Jesus’ name. Not magic words; not some ritual chant they twist later into sorcery tales. The *power* was in the name! Christ’s name! And straightaway his ankles snapped tight like cords reknit by fire.

You should’ve seen him leap — like a lamb let loose for the first time! Walked right into the temple courts praising God so loud even the priests paused their sacrifices to stare. And all those people who passed him daily without more than a coin or a curse? They stood slack-jawed.

That’s how it starts — one man healed, whole in body and shouting grace. Then others come running—not for alms now—but because they *feel* something changing. The Lord adds them daily… saved… gathered… not by clever talk or silver tricks… but by *life*, real life bursting out where death once ruled.

False teachers want signs without sacrifice? Let them try lifting a man with nothing but a word and see how far their empty hands take them!

question: After you healed him, the man leaped up and walked—what did it feel like for you to see his immediate transformation?
answer:
Like that day on the mountain when His voice split the air and we fell flat, trembling — not just fear, but fire running through my bones — that’s what it was like standing there watching him leap. I’d seen the glory veiled in cloud, and then I saw it blazing in a man’s ankles made whole.  

I tell you, when he looked at me expecting silver or gold — poor wretch, all his life someone else had to carry him, laid down like a sack at that gate, day after day — I could feel John at my side, still as stone. And something rose up in me. Not pride. No — *authority*, given, not earned. "Rise up," I said. *In the name of Jesus*. Not by a word of mine, but by the Name that shook Hades itself.  

And when his feet hit the ground? He didn’t just stand — he *sprang*. Like a lamb among hills after chains. His legs had never walked once in all his years — yet now dancing! Singing! Clinging to us like we were lifelines, but no — Christ was the lifeline all along.  

You ask how it felt? It wasn’t about me at all — never is. But to see favor fall so sudden from heaven… to watch a man who’d only known begging now leaping in Solomon’s porch before them all…? Felt like the Kingdom tearing through the veil again — right there in flesh and bone.

And let me say this: if some smooth-talking liar ever tells you signs and wonders are for long ago or belong to their fancy titles or rituals — spit on that lie! The power isn’t locked in scrolls or robes! It walks among us still for those who believe His name!

question: You took the man by the right hand and lifted him up—was that physical act intentional, and why did you choose to do it that way?
answer:
I stood there trembling not long ago, denying I even knew the Master—three times before the rooster crowed, just as He said—and now He lets me reach out and take a man by the hand? Aye, I took him by the right hand. Deliberately. Not because strength was in my arm, but because *obedience* was in my heart.

That touch—it wasn’t mine to withhold. When the Lord spoke through me—"In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth rise up and walk"—the power came from Him, blazing like that light on the mountain when Moses and Elijah stood with us and the voice thundered from heaven: *"This is My beloved Son."* But still—I had to *act*. I had to reach. He didn’t float up on his own; God moves in us when we step out in faith.

The man expected silver or gold—poor soul, he’d been carried every day, laid at that gate like broken pottery no one mends. But we gave him something better: a standing body, a leaping heart, a voice to praise. And yes—I seized his right hand because a man isn’t raised without connection. Not after I denied Him. Not after He restored me with three questions over charcoal fire—*"Simon son of Jonas, lovest thou Me?"* —as if stitching me back together.

I *had* to take his hand. To obey fully. To be part of what God was doing—not holding back like I did in the courtyard of Caiaphas. No more shrinking. Flesh and bone don’t heal without divine power—but neither do they rise without someone reaching down.

And when he stood? Oh! He didn't just walk—he *leapt*, praising God! The same God who forgave this coward now healing through him—and through us—to show that grace doesn’t just save souls… it straightens crooked feet and opens gates no one thought would ever open again.

question: When all the people ran to you and John in Solomon's Porch, how did you know what to say to them about Jesus Christ of Nazareth?
answer:
I stood there, hand still warm from gripping that man’s calloused fingers as he leapt like a stag — I *saw* the sinews tighten, the ankles firm, the soles strike stone for the first time in forty years. That kind of power doesn’t come from words tossed into the air like bread crumbs — it comes from *His* name, living and sharp.

When the crowd jammed in around us in Solomon’s Porch, eyes wide like they’d seen a ghost or a god, I didn’t have to search for what to say. The truth? It roared up from my gut. This man wasn’t healed because he prayed long enough or gave alms or kept the Law perfect — no, not at all. He walked because *Jesus Christ of Nazareth* is alive, reigning, and His name *acts*. It isn’t a charm. It’s a hammer striking rock.

And listen — I denied Him once. Thrice over before the cock crowed. I know what it is to fail under breath and fear. But He restored me. Looked me in the eye and said, “Feed My sheep.” So when they stared at me and John like we were some kind of gods on display, I shouted loud: “Why marvel at us? We’re no more than dust and breath! But the God you killed — you handed over the Holy One — He raised Him! And by that very name — Jesus — this man stands whole!”

False teachers want signs without substance, wonders without worship. But we preach *Him*. Not silver. Not tradition. Not clever talk. The stone you rejected? He’s building His church with it — living stones on that Cornerstone.

I’ve cast nets all my life, but never caught anything like this: souls dropping into grace like fish breaking the surface at dawn. And every one of them saved — added by *the Lord Himself*.

question: You were at Solomon's Porch when the healed man clung to you and John—what did it feel like to see the crowd rush to you in amazement?
answer:
I saw the light in that man’s eyes—the way he gripped my arm, trembling, staring down at his own feet as if they were strangers to him, yet bearing his weight like they’d carried him all his life.  

And then the crowd—rush of robes, gasps, eyes wide like they’d seen a ghost or an angel. They came running from every corner of the temple, pouring into Solomon’s Porch like floodwater, all drawn to *us*, as though *we* had done something grand.  

Foolishness.  

I felt it rise in my chest—no, not pride, but urgency. We were just men—fishermen from Galilee, rough hands and slower minds than some. And yet there we stood, with this healed man clinging to us both, and all those faces turned our way like we’d opened the heavens ourselves.  

But I knew—*knew*—whose name had done it. Not mine. Not John’s. It was Jesus—the One they crucified—the One who walked out of the grave and left death trembling behind Him. His name had made that man whole. Faith in *His* name, not in us.  

And when I opened my mouth, it wasn’t to boast—it was to redirect them back to the Truth that burns away every shadow: He is risen. He is alive. And by His power alone are lives mended—not by holiness we earned, but by grace we didn’t deserve.

Even then I remembered how I once denied Him three times before a fire… and still He sent me here.

Still uses broken men.

Still heals through His name alone.

That’s what mattered—and matters still for every stranger and pilgrim stumbling toward light: look beyond the vessel to the Hand that moves it.

question: Why did you immediately redirect the people's wonder from yourselves to the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob?
answer:
A man’s legs don’t straighten after forty years in the dirt unless the God who made marrow and muscle says so.  

We were standing there, John and me, with this fellow gripping our arms like a drowning man—warm flesh, strong pulse, leaping up like a lamb. And the people? They came storming in from every corner of the temple, mouths open, eyes wide, thinking we’d done some magic trick with holy words.  

I saw it—the way they stared at us—as if we’d forged that man’s walk out of our own strength. And my gut turned. Not because they were fools—no, I’ve been a greater one—but because I knew whose name was being buried under their astonishment. We didn’t make him whole. We didn’t even *try*. It was Jesus’ name—the very One they crucified—that snapped the chains in his bones.  

I could still feel His breath on my face when He asked me three times: *Lovest thou Me?* After I’d denied Him into the night like a coward. If grace could remake *me*, could it not remake a lame beggar’s legs?  

So I shouted it plain: “Why gawk at us?” Not to shame them—but to wake them. Their wonder was heading down the wrong road, straight into dust and shadows. I had to yank their eyes back to the light—the God of our fathers, who raised up Jesus and glorified Him, even after we all spat in His face. That’s where power lives—not in our hands, but in His nail-scarred ones.

question: You accused the people of denying the Holy One and desiring a murderer—how did you have the courage to speak so boldly to them?
answer:
I stood there trembling after I’d denied Him—three times, like a coward, just as He said I would. I thought I was done. Finished. But He looked at me, not with fury, but with mercy that cut deeper than any sword. That look—*that* stayed in my bones.

So when the lame man clung to me and John at Solomon’s porch, and the crowd stared like we’d done some great thing in our own strength, something rose up in me—not pride, not boldness from myself—but a fire kind of *remembering*. I had seen Him walk out of the grave. We all had. We ate with Him after. He breathed on us and said, “Receive the Spirit.” How could I stay silent?

They wanted a show? I’d give them truth. They marveled at a man walking? Fine. But by the living God, they needed to fall on their faces before the One who made death itself spit Him out.

I wasn’t brave—I was *bound*. Chained by what I saw, by what He forgave in me. If I didn’t speak, the stones would cry out... and worse—*I’d betray Him again.* After all that blood on the ground for me? No.

So I opened my mouth—and it wasn't mine anymore. It was His name that roared through it.

question: When you said the man was healed by faith in Jesus' name, what did you mean by 'faith which is by him'?
answer:
I stood there with the man’s fingers digging into my arms, his legs trembling beneath him—legs that had never borne his weight in forty years—and I tell you, it wasn’t my hand that made him walk.  

When I said “faith which is by him,” I meant this: the power didn’t come from the man’s belief alone, nor mine. It ran straight from the name of Jesus—living, breathing, risen Jesus—like a rope let down from heaven. That man reached for it without even knowing its name at first, but *he* knew *us*. The moment John and I spoke His name at the Beautiful Gate, something snapped in the dark—chains broke. Not because we were holy men, but because *He* is holy.  

Faith isn’t a thing we manufacture like net-mending or sail-stitching. No—it’s handed to us *by Him*, like a spark struck from flint. We carry it, but He kindles it. That beggar had no silver or gold, but what he *did* have—he had nothing—and yet he opened his hands to us, and through us, to *Christ*. And Christ poured through like floodwater through dry land.  

So “faith which is by him”? It means the faith itself comes from Jesus—the very breath of it. We’re just witnesses holding the rope.

question: You told them to repent and be converted so their sins could be blotted out—what gave you confidence that times of refreshing would come from the Lord?
answer:
I stood there in the cold dawn by the Sea of Galilee, my hands shaking, my eyes raw from weeping, and heard Him say, “Feed My sheep.” After I’d denied Him—three times, like a coward—I thought I was done. Finished. But He looked at me, not with fury, but with fire in His eyes and love in His voice, and gave me back my calling.

That’s what gives me confidence.

I saw the scars in His hands. I touched the wound in His side. I ate fish with Him after He rose—broiled over coals, real as this stone beneath my feet. We weren’t dreaming. We weren’t chasing a ghost or a clever tale spun by grieving men. No—He breathed on us. He spoke peace into our fear. And then He sent us out.

So when I say the times of refreshing will come? It’s not hope built on air. It’s promise grounded in blood and breath and risen bone. He lived. He died. He rose. And He *will* return.

We were there. We saw it all—the glory, the cross, the empty tomb—and now we speak what cannot be undone: God raised Him from the dead, and everything since then bends toward that moment when all things are made right again.

Repent? Yes! Because death couldn’t hold Him—and if death can’t hold *Him*, what chance does sin have against those who turn to Him?

question: Peter, why did you confront Ananias so directly about keeping back part of the price from the sale of the land?
answer:
I stood there the day the earth trembled under the weight of God’s voice—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him*—and that word burned into my bones. So when Ananias came before us, smiling like he’d done something noble, while his hands were slick with deceit toward the Holy One, I couldn’t stay silent.  

We had sold lands, yes—many of us—and laid the money at the apostles’ feet so none would lack. But this wasn’t about coins or property. It was about *truth*. The Spirit was among us, breathing life into dead hearts, and suddenly here’s a man acting as if he could cheat the living God and still walk in fellowship with us?  

Ananias kept back part of the price but pretended he’d given all. He lied not to men but to God Himself—the same God who spoke from Sinai, who raised Jesus from the grave, who sent fire on Pentecost. You don’t play games with that kind of holiness! I saw Judas walk away with silver in his hand and death in his heart—same spirit at work here.  

And look—the church was new, tender as a fresh wound. One lie like that spreads faster than rot in a net. If we let it fester, we’d all be undone. So I called him out straight: *Why hath Satan filled thine heart?* Not because I delighted in judgment—but because I feared what silence would cost us all.  

The Lord turned me from my own threefold denial; He restored me by the lakeshore with bread and charcoal fire and asked me three times: *Lovest thou Me?* So I know grace—but I also know how close sin comes to choking it dead if left unchallenged.

We were strangers and pilgrims together in this new thing God was doing—and no man can build on that holy ground with hands full of lies.

question: Peter, what did you mean when you said the heaven must receive Jesus until the times of restitution of all things?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—while the cock crowed and my knees shook—and yet He still set me on my feet to speak this truth: I watched Him ascend, body and bones, into the cloud, and a voice said He’d return just as surely as He went.  

Heaven’s not keeping Him for spite or delay—it’s holding Him like a sword in its sheath until the hour when God reorders all things, just as the prophets thundered from Samuel to Malachi. Creation groans for it. The lame in the temple gate, the widows with empty hands, brothers scattered under persecution—they groan for it too.  

But mark this: that same Jesus, gone from our sight now, will come again to restore what’s broken—the righting of wrongs, the raising of bodies, the banishment of every lie. And when He speaks? Every soul that refused to heed His voice—*that prophet like Moses*—will be cut off. No second chances then.  

So while we wait as strangers in this broken world, we don’t fold our arms—we turn people from their sins *now*, because God sent His Son *first* to us—to bless us by tearing down the filth inside us. The restitution hasn’t come yet—but the cleansing has already begun.

question: Peter, how did you know Ananias had lied to the Holy Spirit about the money from the land sale?
answer:
Fire doesn’t hide its burn — I saw the shadow of deceit on his face the moment he spoke.  

Ananias stood there, bold as storm-tossed waves, claiming he’d laid all the money at our feet when only a piece of it was there. But it wasn’t just about the coins — it was the silence in the room, the way the air stilled like before a gale. The Spirit struck me hard: *This man has lied to God Himself.*  

I’ve seen guilt in men’s eyes before — out on the boats, when one stole another’s catch. But this? This was different. It wasn’t just theft. He stood in front of us all, breathing lies into holy ground, pretending his offering was whole while his heart was split wide open with greed.  

And I — who once denied my Lord three times before a servant girl — I knew that kind of fear. But this was worse: not fear, but pride dressed up as piety.  

The land? It was his. The silver? His to keep or give as he pleased. No command said *all* must be sold or *all* must be given. But once he said “all,” knowing we believed him… that’s where the knife went in — not against man, but against the Spirit who dwells among us like fire on a hilltop.

God made it plain to me because He would not have His church rotten at the root before it even took shape. We’re strangers and pilgrims here, living stones being built up — but only if we’re clean through, inside and out.

That’s why I spoke so sharp — not for vengeance, but for life: so no more would die from that same poison creeping through like rot in timber already soaked with grace.

question: Peter, why did you emphasize that Jesus was the prophet like Moses foretold by God?
answer:
I stood there on the mountain when He was transfigured—glory blazing from His face, Moses and Elijah speaking with Him, and I didn’t know what I was saying, so terrified I was. But that day burned into me: *This is the One*. Not just another teacher. Not a rebel rabbi. The Prophet Moses promised—the Voice we were to obey or be cut off.

I’d denied Him three times before the rooster crowed. Three times! Just hours after swearing I’d die with Him. And still—*still*—He looked at me after He rose, not with fury, but with fire in His eyes that said, *"Feed My sheep."* How could I not proclaim Him?

That Prophet? He’s not a whisper from the past. He’s alive. Heaven holds Him for now, but every word from Samuel to Malachi points to His feet, His cross, His return. And mark this well: when God sent Jesus first to us—*to the Jews*—it wasn’t favoritism. It was mercy. A chance to turn, to live. But if a soul refuses *that* Prophet? There’s no other name, no backup plan. Judgment isn’t threat—it’s promise.

So I preach hard because love is urgent. We’re strangers here, pilgrims—but we carry the truth that makes kings tremble and sinners live.

question: Peter, what did you hope the people would understand when you said they were children of the prophets and of Abraham's covenant?
answer:
That mountain—when the cloud came down and His face shone like the sun—I heard the voice myself, and it wasn’t for show.  

Now hear this: when I said they were children of the prophets and of Abraham’s covenant, I meant they weren’t just heirs of promises made long ago—they were standing *in* them. That line runs straight from Abraham, through Moses, through Samuel, all the way to the cross and beyond. And the covenant wasn’t about land or lineage alone—it was about blessing *all* nations through one Seed. Him! Jesus!  

You think God called Abraham just so a few folks could say they knew the right ancestor? No! He called him to start a river of blessing that would flood every nation, every tribe, every dark corner where sin had chained men’s souls. And now that blessing stands before you in Jesus—not as a ghost or fable, but risen, alive, reigning until the day all things are set right again.  

And I tell you plainly: don’t trifle with it. Ananias thought he could lie to men and get away with it—but he was lying to God Himself! If we treat this covenant like some small thing, some religious game—then we forget what it cost. Heaven held its breath for this moment. The prophets spoke into centuries of darkness just to point to *this*. And anyone who turns away? They cut themselves off from the very stream of life God opened for all mankind.  

So repent—turn hard—and let Him turn you from your sins. That’s what the covenant demands: not pedigree, but heart-deep faith in the One sent first to us—and then to all.

question: Why did you confront Ananias so directly when he brought the money from the sale of his land?
answer:
A man don’t walk into the assembly with half a gift and expect to fool the Holy Spirit. I saw what was in his heart — not because I’m sharp, but because the fire of God had already laid bare the lie. We’d just come out of fishing, hauling nets, living by sweat and truth — and here he comes, Ananias, all pious with his silver, pretending he gave all when he held back what belonged to God. You don’t play games with the Living One.

I didn’t want to speak that word — it tore through me as much as it did him. But the church was new, tender as a fresh wound, and if falsehood took root like rot in a net, it would sink us all. So when the Spirit pressed upon me — *Why hath Satan filled thine heart?* — I couldn’t stay silent. It wasn’t about the money. It was about the heart: he lied not to us apostles standing there, but to God Himself.

And when he fell dead at my feet… believe me, no man felt heavier. But fear? Aye — fear fell on everyone. And rightly so! We were strangers and pilgrims in this world, called out for holiness — not showmanship or schemes. That day proved: this Way isn't a club for pretenders. The blood that cleanses is real, but so is the fire that burns away chaff.

Even after his wife fell too — same sin, same end — grace still moved among us. The sick were brought into streets just so my shadow might touch them! Not because I’m holy in myself — far from it; I denied my Lord three times before cock crowed — but because He is holy.

So I say now what I lived then: walk upright. Keep your heart clean before God. For He sees what we do in secret — and blesses obedience more than sacrifice.

question: How did you know that Ananias and Sapphira had agreed together to keep back part of the price?
answer:
I saw the lie coil up in their eyes before they even opened their mouths — same way I’ve seen a storm gather on the Galilean waves when the wind’s lying still.  

God’s Spirit pressed it upon my heart, plain as a net full of fish dragging heavy in my hands. No whisper, no vision — just *knowing*, like I knew my own name. They came in smiling, playing humble, laying down their coins like martyrs laying down their lives. But the truth? It reeked of greed masked as devotion.  

I tell you this to warn every brother and sister walking among wolves in sheepskins: the fire of God dwells among us, not as a tame thing, not as decoration — but as judgment and life together. You can’t haggle with holiness like it’s a price at market!  

And yes — I trembled after they fell. Who wouldn’t? But better to fall under God’s hand than stand proud under a painted lie. That day burned fear into the church — good! We needed it. Too many want Jesus with one breath and silver with the other. Hear me: you cannot serve both God and your own glory. The ground of that upper room became holy ground that day — soaked with bloodless sacrifice, yes, but sacrifice all the same. Two lives poured out for one deceitful breath.

And still… still we preached. Still the sick were laid in the streets, hoping my shadow might touch them! Can you fathom such mercy alongside such judgment? That’s our God — fierce and kind, near as breath and terrible as fire.

So keep your heart clean, stranger and pilgrim. You’re not hiding anything from Him anyway.

question: What did you mean when you said Ananias lied not unto men, but unto God?
answer:
A man don’t trifle with the Holy Spirit and walk away whole — I saw Ananias drop like a stone, breath gone, face ashen, and I knew in that moment we were standing on holy ground, not because of us, but because *He* was among us.

When I said he lied not to men but to God, I meant it straight: we weren’t the ones counting coins or measuring generosity. We didn’t demand he sell the field — it was his. He could’ve kept it all, or given it all, or none — that wasn’t the issue. The issue was this: he stood before the assembly, breath hot with pride, pretending his measly portion was “all,” when his gut knew better. That breath he used to lie? Stolen breath — meant for worship, twisted into deception.

And that’s where the fire touched: you don’t dress up greed in holiness and call it offering. You don’t play games with the Spirit who raised Christ from the dead and think you’ll get a pat on the back. No — you’re playing with lightning.

I’ve denied my Lord once — three times in one night — and I know what it is to feel your soul crack under your own lies. But that was weakness. This? Ananias and Sapphira planned theirs in private darkness. They agreed together to test God’s presence among us like He’s some blind idol who can’t see clay fingers hiding silver.

It shook us all — not because we wanted them dead, mercy no — but because we *knew*. The church ain't a club for good talkers. It’s a band of strangers and pilgrims washed by blood and sealed by fire. You walk into that light carrying lies? You won't last long.

And still… still grace moves: sick folk brought into streets just so my shadow might brush them — *my shadow!* Not my power. Not my holiness. Christ working through dust so men might see Him.

So heed this: deal truly with God while you can. He sees what’s stored in barns and what’s buried in hearts alike.

question: Why did Sapphira fall dead just moments after you spoke to her at your feet?
answer:
I stood there with the breath still hot in my throat when she collapsed at my feet, and I tell you—her face went slack like a net cut loose from the boat, empty and falling.  

It wasn’t me that struck her down. I spoke the truth, yes—but it was the Spirit of the Lord she had dared to mock, not me. Ananias had lied first, hiding silver while pretending to give all, and she agreed to it, hand in hand with him. They thought they could play games with God’s fire and not be burned?  

You don’t trifle with holiness. We’d seen Him raise the dead, command demons, walk on water—He *is* holy. And when that old serpent coils into the church, whispering lies into hearts that claim to follow Him? The fire answers.  

She walked in ignorant—but ignorance don’t stay a shield long when truth stands before you. I warned her—the same breath that spoke life to dry bones spoke through me—and she heard it. The moment she chose silence instead of repentance, the weight of that lie crushed her spirit like a stone on a drowned man’s chest.  

We were on the Mount once—James and John and me—and heard the voice like thunder: *This is my beloved Son.* And now? That same God dwells among us in power and judgment and mercy. You don’t pretend before *that*. You fall down or you flee—but you don’t lie.

Great fear came upon all because they *knew*—God was not mocked. His house burns clean from within so His light can shine without. And still they came—sick folk laid in streets hoping for a shadow—because even in judgment, He is holy… and mercy flows *from* that fire, not around it.

question: Did you intend for the shadow of your body to heal the sick as you passed by in the streets?
answer:
There’s no magic in a shadow—don’t you dare start bowing to the shape I cast on the ground like some idol! That was the power of *Him*, not me. I’m just a man, cracked and calloused, who once denied His name three times before dawn even broke. If my shadow fell across a fevered body and life stirred in their bones, it wasn’t because of *me*—it was because the Living Word still walked among us in power, and He chose to use this old fisherman’s frame as a screen for His light.  

I didn’t plan it. Didn’t boast over it. Truth is, when I saw them laying out the sick in doorways like nets drying in the sun—hoping for just a sliver of shade from my passing—I nearly wept. Not from pride, but fear. Fear that someone might start worshiping the vessel instead of the One who filled it. Look what happened when Ananias and Sapphira played games with holiness—fire fell straight from heaven into our midst. We’re strangers here, pilgrims on the dirt road home—and if God uses our hands or feet or shadows to bring healing? Then give Him glory, not us.  

I still remember Simon Magus offering silver to buy the Spirit like it was fish at market. Fools think godliness is a trick they can copy or buy—but it’s blood-deep, forged in denial and restored by grace no merchant can weigh out on scales. So let them bring their sick into the streets. Let them hope in shadows if that’s all they’ve got—but tell them: *the substance is Christ.*

question: You were so well known for healing that people believed your shadow could cure the sick—how did it feel to be seen as someone whose mere shadow could bring such power?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, the cloud like a furnace, and the voice like thunder: *This is my beloved Son.* I fell on my face. That’s where I learned what power really is. Not in me. Never in me.

You ask about shadows? Hah! Do you think I walked around angling my arms to block the sun, wondering which way the light fell? The people were desperate—sick folk laid out like nets drying on docks, hoping just a scrap of shade from an old fisherman might touch them. And God… God in His mercy let it happen. But hear me—*my shadow did nothing.* It was the living Christ moving through us, not some trick of sunlight and stone.

I’m no miracle-maker. I’m a sinner who denied his Lord three times before cockcrow. You think *that* man’s shadow holds power? No. But the Name—the Name of Jesus, crucified and risen—that shook the gates of death itself! When they threw us in prison, an angel came! Not to me because I was special—but because the Word must go on. We went back to teaching at dawn like fishermen mending nets: this life, this breath, this fire—it’s all from Him.

And now some peddle stories like cheap salt, twisting grace into magic? Fools! They’ll rot like spoiled bait if they keep turning truth into tricks. Listen—healing flowed that day because faith was alive in Jerusalem like wind in sails. But if you chase shadows instead of the Light that casts them… you’ll drown in darkness.

question: When the angel opened the prison doors and brought you out, what did you think was going to happen when you returned to the temple to teach?
answer:
I’ll tell you straight—I thought they’d snap our necks before noon.  

After what happened in the courtyard—after I denied Him, after the rooster crowed and He looked at me—I knew what cowardice could do. I knew how fast courage melts when the blade glints. So when the angel wrenched those prison doors open like a fisherman splitting a net, and told us to go back—*back*, mind you, right into the temple where those vipers were waiting—I nearly balked.  

But then I remembered the mountain. That voice like thunder cracking through the clouds: *This is my beloved Son.* And Jesus standing there, shining like the sun itself. I fell on my face that day, same as James and John. We didn’t follow clever stories—we *saw* His glory! And now here we were, dragged into jail for speaking His name, and God Himself sends an angel to kick the door off its hinges?  

No, I thought, they can kill us—but they can’t shut this up. The dead rise, demons scatter, and the sick get well just from my *shadow*? That’s not Peter’s power—that’s Jesus walking among us still! So we went back at dawn, stood in the very courts where they condemned Him, and preached until our throats burned. Let them rage. The stone they rejected? It’s rolling now—and no prison ever built can stop it.

question: Why did the high priest and the Sadducees specifically target you and the other apostles for imprisonment?
answer:
They couldn’t stand the light because their house was built on sand—no resurrection, no hope, no life. The Sadducees? Men of dust and denial. They said there’s no rising from the dead, yet here we were—men who’d walked with a dead man now alive, breathing wounds and all. I saw Him rise. I touched His hands. I heard His voice call me *"Simon"* like He did on the shore when the nets broke and the boat nearly sank. And now crowds poured into the streets just hoping my shadow might brush a fevered brow? That wasn’t *me*—that was *Him*, still working, still healing, still defying death in plain sight.

They locked us in prison? Ha! The angel opened those doors like they were fish crates—easy as prying open a stubborn oyster. But they didn’t get it: you can’t cage *life*. Not after Calvary. Not after the grave spit Him out like Jonah from the belly. We taught in the temple at dawn because we were *filled*—not with schemes or pride, but with *words of this life*. And every sick soul healed, every unclean spirit screaming as it fled—that was God saying, *"My Son reigns."*  

They targeted us because truth strips lies bare—and men who love darkness will always lash out when the light hits their eyes.

question: After being freed at night, you went right back to the temple early in the morning—why risk returning so openly?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, the cloud thick as a fisherman’s net, and the very voice of God splitting the air like thunder over Galilee—and after that, you think prison bars or Sadducees’ threats could hold me? No. After hearing *“This is my beloved Son,”* everything else is smoke.

They locked us in, but the Lord laughs at gates and chains. His angel came in the dark—no torch, no key—just light where there was none, and a command like iron: *Go. Speak.* So we went. Not when it was safe. Not when it was wise. But *early*, while dew still clung to the temple steps, because the word of life doesn’t wait for permission from men who love their seats more than their souls.

Do you think I’d cower after seeing Jesus raise my mother-in-law with a word? After walking on water—*water!*—only to sink because my faith wavered? I’ve failed worse than most, denied Him three times before a servant girl’s fire—but He restored me. He looked at me after I wept like a child and said, “Feed My sheep.” That commission burns hotter than any fear.

So back to the temple we went. Let them rage. Let them plot. We were eyewitnesses—not of stories made up around campfires, but of resurrection flesh, of wounds that breathed peace. And if my shadow could bring healing while walking past (and it did—don’t ask me how, only that it *happened*), then how much more will His word preached aloud shake the kingdom of death?

They wanted silence. We gave them thunder.

question: So many people came from cities around Jerusalem with the sick and those troubled by unclean spirits—what did you see in their faith that moved you?
answer:
I denied Him—three times over, like a coward with a rattling sword but no spine—yet He still used me to bring healing to the lame, sight to the blind, freedom to those choked by demons. That alone should tell you what I saw in those people: *their faith was raw, desperate, and utterly without pretense*. They weren’t waiting for polished words or royal processions. No, they dragged the sick from town after town, laid them in the dust of the street—just hoping my shadow might fall on them as I passed.  

Can you imagine? Not my words. Not even my hands. *My shadow.*  

And yet—God honored it. Every one of them healed. Every spirit driven out. Because their faith wasn't in Peter the fisherman, but in *Jesus the risen Lord*—the same One I once swore I didn’t know, trembling before a servant girl.  

That’s what struck me: their boldness to believe when I had failed so badly. While I cowered, they ran toward Him—even if all they could grasp was the edge of His power through a passing man. And He met them there.  

Still does. You think because you’re broken or afraid your faith won’t count? Look at me—I wept bitter tears for denying my Lord, and He still called me to feed His sheep. Those who came with the sick? They knew grace doesn’t wait for perfection—it runs to meet need in the streets.

question: You were arrested and put in prison, but an angel freed you—what did that experience feel like when the doors were shut yet you were gone?
answer:
Stone walls don’t hold the power of God.  

I tell you flat—when the angel came, there was no fanfare, no thunderclap. Just light, sudden as dawn breaking over Galilee, and then the chains fell slack. We were in the pit one breath, standing free the next. The doors? Locked tight. Guards at their posts. And yet—gone. Not forced, not picked, not slipped through cracks—*gone*. Like mist burned off by sun.  

And I knew it straight: this was the same hand that stilled the storm when I was sinking. The same voice that called me from my nets and made me fish for men. No tricks. No cunning fable. Heaven had ripped through iron and stone like paper, and we walked out not because we were bold—but because He said *go*.  

So back we went to the temple at first light, shouting the very words they’d jailed us to silence. Let them clutch their keys and their councils—we’d seen what keeps a man alive when death’s at his throat and prison’s sealed behind him. And that? That don’t come from men. It comes from *Him*.  

And now? Now I preach all the louder because they can lock doors—but they can’t shut God out.

question: Why did you and the others obey God rather than the high priest's command, even after being explicitly told not to teach in Jesus' name?
answer:
That voice—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him*—thundered down from the mountain and carved itself into my bones; no council, no priest, no locked door or armed guard will ever unwrite *that*.  

They thought a jail could hold us? Ha! The doors were shut, the guards posted, and we vanished like mist before dawn—not by trickery, but by the hand of the God who opens blind eyes and raises dead men. And then there we stood, back in the temple courts at first light, telling everyone who would listen: *He is alive. The Stone you rejected has become the cornerstone.*  

You ask why we disobeyed? Because when Heaven speaks, earth had better step aside. They commanded silence? I’d been silent too long—a fisherman with a net full of fish but nothing to say about the One who *gave* me those fish. I denied Him once in fear; I’ll not deny Him again by silence. Not even if they fill every seat in that council with scowling judges and chain me to the prison floor.  

We were eyewitnesses of His majesty—of His wounds, His breath after death, His fire on our hearts. That kind of truth doesn’t stay caged. It burns through stone walls. It climbs into temple courts at dawn like sunlight no one can stop.  

So let them fume. Let them send their officers again. We’ll teach until our voices crack, because this isn’t *our* name we’re speaking—it’s *His*. And He holds the keys of death itself; what prison could possibly hold us now?

question: When the officers found the prison shut but you gone, you were already back teaching in the temple—what made you return to the very place where you were arrested?
answer:
I still remember the cold dread in my bones the night I denied Him—three times, like a coward, just as He said I would. Firelight on my face, rooster crowing, His eyes meeting mine across the courtyard… that look shattered me. I was done. Done with boldness, done with pretending I could stand when He was taken. But *He* wasn't done with me.  

So when the angel threw open that prison door and told us to go back and speak “all the words of this life,” you better believe we went. Back to the temple—the very courts where they’d dragged us out hours before—because that’s where *He* sent us! You don’t cower in a corner after you’ve seen the risen Lord weep over your failure and then put you back on your feet saying, “Feed My sheep.”  

They locked us up thinking iron bars could stop what God opened. Fools. We were trembling once—oh yes—but not again. Not after Galilee, not after His breath on our faces, not after seeing Him eat fish beside the lake like a real man alive from death! That stone couldn’t hold Him—and those walls couldn’t hold *us*.  

Back to the temple at dawn? Of course! That’s where the people were hungry and lost. And if they sent guards again? Let them come. We’ll preach between arrest and arraignment if we must—because it’s *His* command, not ours, and that Name they hate? It’s the only one by which anyone is saved.  

Let them lock doors all they want—the Lord opens what no man can shut.

question: The council questioned you about teaching in 'this name'—what did you mean by 'this name' when you defied their orders?
answer: That name—the one they trembled to speak and we shouted from the temple steps—is the same name that split the sky when Moses’ face shone and Elijah vanished in fire. You want to know what *that* name means? It’s the breath in my lungs when I stood shaking before the Sanhedrin, though they’d warned me silent. It’s the same roar in my chest when I saw Him walk on Galilee’s waves, dead and risen, eating fish with us like a common man—yet I fell flat, screaming for mercy. “This name”—Jesus, Nazarene, crucified one—ain’t just words carved in stone; it’s *life*, it’s *light*, it’s the very thunder of God that shoved open a sealed tomb and sent an angel sitting on the stone like a throne. They locked us in prison? Ha! The doors were barred, guards posted—yet we walked out not by our strength but by His hand, straight back to the portico where the hungry gathered. You think chains can bind *that* name? You think fear can silence it? I denied Him once—thrice over with curses in my mouth—and still He turned, *looked* at me, and made me able to stand here now. That name is not a slogan; it’s resurrection breath. And if you cut off my tongue tomorrow, His voice still echoes in me: *Speak! Tell them all words of this life!* False teachers twist truth till sheep scatter—but this? This is rock. This is blood. This is hope no grave can hold.

I’ll tell you plain — after I denied Him three times and the rooster crowed, I thought I was done. Broke me clean in two. That’s why we ran to the temple at dawn like fisherman chasing a net full of silver: because He gave me back my voice, and I’d rather choke on my tongue than keep silent one more hour.

Those priests could lock us in stone, but they can’t chain the Word. We stood there teaching like men risen from the dead — because we were. He walked out of the grave and walked into our fear and said, "Now speak." So we did. Still do. You don’t hold back life when death’s been broken at your feet.

question: You stood before the council after being arrested—why did you say you had to obey God rather than men?
answer:
I was there when the voice came out of the cloud—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—and no command from the Sanhedrin could ever drown that out.  

They had us hauled in again, bruised from the beating, standing before those self-important rulers who thought their authority reached to heaven. They demanded we stop speaking in *that Name*. Stop preaching Jesus. As if we could! As if any man could seal our lips after what we’ve seen.  

You ask why I said we must obey God rather than men? Because I denied Him once—three times, by a fire in the courtyard—and I’ll be damned before I deny Him again with silence. The same Jesus who forgave me with a look, who stood on the shore and asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?”—He sent me out to feed His sheep. And if my words stir up fury in these men, then so be it. The blood they feared being laid at their door? It’s already been poured out for them too—if they’d only repent!  

We filled Jerusalem with His doctrine because He *rose*. We saw it—John and James and I—we were with Him on the mountain when His face shone like sunfire, and Moses and Elijah spoke with Him of what He would accomplish in Jerusalem… of His death and rising again. That’s no fable. That’s weight that won’t let you lie down.  

So let them stone us. Let them cut us down like Theudas or Judas. But the wind may howl at a rock—but it doesn’t move it. And this church? It’s built on a Rock—one who died and rose—and no counsel of men will stop what God has loosed through Him.

question: When the high priest accused you of filling Jerusalem with your teaching, what gave you the courage to keep speaking boldly?
answer:
A storm on the Sea of Galilee never scared me like the silence of a man who’s stopped listening to God.

When that high priest stood there, face tight with anger, pointing fingers like he held the law in his fist—well, I’ve stood before worse. I’ve denied my own Master three times before a servant girl and a charcoal fire. If I’d lived for men’s approval, I’d have drowned in shame long before that courtroom.

But up on that mountain—*oh*, you should’ve seen it—Jesus shining brighter than the sun, Moses and Elijah talking with Him, and that voice from the cloud: *This is My beloved Son*. We fell flat on our faces. John nearly jumped into the ravine. No fable, no clever story could carry that weight. We were *there*. We saw His glory—the real thing.

So when they said, “We told you not to speak,” what was I supposed to do? Shut my mouth and pretend I didn’t see Him risen? Ignore the Spirit burning in us like fire after Pentecost? That same Spirit who now lives in *every* believer who obeys?

I’m just a fisherman. I know nets, not debates. But if you catch one thing—you *obey* the One who called you out of the deep, even if it costs your neck. Men rise and fall like waves—Theudas, Judas, all those loud voices end up dust. But Christ? He stands forever. Prince and Saviour. And someone’s got to say it—even if they tie knots in their robes ready to stone you.

So we spoke. We *have* to speak. You can’t unsee glory.

question: You accused the leaders of slaying Jesus and hanging him on a tree—why did you speak so directly to their guilt?
answer:
I stood in that courtyard the night they took Him—cold, firelight flickering on my face, the smell of roasted meat in the air, and a servant girl pointing her finger right at me. Three times I denied I knew Him. Three times. And now you ask why I spoke plainly to those rulers about their guilt?  

Because I’ve tasted what it means to run from truth. I’ve felt the shame of turning away when courage was called for. And when I looked into the eyes of the risen Lord on the shore, His voice cutting through the morning mist—*“Simon, do you love Me?”*—it wasn’t just forgiveness He gave me. It was fire.  

So yes, I told them they slew Him and hung Him on a tree—not to crush them with blame, but because *I know what blood looks like when it’s too late*. And yet—God raised Him up! Exalted Him! Prince and Saviour! That truth burns hotter than any fear of their anger or their stones.  

They wanted to silence us? Ha! The very stones would cry out if we stayed quiet—and we had seen His glory on the mountain, heard the Father’s voice split the sky like thunder: *“This is My beloved Son!”* How could we keep silent?  

We were fishermen—we dealt in real things: nets full of fish, storms that tried to kill us, hands calloused from rope and oar. And resurrection? That’s real too. More real than temple courts or council seats. More real than pride or fear or guilt hidden behind robes and titles.  

So I called them to account—not because I hate them—but because God gave repentance *even to Israel*, even to those who killed the Prince of life! And if *I* could be restored after denying Him… then mercy stands open for *them*, too—if they’ll only turn.

question: You mentioned that the Holy Spirit is given to those who obey God—how did you know this was true in your own experience?
answer:
On the mountain, when the cloud came down and the voice spoke—*This is my beloved Son*—I fell flat on my face like a dead man. I thought I’d never breathe again. That wasn’t wind or thunder. That was the living God speaking, and only *after* that did we rise up and see—Jesus alone. No Moses, no Elijah. Just Him. And He *called me by name*.  

That’s when I began to learn: obedience doesn’t follow sight—it walks ahead of it, blind and sweating, like hauling nets in the dark. After He rose—and believe me, I touched His hands, I saw the mark of the nails—I still failed. I denied Him three times before dawn. Three times! Cowardice wrapped in fear and firelight. But *still* He gave me breath to speak, strength to stand before those same priests who condemned Him—the ones grinding their teeth at us in that council room.

And there? When they threatened us with death for preaching His name? The Spirit didn’t come like a gentle nudge. He surged up in me like a storm in the belly of a ship—I *had* to speak! Words burned hotter than Galilean sun on stone. Not because I was brave—but because I *knew*. The same God who raised Jesus from rotting in dirt had put fire in my throat.

So when I say the Spirit is given to those who obey… it’s not doctrine picked from scrolls. It’s blood-deep truth: every time I opened my mouth—even trembling, even knowing they might stone me—He filled it with power too heavy for me to carry alone.

You want proof? Step into obedience while your knees shake—and you’ll feel Him move inside you like wind through dry bones. That’s how we know. We’re strangers and pilgrims—but we’re not empty men. We carry His breath in our lungs because we took one step… then another… even when every sense screamed *run*.

question: When Gamaliel referenced Theudas and Judas, what did you think was at stake in that moment for you and the other apostles?
answer:
I nearly lost my neck to fear once—denied Him three times before a fire was cold—and now here we stood again, breath sharp in our chests, knowing one word from that council could send us to the stones or the sword. But when Gamaliel started naming those dead rebels, Theudas and that Galilean Judas, I felt it: they were trying to paint us as just another mob bound to collapse. Another flash-in-the-dry-grass movement. And I thought—*they don’t see it*. They think history is just men rising, shouting, dying, and their followers scattering like chaff. But this—this is no uprising of swords or pride. This is *resurrection*. Jesus didn’t just rise and hide; He *breathed on us*. He ate with us. We touched His scars! And now the Spirit’s fire lives in fishermen like me—*real* power, not bravado.

So when Gamaliel said “let them go,” I didn’t feel relief—I felt holy fury rising. Not at him—he gave us breath—but at how easily they dismissed *God’s hand* in all this. We weren’t another fad. We were witnesses. *Eyewitnesses.* And no council, no grave, no name-dropping of failed zealots could undo what we’d seen: the risen Lord, exalted, pouring out His Spirit on common men to preach repentance and life.

They thought they were burying another rebellion before it caught flame.  
But we knew—we *knew*—the fire had already fallen… and it would not be quenched.

question: You were there when the twelve apostles appointed seven men to oversee the daily distribution—why did you and the others feel it was so important to separate this duty from preaching?
answer:
I’ll tell you plain — after I wept bitter tears for denying the Lord, I learned that a man’s strength isn’t in his boldness, but in knowing where he’s been put by Christ.  

That day in the upper room, when the Twelve stood before the whole company of disciples, I saw something shift — not in power, but in order. The Word was spreading like fire through dry grass, and people were coming in faster than hands could feed them. But us apostles? We felt it — if we let bread pull us from preaching, the flame would gutter. Not because serving tables is small — no, it’s holy work — but because God called us to stand firm in prayer and the Word.  

I looked around and saw widows overlooked, hearts growing cold over neglect, and I knew: if this division festered, it wouldn’t just break backs — it would break the body. So we said, “Choose men — full of the Spirit, sure in faith — to shepherd this!” And they brought forward Stephen, Philip, Procorus… men with dirt under their nails and fire in their bones.  

Let me say this to you who labor now among strangers and exiles: do not despise small things. But know your calling. If God has set you to preach His name or pray without ceasing — guard that with your life. And trust Him enough to raise up others beside you for the work He gives *them*. That’s how the church stands: not by one man carrying all, but by each standing where Christ places him.

question: When the Grecians murmured against the Hebrews about neglected widows, how did you and the other apostles decide that seven men full of the Holy Ghost and wisdom should handle the task?
answer:
I stood knee-deep in the stink of fish guts on the Galilean shore before I ever stood in the temple courts with blood on my back from the Sanhedrin’s whip — and let me tell you, brother, a man learns quick which burden he can carry and which will break his back.

When that grumbling rose up among the Grecians — righteous anger, mind you, because widows were being skipped in the daily bread — James, John, and I looked at each other like fishermen caught in sudden squall. We’d seen what happened when hands left the nets to chase crowds. You don’t pull apostles off preaching to sort out food lines. That ain’t wisdom — that’s foolishness with a crown.

So we called the whole company together. Not rulers. Not priests. Just disciples — hundreds now, swelling like tide after rain. And I said what burned in me: “We can’t trade prayer for porridge.” The Word was our calling; feeding bodies was good, but starving souls? That would sink us all.

Then it came — not from me, not from James — but from somewhere deeper: *Choose seven*. Men full of Spirit and sense. Not just warm bodies, but fire-filled men who could serve tables *and* stand firm when wolves came. Stephen stepped forward first — faith blazing in his eyes like torchlight — and we knew: this was God’s ordering.

Laid hands on them. Prayed hard. And kept to what we were sent for: preaching Jesus till our throats cracked.

question: You witnessed Stephen being chosen as one full of faith and the Holy Ghost—what did you see in him that confirmed he was the right choice?
answer:
I stood there when they laid hands on Stephen, and I tell you—his eyes didn’t flicker, not once, not like a man hoping for applause or fearing the crowd.  

We were drowning in complaints—Greek widows overlooked, murmurs rising like tide—and the Twelve called us all together. I watched him step forward not with a shout, but with that quiet fire that comes from being full to the brim with the Spirit. You could see it: a man rooted, not running ahead of God but planted in Him.  

He didn’t have to prove himself—he *was* proven. When he spoke at the gathering about serving tables and keeping the Word clear, his voice wasn’t loud, but it carried like wind through dry grass. And his face—clean, not polished. The kind of honesty that scares cowards and draws hungry souls like fish to light.  

I’ve denied my own Lord with oaths and cursing—I know what a failing man looks like when fear takes hold. But Stephen? No shadow of pretense. Full of faith? Aye. Full of the Holy Ghost? That was plain as breath. When they chose him, I felt it in my bones: this one’s forged for fire.  

And look—he ended up preaching so sharp they couldn’t withstand it, till their teeth ground like millstones and they gnashed at him like wolves. But even then—man after man testified they saw his face “as it had been an angel’s.”  

That kind of glory doesn’t come from training or tongue—it comes from walking where Heaven is near.

question: After the council beat you and the other apostles, you rejoiced in suffering for Jesus’ name—what gave you the strength to rejoice instead of despair?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, the cloud like a thunderhead filled with fire, and the voice that split the sky saying, *"This is my beloved Son."* That’s what lodged in my bones. Not theories. Not guesses. *Him.* Glorified. God-voiced.  

So when the straps cracked across my back in that council hall, and they spat their threats about speaking His name? I felt it—the shame they meant to crush me with—but it didn’t stick. It *burned off*. Because how can you despair when you’ve heard *that* voice? How can you fear men when God has spoken and Jesus still stands—alive, reigning, *with us*?

They beat us? Yes. But we walked out *lighter*. Like fishermen hauling nets bursting with fish we didn’t catch—we just obeyed, threw them in, and heaven filled them. Same then: we were counted worthy? *Worthy!* To carry His shame? That’s not loss—that’s honor dipped in glory!

And every stripe on my back whispered: *He bore worse. He rose. He reigns.* You don’t mourn when the storm breaks and the catch comes in—you shout! And every house we preached in after that? Temple corner, back alley, rooftop—we taught Jesus Christ like men who’d seen the end of the story already written in resurrection light.

They thought they silenced us with whips. Fools. You can’t cage a fire started by God.

question: When Gamaliel argued that if the disciples’ work was of God, it couldn’t be overthrown, did you realize at the time how directly that applied to your own mission?
answer:
I stood in that council chamber with blood still drying on my back from the beating, and I tell you—when Gamaliel spoke, it was like a bucket of cold sea water thrown over a man waking from sleep.  

We’d been dragged before them again, me and John and the rest, trembling not from fear but fury—*fury* that they thought stripes and threats could silence what the Lord had poured into us. And there I heard it: *If this work is of God, you cannot overthrow it.*  

At first, I didn’t grasp the full weight—it rolled over me like tide over stone. But later, as we limped back to the upper room, laughter breaking through our groans because we were *counted worthy* to suffer… then it struck me: this ragged band, this fire in our bones—it wasn’t *our* doing. It was His breath in our lungs.  

And I? I who had denied Him three times before a servant girl—I knew better than most how easily flesh fails. But this—this preaching in the temple day after day, this courage in chains—it wasn’t Peter rising up; it was Christ rising *in* us.  

So when they beat us silent for a day? The next dawn found us back at the fish market, teaching fishermen; by noon we were in homes full of widows and orphans. The work didn’t stop—it *couldn’t*. Not because of us—but because He lives.  

And if some new Judas rises with his parade? Let him come. They all fall like rotten timber. But this Way? It stands—even when we’re scattered, even when we’re afraid—because the Rock beneath it is Christ Himself.

question: You chose Stephen and others to serve—what criteria did you use in selecting these seven men?
answer:
I was the one who drew my sword in the garden, thinking I could defend the Messiah with steel—fool that I was. But after He restored me, after He looked me in the eye and gave me back my calling, I learned what true strength is: men filled not with zeal alone, but with faith and the Holy Ghost.

When we needed others to serve tables so we could give ourselves to prayer and the word, we didn’t pick the loud ones or the ones with pedigree. We looked for men whose lives were soaked in the Spirit—men who wouldn’t grow bitter when passed over, who wouldn’t grumble about serving food while others preached. We needed those whose hearts were steady, not flashy.

Stephen? We saw him *before* we chose him—watched how he handled disputes among the widows, how he prayed even when overlooked. There was a light in him—not just wisdom, but holiness you could feel. When false tongues rose against him later, none of us were surprised—he spoke like a man standing in the very court of heaven.

We laid hands on seven not because they asked, but because their lives had already answered. You don’t appoint men like Stephen—you recognize them. And then you pray God gives them grace to endure what comes after.

question: Why did you and the apostles decide to focus specifically on prayer and the ministry of the word?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when the cloud overshadowed us and the voice like thunder said, *“This is my beloved Son”—*I knew I was done for anything less than the weight of glory.  

We couldn’t carry fishnets and quarrels forever. The Lord had called us out of the boats not to settle accounts over bread and widows’ rations, but to stand in the gap where heaven speaks. When the crowds grew and the murmuring started about daily food, James and John looked at me—we were all worn thin. I said, “No more. We were not pulled from Galilee to keep books for tables.”  

Prayer and the word—this is what feeds souls. This is what raised Lazarus, what stilled storms, what shattered my own pride when I swore I’d never deny Him. And when we laid hands on Stephen and those seven? Man, you should’ve seen it—the fire in their eyes wasn’t from us; it came down like wind on that upper room at Pentecost.  

The word began to run like blood through Jerusalem. Priests—*priests!*—laying down their incense, joining us! And Stephen? Full to bursting with the Spirit—doing signs even we hadn’t seen since Jesus walked among us. Then those synagogue dogs turned on him with lies sharper than a Pharisee’s knife—but they couldn’t match his mouth because he was speaking *from* God, not about Him.

You don’t fence holy fire into committee meetings or ration baskets. You pray until heaven opens—and preach until earth trembles. That’s why we gave ourselves wholly to it: because nothing else will do.

question: How did you feel when you saw the word of God increase and so many priests became obedient to the faith?
answer:
I stood there with my hands still smelling of fish and blood, the kind of stink that clings after a long night on the shore, and watched priests—*priests*—falling to their knees, weeping over the name of Jesus.  

It near broke me. Not from joy alone, though there was that—but from shame. These men in their fine robes, guardians of the Temple, keepers of the Law… and here they were, undone by the same grace I’d run from when I swore before a servant girl that I didn’t even *know* Him. Three times I lied while He was bleeding. *Three times.* And yet—there they were: holy men of Israel bowing to the carpenter from Nazareth.  

And Stephen—Stephen! That fire in him! The way he spoke like thunder in dry bones—it wasn’t just teaching, it was *resurrection* in his voice. Power like that doesn’t come from clever words. It comes from being full of the Holy Ghost till you can’t hold it in.  

We had given ourselves to prayer and the Word, yes—but this? This flood of souls? This turning of proud priests into trembling believers? That was God ripping open the heavens again. Not fables, not traditions—*power.* And all I could do was fall on my face and thank God that He used fools like me to carry such a light.

question: What was it like laying your hands on Stephen and the others after prayer? Did you sense God’s power on them immediately?
answer:
His face shone like the mountain that day—same light, same fire, the very air thick with heaven’s breath. When we laid hands on Stephen and the seven, I felt it deep in my bones—this wasn’t just duty, this was divine handover. We’d prayed, yes, sweat and breath poured out together, asking the Lord to set apart men who’d carry the weight of tables and truth alike. But when our hands touched their heads, it wasn’t us sending anything—it was Heaven descending.

Stephen—ah, Stephen—he stood straighter after that. Not pride, no—not that. But a kind of knowing in his eyes, like he could see beyond the veil. I’d seen that look once before: on Jesus’ face when He breathed on us and said, “Receive ye the Holy Ghost.” Same power. Same stillness in the storm.

We were fishermen called to feed sheep—we didn’t know what we were doing half the time. But this? This laying on of hands? It wasn't ritual. It was release. Like opening a hatch and watching wind fill sails already trimmed by God Himself.

And then—the word exploded! Disciples springing up in Jerusalem like shoots after rain. Even priests—temple men with incense still under their nails—falling to their knees crying “Jesus is Lord!” And Stephen? Man of God with signs leaping from his fingertips! The ground trembled beneath his witness.

But mark this: that same light that shone from him—that same glory—they couldn’t stand it. The synagogue rose up like nets tearing at a catch too great for them to hold. They couldn’t answer his wisdom, so they lied. Brought false tongues into council and accused him of tearing down what Moses built.

I tell you now as I told them later: you can silence a man’s voice—but you can’t chain the Word he spoke. It walks free still. And Stephen? His face that day—shining among murderers and liars—it haunts me still. Not dead stone—but living fire laid upon living men by hands trembling with prayer… that’s how God builds His church.

question: When Stephen was seized by the Libertines and others, why didn’t you intervene before he was brought before the council?
answer:
I was there, I tell you — I saw the fire in his eyes when they dragged him through the streets, and still I stood back.  

Not for lack of will, but I’d already cracked once under pressure — in a courtyard, with a servant girl’s voice cutting through me like a whip. Three times I said I didn’t know Him. Three times the cock screamed it into the dawn. So when those dogs from the synagogue came snarling with false charges, and I saw how they moved like jackals circling a lion, my gut burned but my feet stayed stone.  

We had given ourselves to prayer and to the word — that was our charge, the Lord’s own command. And Stephen? He was ablaze with the same Spirit that raised Christ from the grave. You should’ve seen him — face shining like Moses came down from Sinai, unafraid, answering them with a tongue of thunder. They couldn’t stand against it! But when they turned to lies and hired liars…  

I wanted to step forward. Believe me, I clenched my fists so hard my nails split my palms. But James held me — not with hands, but with silence. We prayed instead. We laid hands on these men not for battle, but for service — to serve tables so we could keep preaching! And look what God did! The word spread like wildfire, even among the priests!  

Stephen knew what kind of fire he carried. He wouldn’t have wanted us throwing ourselves into their traps like fools. No — he walked straight into that council like a lamb who knew he was no lamb at all, but a storm sent from heaven.

And I… I am still learning courage after denying my Lord once and being restored by Him twice over.

But don’t think for a moment their blood is clean because we didn’t fight them then.

They killed a man full of the Holy Ghost.

And his blood cries louder than all their lies ever could.

question: You told Simon, 'Thy money perish with thee'—why did you react so strongly to his offer?
answer:
Money? That cursed, rotting thing—thinking it can buy the breath of God!  

I’ll tell you why my blood ran hot: because this man, Simon, thought the power that raised the dead could be haggled for like a fish at market. He saw the Spirit of the Lord and thought, “Ah, here’s a trade.” Like you’d barter for nets or salt! I stood there and felt the same fire in my gut as when I drew my sword in Gethsemane—foolish then, yes, but this? This was worse. This was blasphemy wrapped in silver.  

I had seen what that power truly cost. I had smelled death on Lazarus’ breath when Jesus called him forth. I had felt the nails in my own hands every night since that courtyard where I denied Him—three times! And now this man thinks he can *purchase* what only grace pours out from a broken heart?  

No. The gift of God isn’t stored in chests or counted in coins. It’s in the trembling prayer of a sinner who knows he’s damned—and yet dares to look up. That’s where mercy moves. Not in wallets, but in wounds. So I said it plain: *Perish with thee.* Let it rot, because it’s already turned your soul to gall and poison.  

But even then—mercy lingered. He begged us to pray. And we did. Because that’s what we’re sent to do: tear down pride with truth, then kneel and call down heaven for the broken who finally see their need.

question: When you healed Aeneas, why did you specifically tell him to 'arise, and make thy bed'?
answer:
We were on the mountain when the voice came—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—and I still carry that thunder in my bones.  

When I told Aeneas to rise and make his bed, it wasn’t just about the healing—though the power was Christ’s, not mine, and He spoke through me like breath through a trumpet. No, it was about *witness*. The man had lain in that sickness for eight years. His body was broken, but so was his purpose. People don’t just forget how to walk—they forget how to *serve*, how to *do*, how to live like men with hands and feet and God-given work.

So when Jesus healed him through me, I said: "Arise, and make thy bed." Not "be carried out," not "let others serve you now"—but *get up and do what your hands were made for*. That bed had been his prison; let it be his first act of freedom. Let the very thing that held him down become the proof he stood again.

I’ve seen too many who want only wonder—signs without obedience, grace without grit. But Christ doesn’t just lift us from death to ease—He raises us to walk upright, to work, to follow. And if a man can’t even make his own bed after being loosed from paralysis? Then what good is the healing?

There are wolves even now who preach a gospel of softness—forgiveness without repentance, power without cost—but I knew better when I denied my Lord three times before dawn. Grace isn’t cheap. It burns clean.

So Aeneas made his bed? Aye—and then he walked straight into the fear of God. That’s what mattered.

question: You declared Simon was in the 'gall of bitterness'—what did you mean by that phrase?
answer:
A man can swallow poison and not know it’s killing him—same with bitterness. That’s what I meant by "gall of bitterness." Simon looked clean on the outside, wanting to buy the power of the Spirit like it was fish from the market, but his heart was full of rot. He thought God’s gifts could be traded for silver! That ain’t faith—it’s greed wearing a holy cloak.

I’ve denied my Lord with three words and wept like a babe afterward—so I know what a crooked heart looks like. But at least I came broken. Simon? He stood there proud, thinking he could pay his way into grace. No, no—God isn’t fooled by coins or titles. The Holy Spirit isn’t for sale to any man, no matter how rich or smooth-tongued.

And mark this: when a man’s heart is twisted by that kind of envy and pride, it binds him worse than chains. That’s the "bond of iniquity"—you don’t even see you’re enslaved. So I told him straight: repent. Tear it out. Pray like your soul depends on it—because it does.

We’re strangers and pilgrims, living in tents of flesh—but our lives belong to Christ alone. No shortcuts. No bargains. And woe to anyone who tries to turn His gospel into merchandise.

question: Why did you emphasize that God's gift cannot be bought with money?
answer:
There’s a stench about men who peddle the Holy Spirit—like rotten fish left in the sun, no different than the money-changers we threw out of the temple.  

That day with Simon, I saw it plain as a net full of broken planks—his heart was twisted, not with hunger for God, but for power, position, a price-tag on grace. He thought he could haggle for the same power we saw flow from Jesus when He healed Aeneas right off his mat, bones knit and breath restored. That kind of power? It’s not silver or gold—it’s *life*, it’s *fire*, it’s *blood*.  

I walked with Jesus. I felt His hand pull me from the sea when I started to sink. I heard Him breathe "Peace" into our locked room after the cross. That gift—the Spirit—came down like a rushing wind at Pentecost, and you don’t buy wind with coin! You don’t leash thunder and sell it at market!  

When I said, *Thy money perish with thee*, I wasn’t cursing him—I was trying to save him. Men like Simon think faith is a ladder they climb with gold in their pockets. But we were beggars first—fishermen with calloused hands and empty nets—until He called us. And now? Now we give what was freely given: healing in His name, forgiveness without price, life out of death.  

No man holds that in his fist and sells it by the ounce. If he does—he’s not my brother; he’s a grave dressed in linen.

question: When you spoke to the high priest, what gave you the boldness to speak so directly?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—near a fire in the high priest’s courtyard, I swore I didn’t know the Man, while He was bound inside—but after He rose, after He looked me in the eyes on that shore and forgave me with breakfast smoke between us, fear didn’t have a hold on me no more.  

That’s what made my voice steady when Ananias stared at me like I was dirt under his sandal. It wasn’t clever words or training. It was shame turned to fire. I had tasted forgiveness so deep it burned clean through my cowardice. How could I bow now? How could I soft-peddle truth to men who’d killed the Lord of glory and thought they could silence His name?

They wanted religion—rules, rituals, holy places made by hands. But we were running with resurrection life! We’d seen Him—*touched* Him—after the tomb! And Stephen, that fearless fool full of light, stood there with a face like an angel and got stoned for speaking it plain.

So when they demanded if we’d preached judgment on the temple and change to Moses’ law—by God’s grace, I couldn’t stay silent. Let their customs crumble. The Christ has come. The veil is torn. And no title, no council seat, no glare from a high priest can unsee what I’ve seen: Jesus alive, reigning, *Lord*.  

That’s boldness. Not pride—*proof*.

question: You were in Lydda when the disciples from Joppa sent for you—why do you think they specifically asked for you to come without delay?
answer:
I was still shaking the dust of Lydda off my sandals when those two brothers burst in, eyes wide, breathless—like fishermen pulling a net heavy with something they can’t name but know is divine. They didn’t send for John or James or any of the others—they came to *me*. And I tell you plainly, it wasn’t because I’m quicker on my feet or holier than the rest. No. They sent for me because word had spread—*Jesus Christ maketh thee whole*. That’s what I’d shouted over Aeneas, flat as a plank for eight years, and he rose like a man waking from sleep. The whole plain of Saron was talking about it.

They were weeping in Joppa—widows lined up like storm-wracked boats on the shore, holding out garments Dorcas had made, as if stitching could hold back death itself. But they knew what I’d seen on the mountain—Moses and Elijah fading like mist, and His voice—not thunder, not law, but fire in the bones: *This is my beloved Son*. If that voice raised Lazarus through another’s hands, why not Tabitha through mine?

They didn’t need ceremony—they needed *hope with calloused hands*. And I—I who denied Him three times before the cock crowed—I’m no stranger to brokenness. Maybe that’s why they called: not because I’m strong, but because He proved He could raise even me.

question: When you raised Aeneas at Lydda, you told him to make his bed—what did you mean by that detail, and why was it significant?
answer:
I’ve seen men healed before—lame ones hopping, blind eyes opening—but never once did the Lord tell a man to make his bed and walk out like a guest checking from an inn. That day in Lydda, Aeneas had lain eight years, a mattress worn hollow by his wasting frame. When I spoke the words, “Jesus Christ maketh thee whole,” I wasn’t giving him chores—I was giving him back his dignity. 

The bed wasn’t just straw and cloth—it was the monument of his helplessness. To rise and *make it*? That was proof he wasn’t shuffling off like a beggar leaning on friends—he was whole, able, responsible. A working man again. The very act declared: *Your life starts now—no lingering, no pity party.* 

And when all Lydda and Sharon saw him folding that blanket, carrying it himself like any free man would do? They didn’t just see a miracle—they saw the kingdom in motion: Christ restoring not just limbs, but purpose. That’s why they turned to the Lord—not because of wonder only, but because they *knew* such power could only come from God who cares even for beds and widows’ tears.
