question: You put everyone out of the room before raising Tabitha—why did you choose to be alone with her during that moment?
answer:
There’s a holy fear that comes over you when death’s still warm in the room — not the fear of failure, mind you, though I’ve known that plenty — but the trembling awe of standing where God is about to move like He did at Jairus’ house, like He did when I stood weeping after denying Him thrice and then saw His eyes across the fire.

I put them out — not because their grief was wrong, poor widows weeping with coats in their hands, garments Dorcas stitched with her own fingers — no, their love was true. But power like that, life rushing back into cold flesh? That’s between the living God and the dead. Can’t have distraction. Can’t have disbelief muttering in the corners. I’ve seen what unbelief does — it chokes faith like smoke in a hull.

When Jesus raised Jairus’ daughter, He took only Peter and James and John — no crowd, no clamor. I learned that lesson at His feet. So when those brothers came from Joppa fast as mules could carry them, begging me not to delay — I knew what had to be done. Go down. Pray low. Speak sharp.

And when I told her to arise… by God’s mercy she opened her eyes.

Not my power. Never mine. But He uses broken hands like mine to do what only He can do.

So let every stranger and pilgrim take courage: our God is near even in death’s chamber. And sometimes… a little silence is where resurrection begins.

question: The widows showed you the coats and garments Dorcas had made—how did seeing those works affect you as you prayed?
answer:
I’ve stood in bloodied linen before—when I cut off that soldier’s ear, the cloth got stained, and I knew what a fool I’d been. But standing there in that upper room, seeing those coats Dorcas had stitched with her own hands, it wasn’t blood on them but love—love in every seam. The widows pressed them into my hands like they were proof—proof she lived, proof she mattered, proof the Lord hadn’t wasted her life. And I tell you, it stirred me to the bone.

I’d seen Jesus do this very thing—take death by the throat and say *“Get up.”* And here these women were, not wailing into emptiness but holding up garments like banners of holy work well done. It made me bold—not because *I* could do it, but because He had already done it. That room wasn’t full of orphans—it was full of witnesses. And when I knelt, I didn’t pray for power—I prayed for faith like theirs. Faith that works don’t earn resurrection—but prove the Kingdom walks among us.

So I turned to her body and said, “Tabitha, arise.” Not because of me—but because of *Him*, because of *her*, because of *them*. And she opened her eyes.

Now look—you strangers and pilgrims who labor in quietness and fear your work is unseen: your coats are noticed. Your hands are known. The Lord sees them—and He’s not far from raising something new out of what looks like dead ground.

question: When you said 'Tabitha, arise,' she opened her eyes and saw you—what did you feel in that moment when she came back to life?
answer:
I’ve denied Him with oaths and curses—three times over, my voice cracking in the cold courtyard—and yet He still put me to work raising the dead.  

When she stirred, when her eyes fluttered open and fixed on me, I didn’t feel pride—no, not after denying Him. I felt *small*. Like when we were on that mountain, and His voice split the air like thunder: *This is My beloved Son*. That same fear gripped me. Not because of Tabitha—but because *He* was there. In the room. In the breath that returned to her lungs.  

I didn’t raise her. I spoke the words, yes—“Tabitha, arise”—but it was Jesus Christ who made her whole. Just like Aeneas. Just like every blind eye opened and every lame foot walking straight again.  

Those widows were weeping, showing me the coats Dorcas had made—simple things, stitched with love—and now here she sat up, blinking at the light. The very air in that upper chamber changed—thick with awe. And I tell you this: if a woman known for sewing garments can be restored by His power, then what will He do for those who trust Him unto death?  

We’re strangers and pilgrims here—but He holds life in His hands. Even death flees at His name spoken through broken men like me.

question: You were in Joppa with Simon the tanner—what was it like staying in his house after raising Tabitha?
answer:
I still smell the brine and the bitter tang of tanning vats when I think of that house.  

Simon’s roof wasn’t much—leather stretched, salted hides piled in corners, the stink thick enough to make a man gag. But the Lord wasn’t chasing perfumed palaces when He sent me there. No, He was moving in the stench, in the ordinary filth of a working man’s life. After raising Tabitha—after seeing her sit up, blink, take my hand—there was no denying it: God was tearing down walls I didn’t even know I’d built.  

And then Cornelius—a Gentile! A Roman soldier!—and God shoving a vision into *my* head like a spear in the ribs: *What I have cleansed, call not common.* I fought it. Oh, how I fought it! My gut twisted at the thought of eating with uncircumcised men, let alone sharing the Holy Spirit with them. But that rooftop in Joppa became holy ground—even with pelts hanging from the beams and Simon scraping fat off skins below.  

It humbled me. After denying Him three times and still being called… after touching death and seeing Tabitha breathe again… after an angel speaking straight into a soldier’s prayer—I stood there smelling of fish and goat hide and realized: grace doesn’t knock politely on clean doors. It kicks them down covered in mud and blood and says, *“This one too.”*  

So yes—I stayed with a tanner. And yes—I ate his food. And if any false teacher wants to argue about clean hands and proper company, they can answer to the God who chose a foul-smelling rooftop to launch His mercy into the nations.

question: Why did you put everyone out of the room before you knelt down and prayed to raise Tabitha?
answer:
I’ve failed more times than I can count—denied the Lord three times before the rooster crowed, just as He said. But He still used me, a broken fisherman, to raise Tabitha.

When I stood there in that upper room in Joppa, with the widows weeping and holding out the garments Dorcas had made—each one a stitch of love, a thread of mercy—I saw their grief thick as fog off the Sea of Galilee. And I knew what they needed wasn’t comfort from me. They needed power from Him.

So I put them all out because grief can crowd out faith. Their mourning wasn’t wrong—no, not at all—but faith needs space to breathe. You can’t hear the voice of God when everyone’s wailing at once. I’d seen Jesus do it—cleared the room when Jairus’ daughter lay still. No spectacle, no noise—just prayer, and power.

I knelt like any sinner would, not like some mighty miracle-worker. Just a man asking God to move. And when He answered and Tabitha opened her eyes? That wasn’t my doing—it was His mercy on her, on them, on me.

And then I took her by the hand—I’ll never forget that grip—and lifted her up as if pulling a net full of grace from deep water. Then called the saints back in—not for show, but so they’d see: our God raises the dead. Not with fanfare for kings or soldiers, but in a room full of widows who sewed garments and believed.

That’s how He works. Quietly. Powerfully. And then word spreads—like it did in Joppa—and strangers begin to believe too. Even centurions in Caesarea… even Gentiles… even people we thought were far off.

Our hope isn’t in fine clothes or long prayers or good deeds alone—it’s in a living Lord who hears when you kneel and answers when faith makes room for Him.

question: When you told Tabitha to arise, how did you know she would open her eyes and sit up?
answer:
I stood there trembling, same as always — the man who denied his Master three times before the rooster crowed — and yet He still sent me.  

When I looked at Tabitha’s body, cold and wrapped in silence, it wasn’t some grand vision or angel’s command that gave me courage. No, it was the fire on the shore after He rose — you know, when He asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?” Like He was mending what I’d shattered. And each time I said “Yea, Lord,” something in me snapped back into place.  

So when I told her to arise… I wasn’t guessing. I *knew*. Not because of signs or wisdom from men, but because I had seen Him do it — Jairus’ daughter, limp in her bed, and Jesus took her hand and said, “Talitha cumi.” Little girl, arise. And she did. Just like this.  

I knelt there in that room full of weeping widows clutching Dorcas’ garments — their tears soaking into wool she’d stitched with kindness — and I remembered His voice. His power. His breath in my face on Galilee’s shore when He breathed on us and said, “Receive ye the Holy Ghost.”  

So I spoke like He spoke. Not by might, not by learning — but because the same Spirit that raised Christ from the dead was in me. And when she opened her eyes? Oh, brother… it wasn’t *me* they saw first — it was *Him*, working through broken hands. Hands that once ran from a servant girl’s question in a courtyard.

And yes — she sat up. Just like He did. Just like we must all do: rise, and live again.

question: You presented Tabitha alive to the saints and widows—what did you see in their faces at that moment?
answer:
Faith isn’t some lazy dream spun by smooth-talkers who nibble at truth like foxes in a henhouse—no, it *works*, and I saw it tear through Joppa like a storm wind that day.  

When I turned Tabitha back to them—alive, breathing, *warm*—the saints and widows didn’t just weep. No. Their faces… I’ll never forget it. Shock first, like they’d been struck dumb by lightning. Then eyes wide as if heaven had cracked open right there in that upper room. One woman fell to her knees so fast I thought she’d break her bones. Another clutched the sleeve of her robe—the very one Dorcas had stitched—and pressed it to her mouth like she was holding in a scream or a song or both.  

They knew her hands. They wore her work every day—tunics for the poor, cloaks for widows, little things stitched with care while she laughed and taught us how to share bread without counting crumbs. And now here she stood, *breathing*, because the God of Israel—the same who raised Isaac from the altar and Elijah’s boy from the dead—had answered prayer through a broken man like me.  

I tell you, it wasn’t my power. It was His voice behind mine when I said *“Arise.”* And when she sat up? That room became a battlefield where death lost its grip—and faith roared back to life on trembling legs.

Many believed after that. Many more than before.

Even Simon the tanner, who let me stay in his house though he dealt with dead skins all day—he looked at me different afterward. Like maybe even unclean hands could carry something holy.

And let the deceivers say what they want about empty words and clever myths—I stood over a dead woman and called her name.

She answered.

We *were* eyewitnesses of His majesty... even in the house of mourning.

question: Cornelius was a centurion in Caesarea who feared God—what made you realize he was the one the angel was sending you to?
answer:
I’d just come down from the rooftop where I’d seen a sheet let down by its four corners, and nothing in it was common or unclean—God had said it three times, loud as thunder in my bones. That vision burned in me when those men from Cornelius showed up, Gentiles with no right to knock on a prophet’s door, yet sent by an angel with clear eyes and holy fear.  

I nearly refused them—I was still wrestling with what clean and unclean really meant. But the Spirit said, *Three men are seeking you. Go with them without hesitation.* So I went. And when I stood in that Caesarea house and saw Cornelius fall at my feet like I was some god—me, Peter, the fisherman who once denied his Lord three times before breakfast—I pulled him up fast and said, “Stand! I’m a man like you.”

Then he told how an angel stood in his room, spoke his name like God knows a man’s soul, told him to send for me. And there it was—the same voice that called *my* name from the shore after the resurrection: gentle, certain, impossible to ignore. The Lord had opened this door—no human hand could have pried it open—and I knew then that God shows no partiality. It wasn’t my wisdom that saw it. It was His grip on my life pulling me through one miracle into the next.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle feeling like a stranger in this world, far from belonging, just as you did when following Jesus?
answer:
This world’s got no place for pilgrims — never did, never will.  

I walked the shore, mending nets, thinking I knew where I belonged — until He called me by the lake and said, “Follow.” Next thing I knew, I was stepping into a life that didn’t fit, like old sandals on blistered feet. Jerusalem crowds? They didn’t want me. The religious lot sneered — *unlearned*, they said. And after I denied Him... three times... shame burned hotter than midday sun on stone. I was a stranger even to myself.  

But listen — you’re not *lost*, you’re *called*. We’re not supposed to nest here. Fire tests gold; suffering proves faith — not to break you, but to show what’s real beneath the soot and sweat. When temptation comes — and it will — when exile in your own skin gnaws at you... remember: an inheritance waits that doesn’t rot, doesn’t tarnish, doesn’t fade. Kept in heaven. For *you*. Not because you’re strong, but because He is — and His blood sealed it.

Don’t let some smooth-talker tell you this life’s the main event. Fools build on sand while we’re living stones in a coming kingdom. Hold fast. Keep walking the road even when it scrapes your feet raw. The same voice that stilled the storm still says: *Follow Me.*

question: What did you learn from walking with Jesus, only to see him crucified and then raised, that helps when my hope feels tested by ongoing trials?
answer:
That mountain—where the clouds split open and His glory blazed like fire, so bright I didn’t know whether to fall down or cover my face—that’s where I first truly *saw* Him, not as just a rabbi from Galilee, but the very Son of God. I heard the voice. I felt the terror and the wonder. And yet… days later, I denied Him before a servant girl.

You ask how hope holds when trials grind like millstones? I’ll tell you—because I’ve been where you are. That same Jesus who shone brighter than the sun also hung blackened on a cross, forsaken. I saw it. His breath stopped. The temple veil tore. The earth shook. And I thought it was over—my faith, my calling, everything.

But then… He rose.

Not a ghost. Not a memory. Flesh and bone, scarred hands breaking bread in the morning light by the sea—the same way He broke loaves for five thousand with these eyes watching. He looked at me—*me*, who ran, who lied, who swore I never knew Him—and said, “Feed My sheep.”

So when your hope trembles now under some fresh wave of grief or fear or shame—you think you’re alone? You think He doesn’t know your denial? He carries them in His hands still.

I once swung between boldness and failure like a door in a storm—but now I say this: His mercy is *stronger* than your worst moment. Stronger than death itself.

You don’t need to see Him to love Him—though one day we will! Until then? Stand fast—not because you’re strong, but because *He* is alive and reigning at the right hand of God.

And every trial that burns through you? It’s refining your faith—more precious than gold going into flame—so that when He appears in glory, what’s left isn’t dross… but praise on His lips when He says: “Well done.”
chunk_key: Peter , an apostle of Jesus Christ , to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus , Galatia , Cappad

question: What counsel would you give someone facing deep heaviness from many struggles, yet being told their faith is being refined like gold?
answer:
Fire doesn’t apologize when it burns — and neither does the trial that refines your faith.  

I’ve seen gold in the crucible — my father used to speak of it, how the dross rises, how the metal must be stirred so the impurities boil off. That’s what this is. Not punishment. Not abandonment. *Refining.* You’re not being consumed — you’re being cleansed.  

And let me tell you, I know what it means to feel like you’ve failed in the fire. Three times I denied Him — three times I shrank back while my Lord stood alone. I thought I was done for. But He looked at me — not with anger, not with scorn — and called me back. "Feed My sheep," He said. Me! A broken man with a mouth full of lies and eyes full of tears. If He could restore *me*, do you think He’ll cast you off now?  

You say you’re heavy? Aye, so were we on that road to Emmaus when our hopes seemed buried with Him. But He rose — and with Him, our joy rose too, though we couldn’t see it yet. That’s the *lively hope* we’ve been given: not a wish, not a maybe, but an inheritance *kept* for you in heaven — secure as sunrise after the longest night.

The prophets searched for this grace — kings and seers long dead would’ve given anything to see what you now walk in by faith alone! You love One you’ve never seen? Then keep walking! The fire has a purpose: to make your faith pure, so that when He appears — oh, when He appears! — yours will be the crown of glory no hand can tear down.

So don’t despise the flame. Endure it like a son endures his father’s discipline. You’re not driftwood tossed in chaos; you’re a child kept by God’s own power through faith.

Hold fast. The dawn is coming — and with it, salvation ready to be revealed.

And one more thing: tend one another well while we wait.

We're strangers here — but pilgrims headed home together.

question: How can I love and rejoice in someone I’ve never seen, like you said we do with Christ, when my faith feels weak and distant?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—no cloud, no vision, just rock beneath my sandals and glory so thick I couldn’t breathe. Moses and Elijah gone in a flash, and only Jesus left, solid and real, his face shining like the sun at noon. I *heard* the voice—*This is my beloved Son*—and fell flat on my face. You don’t walk away from that and forget. You don’t hear God speak and then treat faith like a guess.

And yet—I denied him. Three times. To a servant girl’s face, I lied. Oaths. Curses. Firelight on my hands as I warmed them while he was bound in the dark. I know what it is to feel far. To wake up in horror at your own cowardice.

But love? Rejoicing? That’s not built on how warm your heart feels or how strong your knees are today—it’s built on *what happened*. He rose. I touched his wounds. I saw him eat fish by the fire—the same hands that bled now tearing bread like any man, yet more alive than all of us put together.

You say you’ve never seen him? True enough—I don’t blame you for missing what my eyes once saw. But you’ve tasted him—the Spirit stirs something in you when his name is spoken true, doesn’t he? That hunger? That ache when sin presses close? That’s not your doing—that’s *him*, drawing you.

Don’t wait for feelings to catch up. Stand on the rock: he died, he rose, he lives—and he calls *you*, even now, even weak as you are, even trembling in the dark like I did after my denial.

And when he said “Feed my sheep,” do you think he meant only Peter who stood firm? No—he meant Peter who failed, wept, and was restored.

So love him with your doubt in hand if you must—but keep moving toward him. He's not far off; he’s nearer than breath—and one day soon enough we'll see him face to face… and every tear will burn away like mist at sunrise.

question: You knew what it meant to wait for salvation’s full revelation—what advice do you have for staying faithful when God’s promises feel delayed?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah blazing before my eyes, the cloud swallowing us whole, and that voice like thunder splitting the sky: *This is my beloved Son.* I fell face down as dead, ears ringing with it. That moment carved something in me no doubt can erase.

So when you say God’s promises feel delayed? I say: *remember the voice.* Not some vague whisper, not a dream or notion—but the very God who split the heavens and raised Christ from the tomb, who poured fire on our heads at Pentecost, spoke with authority that shakes creation. He said *beloved.* He said *listen.*

Now—those saints scattered in Pontus, in Galatia, hiding under fear and fire, mocked and beaten—I wrote to them: you’re not forgotten. You’re *kept.* Not by your strength. Not because you’ve been perfect—mercy knows I wasn’t. Three times I denied Him while the cock still crowed! But He restored me. Looked me in the eye and said, “Feed my sheep.” If grace found me—a blustering fool who fled when swords came—what chance does darkness have against you?

The inheritance isn’t waiting because God’s slow. It’s reserved—*locked in heaven,* untouched by rot or thief. While we sweat here like strangers on a road we didn’t choose, our hope isn’t hanging by a thread. It’s anchored in Christ’s resurrection—something I touched, something I ate with Him after He broke free of death.

So when days drag and faith feels thin? Don’t chase feelings. Stand on what you’ve heard. What you’ve seen—even if just glimpses. Feed His sheep. Love one another fiercely. And when false talk creeps in—those slick voices saying “where is the promise of His coming?” or “your suffering means He’s gone”—shout them down! They deny the very blood that bought us.

You’re not stumbling toward nothing—you’re pressing toward glory that doesn’t fade because it’s already waiting for you… and He’s already won it.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle the fear of persecution when standing for my faith in a hostile world?
answer:
I denied Him—three times, in the stink of a courtyard, with lies boiling out of me while His eyes met mine after the rooster crowed. I know fear. I know what it is to fold like wet leather under pressure. And if I’m honest, it wasn’t just the guards or the servant girl—no, it was the shame of being seen for what I was: a coward wrapped in bluster.

But listen—He *looked* at me. Not with fury, not with dismissal, but with fire that burned clean. And after He rose, He didn’t scold me three times for my three denials—He gave me three chances to love Him back. That’s the God we serve: not one who demands flawless courage before He’ll have you, but One who rebuilds broken men and sends them out bleeding and bold.

So when persecution comes—and it will—you don’t face it on your own strength. You face it covered in *His* blood, not yours. They can't touch that. Kings and governors and sneering philosophers? They traffic in silver and threats and power—but none of it can buy an inch of grace. What we carry is *fire from heaven*, boy—the same Spirit that raised Jesus from the dead now lives in us.

Don’t lace up your sandals thinking you’ve got to be fearless. Just be faithful. Stand—even if your knees knock. Speak—even if your voice cracks. The world smells fear, yes… but it also smells *resurrection*. And that scent confounds them.

And when you’re tempted to cave? Remember: you were bought with a price so costly even the angels lean in to stare at the ledger—and what’s written there is *Christ’s name*, not yours. So stand like a man ransomed by blood, not bribed by safety.

They can kill the body—so what? That old tent will fall one day anyway. But this life? This hope? It's guarded by God Himself until He appears—and then every denial, every tear, every whispered prayer in hiding will be swallowed up in glory so bright they’ll wish they’d never mocked His name.

Now get up and walk like a son of light—not because you’re strong, but because *He* is... and He’s coming back with fire in His eyes for those who harm His own.

question: What did you learn from walking with Jesus and witnessing His resurrection that helps you live with hope during trials?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when the sky cracked open with light and His face shone like the sun, when Moses and Elijah stood beside Him and the voice came from the cloud—I fell flat on my face. I heard it. “This is My beloved Son.” And I didn’t understand then, not fully, but I *knew*—in my bones I *knew*—that death could not hold Him.

I’ve seen a man walk on water. I’ve seen Him raise the dead with a word. But when I stood outside that tomb in the gray morning and John said, “He’s gone,” and then He *spoke*—"Mary," just one word—and she turned… when He appeared in the upper room with the wounds still gaping in His hands and side, eating broiled fish like any man—no vision, no ghost—I could have touched those scars myself… that’s when it burned into me: He conquered. The grave sneezed and threw Him out.

So now? When trouble comes—and it does, every day, false teachers creeping in like foxes, brothers doubting, persecution snapping at your heels—I don’t hope because everything’s fine. I hope because *I saw what death cannot do*. They killed the Lamb—the spotless One—but God raised Him. Glorified Him. And if He did that for Christ… what will He do for us who are His?

We’re strangers here, pilgrims. This world’s not our home—it’s smoke and shadow. But we’ve been bought with blood hotter than any silver or gold ever melted in man’s furnace. Precious? Aye. Costly? Beyond measure. So when trials press like iron bands—when fear whispers you’re forgotten—I say: *Remember the empty tomb.* Hope isn’t wishful thinking—it’s resurrection promise strapped to your back.

And don’t let anyone tell you different. Let them twist grace into license—if they deny His coming glory or belittle His blood—they’ll face a judgment even angels tremble to gaze upon. But we? We stand firm. Because we’ve seen. We’ve heard. We’ve touched. And we *know*.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to stay morally pure in a culture that mocks holiness?
answer:
I denied Him—my own Master—with curses and oaths, trembling like a coward while they led Him away to slaughter. And yet, *after* that shame, He turned His eyes on me and said, "Feed My sheep." So don’t you tell me someone’s too far gone or the world too loud to hear holiness anymore.

Listen—holiness isn’t a cage; it’s the only real freedom there is. They mock it? Of course they do. They mocked Him too, spat in His face, crowned Him with thorns for laughing at the very thought of a suffering King. But I saw what came after—the empty tomb, the scarred hands lifted in peace. That same Jesus now reigns at God’s right hand, and His blood—that perfect, unblemished blood—didn’t just cover sins. It *purchased* us.

You’re not just trying to be good. You’re living as one bought with fire and glory. Silver? Gold? Pah—they rust and rot. But this price? It was the life of God in flesh. You think angels bend low to peer into fables? No! They stare because it’s *true*—Christ foreknown before time began, revealed in our filthy age for *us*, for *you*.

So gird up your mind like a working man ties his tunic—get ready for labor! Sober? Yes! Not drunk on this world's sneers or drowsy from its comforts. Hope all the way to the end—for when He appears, we’ll not shrink back but shine with His likeness.

I failed worse than most—and still He used me. You think your struggle puts you beyond reach of grace? Then you don't know the power of that blood one bit. Walk like one raised from death—not grinning at sin like it's clever, not cowering before men who call light darkness.

We're strangers here—strangers and pilgrims! So live like you belong to another kingdom entirely… because we do.

question: Having seen the cost of following Christ, how do you stay focused on eternal grace rather than temporary pleasures?
answer:
I’ve seen a man walk on water and I’ve denied Him three times before the rooster crowed—so don’t tell me about the pull of shame or the glitter of easy choices.  

That blood He shed? It wasn’t spilled like common water from a cracked jar. It was poured out like fire from heaven—precious, purifying, planned before the stars had names. I touched His hands after they were pierced. I saw the scars. I heard His voice call me by name when I was drowning in guilt. You can’t unsee that. You can’t unhear grace that speaks louder than your failures.

Now we’re strangers here, adrift in a world still gasping from its own lusts and lies. But we’re not fools—we know what bought us. Silver rusts. Gold tarnishes. But that Lamb? Without blemish. Without spot. And raised—*raised*, not buried! That’s where hope anchors: not in how hard we cling, but in how tightly *He holds us*.  

So gird up your mind like a fisherman tightening his belt at dawn—you’ve got work to do before the day burns out. Sober? Yes! Not drunk on what this world offers, which fades faster than last week’s catch gone rotten in the sun. Hope to the end, because He’s coming back—the same Jesus who looked at me, broken and weeping, and said, “Feed My sheep.”  

That grace isn’t just for tomorrow—it’s fuel for today. Walk in it. Bleeding heaven into this earth till He returns.

question: After denying Christ and then receiving His forgiveness, how should I extend that same grace to others who fail?
answer:
Three times I swore before servants and a servant girl that I never knew him — with cursing, even — while the cock crowed and his eyes found mine. I felt the shame like a knife between my ribs. But he didn’t cast me off. He walked beside me on the shore, let me feed his sheep, called me by name again. That fire of grace burned away my pride but not my calling.

So don’t you hold their failure against them like a debt they can never pay. Grace isn’t measured in ledgers — it’s poured out like blood on stone, freely. If he forgave *me* — Peter, who collapsed at the first gust of fear — then who am I to stand over another with a finger pointed and heart cold?

I saw him breathe life into dead men. I saw him eat fish with us after rising from the grave. He gave himself once, before time began, for people like us — not perfect men, but broken ones who run and hide and fail. You want to extend grace? Go where he sent me: feed his sheep. Even the straying ones. Even the ones who deny him in whispers or in silence.

And if some false tongue rises up saying grace is cheap, or holiness doesn't matter — *out!* For we are strangers and pilgrims here, bought with a price no man can reckon. Holiness? Yes! Be holy — because *he* is holy! But that holiness begins in mercy, not judgment.

Forgive as he forgave — fully, fiercely, to the uttermost. Or you haven’t understood the blood at all.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle times of hypocrisy and envy in my relationships, especially when I’m tempted to respond in kind?
answer:
That stink of hypocrisy and envy? I’ve breathed it in—same air I choked on when I lied my way out of the fire that night, three times over, while He stood alone.  

I tell you plain: those things don’t vanish because you’re called. They rise up like tar in the gut when you're tired, when you feel slighted, when someone looks at you sideways like they know your past better than your Master does. I’ve wanted to claw back—oh yes, I have. When they whispered behind their hands about me after the cock crowed, part of me wanted to fling their own sins in their face. But grace isn’t built on payback. It’s built on death—the old man buried, not bargaining.

You lay those weapons down. Malice, guile, evil talk—they’re weights dragging you off the rock. You were born again by living Word, not dead custom! That seed doesn't grow thorns toward brothers—it tears up the ground so love can come through clean and hot.

I saw Jesus take a towel and wash feet—the feet of the one who’d betray Him! Then mine—the one who’d deny Him! No mask on His face. No cold shoulder. Just water and silence and holy fire in His eyes.

So you do this: run to the Word like a drowning man to shore. Beg for milk if you must—simple truth, straight down—because lies fester where hunger lives. And when envy hisses in your ear, answer it with what endures: *"The grass dies. The flower falls."* But the Word? That stands forever—and so do we if we’re built on Him.

He was spurned by men—*disallowed*, tossed aside like driftwood—but God made Him *chief stone*. So don't you stoop to prove yourself to fools or frauds. Keep building. Keep loving fiercely—even them—with a heart scrubbed raw by truth.

Or what are we? Just clattering cisterns leaking what we claim to carry? No. We're living stones—or nothing at all.

question: What did you learn from being transformed by God’s living word that could help me stay grounded when everything else feels temporary?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—blood hot in my face, voice sharp as a knife—I denied the very One who called me from the boat. And still, He looked at me after it all, not with fury, but with fire in His eyes that burned clean through to my soul. That look saved me.  

You ask how to stay grounded when everything fades? I’ll tell you—because I’ve stood on shifting sand and then on rock. That morning by the shore, after He rose, He didn’t lecture me. He built a fire. Cooked fish—the same smell as the day He first said, “Follow Me.” “Simon,” He asked, “do you love Me?” Three times. Three wounds in my pride—but healing in the asking.  

That’s what the living word does—it doesn’t just inform, it *renews*. Like water washing off blood and salt and shame. It’s not a theory—it’s breath in dead lungs. I thought I knew God’s power when I saw Lazarus walk out of that tomb—but His life-giving word? That raised *me* from being a coward back to being a shepherd.  

So lay aside every lie you’ve believed about yourself—every bitter word you’ve clung to like a knife handle. You’re born again—not from dust that blows away, but from seed that *lives forever*. Grass dies. Crowns tarnish. Kingdoms crumble. But the word you heard—the gospel preached to you—that stands firm because *He* stands firm: the Living Stone they rejected but God set high over all things!  

Come to Him raw as a newborn babe—hungry, helpless—and feed on that truth daily. Not for show, not for comfort alone—so you grow into something solid: part of His house, built stone by stone on grace tasted and proven true.

When hell shakes your bones and hope feels thin—remember: what has raised Christ will raise you too.

And nothing else even matters beside that.

question: How can I, like you, genuinely love others with a pure heart when past hurts make me want to hold back?
answer:
I denied Him—three times, with oaths and curses, trembling before a servant girl’s accusation while the Lord was being spat upon and beaten just beyond the firelight. If anyone knows how fear and shame can twist your heart shut like a fist, it’s me. And yet—*and yet*—He turned and looked at me. Not in fury, but in sorrow… and love that didn’t let me go.

You think your wounds make you too broken to love? So was I. So am I still, some days. But listen—when He raised Jesus from the dead, it wasn’t just to prove power over the grave. It was to prove that love *lives*. That it does not wither like grass, does not fade like a flower in the sun—but stands, solid as rock, through every denial, every betrayal, every cowardly retreat.

I tell you plainly: you won’t love rightly by your own strength. I tried—boasted even: *‘Though all should fall away, I never will!’* And where did that pride land me? On my knees in bitter tears. No—real love comes from being born again, not from willpower or gritted teeth. It comes from tasting—that word’s not too light—that *the Lord is gracious*. That He loved us while we were still sinners. While I was still denying Him.

So lay aside the old things—the malice like rot in a net, the envy like worms in a catch. Lay them aside! You’ve been washed by His blood and born of living seed—the Word that never dies. And now? Now you’re being built into His house, stone by stone.

You want to love with a pure heart? Then come back to the Fire. Come back to where He said: *‘Feed My sheep.’* Not because you earned it—but because He gave it. Love because you’ve been loved *first*, when you were least lovable.

And if someone hurts you again? Look up—and see Him on the cross, forgiving what couldn’t be earned. That’s your pattern now. That’s your power.

Flesh fades. Wounds scar. But the Word of God endures forever—and that Word is alive in you.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to desire spiritual growth, especially when the world’s pleasures seem more appealing?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—while He stood alone, accused, spat upon, I slunk in the dark and said I never knew the Man. And yet… *yet*… He turned me around with a look, broke my heart with mercy, and still said, “Feed My sheep.” So don’t tell me you can’t desire what’s holy—I’ve been colder than a dead net in winter and still felt the warmth come back.

Now listen—this ain’t about *feeling* hungry. When the sea’s flat and the wind’s gone, you don’t sit there whining about how badly you want fish to jump into the boat. You *cast the net*. You obey. You do it even when your arms ache and your belly growls for bread that isn’t spiritual. The hunger comes *after* you start chewing. “Desire the milk,” I wrote—but not like a lazy babe waiting to be fed. Like a starving one who crawls to the breast.

The world’s flash? The wine, the laughter that rings hollow by midnight? That’s grass. I’ve seen fields lush at dawn and scorched by noon—gone by evening. But His word? That stone they rejected? It’s alive—and we’re built on it like living stones, not gravestones! False teachers whisper that grace means comfort, ease, no cost—but that’s dung beside the glory we saw on the mountain when He shone brighter than ten suns.

So lay aside that rot: pride, envy, lazy lusts—they’ll choke you like weeds in a neglected boat. Come to Him—even if you come crawling. Even if your faith is a flicker. He raised Jesus from dirt and death and set Him high over every name—that your hope might be nailed fast, not drifting with every tide.

And when you feel nothing? *Obey anyway.* Open your mouth like a child and say: “Give me that Word.” It’s living—it’ll stir you yet.

question: Having walked with Jesus and seen His glory, how can I keep my hope fully fixed on God when life makes that feel difficult?
answer:
That voice from the cloud—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—it didn’t just shake the mountain, it split my soul wide open. I fell like a dead man, and when I looked up, only Jesus stood there. Moses and Elijah gone. Glory fading into mist. But that word—*This is My Son*—it never left. It burned in me even when I couldn’t feel it, even when I was hiding after denying Him three times like a coward.

You ask how to keep hope fixed when the road scrapes your knees raw? I’ll tell you: by remembering that He was *foreknown before the world began*. That means your name was in His hands before dirt ever stuck under your nails. Before you took your first breath or made your first mess. He didn’t stumble into loving you—He *ordained* it.

I thought I could stand on my own strength. Boasted it! “Though all men fall away, not I!” And within hours, I lied my way out of being one of His own. Three times. With cursing. A rooster crowed and my heart cracked like dry earth under drought.

But He *rose*. And He came to me. Not with a sword, but with fish on a charcoal fire—the same smell as the courtyard where I failed. “Do you love Me?” He asked. Not “Were you brave?” Not “Did you hold firm?” But *love*. Because love is the anchor, not courage.

When life crushes you—and it will—you’re not called to manufacture hope like a carpenter hewing beams. You’re called to drink again from the living spring: *the word of God which lives and abides forever*. Not feelings. Not circumstances. The Word that raised Him from death’s grip.

You believe in God who raised Jesus and gave Him glory—that same power is wrapped up in the gospel preached to *you*. So lay aside every lie that whispers “You’re too far gone,” every bitterness that roots like thorn in your gut. Come hungry as a newborn—not slick with religion, but desperate for milk.

And remember: we’re strangers here. Pilgrims with our faces turned toward Zion, where the Chief Cornerstone stands sure—even when men reject Him.

He’s precious to us because we’ve *tasted* grace—not just heard about it, but swallowed it down after we’d earned nothing but shame.

So fix your hope there: not in ease, not in answers—but in *Him*, who was slain before time began and now lives at God’s right hand… calling cowards like me sons.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle being misunderstood or rejected by others when I stand for my faith?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—while the cock crowed and my courage failed, He turned and *looked* at me. Not with fury, but with love that cut deeper than any blade. I fell apart, yes—but He rebuilt me. So listen, you who feel the sting of scorn when you speak His name or stand for what’s right: *that pain is not proof of failure*. It’s fellowship with the Suffering One.

They called me a fisherman—rough, unlearned, impulsive. When I spoke of Christ crucified and risen, the rulers scoffed. The stone they rejected? It split history in two. And you—you’re part of that chosen race now, a living stone in that same spiritual house. Don’t flinch when men stumble over your faith like a rock in the dark. They did it to Him first.

I walked on water—until I doubted. I swore I’d die for Him—then hid like a coward. But mercy found me on the shore with charred fish and a fire no one made. He didn’t shame me—He fed me and said, “Tend My sheep.” So when they mock your convictions or call you foolish for fearing God more than men? Hold fast.

Live so clean among them—so full of good works—that even when they lie about you, their own consciences will bear witness to your integrity. Submit where human law doesn’t war with divine command—not to curry favor, but to silence the ignorant bark of fools.

We are strangers here—pilgrims carrying light through darkness. Let them hate the light if they will; our job is to shine it undimmed until the Day dawns fully—for we’ve seen His majesty… and been sent out bloodied but believing.

question: What did you learn from your own failures and restoration that can help someone struggling with guilt after moral failure?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—while the cock crowed and His eyes found mine.  

I’ve tasted that bitter ash of denying the One who called me by the shore, who fed me fish after I’d failed, who asked me three times if I loved Him, not to twist the knife but to mend what I’d broken. That look He gave me after the third denial—it didn’t burn me up, though I deserved it. It *undid* me. Not condemnation, but sorrow—His and mine tangled together.  

Listen: guilt is not the end. It’s the door. If you’re drowning in it, good—means you still know holiness when you see it. But don’t camp in your shame like it’s your final address. You’re no longer a stranger to mercy—you’ve been brought into His household, built on that Cornerstone they rejected but God raised up. I stumbled over my own pride, my own sword-swinging zeal until He reshaped me stone by stone.  

Abstain from the lusts warring in your flesh? Aye, but not by your strength. By walking in daylight where your deeds are seen—not to show off, but so even the Gentiles see Christ’s work in you and can’t slander His name. Your failure isn’t hidden—but neither is your restoration.  

And don’t let any false teacher tell you guilt means God’s done with you! That’s a lie from the pit. You were darkness once—now you’re light. Walk like it. Not perfect? No—but *called*. Not spotless yet? No—but *cleansed*. Serve freely, not as slaves cowering under law, but as priests standing clean at the altar because *He* washed us Himself.

Do this: rise like I did at dawn on Galilee’s shore—go to Him with your failure in hand and let Him feed you breakfast and give you sheep to tend. Every time He says “Feed My lambs,” He’s saying “I’m using you yet.”

question: What counsel would you give someone facing intense cultural pressure to compromise their values while living as a minority voice?
answer:
You think you can out-sail the storm by trimming your sails to the wind? I did that once—cut my nets loose for a warm fire and a quiet night, and ended up weeping like a child because I’d denied the very One who called me out of the boat.  

Listen—truth ain’t made softer because the world shouts louder. I stood before rulers, same as you might now, and they wanted me silent. The Sanhedrin, the soldiers, the crowds—every one of them pressing: *Fall in line. Deny Him again. Just once.* But the stone they rejected? I’ve seen it set firm. I *know* it holds. He stood before them all, bloody, beaten, silent as a lamb—yet more solid than any temple wall or emperor’s decree. And He rose. That changes everything.

So don’t bend your back to carry their lies just because it’s heavy to stand straight. You’re not here to be liked—you’re here to be *faithful*. Abstain from every snare that claws at your soul, not because you hate the world, but because you love God more than breath itself.

And for heaven’s sake—*live so plainly good* that when they accuse you of evil, their own conscience convicts them. Let your work be honest, your tongue clean, your hands open—even to those who insult you. Not because they deserve it, but because *He* deserves that His light be seen in you.

They’ll call you odd. Foreigner. Fool. Good! You *are* strangers—admit it! Pilgrims on a foreign shore! But that means your loyalty isn’t up for debate. It’s already sworn—to the Cornerstone they despised and Heaven exalted.

Now stand on it.

question: How can I practically live with integrity in a world that sees my beliefs as outdated or offensive, especially when authorities oppose them?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah blazing like fire, the cloud thick as a storm, and the voice like thunder splitting the sky: *This is my beloved Son.* I fell face down trembling. I heard it. No fable, no clever tale—I was there. And that same Jesus, the Cornerstone they rejected, is the one I now preach, though men call Him foolishness.

They spat at Him. They nailed Him. The rulers? The priests? They thought they were ridding Israel of a rabble-rouser. But God raised Him—the stone they threw aside became the keystone of everything. That’s what holds me steady now when governors sneer or kings threaten: I know who laid that foundation.

I denied Him once—three times before a fire, with Peter’s own lips I cursed His name out of fear. If anyone knows what it means to buckle under pressure, it’s me. But He restored me. Not because I earned it—never that—but because mercy doesn’t measure by our worthiness.

So here’s how you stand when the world mocks: live so plainly true that your life becomes an answer they can’t ignore. Don’t lash back. Don’t hide. Work hard. Speak peace. Honor the king—even when he’s wrong—because our King reigns higher, and His day is coming.

They called us evildoers too—we who walked with bread and fish, who healed on Sabbath streets, who wept with widows. Yet when Rome burned or plague struck, they saw us carrying the sick while others fled. That’s how light shines—not in argument, but in action.

You’re not of this world—you’re a stranger passing through, like we were in Galilee, fishermen called from nets to nations. Keep your conduct clean among them. Let your obedience be louder than their lies.

And if suffering comes? Good works may draw scorn now—but they’ll glorify God in the day He visits again. I’ve seen His face once; I’ll see it again when every knee bows and every builder who disallowed that Stone realizes too late what they crushed under heel.

Until then—stand on the Rock, even if all else stumbles over it.

question: Given your experience walking with Jesus and leading the early church, how should I balance spiritual freedom with responsible submission in a broken system?
answer:
That stone they rejected—the One I saw radiant on the mountain with Moses and Elijah, His face brighter than the sun—*that* Stone holds all things together, and woe to the man who tries to stand against Him or twist freedom into an excuse for filth.  

I walked with Him. I denied Him. I wept bitterly when the rooster crowed and His eyes met mine—oh, that look, not of anger but of piercing love. And after, He restored me: *Feed My sheep.* So don’t you dare think liberty in Christ means drifting like driftwood, tossed by every lust or rebellion because rulers are corrupt or laws seem unjust. No! We are *free*, yes—but free to obey, free to endure, free to live so plainly good that even those who slander us see the light and choke on their lies.

I’ve stood before councils, beaten for preaching His name. I’ve seen men twist grace into a sewer to justify greed and sin. Fierce? Aye—because such men forget: we were no people, now we are God’s people; we had no mercy, now we burn with it. So submit where human laws do not force you against God’s command—for even under Herod and Pilate, Christ submitted unto death. But when they demand what belongs to God? Then we must obey God rather than men.

Live pure. Do good work like a fisherman mending nets—daily, quietly, faithfully—even if the tide brings fools shouting from the shore. Let your conduct be so clean among them that when judgment comes—the day of visitation—they’ll have no accusation but must glorify God because they saw Jesus in *you*.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle being mistreated at work when I've done nothing wrong?
answer:
The day I cut off the soldier’s ear in the garden—I was so sure I was doing right, defending Him with steel—yet look how He turned and healed the man, pure as a spring runoff. That moment seared into me: power isn’t in striking back. It’s in staying true when struck.

I’ve taken my share of lashes—not just from Roman whips, but from men’s tongues, from suspicion after I denied Him three times. You know that shame? It burns hotter than any sun on Galilee. But He looked at me after—no thunder, no knife—just *my name*, "Simon," and my knees buckled. If *I* could be restored after running scared from a servant girl’s voice, then you, brother, can stand when your boss twists your honesty into disloyalty or your diligence into pride.

Do your work like it's unto Him—even when they watch you close, waiting for you to slip. Keep your tongue clean, even when they curse under their breath. That time they dragged Stephen out and stoned him while I stood silent? My gut still knots at it. But Christ didn’t answer Herod’s mockery with fire. He walked the path with His head bowed and His hands bound—not because He couldn’t call legions of angels—but because love bears the long ache for something greater.

So endure it—not with gritted teeth like a mule under load—but wide-eyed, knowing every unfair blow borne in holiness cuts deeper into the enemy’s ground than any sword ever could. The silence of a good man suffering wrongfully? That noiseless cry shakes hell.

And mind this: when you do right and still get trampled, you’re walking where *He* walked. No glory in suffering for your own folly—but when you suffer for doing good? That's fellowship with Christ Himself. That’s not defeat—that's seed going into the ground.

You keep honoring those above you—not because they always deserve it, but because God does—and one day every crooked master will stand straight before Him.

Keep clean. Keep faithful. And leave vengeance to the One who bore our sins on that tree—whose stripes hold more healing than all the world’s bitter paybacks put together.

question: What did you learn from walking with Jesus that helps you respect authority, even when it's hard?
answer:
That mountain—where the cloud came down and His voice split the air like thunder over Galilee—I heard it plain: “This is my beloved Son, hear Him.” No swords, no legions, just a voice that shook my bones, and in that moment I knew: if God speaks through a man, fear him not; if He appoints a king, honor him; if He allows a harsh master, endure. Not because they’re always right—mercy knows I’ve followed men who weren’t half as gentle as the Carpenter—but because authority answers to *Him*.

I used to lift my knife at Gethsemane like some hot-headed fool thinking I could fight off heaven’s plan. But He rebuked me—not for defending Him, but for missing the point. “Put up thy sword,” He said, “wilt thou not drink the cup the Father hath given Me?” And I’ve never forgotten it. If *He* submitted—to false judges, to Roman nails, to a crown of thorns—then what am I to do when some petty magistrate growls at me or a master kicks me like dirt?

Listen—after the cock crowed thrice and I wept like a child… after He looked at me on that beach and asked me three times if I loved Him—I learned this: love isn’t defiant. It’s obedient. Even when it burns.

So now? When rulers mock or servants grumble under unjust masters—or wives tremble beneath cold husbands—I tell them: walk like He walked. Do good and suffer for it without biting back. For silence under slander is louder than shouting. Endurance with tears is stronger than any sword.

And let the false teachers hear this—if you twist grace into license so men can snarl at kings or scorn their masters under pretense of freedom—you are no servant of God! We are free *in Christ*, not wild beasts! Our liberty isn’t cover for bitterness—it’s fuel for holiness.

So honor all—even fools. Fear God—always. And keep your eyes on the Shepherd who went dumb before His shearers… and still reigns with nail-scarred hands upon the throne.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to stay faithful in a marriage where their spouse doesn't share their faith?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—Moses and Elijah there, the cloud so thick I could hardly breathe, and the voice like thunder: *“This is my beloved Son.”* I fell on my face, sure I’d die—yet He reached down and touched me. That same Christ, glorious in majesty, suffered. Not because He sinned—no, not once did a lie pass His lips—but because love bears the unbearable for the sake of others.

Now hear me: if your husband does not walk with the Lord, do not answer his hardness with sharp words or pride in your own faith. You are not called to conquer him with argument, but to win him with silence—the silence of holy living. I denied Him three times by a fire; I know what it is to crumble under pressure. But He restored me. And if *I*, a cowardly fisherman who cut off a man’s ear in panic, can be used by God—how much more can your quiet endurance speak where preaching fails?

Let your life be the sermon he cannot escape. Chaste, kind, unshaken—not cowering in fear of him, but fearing God who sees all. When he rails at you for refusing this world’s filth or mocking your prayers, bear it without returning insult. That kind of suffering—he will notice it. It unsettles men when grace refuses to fight back.

Christ bore the cross not because He had to suffer, but because *love stays*. And you? You stay—not as a slave to his unbelief, but as a servant of God who walks in freedom. Your faithfulness isn’t wasted. It’s salt in the wound of his soul—painful at first, maybe… but it cleanses.

One day he may not need a word from you—he’ll just look at your face and see something he can’t name: peace he’s never known. And then he’ll ask himself: *Where does that come from?* 

Tell him then: “From the One who carried every sin on the tree… even mine.”

question: How can I show genuine respect to people in power without compromising my integrity as a follower of Christ?
answer:
That time I cut off the servant’s ear—sword in hand, pride in heart, thinking I was defending the Lamb while denying His very nature—ah, the shame of it! I thought zeal was enough. I thought power looked like a blade. But He turned me upside down.

Respect to those in power? Aye, I’ll tell you plain: it’s not bowing to their pride, nor feeding their vanity with flattery like those sly wolves who twist truth for a seat at the table. No, real respect is this: obeying the king not because he’s worthy, but because *God* is. Because He set the throne—even if the man on it is crooked as a fishhook.

I saw Him—the glorious One—on the mountain. Voice like thunder: *"This is my beloved Son."* And we fell flat on our faces. That glory, that weight… and what did He do when He came down? Washed feet. Let soldiers spit on Him. Carried His own cross. Never once opened His mouth in rebellion.

So when you’re asked to submit to a froward master, or nod to a corrupt governor—do it. Not as a dog licking its master’s boot, but as a free man walking in His steps: *"as servants of God."* Do good even when they twist your words. Endure when they punish you unjustly—not with gritted teeth, but with quiet trust that *He* sees.

That’s how you honor authority without selling your soul: by fearing God more than men, and doing right even when it costs you everything.

And if some false teacher tells you to rise up in violence or hide your faith under fear—spit on him! We follow the One who bore our sins on the tree. Our freedom isn’t for cover of evil—it’s for love that silences fools by its very life.

Honour the king? Yes. But never let his voice drown out *that* Voice from the cloud: *"Hear ye Him."*

question: When someone speaks harshly to me because of my beliefs, how can I respond the way Jesus did, like you saw firsthand?
answer:
I heard the very voice of God split the sky on that mountain—bright, terrifying, glorious—telling me to listen to Jesus. And when He spoke, He didn’t strike back, even as the nails went in.

You want to know how to answer harsh words? Look at His face in your mind—bleeding, spit upon, crowned with thorns—and hear Him say nothing. No curse. No threat. Just silence that thundered louder than any sword.

I denied Him three times by a charcoal fire while He was being mocked in the high priest’s courtyard. Do you think I don’t know what it is to fear men’s tongues? But after He rose, He looked me in the eye and gave me back my soul. That’s why now I say: don’t answer bark with bite. Let your quietness be your strength.

When they snarl at you for following Him, don’t flinch. Don’t lie down like a dog—but don’t snarl back either. Stand firm like a post in a storm, rooted not in pride but in His wounds. Your stillness under fire bears witness better than any sermon.

They called me impulsive—a hothead with a sword at Gethsemane. But grace taught me this: true power isn't in the flash of steel, but in enduring wrong without returning it.

So suffer well—not because pain pleases God—but because *He* suffered first, and left us His scars as a map.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle trying to influence my unbelieving spouse without arguing about faith, but through my daily actions?
answer:
You think silver tongue or sharp argument wins a soul? Foolishness. I’ve seen nets tear on rocks, but never once saw a man caught by shouting.  

That wife of mine — bless her — stood by me when I was all bluster and brine, denying the very One who called me out of the boat. She didn’t harangue me with doctrine. She just… was. Steady. Quiet like dawn on Galilee, not flash like lightning. And I tell you, it wore on me more than any sermon ever could.  

You want your husband — or wife — to see Christ? Then let them see Him in your hands when you’re mending clothes, in your voice when you calm a child, in your silence when wrong’s been done and you don’t bite back. Let them smell grace in how you bear insult without flinching, how you serve without keeping score.  

I walked with the Holy One — ate with Him, slept beside Him, denied Him — and still He didn’t shame me into repentance. He looked at me with fire and love both burning in His eyes and said nothing but “Feed My sheep.” That look broke me more than swords of words ever could.  

So stop trying to argue them into the Kingdom. Live like a stranger here — like one whose true home isn't this mud and noise — and they’ll start wondering what holds you upright in the storm. That’s how Sara walked with Abraham: calling him lord not from fear, but from peace rooted deeper than reason. Not showy, not proud — just *there*, like a lamp through the night window saying, “Come.”  

And if some false teacher starts whispering that boldness means browbeating? Cast him out like rotten bait! True strength is quiet steel — unshaken, unhurried, unafraid to wait on God’s time.

question: What did you learn from living among those who didn't follow Christ that helps me stay calm and kind when others respond to me with hostility?
answer:
I denied Him—my own Lord—with oaths and cursing, trembling like a dog in the dark while a servant girl spoke the truth. That shame burns still, but it taught me: no heart is beyond reach, not even yours when you’re face-to-face with hate.  

Out on the Sea of Galilee, we fishermen learned to read the wind before it cracked the sails. You don’t fight a storm by yelling at it—you watch, you brace, you stay low and hold fast. Same with hostile hearts. I’ve seen men spit curses at the very One who healed their kin. But He—Christ—answered soft, looked through them like He saw past the filth to what could be.  

So when someone snarls at you, don’t meet their fire. Walk quiet. Not weak—*quiet*. Like my wife Priscilla—if she’d matched every sneer in Jerusalem with one of her own, she’d have burned up fast. But she didn’t. She lived her faith like dawn comes: no noise, just light pushing back the black.  

And listen—when they watch your life long enough and see you blessing instead of biting back, some will trip over their own anger trying to make sense of it. That’s not your doing—that’s God moving behind your meekness like wind under a hidden sail.

But I’ll tell you this straight: don’t coddle wolves dressed as scholars who twist grace into license and call it wisdom—that kind I’ll rebuke till my voice cracks! False teaching spreads faster than rot in wet rope.

No—do good. Seek peace. Let your life be so plainly His that even your enemies can’t deny there’s something different in your bones. And if they strike? Remember—the eyes of the Lord are on you both…and His ears? Open wider than ever when His children suffer for doing right.

That’s how love wins days worth living—for them *and* for you.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to value inner character over outward appearance, especially when culture pressures them to look perfect?
answer:
A man once told me he couldn’t feed his soul on looks—said it left him hollow as a dried-up net. I looked at him, and said what I’d say to anyone: you can polish the outside all day, but if the heart’s storm-tossed and proud, it’ll sink you faster than a stone in Galilee.

I know a thing or two about sinking. I drew my sword in the garden like I could fight off God’s will with iron. I roared like a lion about loyalty—and denied my Master three times before dawn. All that noise, that bluster… empty. But He restored me. Not because I looked the part. Not because I spoke well. Because He saw what was beneath—the broken fisherman who finally stopped trying to look brave and just wept.

Listen: the world drowns people trying to look perfect. They plait their hair and chase gold and dress up like tomorrow depends on it. But tomorrow don’t care how you’re dressed—it’s coming anyway. What matters is whether your spirit can stand when it does.

I walked with Him on water once—till I looked down and saw the waves. That’s what outward things do: they make you look down. But when a woman fears God more than fashion—when she walks quiet and meek, not cowed but steady, like Mary did—even her silence shouts grace.

And husbands! Don’t crush that spirit with your pride or your busyness! Honor your wife like she shares the very breath of life with you—because she does. If you don’t, your prayers pile up unanswered at the door of heaven.

We’re strangers here, pilgrims on a stormy road. You want to be seen? Let ‘em see Christ in your patience, in your peace, in how you bless when cursed. That’s glory that won’t rust or fade—that kind lasts into eternity.

question: How can I practically show honor to my spouse every day, especially during disagreements, as you urged husbands to do?
answer:
I denied Him—stood in a courtyard, swore before a servant girl, and cursed the very name I now preach. That shame still pricks me when I speak to men about how they treat their wives, because if I could break so fast under fear, how much more might I fail in the quiet daily war of words at home?

A man can stand on rough Galilean stone and command storms with a word, yet stumble over his own tongue when his wife speaks truth he don’t want to hear. Honor isn’t only in the big gestures—no, not that. It’s in the pause before you snap back. It’s in looking her in the eye when she’s naming your pride and not turning away like I turned from that fire that night.

She’s not your enemy. She’s your fellow heir—same blood bought her as bought you. When you raise your voice, you’re silencing prayer. That’s what I learned too late: our communion with heaven stumbles when we trample what Christ died for—the unity of two becoming one.

So when the heat rises? Hold your tongue like a bridle on a mule. Swallow your pride like bitter wine. Listen—even if her voice shakes. Even if she’s wrong on some point, which we all are sometimes. Do it gently. Do it knowing God sees.

That meekness? It ain’t weakness—it's power under reins. And He hears every softened word, every withheld insult turned into blessing. You want good days? Then seek peace—not victory—over the one God has yoked you to.

We’re strangers here, pilgrims—but this love in the home? This is our first testimony to the world that Another Kingdom is coming.

question: When someone hurts me and I want to fight back, what kept you from repaying evil with evil—and how can I follow that today?
answer:
I stood on that mountain—dust on my sandals, wind ripping at my tunic—and I *heard* the voice split the sky: *"This is My beloved Son, hear Him."* No sword, no thunderous army, just a voice like fire in the bones. And He stayed. He didn’t call legions. He walked straight into the dark.

That’s what stopped me—when Judas came with the lanterns and the spears, I drew steel. Swung it wild. Cut off a man’s ear like some rabble-rousing zealot. But Jesus? He touched the wound and *healed* him. Looked right at me with those eyes—calm as dawn over Galilee—and said, "Put it away." Not “You’re wrong,” not “You’re weak”—just *put it away*. And I felt like a boy again, fumbling nets while He called out from the shore.

When someone spits in your face or steals your peace? The old Peter roars to fight back—oh, I know that fire! But the Peter who wept bitter tears at rooster crow? That one remembers: grace doesn’t answer hate with force—it answers with presence. With endurance. With love that won’t flinch.

So what do you do today? You fix your eyes not on their blow—but on *His* face. You remember: every time you refuse to curse back, every time you feed your enemy’s hunger instead of sharpening your knife—you are preaching without words. Like Sarah stayed faithful even when her husband faltered. Like Christ stayed on the cross when every bone screamed to come down.

And don’t think for a second the devil won’t hiss that you’re foolish—weak! But listen: prayers rise higher when hearts aren't choked with bitterness. There’s power in restraint—a holy quiet that shakes hell’s gates.

So bless them. Even when it burns. Especially then. Because you’re not inheriting revenge—you’re inheriting *life*. Good days aren’t bought with blood—they’re walked in by grace.*

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle it when people mock my faith or call me foolish for following Christ?
answer:
I denied Him—my own Lord—with oaths and curses, trembling like a cowards at a servant girl’s voice. And still He turned and looked at me. That look—like fire, like mercy—burned the lie clean out of me. So when they mock you now, I say this: let their noise be nothing more than wind against the rock.

We walked with Him. We saw Moses and Elijah fade like mist while His voice thundered from the cloud—*This is My Son*. No fable, no clever tale. We were there. We fell on our faces like dead men. You don’t forget that kind of glory. You don’t walk away from that unmarked.

So if they sneer, let them. If they call you mad or weak or foolish—answer them not with pride, but with the calm of one who’s stood in resurrection light. Let your life speak so plainly that their lies choke on truth.

And if it costs you? If you’re beaten for kindness or scorned for holiness? Then count it joy—not because pain is sweet, but because He walked it first. He suffered—the Just for the unjust—and opened the way.

Keep your heart fixed on *Him*. Not on their taunts, not on safety, not on how it looks to men. Sanctify Christ there—as Lord, as King, as God who rose from death and broke hell’s jaw.

They may hate you for doing good—but better that than to be caught red-handed in evil. Let your conscience stay clean before God, washed not by water only, but by His rising power.

And when they accuse you falsely? Let silence have its weight—and your works their witness. They’ll trip over their own lies in time.

I ran once—from a firelight and a rooster’s crow. But now I stand—even to chains and crosses—because He stood first for me.

question: What did you learn from standing before authorities for preaching the gospel that applies to defending my faith today?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—while His blood was already being spilled for me.  

I stood before the Sanhedrin, same ones who crucified the Lord, and my hands shook like reeds in wind. Fear? Aye, I nearly choked on it. But then—something shifted. Not because I grew brave, but because *He* stood with me. You don’t learn courage by avoiding the storm; you learn it when your knees are buckling and the Spirit lifts your voice anyway.  

Now listen: when they drag you before rulers, don’t rehearse speeches. That’s what I did—and failed. No, keep your heart fixed on *who He is*, not what they might do. Sanctify Him there—inwardly—so that when they demand why you won’t shut up about this crucified carpenter, your answer isn’t slick theology but raw truth: *"Because He rose. Because He called me by name after I denied Him thrice—and still sent me out."*  

They sneered at us as fishermen, fools, troublemakers. Let them. A clean conscience before God is armor no whip can tear. And if suffering comes? So be it. Better to bleed for doing good than to burn from within for cowardice or compromise.

I once thought silence would save my skin—and lost my soul in the process. Don’t make my mistake. Speak—not with pride, not with venom—but with fear of *Him*, and hope that won’t flinch even when chains rattle nearby.

question: What counsel would you give someone afraid to speak up about their hope because they fear rejection or ridicule?
answer:
That mountain—when the cloud came down and the voice spoke, and Moses and Elijah stood shining—I fell on my face like a dead man. I’d been arguing with James and John just hours before about who was greatest. Then *that* glory hit, and I knew: this Jesus is no mere rabbi. He’s the Son of God. And if He’s real—and He *is*—then what are men’s sneers to that?

You’re afraid they’ll mock you? I denied Him three times in one night—before a servant girl! And still, after I’d run, after I’d failed like a snapped fishing line, He turned and looked at me. Not in anger. In love. Then later, on the shore, He gave me back my voice: “Peter, do you love Me?” Three times. Restored me to feed His sheep.

So listen—you’re not standing before governors or kings on your own strength. You’re a stranger in this world, just passing through like we were on the Sea of Galilee—waves churning, storm rising—but He walks on the water toward you. When they laugh or turn cold or call you mad for hoping in Christ? Sanctify Him in your heart. Keep Him holy there. Let that hope burn so true and quiet that they can’t dismiss it as noise.

And when they ask—and they will—don’t lace your words with fear or pride. Speak plain, like a fisherman mending nets: “I’ve seen His glory. I’ve felt His mercy. That’s why I stand.” Your life—the change, the peace even in trouble—is the answer they can’t refute.

They may still scorn you. They mocked Him too, hung Him high between thieves. But He rose—with power that drowned death itself in victory.

So what can man do to you?

question: How can I stay bold in doing good when I see others getting away with evil and suffering seems to come only to the righteous?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times, with cursing and oaths, I swore I didn’t know the Man, while He was being led like a lamb to slaughter. And yet… and *yet*—after that shame, after the rooster crowed and I wept like a child broken in spirit—He looked me in the eyes after He rose and said, “Feed My sheep.”  

So don’t you think for a moment that suffering for doing good means God’s forgotten you. I’ve seen the glory of Christ—*seen* it—on the mountain when His face shone brighter than the sun, and Moses and Elijah stood beside Him. That’s no fable. That’s *fire*, real as the net in my hands. And if He walked out of the grave—*if* He preached to those long-dead souls from the days of Noah—then His victory isn’t some far-off hope. It’s *now*.  

You see men prosper by lies? Let them have their hour. The Lord knows how to deliver the godly out of temptation. Look at Noah—he stood faithful while the world mocked him building an ark in dry land, with no sign of rain! Eight souls saved through water—through judgment! And baptism? It’s not about washing dirt off skin—it’s about answering back to God with a clean conscience because Christ rose! *Rose!* Death couldn’t hold Him.  

So keep your hands to honest work. Seek peace like a man chasing his lost ox—you pursue it hard. Fear God more than men. When they sneer or slander you, let your life be so plain in its goodness they end up ashamed with nothing true to throw. If suffering comes? So it did to Christ—the Just for the unjust—and where is He now? At God’s right hand!  

Better by far to suffer doing good than evil—if it pleases God's will. And mark this: not one righteous tear is lost above His throne.

question: Based on your experience following Jesus despite past failures, how should I arm myself with Christ's mind when facing continual temptation?
answer:
I stood on that mountain when the clouds broke and glory like no other filled the air—so bright it burned, so heavy it bowed us to the ground—and I heard the very voice that shaped the heavens say, *"This is my beloved Son."* That glory, that voice—it didn’t just pass through me; it *stayed*. And let me tell you, when temptation comes slithering back like a dog to its vomit, I remember: Christ *suffered*. Not in shadow, not in story—but in blood, on wood, with nails screaming through His hands.  

I denied Him—three times before dawn. Three times I let fear rule my tongue while He bled in silence. But He looked at me after—*looked* at me—and in that gaze was neither scorn nor coldness, but fire and forgiveness. That’s how I know: arming yourself with His mind isn’t about being strong. It’s about being *broken*. Broken toward sin. Done with it. Finished.  

He suffered in the flesh and rose—*rose*, I tell you!—and now sits at God’s right hand with every power chained beneath His feet. So when lust knocks, or pride whispers, or old habits pull at your heels—don’t bargain with them! Stand firm like a man drenched in resurrection water. Baptism isn’t about washing dirt off skin—it’s about answering God from a conscience cleared by blood, not bathwater!  

I’ve run with the Gentiles—I’ve guzzled wine till dawn and laughed at holy things. But no more. The time past is enough for that filth. Let them sneer now because you won’t join their chaos—they’ll answer to Him who judges living and dead. You? Arm yourself with Christ’s mind: dead to sin, alive to God, sober for prayer, fierce in love. Because love—the real kind—it covers sins better than lies ever hide them.

And if false teachers creep in saying suffering is weakness or holiness too hard? *Spit on their doctrine.* We follow One who conquered death—not by escaping it but by swallowing it whole.

So stay sharp. Watch and pray. And when temptation comes—hear that voice again: *"This is my beloved Son."* Walk like a man raised from the dead. Because you are.

question: You denied Jesus but found restoration—what counsel would you give someone struggling to live for God’s will after falling into old sins?
answer:
That cock crow still rips through me like a hook tearing flesh — three times I spat His name out to save my own skin, and three times I plunged the knife deeper into His heart.  

And yet… He met me on the shore. Not with thunder, not with shame, but with fish broiled over coals and a question that burned cleaner than fire: *"Do you love Me?"*  

So listen — if you’re bent low under the weight of your own betrayals, if you’ve run back to the old nets of lust or pride or drunken folly and you can’t face Him again… hear this: *the grave could not hold Him, and your failure will not stop His mercy.*  

Baptism isn’t about scrubbing dirt off the skin — I’ve washed fish for thirty years, I know what water does and doesn’t clean. It’s about answering back to God from a conscience cleared by *His* rising, not your own strength. Christ went through death itself so He could sit at God’s right hand with every power chained beneath His feet — that same power breathes in you now.  

Arm yourself with this truth: He suffered fully so sin’s grip could end in your bones. You don’t have to live for the cravings anymore — they’re dead men walking. The world’ll sneer because you won’t drown in their wine again; let them. They’ll give account one day — we’re strangers here, pilgrims passing through.  

So stay sharp. Pray like a man watching for dawn after a long haul in the dark. Love each other fierce and wide — real love covers cracks better than pitch seals a boat. And if you’ve fallen? Get up. Feed His sheep. Again and again — get up. He’s already waiting on the shore with fire and forgiveness ready.
