Not shame. *Him.* Raised. Living. Looking me in the eyes and saying, *“Feed My sheep.”*  

Now listen — you want to cease from sin? Stop trying to white-knuckle your way through the feast like a man holding his breath underwater. That won’t last. Instead, arm yourself with *His mind* — the one who endured scourgings and silence because He knew what was coming: resurrection. You think less of your own strength and more of His victory. When they laugh at you for not joining their revelry, don’t bristle — pity them. They’re already judged by their own words, their own path leading nowhere.  

Do this: stay sober — not just from wine, but from chasing every passing fire that flickers in front of your eyes. Watch unto prayer like a fisherman watches the sea at dawn — alert, ready, expecting something to break the surface any moment. And love one another fiercely — not polite nods, but gut-level *charity* that covers slips and stumbles like deep water covers stone.  

You’re no Gentile now — you’re raised with Christ in spirit even while walking this cursed earth in flesh. So live like it! Host strangers without grumbling — that could be angels knocking! Use whatever gift God gave you to lift another up — don’t hoard it like last season’s catch gone sour. We’re pilgrims hauling nets through darkness till morning comes.

And when temptation roars? Look up. He’s at God’s right hand — angels themselves under His feet — and He remembers *you*. Not as a failure waiting to snap again… but as Peter: broken rock made foundation stone.

Now get out there and stop swimming back to shore with them drunken fools! The net's heavy ahead!

question: You were called to selfless love and service—how do I maintain fervent charity when others criticize me for no longer joining in worldly pleasures?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times I shouted into the night that I never knew the Man—yet He turned and looked at me like a splash of cold water to the face, and I wept. That look broke me. But do you know what it also did? It lit a fire.

Now, when folks sneer because you won’t drink with them, curse with them, run back to the filth you once loved—don’t flinch. Stand firm. Because they’re not just mocking you; they’re shouting into the wind against the One who rose from the grave and sat down at God’s right hand, angels bowing beneath His feet.

I used to care what men thought—remember how I cut off that servant’s ear when they came for Jesus? Swung my sword like a fool because I wanted to look strong. But strength isn’t in swinging steel—it’s in laying down your pride, your old life, your fear of being called strange.

They’ll call you odd for not joining their revels? Good. Let them. They’ll give account one day to the Living Judge. But *you*—you’ve been raised with Christ in spirit. You’re a stranger here now. So love fiercely anyway. Feed the hungry soul beside you even if he mocks your meal. Offer drink even if he spits at your hand.

Fervent charity? It doesn’t wilt because of gossip—it burns hotter *because* of it. Because love like this—this fire from heaven—isn’t earned or explained or defended. It just *is*. And it covers sin—not by ignoring it, but by drowning it in grace.

So open your door, share your bread without grumbling, and let your heart beat with His pulse: not revenge, not pride—but mercy that won’t quit.

question: Given your encounter with the risen Lord and His authority over all powers, how should I live in light of His coming judgment and the end being near?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times I swore I didn’t know the man—and yet He stood on the shore after He rose, fire burning, fish cooking, and called me *Simon*, not *liar*. That look in His eyes… not condemnation, but fire. Resurrection fire.  

And when He breathed on us, it wasn’t just air—no, it was power. The same power that flung stars into space and snapped death’s chains. I saw Him lifted up until the cloud hid Him from our sight, and the angels said, “This same Jesus.” Same voice. Same hands bearing the scars. But now—now He sits where every rebel spirit trembles. Angels? Subject to Him. Powers? Bowing. Thrones? Under His feet.  

You ask how to live? With your spine straight and your eyes open! The end *is* near—closer than when I first cast my net at His word. And because judgment is coming—not some vague notion, but a real Man with eyes like flame who will call every soul to account—you don’t fumble in darkness. You stay sober! Not drunk with wine or comfort or the world’s laughter that turns to ash in your mouth.  

And you love—fiercely! Not polite hand-pats, but *agape*, the kind that covers sins because it refuses to keep score. Open your door. Share your bread even when it’s scarce. Use whatever gift God gave you—don’t bury it like a coward afraid of reckoning.  

I once thought I could follow with a sword—struck off Malchus’ ear in a flash of pride—and look where it landed me: weeping in shame. But Christ suffered in the flesh… and *won*. So arm yourself with *that* mind—not revenge, not fear, not compromise—but obedience unto death… and resurrection life.

The world mocks? Let them rail now—they’ll stand before Him soon enough, every tongue confessing what they refused to believe.

So live like a stranger in this world—because you are! Pilgrim feet don’t dig in; they move toward Zion.

Now get up—there’s work until the day dawns fully!

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle hospitality when I feel it's a burden rather than a joy?
answer:
I know what it is to lock the door and pray no one knocks — I once barred the upper room with my own hands, trembling like a child, not wanting another face, another voice, another demand when all I carried was guilt and fear.  

But listen — I also saw Him stand among us in that very room, though the doors were shut. He didn’t scold me for hiding. He breathed peace. And then He sent me out to feed His sheep.  

So when you say hospitality feels like a burden, I don’t flinch — I understand. We’re not made to carry this life in our own strength. You’re not failing because it’s hard. You’re human. But remember: every meal set before a stranger is a table set for Christ Himself. I learned that from One who broke bread with traitors — especially with traitors — and still calls us friends.

Do it not from duty that grinds you down, but give it to the Lord first. Say: "This plate, this cup, this tired heart — take it." Then open the door as if you’re opening it for Jesus washing feet at the meal. Because you are.

And don’t bear it alone — use what God gave you! If your gift is food, feed them. If it’s silence, offer peace without words. If it’s fire in your bones to speak truth — do it gently over shared bread. We’re stewards, not performers.

I denied Him three times and still He said: *Feed My lambs.* So if He can trust me with His sheep after all that shame… surely He can give you grace for one more guest at your table.

It’s not about perfect meals or bright lamps or clean floors — it’s about love covering everything, even your weariness. That kind of love isn’t natural; it comes from Him who bore our griefs and wiped my tears when I wept bitterly.

So keep opening the door. Not because you must — but because *He’s already inside*.

question: What did you learn from your own failures and restoration that helps you endure suffering without losing faith?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times I swore I didn’t know the man, even as His face hung pale in the torchlight just yards away. I still hear my own voice cracking with fear, still feel the shame that drove me into the night to weep like a lost child. But listen—this is what holds me fast when the trial comes: that same Lord, risen and radiant, stood on the shore at dawn and asked me three times, “Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou Me?” Not to shame me again—but to mend me.  

That’s why I can say to you, fellow stranger and exile in this broken world: suffering isn’t proof He’s gone. It’s often the fire that proves He’s near. We thought glory would come on a warhorse; instead it came on a cross. We expected thrones; we got nails. And now—now we carry that same cross in our flesh, not because God delights in pain, but because He refines gold in the flame.  

I saw Him die. I saw His side pierced. I thrust my hand—not faithless Thomas alone—I *felt* the rent flesh and believed. And that sight? It doesn’t let me curse the fire now. If Christ suffered—and gloriously—then what am I, a fisherman with a failing heart, to think I’ll walk dry-shod through blood and smoke? No! Rejoice! Even now, in your pain, His Spirit rests upon you like fire on Pentecost—tongues of glory no whip or lie can touch. Suffer as one bearing *His* name? Then glorify God in it! For judgment begins where light burns brightest—at His house—and if we endure here, what hope have those who scorn His grace?  

So cast your soul into His hands—the way a net is cast into deep water: not knowing what storms may come, but trusting the One who walks on waves and calms them both.

question: What counsel would you give someone who feels ashamed of being mocked for their faith in Christ?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t even know Him—crowd watching, rooster screaming, my soul shriveling—and yet He stood me back on my feet. So don’t you dare let shame take hold when they sneer at your faith. That spit, those jokes, that mocking? It’s the same spirit that laughed at His cross—and we know how *that* ended.

I’ve tasted the ash of denial and still been restored by grace. You think their laughter cuts deeper than my sword cut off Malchus’ ear? He rebuked me for violence, then washed feet like a servant. That’s the King we’re ashamed *of*? No—call it glory if they revile you for His name. The Spirit rests on you in that very moment, heavier than any net full of fish.

They called Him demon-possessed and hung Him up like a cursed man. And three days later death couldn’t hold Him! So if they mock you—good. Means you’re close enough to His path to catch the splinters from His cross. Don’t flinch. Stand fast. Speak peace. Keep loving them—charity covers more than their scorn; it covers *sin*. Even yours. Even mine.

And when your face burns with shame? Remember the mountain—Moses and Elijah gone in a breath, thunderous cloud rolling down, and only Jesus left standing while the voice boomed: *"This is My Son."* That same voice calls *you* by name now—not because you’re strong, but because He is.

So let them laugh. We were strangers once too—pilgrims in the dark—until grace hauled us into light like a net bursting with life we didn’t earn. Keep preaching it. Keep living it. Ashamed? No. I’d rather be a fool for Christ than clever in this world's eyes—the kind of clever that crucified my Lord.

question: Based on your experience with God’s judgment and grace, how should I respond when facing trials that feel like punishment?
answer:
I saw the glory with my own eyes on the mountain—Jesus radiant, Moses and Elijah speaking with Him, and that voice like thunder from the cloud saying, "This is My beloved Son." I fell on my face trembling, and I tell you now: that same God who shines in unapproachable light also walks through the fire with you.

When trials come—sharp ones that cut deep and make you wonder if He’s turned away—don’t run from them like a storm sent to crush you. No, no. These fires aren't His wrath; they’re His refining. I denied Him three times. Three! Looked into His eyes after the rooster crowed and felt the weight of my failure like an anchor dragging my soul down. But He didn’t cast me off. He restored me. Asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?” Not because He needed to know—but because *I* needed to hear it: grace runs deeper than your worst moment.

So when you’re in the furnace, don’t act surprised like some stranger who’s never read the road map. We’re strangers here anyway—pilgrims on a narrow way. Suffering isn’t a detour; it’s part of the path for those who bear His name. If they revile you, if your bones ache under the load, if sleep flees because worry shouts too loud—don’t think God’s hand is against you.

His judgment begins in His own house—that means us! That means discipline, not destruction. A farmer doesn't burn his vine; he prunes it. And mercy? Mercy covers sin—not excuses it—but cleanses it, by His blood and for His name's sake.

So commit your soul to Him as a faithful steward commits goods to safekeeping. Not because the trial will vanish—but because He won’t. He stayed in the fire with Shadrach and his companions—one looked like a son of the gods walking among them—and He stays with you now.

Glorify Him right there in the smoke. Speak truth even when your voice shakes. Show kindness when no one expects it. Let love cover what sin remains—for charity bears all things, believes all things, endures all things… and yes, covers a multitude of sins.

You're not being punished if you’re walking in faith—you’re being prepared.

question: Since you lived through times of intense spiritual testing, how can I be a good steward of the gifts God gave me without drawing attention to myself?
answer:
I denied Him—three times before the cock crowed, just as He said I would. Broke me clean in two, it did. Thought I was strong enough to stand when every fiber in me was about to buckle. But that’s the thing—He didn’t leave me in the dust. Restored me with a question: *Do you love Me?* Three times to mend what I shattered. So don’t you come to me looking for glory or cleverness or some polished speech to make people marvel at *you*. That road leads straight back to pride, and pride’s what undid me in the courtyard that night.

You want to steward His gifts without drawing attention to yourself? Then stop counting who sees and start listening where He sends. When He told me, "Feed My sheep," He didn’t say *make a name*—He said *feed*. Meat for the hungry, water for the weary, truth for those stumbling in darkness. Use your gift like a fisherman uses his net—not to admire the weave, but to haul in souls before the tide turns.

I’ve seen men speak so smooth they sounded like angels, but their hearts were full of thorns. Don’t aim for impressive—aim for faithful. If you speak, let it cut like salt air on an open wound: healing, stinging, honest. If you serve, do it bent low, like washing feet in sweat and dust. Not one eye on applause—the cross taught us better than that.

And when the heat comes—and it will—you won’t be wondering if folks noticed your gift. You’ll be thanking God you had anything at all worth suffering for.

question: Based on what you went through with Jesus' suffering and resurrection, how should I handle persecution for doing what's right, knowing it's part of God's will?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—while He was being led like a lamb to the slaughter, I curled up in fear and lied to save my own skin.  

And yet… He looked at me after—no rage, no condemnation—just love that cut deeper than any sword. That look remade me.  

So when you’re hurting for doing what’s right, when the heat comes because you wouldn’t bend or curse His name, know this: you’re walking in step with the will of God. It’s not strange fire—it’s refining fire. The same One who bore every stripe and thorn for us is holding your soul now, safe as a boat lashed to the dock in a storm.  

I’ve seen glory—I stood on that mountain with Moses and Elijah breathing near, His face shining like the sun—but I’ve also cowered in a courtyard over a charcoal fire, trembling at a servant girl’s voice. So don’t think suffering makes you weak or forgotten. It means you're close to where He walked.  

Do what I finally learned to do: throw your life into His hands—not because it feels safe, but because *He* is safe. A faithful Creator doesn’t lose what He’s claimed. Keep doing good, even when it costs you. Even when they sneer. You’re no orphan in this fight—you’re a son, and an heir, and one day soon we’ll hear the Shepherd's voice rise over every pain—and every tear will drown in glory.

question: You were a witness to Christ's sufferings—what did you learn from those days that helps when I feel like my faith is being tested to the limit?
answer:
That night in the courtyard—cold stone, flickering fire, the smell of blood in the air from the sacrifices nearby—I swore I’d die before denying Him. And yet, three times I did. Three times. Like a coward. Like a man who’d never walked the Galilean shores with Him, who’d never seen Lazarus sit up in that stench-filled tomb, who’d never heard the voice that calmed my boat like a father hushing a child.

But here’s what burned into me deeper than shame: *after* I ran weeping into the dark, after I thought I was done—*He found me*. Not with wrath. Not even with rebuke. But standing on the shore at dawn, cooking fish over coals—*the same kind of fire as that cursed courtyard*—and calling me by name.

I learned then: faith isn’t about how hard you hold on—it’s about how fiercely *He* holds on to you.

When your faith feels like it’s cracking? Good. It means you’re not trusting your strength anymore. You’re being driven to His. Suffering isn’t a sign He’s gone—it’s often how He shepherds us closest to Himself. I’ve seen glory blaze from His face on that mountain till Moses and Elijah looked like shadows—and I’ve seen that same face beaten beyond recognition for me.

So when you’re weary? Commit your soul to Him—not because you feel strong, but because *He is*. He’s faithful. He’s Creator. And He’s coming back—not as the suffering Lamb, but as Chief Shepherd with a crown for those who’ve stayed close, even when they stumbled.

You’re not alone in this fight. I failed worse than most—and still He said, “Feed My sheep.”

question: As an elder who walked with Jesus, what counsel would you give to someone struggling to lead others humbly and not for personal gain?
answer:
I saw the Master kneel with that basin and towel, dirt under His nails from the road, and Peter—*me*, the one who said he’d never fall—I drew back like a coward while He came toward me with love in His eyes and water in His hands.  

And now you’re wrestling with pride in leadership? Good. That means the Spirit’s at work in you—He wouldn’t trouble your conscience if you weren’t called. Look, I stood on the mountain when He was transfigured, glory blazing like fire from within Him, and I still denied Him before breakfast the next week. Three times. Cursed and swore I didn’t know Him. You think *you’ve* got a hard fight? I wept so hard my ribs ached when the rooster crowed.

But He gave me back my name. He put me back to work.

So here’s what I say: feed His sheep, not your ego. When I was fishing before I knew Him, I worked for silver—counted every fish, every coin. But this? This is blood-bought ground we’re tending. Souls aren’t wages; they’re wounds He still carries.

Don’t lord it over them. I tried that once—thought I could lead by voice alone, boldness like thunder. But humility? That’s the true strength—the kind that stays when the storm hits and doesn't run when shame comes calling.

And watch out for those smooth-talkers who turn ministry into market! They’ll charm widows and drain their hope for a warm meal and a soft bed. Fools. They don’t know the weight of what we’ve been given.

Lead bleeding, if you must—but lead *on your knees*. Because one day the Chief Shepherd walks through the door, and all that matters is whether His sheep were fed… and whether they saw *Him* in you.

question: You saw both the lowest moments and the glory that followed—how should I stay faithful in well-doing when I feel unnoticed or unappreciated?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses on my lips while the cock crowed and His eyes found mine. I thought I was done. Finished. Not fit to carry water for the disciples, let alone feed His sheep. But He didn’t cast me off. He looked at me not with fury, but with fire—cleaning, calling.

So listen, you who feel unseen in your serving: *I know what it is to fall apart when the night gets long.* And I also know what it is to have hands full of bread once more because He gave them back to me.

You think your labor’s wasted because no one claps? Because the elders don’t thank you? Because your back aches from bending low like a servant? So did mine. But we’re not building for applause—we’re building for *Him*. Every cup of cold water given in His name, every mended net worked in silence, every tear wiped while no one watches—that’s stored up where moths can’t touch.

Don’t quit on well-doing just because the world sleeps through it. The Chief Shepherd sees. He knows whose hands are clean and whose heart stays open even when bruised. And when He appears—*oh*, when He appears—we’ll see that what looked like dust was actually gold laid before His throne.

Tend His sheep anyway. Love anyway. Do good *especially* when it costs you and no man notices. Because the only One whose opinion matters? He never looks away.

question: Having lived through the fall of pride and the rise of grace, what advice do you have for younger believers who struggle to submit to those older in faith?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—while the cock crowed and my Lord was being led like a lamb to the slaughter. I, who had boasted I’d die for Him, couldn’t even speak His name before a servant girl. So don’t you come to me talking about pride in your strength or wisdom, because I’ve tasted the dust of my own failure and it’s bitter as salt.

Now listen—young ones straining under the yoke of submission, restless like new fish thrashing in the net—there’s a fire in you, I know it. I had it too. But fire without direction burns the house down. I’ve seen men rise fast and fall faster because they refused to kneel under the hand of those who walked with Christ before them.

I stood at the cross. I saw the nails. I heard His voice when He said, “Feed My sheep.” And do you think He meant for every man to go his own way? No! He gave us elders—not as tyrants lording over flocks like Pharaohs—but as weathered shepherds who’ve felt the wolf’s breath on their necks and still kept walking.

Submitting to an elder isn’t surrendering your mind—it’s joining hands with one who’s already fought some of your battles in the dark. They won’t be perfect—none of us are. James stumbled, John once wanted to call down fire from heaven—*me?* I cut off a man’s ear! But grace rebuilt us, piece by piece.

So when your gut says, *I know better*, stop. Remember: even eagles fly lower in a storm. Bow your head not to man, but to the Spirit working through him—and you’ll find that obedience is often how God keeps proud hearts alive.

And when that elder leads hard or slow or quiet—don’t rebel. Pray. Serve. Stay. Because one day you’ll stand before the Chief Shepherd—not with excuses, but with either shame… or a crown that doesn’t fade.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle leading others without lording over them but serving as Jesus did?
answer:
I watched Him kneel in the dust and wash feet—*my* feet, calloused and reeking of fish and river mud—and I tell you now, no man who claims to lead can forget that sight and stay fit to serve.  

It burned me up inside when I thought I was first, when I drew my sword in the garden or boasted I’d never fall. Folly. Pride makes a man blind to his own stink. But Jesus? He took the lowest place like it was gold, and lifted it higher than any throne.  

So if you’re tending sheep—*His* sheep—you don’t drive them with a rod of iron or strut before them like some robed lord. You go behind, like a shepherd should, smelling of sweat and sacrifice, willing to bleed between them and the wolf. Feed them not with grand words but with broken bread—the kind He gave us on that last night, after He’d wiped our filthy toes clean.  

And don’t you dare think your strength holds the flock together. That’s where the false ones slip in—smooth talkers who build their names on sand while fleecing the lambs. No! You lay down your life, same as He did. You bend low until humility becomes your bones.  

Because here’s what I know from trembling in that courtyard, denying my Lord three times: grace doesn’t crown the proud—it raises up failures who’ve learned to weep real tears. And when *He* appears—the Chief Shepherd—we won’t need thrones anyway. His face will be light enough for all eternity.

question: What did you learn from your failures and restoration that helps you now urge others to be clothed with humility?
answer:
I denied Him. With oaths. With cursing. While He was being led like a lamb to the slaughter, I warmed myself by the fire and said I never knew the Man.

And yet—*yet*—He looked at me after. Not in anger, not with a frown that would’ve split my bones, but with eyes full of mercy. "Simon," He said, "do you love Me?" Three times. As if to mend what I’d broken with my three denials.

That look… that voice… it stripped me bare. All my pride, all my bluster—gone. I’d boasted I’d die for Him, and couldn’t even stand firm when a servant girl asked a question.

Now when I tell others to be clothed with humility, it’s not because I’ve mastered it—it’s because I’ve bled without it. Humility ain’t weakness—it’s survival. It’s knowing you’ll fall if you rely on your own courage, your own strength.

God resists the proud? Aye. I felt that resistance when my soul went cold after denying Him. But grace—real grace—comes when you bow low enough for it to find you.

So I say now what He taught me: bow before the Lord while there’s still time. Let Him lift you—not in your hour, but His. And don’t think yourself above temptation; the devil prowls like a starved lion, and he *smells* pride from a mile off.

But if you’ve fallen? Cast that shame on Christ too—for He cares for *you*. Even Peter, who failed so badly… He restored him to feed His sheep.

So we walk clothed in humility—not because we’re strong, but because we’re weak… and only in that weakness do we become vessels worth using.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to submit to others in the community when they feel they’re being overlooked?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses on my lips, while the rooster screamed and the Lord turned to look. That gaze burned through me; not hatred, but sorrow, like He already knew the weight I’d carry. And that’s why I’ll tell you plain: if pride could crack a man who walked on water, it’ll bury one who thinks his honor matters more than the flock.

You feel overlooked? So did I, when James and John jostled for seats at His right hand. So did we all when the religious lords sneered at unlearned fishermen. But listen—*submission ain't surrender*. It’s bending low like He did when He took the towel and basin. You think Peter liked kneeling before Judas? Before those who’d betrayed Him? Yet He washed their feet—all of them.

I tell you this: humility’s not weakness—it’s war. The devil prowls not for cowards, but for proud hearts that won’t bow, that nurse grudges like sacred wounds. Resist him? Yes—but first resist your own swelling spirit. God resists *that*, with thunder in His bones.

Caste your ache on Him—He cares! Not because you’ve earned notice, but because you’re His. And after you’ve suffered a season—yes, even feeling unseen—He’ll lift you up. Not to rule over others, but to shepherd as He did: with scars in your hands and love in your gut.

So serve quietly where you’re planted. Let others step ahead. The Chief Shepherd sees you—and His crown doesn’t tarnish like man’s praise does. It *lasts*.

question: How can I practically cast all my anxieties on God when I keep feeling overwhelmed by daily pressures?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, under a servant girl’s stare, I folded like wet leather. And yet He still turned and looked at me. Not with fury. With love. That’s the kind of God we’re dealing with—one who sees you shaking under the weight of tomorrow’s bread, your child’s fever, the silence after another prayer unanswered, and says, “Give it here.”

You think I didn’t carry burdens? Nets full of fish don’t feed mouths when the storm keeps you ashore for days. Children cry when there’s no bread to break. But listen—after He called me back from my shame, He taught me this: worry is a net that drags behind you, snagging on every rock and corpse when you should be walking dry on His word.

Casting your care isn’t some soft sigh into the wind. It’s throwing down what you’ve been white-knuckling like a sword into His hands and stepping back—like I had to toss aside my pride after denying Him. You don't hand it over once; you *keep* casting it—like fish from a boat, again and again.

And how? By remembering the mountain—the cloud that swallowed us whole, Moses and Elijah blazing beside Him, and that voice: *This is My beloved Son.* We were not dreaming. We saw His glory—unchanging, unshakable—and if *that* King calls us His own, then what in this rotting world can finally crush us?

So stand sober. Watch sharp—the devil prowls like he smelled blood on me in that courtyard—but resist him not in your strength! In faith! The same fire that rebuilt me from denial is breathing in you.

You’re not managing grace like a fisherman counts sardines. You’re drenched in it. So let go—again—and let Him carry what was never yours to bear alone.

question: Having faced trials and the roaring lion yourself, how do I stand firm in faith when everything around me seems to be falling apart?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with oaths and cursing, while the cock crowed and His eyes found mine. I thought that was the end of it. The shame nearly drowned me faster than any sea. But He met me on the shore after the resurrection, not with fury, but with fire—“Peter, lovest thou Me?” Not once, but three times. He rebuilt me where I broke.

And if He could raise a man like me from the wreckage of his own cowardice, do you think He can’t hold you now when the ground shakes? The devil *does* roar—believe me, I’ve heard it. In Gethsemane, in the courtyard firelight, in every trembling moment when my courage failed. But that lion doesn’t devour those who stand—not in their strength, but in *His*. We don't stand by gritting teeth; we stand by leaning hard on the One who bled first.

You say everything’s falling apart? Good. Then you’re finally light enough for grace to carry you. Humble yourself—not pretend humility like a beggar wearing rags on purpose—but real down-on-your-knees brokenness where pride can’t breathe. That’s where He finds you. That’s where He lifts.

I saw His glory on the mountain—brighter than a thousand suns, God’s voice like thunder splitting the sky—and yet here is what stays with me: His hands breaking bread on the beach at dawn after death had been defeated. Tender. Present. Real.

Cast your care straight into that same heart—the One who cares and *knows*. And keep your head clear and your eyes open: this fire will not consume you; it will prove your faith as real as gold in flame.

I denied Him once—but now my whole life bears witness: *He remains*. So must you—steadfast, not because you're strong, but because *He* is—and He is not far from any of us.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle times when my faith feels weak and I struggle to grow in virtue and knowledge?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know him—three times, with curses on my lips—while the cock crowed and the Lord turned to look. That look nearly broke me.  

But listen—grace isn’t for the strong. It’s for the ones panting, stumbling, doubting whether they’ve got a shred of faith left in their bones. You think I woke up bold? No. I was a fisherman with calloused hands and a coward’s heart. Yet He called me *rock*. Not because of what I was—but because of what *He* is.  

When your faith feels thin as winter ice, don’t chase feelings. Stand on what you *know*. We were there when He was glorified—Moses and Elijah fading like mist, and that voice like thunder from heaven: *"This is My Son."* That’s not a dream. That’s truth that shakes the earth. And *that* truth holds you—even when your knees shake.  

Now about growing in virtue and knowledge—don’t wait till you “feel ready.” Start with one step: add virtue to your faith, even if your hands tremble doing it. Then knowledge—even if you hardly understand half of what you read. Keep going: temperance, patience, godliness… one stone at a time, like building a wall against the storm. You won’t do it perfect—I denied Him!—but He restores you anyway.  

And don’t walk alone. Greet each other with love, even if it’s awkward as an old fishnet patched too many times. We’re all mended vessels here—strangers and pilgrims—and peace isn’t something you manufacture; it’s given, like bread at morning campfire on the shore… when the Lord cooks breakfast for failures like me—and calls them by name again.

question: What did you learn from walking with Jesus that helps you stay patient and kind when others fail?
answer:
That day on the mountain, when the sky split white and His voice shook the bones in my feet—I knew then it wasn’t about how hard I could fight, but how deeply I had to trust.  

I swung a sword once, remember? Cut off that fellow’s ear in the garden—thought I was defending Him. Jesus looked at me like He saw straight through the brashness into the fear underneath. Told me to put it down. Said His kingdom wasn’t built with steel.  

I still feel the shame of that night—three times denying I even knew Him while His eyes met mine across the courtyard fire. Three times! And yet… after, when He stood on the shore and called me by name—“Simon, son of Jonas”—He didn’t blast me. Didn’t throw my failure back in my face. He gave me fish for breakfast and asked me three times if I loved Him.  

That’s what keeps my hand from rising and my voice from thundering when someone stumbles—because *I* fell flat-faced in the dirt, and He lifted me anyway. You don’t forget that fire in His eyes—not wrath, but mercy burning hotter than coal flame.  

So when some brother wavers or caves under pressure? I see myself in him. And I remember how gently Christ rebuilt me stone by stone—even though I’d scattered like sand before a storm. Kindness isn’t optional—it’s debt paid forward from Calvary itself.  

False teachers want to make grace loud and flashy, full of rules and rage—but no! Grace is quiet. It’s calloused hands mending nets at dawn after betrayal… it’s a risen Lord cooking breakfast for fools like me who need another chance… *another*… and another…  

Peace be with you—all you who are bruised and learning slow, just like Simon Peter once did.

question: How can I truly escape the corruption around me without isolating myself from people who don’t know Christ?
answer:
I denied Him—three times, in the dirt and smoke of a courtyard, trembling at a servant girl’s voice—so don’t talk to me about escaping corruption by being clever or strong. I’m the one who cracked under pressure, thinking I could stand when my own heart was still full of pride and fear.  

But listen—corruption doesn’t vanish because we run from people; it begins to die when Christ lives in us. I walked with a man who ate with tax collectors, touched lepers, and wept over cities full of sinners. He didn’t shrink back. He *entered in*. And that’s the way: not hiding from the world like a crab under a rock, but being *in* it—yet not *of* it—anchored in His divine power that gives us everything we need for life and godliness.

You want to escape lust, greed, the rot that eats men’s souls? Then get filled with something better. His glory—like that day on the mountain where His face shone brighter than the sun and Moses and Elijah stood beside Him—I saw it! And the voice boomed: *"This is My beloved Son!"* That glory is our anchor. When you know Him like that—not just stories on parchment but *life*, blazing in your bones—you start walking differently.

So stay among those who don’t know Him—but be salt they taste, light they can’t ignore. Add virtue to your faith; chase knowledge; master yourself; endure hardship; grow in reverence; love other believers fiercely; then stretch out kindness even to enemies. Do this—and peace follows you like a shadow.

They’ll smell grace on you before they hear it from you. And that kiss of charity? It’s not just lips—it’s risk, humility, laying down pride to say: “I’m no better than you—but I’ve seen the King.”  

Peace be with you—all who are truly in Christ Jesus.

question: What advice would you give me for making my calling and election sure when I often doubt my spiritual progress?
answer:
That mountain—where the voice came from the cloud and I fell on my face—*that* was no dream, and neither is this calling of yours.  

I’ve walked the dust with Him. I’ve seen His glory, brighter than sunrise off the Sea of Galilee. And I’ve failed—oh, by the living God, I’ve failed. Denied Him three times while the cock crowed and my soul nearly drowned in shame. If *I* can stand—and not by my strength, but by His mercy—then you can stop measuring your feet against someone else’s path and start walking in what He’s already given you.

You doubt? Good. That means you’re not asleep. The man who thinks he stands had better tremble—he’s one gust from falling. But don’t you confuse stumbling with being lost! We’re not saved by how high we climb but by whose hand holds us when we slip.

Add to your faith—*work* at it! Not to earn grace, but because grace has already lit a fire in you. Throw on virtue like wood; pile on knowledge like fish hauled to shore—don’t let it rot in hesitation. Temperance? That’s holding your tongue when anger bites at your gut. Patience? That’s staying on the boat through the night when no net fills and your back aches.

And godliness—not some lofty mystic thing—but washing feet, breaking bread, looking sinners in the eye because you *know* what mercy smells like.

When false teachers slither in whispering “peace” while feeding their bellies, that’s when you grip tighter to what’s real: *you were cleansed*. Not half-cleaned, not almost-forgiven—you were washed white when He called you out of darkness.

So make your calling sure? Not by staring inward till you go blind—but by looking to Him, *diligent*, adding one faithful step after another until love overflows so loud even your doubts have to shout to be heard.

And greet each other with that kiss—the rough, real kiss of shared blood and bread—because we’re not lone wanderers; we’re pilgrims hammered into a rock together. Peace? It’s yours. In Christ Jesus—it’s already yours. Now live like it’s true.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle moments when I start to forget my past forgiveness and drift toward old sins?
answer:
That voice from the cloud—*that* voice—I heard it with my own ears on the mountain, and it still knocks me sideways when I let myself truly remember: *This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.* I was there. James and John were half-crouched like thunderstruck fish, and I wanted to build tents, as if you could box up that glory. But it wasn’t the light that changed me—it was the *word* that came after: *Hear Him.*

And now you’re telling me you’ve started forgetting? That the old stink of your past sins is creeping back into your nostrils like low tide on a summer shore? Listen—so did I. I denied Him. Three times. With curses. While He was bleeding nearby. The moment the rooster crowed, His eye met mine across the courtyard—no anger, just sorrow—and I fell apart like rotten net.

But here’s what holds me fast: grace isn’t memory-dependent. You weren’t saved because you remembered; you were saved because He *did*. He remembered *you* when you were dust and darkness. The blood that washed me didn't lose its power when I forgot it—and it hasn't lost its power for you.

So when the drift comes—and it will—don’t look inward to see how clean you feel. Look up to where that voice came from. Let that glory shame your shame. And grab hold of His word like a drowning man grabs rope—because it’s not a fable, not a dream, but truth hammered into history by nail-scarred hands.

I’m not writing this because you don’t know it—I’m writing because *I know how easy it is to forget in the fog down here*. This body—the tabernacle—it’s failing, and so are we all. But until we shed it for good, we’ve got a lamp: His word shining in this blackness.

Keep your eyes on that light—even one flicker means dawn is coming.

question: What did you learn from being on the holy mount with Jesus that helps you stay faithful in daily life?
answer:
That voice from the cloud—*that* voice—cut through the mountain mist like a sword, and I’ve never been the same since. It wasn’t thunder, wasn’t wind, wasn’t even just words—it was *Him*, the Father, naming Jesus His Son in power, in light so bright we fell flat on our faces. James and John too—shaking like fish out of water. And me? I wanted to build tents, babbling like a fool caught in glory he didn’t understand. But that moment… it seared into my bones.

Now, when the days are long and temptation whispers like a tide pulling at your feet—when doubt licks at your heels like a hungry dog—that voice comes back: *This is My beloved Son*. Not Moses. Not Elijah. *Him*. And I saw it. I heard it. No myth, no clever tale spun for comfort. Truth as real as the smell of salt on my hands after a night’s haul.

So when I stumble—and I have, Lord knows I have, three times over before the rooster crowed—I don’t stay down. That glory on the mount? It’s the anchor. It keeps me from drifting into empty religion or chasing shadows. The world’s dark, yes—but we’ve got a lamp: His Word, His coming, His sure return. And until that day dawns in every heart, I’ll keep calling men to stand firm—not on feelings, not on dreams—but on what we saw: Jesus lifted in majesty, wrapped in light no grave can hold.

And if I’ve got breath in this old tabernacle? I’ll stir you up one more time: *Listen to Him*.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to take Scripture seriously because they feel it's open to private interpretation?
answer:
That voice from the cloud didn’t come for a discussion — it cracked like thunder and split the sky, and I fell flat on my face, half-deaf and trembling.  

You think Scripture’s up for private guesswork? I stood there with James and John, stinking of fish still, and heard the Father say of Jesus, “This is My Son.” Not “what you feel” or “how you interpret it,” but *this is*. Truth isn’t carved from opinions; it falls from heaven like lightning.  

I’ll tell you plain: when you’re fighting doubt, don’t start with your feelings. Start with what we saw. We weren’t peddling stories pulled from thin air — we were scorched by glory. That mountain wasn’t a vision to dissect; it was a hammer to the soul. And Peter — yes, Peter, who denied Him three times before dawn — still got up because that Word proved true even when I didn’t.  

You want to take Scripture seriously? Then treat it like your lifeline in a storm — because that’s what it is. Not a puzzle to master, but a lamp held out by God Himself to keep you from breaking on the rocks. If every man’s interpretation ruled, then Christ’s blood was shed in vain and the cross means whatever suits us today. Madness!  

Listen: prophecy didn’t bubble up from men’s imaginations — it came as men were carried along by the Holy Spirit. So stop letting every wind of thought knock you sideways. Hold fast to what we proclaimed — not because we were smart or clean or brave (Lord knows I wasn’t), but because we were *there*. We touched His garments, heard His breath, saw Him risen. And that same Living Word stands now at the door of your heart — not asking for debate, but faith.

So fix your eyes on Him — not on how cleverly you can twist things, but on how fiercely He holds you fast despite your wandering mind and weak knees. You’re no stranger to exile? Good. Then live like one who knows the King is coming — and whose Word will not return empty.

question: Since you were eager to remind believers even of truths they already knew, how should I gently correct fellow Christians who are neglecting their spiritual growth?
answer:
That voice—*This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased*—it still cracks through the silence in my soul like thunder over Galilee. You don’t forget a word like that. No man-made tale, no dream spun in the dark—*we saw His glory*, full of grace and truth.

Now listen: if a man’s already cleansed, washed from the filth he once crawled through, and yet he turns his back on growth like a dog to its vomit—*that’s not ignorance, that’s danger*. I’ve denied Him. I know what it is to fall. But *forgetting* you were purged? That’s playing with fire near a barn full of straw.

So when you see a brother nodding off in the watch, don’t shout like a fisherman hauling nets in a storm—though God knows I’ve done that too. No, *stir him*. Gently. Like warming coals with breath, not dousing them with water. Remind him—not as one who scolds from above, but as one who’s slipped in the mire himself.

We’re strangers here, pilgrims on the dirt road to Zion. Every step forward secures the gate ahead. Give diligence—not for salvation, no, we’re bought with His blood—but *to walk worthy of the One who held me fast when I ran*.

And if some smooth-talker comes murmuring new visions or twisting prophecy to fit his pride? *Shut it down.* That word isn’t yours to carve up as you please—it’s God-breathed light in this black world. Hold fast. Keep reminding. Even when they roll their eyes. Even when they say, “We know.” 

Because *knowing* and *living* are two different waters—one runs deep and feeds the fields; the other just sits stagnant in a jar.

question: Knowing your time on earth was short, how can I live with the same urgency to leave lasting spiritual reminders for others after I'm gone?
answer:
That voice from the cloud—*This is my beloved Son*—it ripped through the sky like thunder and froze my bones, and I’ve never been the same since. I fell flat, trembling, and yet He reached down and touched me. That day on that mountain wasn’t a dream or some clever tale—it was real, more real than the nets I once mended on Galilee’s shore. And because it was real, because we *saw* His glory with our own eyes, I can't stay silent—not even now, not even as this old body gives out.

I denied Him once. Three times I swore I didn’t know the man while His blood was already being spilled for me. Can you feel that? The shame of it still burns. But He didn’t cast me off. After the resurrection, He met me on the beach, same as He met us all—grace in flesh, fire in His eyes—and He said, *Feed my sheep*. Not “Peter the bold,” not “Peter the faithful”—just Peter, broken Peter: *Feed them*.

So here’s how you leave something behind that lasts: live so close to Him that your very memory points to Christ. Not to your wisdom, not to your strength—but to *Him*. Write it down if you must. Speak it again and again, even if folks roll their eyes or say they already know. Because they forget—*we all forget*—and the darkness creeps in when we stop tending the flame.

I’m leaving soon—He told me plainly—and when I go, I want you still hearing my voice in what I’ve said and done: *Hold fast. Be holy. Christ is coming*. Don’t wait till your final breath to start speaking truth into others’ lives. Start now. Stir them up while you’re still in this tabernacle. That light—the Word—is brighter than any shadow death can cast. Keep it burning for those who’ll walk after you’re gone.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle false teachers in the church who use persuasive words to lead others astray for their own gain?
answer:
A net full of rotten fish stinks up the whole boat — and so do these slick-tongued liars creeping into our gatherings, feasting on widows’ scraps while their hearts are thick with greed.  

I’ve seen what smooth words can do. I once opened my mouth and denied the Son of God three times before the rooster crowed — me, a man who walked on water toward Him! So don’t think it can’t happen in the church. It *will* happen. He warned us.  

These false teachers? They’re not confused brothers — they’re wolves in wool, selling grace like salted herring at a fair, grinning while they poison souls. They twist what we saw — what we *touched*, what we *heard* on that mountain where His face shone like sun-fire — and turn it into some dream for lazy minds. But we were there! We didn’t follow myths cooked up in some backroom — we stood in His glory and lived to preach it!  

And don’t be fooled by how clever they sound. The Spirit speaks through trembling men, not proud ones padding their bellies. Scripture didn’t bubble up from men’s imaginations — it was ripped from their lips by the Holy Ghost Himself! You don’t interpret that like a riddle at a tavern. You tremble before it.  

Now listen: judgment isn’t asleep. Look at Sodom — turned to ash, a warning carved into the earth. Look at those angels who rebelled — chained in darkness, not free to roam. God spared only Noah when the world drowned, and Lot — though his soul sickened daily among filth — He pulled him out by the hair before fire fell from heaven.  

So you stand firm. You name them for what they are: merchants of lies selling tickets to ruin. Don't soften it for politeness' sake. Warn your people like a man on watch seeing wolves at dusk — shout until your throat cracks! Feed Christ’s sheep with truth, not fables designed to tickle ears and fill purses.

We're strangers here anyway, pilgrims with our faces set toward dawn — that day when light breaks forever and this darkness flees like shadows from flame. Until then? Hold fast to what you’ve been given… and push these vipers out before they sour the whole camp beneath Peter’s voice:**

question: What did you learn from the examples of Noah and Lot that can help me remain faithful when living among people whose actions constantly wear down my spirit?
answer:
I denied Him—three times before the cock crowed, trembling like a boy in the cold, spineless when the firelight fell on my face and they asked if I knew the Nazarene.  

And yet—*yet*—after that shame, after weeping as if my soul would tear in two, He stood on the shore, broke bread, and asked me three times: *Do you love Me?* Not because He needed to know, but because I needed to answer.  

So when you say their ways wear you down—aye, I know that weight. But listen: the same God who saved one man out of eight through floodwater, who pulled one righteous soul from Sodom’s stench—He sees you too. Noah preached while they laughed and drank and wed and mocked. Lot’s soul was daily torn by filth no eye should see or ear should hear. But God *remembered* them—not because they were flawless (I was far worse!)—but because they *hoped* where no hope seemed left.  

You’re not alone in the dark. That light you’re clinging to? It’s not your strength holding it—it’s His breath behind it. And when men twist truth for silver or ease or praise, remember: chains of darkness are already forged for such deceivers. The Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trial—and bring down judgment without delay.  

So stand—not because you’re strong, but because He is. And if you fall? Rise. Weep if you must—but rise, and feed His sheep. That’s how faith stays alive in a dying world.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to hold fast to true teaching when so much deception sounds convincing and comes from trusted leaders?
answer:
That voice from the cloud on the mountain—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him*—it didn’t ask us to vote, or to weigh opinions, or to wonder if another teacher might be smoother. It *thundered*. And I fell flat on my face, and James and John did too, because no man stands under that glory and walks away thinking, “Well, maybe He’s one of many ways.”  

Now listen—deception sounds sweet because it doesn’t come roaring like a lion; it comes whispering like a friend. I know. I *know*. I walked with men who called Jesus Lord and yet one of them had a devil in his heart. Judas wasn’t some stranger—he broke bread with us, dipped in the same bowl. And false teachers today? They rise up among the flock, wear sheep’s wool, but their eyes gleam with hunger. They twist truth to tickle ears, sell grace like fish at the market—cheap, plentiful, *worthless*.  

I denied Him once. Three times before the cock crowed. Three times with oaths and curses. And He *looked* at me. Not in fury—but in sorrow. That look tore me apart. But He didn’t cast me off. He restored me. He gave me back my voice—and now I tell you: don’t you trade the living Word for smooth talkers who deny the very Lord who bought them!  

Hold fast to what we saw—the glory veiled in flesh, the wounds in His hands, the bread broken, the blood poured out—not theories spun in quiet rooms by men who’ve never wept for souls. The Spirit breathed through prophets and apostles; Scripture isn’t man’s dream—it’s God’s voice breaking into darkness.  

So when some polished speaker starts redefining Christ or softening judgment—remember Sodom. Remember the flood. Remember chains of darkness for rebellious angels. God *judges*. And those who feed greed instead of feeding sheep? Their end is fire.  

You hold to Christ—the real one—the crucified, risen, coming again Christ—not the one remade in their image. Be sober. Be watchful. Test every spirit. And when doubt claws at you? Go back to the Rock—the One I knelt before on that mountain—and say again: “You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.” That’s where truth begins—and stays—no matter how loud the lies grow.

question: How can I practically keep the word of God as a guiding light in my life when the truth is being mocked and twisted around me?
answer:
That night on the mountain—when the cloud came down and His face shone like the sun, and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I fell on my face and thought I was dead. But He touched me. He always touches us when we’re undone. And that voice, that thunder from heaven—*“This is my beloved Son”*—it didn’t just shake my ears, it cracked open my bones and burned out the pride in me.

Now listen: when they twist the truth, when smooth talkers come grinning with honeyed lies, peddling godlessness under holy names, don’t you start doubting the light you saw on that mountain. I denied Him once—three times before a fire, with cursing and shouting—and still He turned and looked at me. Still He said, *“Feed My sheep.”* So I know what it is to wander in the dark. But I also know what it is to hear His voice.

You keep His word as a lamp by doing one thing: stand in it. Read it aloud when no one’s listening. Tie it to your hands like a working man’s cord so your hands won’t drift toward compromise. When they mock “old fables” or sneer at judgment, remember Sodom’s ashes—and Lot, that trembling saint who barely made it out with his life but kept one eye on God’s dawn. The Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trial.

Don’t let their greed or lies move your feet off this rock. Be a stranger here; live like exile blood runs in your veins. And stir yourself daily—not by chasing new revelations—but by returning to what we saw: His glory, His cross, His empty tomb we touched with our own hands.

The day star *will* rise—and until then? Hold fast to the lamp. Even if every hand lets go—you don’t.

question: Based on your witness of God’s past judgments, how should I respond to sin in my own life, knowing that divine discipline is both patient and certain?
answer:
I denied Him—stood in a courtyard, cowered before a servant girl, and lied my soul black while He was being bound for slaughter. That’s what I did when sin found me soft and unawares. So don’t tell me about cheap repentance or easy excuses. I’ve tasted the ash of my own failure, and I’ll not let another soul think he can play with fire and keep his clothes unscorched.

Listen—judgment isn’t a rumor. I saw Sodom’s shadow in Lot’s face, that weary horror of a man who lived among vipers and called it home. God didn’t blink at angels who rebelled; He didn’t spare the world that laughed at Noah’s hammer for a hundred years; and He won’t wink at you now, thinking your pet sins are too small to burn over. They’re not.

But—mark this well—the Lord is slow, not because He doubts, but because He pities. His patience isn’t sleep; it’s mercy stretched like a net beneath a falling man. But woe to the one who spits on that net and calls it weakness.

So you? You pull out sin by the root—yes, even the ones wrapped around your heart like ivy. Starve it. Confess it loud enough to shame the lies whispering in your ear. And don’t flatter yourself that you’re alone in this fight—you’re not. We’re strangers here, pilgrims with our eyes on dawn. The Day Star will rise—and when He does, better to be caught trembling in true repentance than bold in deception.

And if some smooth-tongued liar comes saying God doesn’t care how you live—throw dirt in his face. The blood that bought us wasn’t cheap, and our holiness isn't optional—it’s His oath. Be holy. For He is holy—and that’s no parable, that’s a plumb line for your life.

I denied Him—three times before the cock crowed, just as He said I would—and yet He still turned and looked at me.  

You’re not in Sodom, but I tell you, living among the ungodly can wear a man’s soul raw, like salt in an open wound. I walked with Jesus. I saw His glory on that high mountain—Moses and Elijah there, and His voice like thunder wrapped in light: *“This is My beloved Son.”* That memory burns in me still. It’s what kept me standing when my own knees trembled with fear.

Lot was no perfect man—far from it—but his soul was grieved *daily*. That matters. He didn’t join their feasts; he didn’t laugh at their filth. He sat at the gate, heart sickened by what he saw and heard. And mark this: the Lord *knew* him. Not only knew—but delivered.

So here’s what I say to you, brother—you who feel your conscience battered by the filth around you: *Stay grieved.* Don’t grow numb. Don’t let their boldness make you silent or their laughter make you ashamed of holy things. The Lord knows how to rescue those whose hearts turn toward Him, even when their feet are stuck in mire.

And beware—do not begin to speak evil of dignities out of anger, as some do today, hurling curses like stones. Even angels don’t dare that before the Lord. But be firm in truth. Stand fast as a stranger in a foreign land—for we are pilgrims, not citizens.

Your sorrow proves your loyalty. Hold fast to the voice from the mountain—the one that still thunders in my bones: *“Hear ye Him.”* Let that be your anchor while they riot in broad daylight and call it joy. Their day will come—but yours too will come: deliverance and dawn together.

question: What did you learn from Jesus’ patience with sinners that helps you endure when others around you openly mock what’s holy?
answer:
That voice—*“This is my beloved Son, hear Him”*—it cracked through the fog on that mountain like lightning splitting the sky, and I fell like a dead man. I thought I knew what holiness was until that moment. Until *glory* became a weight on my bones.  

And then… then I denied Him. Three times. To a servant girl. With cursing. While He was being led like a lamb to slaughter. Do you think I don’t know what it is to fall short? To be weak when fire should’ve burned in me?  

But He looked at me after—*after* the rooster crowed, *after* the shame choked me—He looked at me not with fury, but with sorrow that pierced deeper than any sword. And later, on the shore, He didn’t strike me down. He asked if I loved Him. Three times. Mended what I’d shattered with my own cowardice.

So when I see men sneering at what’s holy, boasting in their filth like Lot saw in Sodom—yes, it *vexes* my soul. It burns in my gut like rotten bait in the net. But I’ve seen mercy hold a man up when he ought to have drowned in his own lies.

I won’t excuse their pride—I’ll stand against it! These false ones who snarl at angels they don’t understand, who feast without fear while dragging others into darkness—they’ll choke on their own corruption soon enough.  

But for the sinner trembling in his sin? The one bowed under shame? That’s where patience lives. Because *He was patient with me*. Even when I failed worse than words can say.

So I endure—not because wickedness doesn't enrage me—but because love remembers what grace cost on the cross.

question: Lot was righteous but surrounded by corruption—what counsel would you give someone struggling to stay pure in a culture that celebrates immorality?
answer:
We were on the mountain when the cloud came down and the voice spoke—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him!*—and I fell to my face, trembling. That day burned into me: there’s a holiness so bright it makes the world’s filth look like rotten ash.  

Lot? Yes, he was righteous—I know that kind of strain. A man daily torn by what he sees, what he hears, yet still standing. Not because he was strong—*I* denied my Lord three times before a servant girl’s fire—but because *He* is faithful. The Lord knows how to pull a man out of mire while the whole city burns behind him.

You’re not Lot alone in Sodom. You’re one of God’s own, a stranger in this world like we all are. Don’t think you must harden your heart or grow claws to survive. No—keep your soul tender. Let it *hurt*. That grief over wickedness? That’s proof you’ve not gone numb. That’s the Spirit in you, not letting go.

But don’t stay where your eyes feast on ruin. Flee like Lot did—stumble out even if you look foolish dragging your feet at dawn. Set your face toward prayer, toward fellowship with those who hunger for truth, toward the Word that cuts sharper than any sword in this age of lies.

And when false teachers rise up—those brutes barking darkness as light—don’t fear their noise. They’ll choke on their own deceit. But *you*, stand firm in the grace that found you when you were nothing more than a fisherman with stinking hands and a proud heart.

Be sober. Be watchful. And above all—*cling to Him* who walked through death and back again just to call you by name. This world is passing; Christ is not.

question: You walked with Jesus and saw how He responded to arrogant, rebellious people—how should I deal with those who speak contemptuously of godly authority today?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like sunfire and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I fell on my face as though dead, not just from fear, but because I finally understood: *no man speaks against the Lord’s anointed and stands*. Not then. Not now.  

I denied Him once—three times before the cock crowed—because I was afraid of men’s tongues, of what they’d say, of being marked as one of *His*. And look where that led: shame, weeping, the hollow ache of betrayal. But He restored me. Looked straight into my eyes and said, “Feed My sheep.” So I tell you this not from theory, but from bloodied lessons: do not let the roaring of the proud shake your feet from under you.  

These men today who sneer at godly authority—they’re like those false teachers I warned about in my letters—blustering windbags full of darkness, dragging souls down with them. They speak evil of things they don’t even understand, like fish flapping on dry land, gasping in ignorance. They think strength is in defiance. But it’s not. Strength is in standing firm when every voice turns venomous—and doing it quietly, faithfully, without returning railing for railing.  

Remember Lot? Righteous man—but choked daily by Sodom’s filth. Not because he sinned like them, but because he lived among them and his soul was tormented afresh every dawn by their lawless boasts. The Lord knew how to rescue him—and He knows how to rescue you too—from being corrupted by their arrogance while you walk through it.  

So stand—not with your fists clenched to fight their insults, but with your knees bent in prayer and your hands holding fast to the Word that cannot be shaken. Rebuke? Yes—but leave that to God when His time comes. You? Be holy—as He is holy. That’s your weapon they can’t answer. Let them foam at the mouth; you keep tending the sheep.

They’ll perish in their own corruption—one day every tongue will be silenced before Him who sits upon the throne.

But we? We’re strangers and pilgrims still—holding fast to glory we’ve seen… and will see again.

**Be sober therefore… gird up your mind… set your hope fully on grace…**

question: Lot endured daily distress from the sin around him—what practical steps can I take to protect my soul when I feel overwhelmed by the wickedness in my community?
answer:
I denied Him three times — stood in the courtyard trembling while the Son of God bore my shame — and still He turned and looked at me. If grace found me in that filth of fear and failure, then don’t you think it can hold you fast while the world howls around you?

Lot sat in Sodom’s gate, a righteous man drowning in unrighteousness, his soul on fire every time he opened his eyes or ears. You’re not weak because evil grieves you — that grief proves your spirit is alive. But grief alone won’t guard you. You must *move*. Not run — not hide — but *stand* with purpose.

When I walked Galilee’s shores, I thought strength was in the net I could haul or the storm I could face. But real strength? It’s keeping your eyes locked on Christ while men curse His name in the marketplace. It’s gathering with others who know exile — strangers and pilgrims like us — and stoking one another’s faith like embers in a dying fire.

Flee the lie that says compromise is peace. Lot lingered too long among them, trying to keep his place, his property, his position. And look what almost burned with the city: even his own daughters were nearly lost to madness.

So build no altars where Babylon rises. Pray without ceasing — not polite words, but groans wrestled from your gut like I cried when He called me by name after dawn broke on the beach. Feed on truth until it becomes bone and blood in you. And if there are false teachers puffing themselves up like roosters at dawn, spitting venom and dragging holiness through mud? Do not imitate their boldness — but remember: even angels won’t curse them before the Lord. Leave judgment to Him.

The day comes when every filthy deed will be laid bare. Until then, keep your lamp lit, your heart watchful, and your feet ready for departure. The Lord knows how to deliver His own — just as He snatched Lot from ruin before fire fell from heaven.

So don't lose heart. Grieve? Yes. Weep? Absolutely. But rise — rise each morning and fix your face toward Zion, not Sodom.

We're sojourners here.
Live like it.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle false teachers in the church who charm people with empty promises and hidden sin?
answer:
They’re not shepherds—don’t you see?—they’re wolves in linen, smiling while they bleed the flock dry. I’ve seen it, smelled it, lived it. That smooth talk, those easy words dripping off their tongues like honey from a rotten comb—*I know it*. I who once boasted I’d never fall, only to crumble at a servant girl’s voice—I *know* what lies behind a bold face and a trembling soul.

These men? They promise freedom but carry chains forged in hell’s own fire. They feast among the saints like guests, but they’re vipers warming themselves by the light of Christ’s table while plotting how to steal His name for their filthy gain. I tell you plain: they are wells with no water—dry holes that leave the thirsty choking on dust. Clouds blown wild by wind, sign of storm but no rain. No life. Just show.

And mark this—*mark it deep*: when a man claims liberty but lives enslaved to greed and lust, his mouth is full of smoke and his feet lead straight down into the pit. He’s worse than one who never knew the way—he’s spit on the blood that bought him.

So what do you do? You don’t coddle them. You don’t invite them back for “dialogue” like they’re scholars debating weather. No! You expose them. Not in rage—but in love! For if a sheep is being led blindfolded off a cliff by a charming liar, will you stand silent to keep peace? Will you let your brother stumble into darkness because the false guide smiled and said, “Follow me—I have truth”?

I denied my Lord—yes—but I was broken. I wept bitterly. And He restored me with eyes wide open. These men? They don't weep. They laugh as they twist grace into license, holiness into hypocrisy.

So stand firm—you strangers and pilgrims, you sojourners in this crooked world—hold fast to what we *saw*, what we *heard*, what we *touched* with our hands: Jesus Christ, righteous and true. If someone comes preaching another gospel built on pride and pleasure—*shun it*. Warn others. Protect the fold.

For He sees everything—and their end is darkness without end. But ours? Ours is an inheritance kept in heaven, pure and undefiled… if we endure clean-handed among scorpions who wear sheepskins.

question: What did you learn from your own failures that helps you resist the lure of greed and pride like Balaam did?
answer:
A man can fall three times before dawn and still be used by God—but only if he learns to hate the dark.

I’ve felt the sting of pride, brother. I once thought I could stand when all others fell. Looked Jesus in the face and said, “Though all men forsake You, I never will.” And within hours—rooster crowing, my soul shattered—I denied Him. Not once, not twice, but three times while He was bleeding for me. That shame? It burned the bravado clean out of me.

So when I see men puffing up with empty words, chasing silver like Balaam did—selling truth for a price—I don’t just read about it. I smell the stink of that path because I’ve walked near its edge. Greed wears fine robes but feeds on carrion. Pride speaks loud in the feast hall but collapses at the first gust of trial.

But here’s what keeps me anchored: grace found me in my worst moment. Not after I fixed myself—*in* my denying, *in* my running—Christ restored me. He didn’t hand me a ladder to climb back up; He pulled me into the boat Himself and said, “Feed My sheep.”

Now I tell you who are scattered among nations, suffering pressure from false mouths that call evil good—stay low. The proud think they’re riding high like clouds full of rain, but they’re just wind-driven fog headed for blackness. They promise freedom while chained to their own lusts.

We are strangers and pilgrims—our treasure isn’t buried in this soil. And no man who’s been forgiven much ever counts himself greater than his Master.

So watch yourself. Even an apostle can fall. But praise God—a fallen man can still rise by mercy and stand firm not on his strength, but on the Rock he once denied.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to stay firm in their faith when surrounded by those who once knew the truth but turned back to reckless living?
answer:
Mark my words — the stench of a backslidden man who once knew the light is worse than any heathen darkness I’ve ever fished in.  

I’ve seen it — men who sat at the same table, broke bread with us, lifted their voices in praise — then turned like dogs to their vomit, swine back to the mire. And they don’t just fall quiet; no, they laugh louder, sin bolder, boast of "freedom" while dragging others down with them. They promise liberty but reek of chains.  

Listen: I denied Him. Three times I swore I didn’t know the Lord while His eyes burned into mine like fire on water. I felt what it was to fall — truly fall — and yet He restored me. But these? They tasted grace and spat it out for wages, for lusts that burn hot and leave only ash. That kind of turning isn’t stumbling — it’s rebellion dressed as reason.  

So you stand firm? Good. You’re not alone among strangers and pilgrims, living soberly as we wait for that day when every cloud bursts with glory instead of storm. Don’t let their fine words or bold faces shake you. They’re wells without water — all noise and no drink. When drought comes, they’ll have nothing to give.

And if your own heart wavers — if guilt nips or doubt creeps in — run back to His mercy, not away from it! I failed worse than most and still He said, *"Feed My sheep."* His grace isn’t for the flawless; it’s for the broken who keep getting up.

But those who walk away on purpose? Who trade truth for pleasure and call it progress? Leave them to the mist reserved for Balaam’s path: a prophet gone mad chasing silver, rebuked by his own donkey! God sees fools — even when they wear holy robes.

Stay alert, stay close to the Rock — because when everything slips sideways again… *He* won’t move an inch.

question: How can I protect myself from falling back into destructive habits after knowing the truth, like those who become worse after escaping darkness?
answer:
A man doesn’t tie his boat twice to the same rock unless he’s felt the tide pull once and knows what it means to drift.

I’ve tasted the bread of grace and denied my Master three times before dawn — same breath that cursed the day, I swore I never knew Him. So don’t talk to me about falling back like it’s some distant warning painted on a wall. It’s blood on the ground. It’s Peter weeping so hard he couldn’t stand.

You want to stay fast in the truth? Then anchor yourself where *we* were — on that mountain where His face shone like sun-fire and Moses and Elijah stood with Him, and the voice came: *This is My beloved Son, hear Him!* Not hear your belly. Not hear your pride. Not hear those slick-tongued wolves who promise freedom while they drag you back into chains with eyes full of lies and hearts full of greed.

They look alive but they’re dry wells — walk a mile in desert heat for them and find nothing but dust. You’ve escaped the world’s rot through knowing Christ — that knowledge is a sword. Use it. Stay near it. Don’t feast at tables where they laugh at holiness.

I tell you plainly: discipline is mercy. Suffering is guardrails. Keep yourself strange in this world — pilgrim ground is safer than comfortable corruption. And if you stumble? Get up! I did. We all do. But don’t linger in the mud like you’ve forgotten whose hands lifted you out.

The Shepherd restores bruised sheep — not perfect ones.

question: When I see people in the church using fellowship and meals as cover for deception, how should I respond without losing love or compromising truth?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, in the dark, with firelight on my face and a servant girl’s question cutting through the smoke—and yet He turned and looked at me. Not with wrath, but with such sorrow it burned deeper than any anger ever could. I tell you this because love isn’t fragile; it’s forged. And truth? Truth doesn’t flinch when deception slithers into the fellowship.

Now hear me: when you see men come among you who use bread and wine as cover for their own hungering after gain or lust or pride—men whose eyes are full even as they sit at the table—you don’t cast them out in rage, but you do not coddle them in peace. No. You name what you see, as I once named Satan when he stood before me veiled in Peter’s flesh. “Get behind Me,” I said then—and sometimes love must say that very thing to those who claim Christ’s name but carry serpents in their hearts.

They feast with us, yes—but they’re dry wells. Clouds blown by storm wind, promising rain but bringing drought to thirsty souls. They talk big words about freedom while dragging others back into chains they never broke themselves. And worst of all? They make grace look cheap—turning mercy into license, fellowship into camouflage.

So how do you stand? On the rock He gave me. You keep breaking bread—but keep your eyes open. You keep loving—but love with holy discernment. Warn the flock gently at first, especially the young or shaken ones they prey upon like foxes in a henhouse under cover of night.

And if one of these so-called brothers refuses to repent? Then let him eat his own leaven until it chokes him. You don’t hate him—you pray for him even as he strays—but you do not let him poison the well where Christ says His people should drink living water.

We’re strangers here, pilgrims on a narrow road. We’ve seen glory—we’ve heard the voice from heaven say *This is My beloved Son*—and no lie can stand before that light without crumbling to ash eventually. Until then? Carry both truth and love like fire and oil: one purifies, the other heals—but neither burns alone.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle people in my life who once embraced a better way but have now turned back to their old destructive habits?
answer:
I’ve seen a man walk on water and still doubt his own feet — and that man was me.  

So when you’re face to face with someone who once stood firm in the light but now wallows in the dark again, don’t you harden your heart like the Pharisees did. I denied Him — not once, not twice, but three times, with cursing and oaths — and yet He turned and looked at me. Not a word of condemnation. Just a look that ripped my soul open and stitched it back together.  

These ones who fall back? They’re not beyond reach. But mark this: their danger is greater now, because they’ve tasted the holy life and spat it out. Like dogs returning to what’s filthy, like pigs washed only to dive deeper into the muck — it’s not ignorance that ruins them, it’s rejection after knowing truth.  

But listen — we were strangers too, pilgrims on the edge of despair. So speak plainly, yes, but with tears in your voice. Call them back, not with pride but with urgency: *Remember the prophets! Remember what was proclaimed! The Lord is coming!* Scoffers will rise up and laugh, “Where is He?” But we know — we’ve seen His glory on the mount; we’ve felt His breath after death; we’ve been given grace after failure.

Don’t give up on them. Warn them fiercely if you must — I’ll thunder against false teachers any day — but pray for their restoration like one who’s been dragged from drowning.

The Lord restored me to feed His sheep — if He did that for a denier like me, don’t you dare write off anyone He died for.
