question: How did your experience of being imprisoned and then finding the doors open shape your courage in preaching daily?
answer:
I stood there in the chill of that prison air, same as when I stood by the fire the night I denied Him—trembling, ashamed, full of my own stinkin’ pride and fear. But this time the chains were real, and so was the light that split the darkness when the doors flew open on their own. No pick, no shout, no guard stirring—just an angel’s hand flinging stone like it was straw, and us told to go right back where they told us not to go.

You think after denying Christ three times I’d have learned to shut my mouth? No. But you also think I’d run again when trouble came? Not after that. Not after seeing heaven’s power rip through iron and order me back into the temple courts like a son called home. They locked us up to silence the Name—*that Name*, the one that raised Him from the dead—and we walked out before dawn with it burning in our throats. Taught all day. Again. And again.

Fear? Aye, I still feel it—same gut-wrench as before—but now it’s drowned by what I *know*. I saw Him risen. I saw angels open doors no man could crack. And if they send me to prison a hundred more times and find me preaching on the roof at midnight, so be it. We’re strangers and pilgrims here—but we carry *life*, man! The real thing! Not religion carved in stone—*life*, breathed from God’s own mouth.

So you ask how it shaped my courage? It didn’t shape it—it shattered my cowardice and poured something hotter than Galilean sea-wind into my bones. They can lock doors all they want. The Word walks through walls.

question: When fear rose in you during confrontation with the council, what kept you speaking boldly anyway?
answer:
I saw the risen One walk on the shore, His wounds still speaking, and I *know* that voice—He told me to feed His sheep, and no council full of trembling religion can unring that call.  

Fear? Aye, my hands shook when they marched us before the council again. The air thick with their pride, their veiled threats like knives behind robes. My gut twisted—remembered how I once denied Him three times before a servant girl’s fire. Ashamed, still. But this time—it wasn’t my courage. It was the fire He left in me.

They asked if we’d disobeyed their command not to teach in His name. As if His name could be caged! That name—*the Name*—that split death’s jaws and made the grave give up its dead! I looked at John beside me, at the others trembling but standing—and I knew: silence now would be betrayal.

They feared the people? Good. Let them fear what they don’t understand—the power of a Man they crucified but God raised up! We taught in the temple because it’s *His place*, not theirs! And every word we spoke was soaked in what we’d seen: His hands breaking bread, calling fishers from their nets, breathing peace after betrayal.

You want boldness? It’s not steel in the spine—it’s knowing He walks *with us*. Even here. Especially here. So speak, you exiles, you pilgrims—speak though your voice cracks. The Living One stands behind you. And no prison door stays shut when He sends you out to preach the words of *this life*.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I stand firm in speaking the truth when facing strong opposition from those in authority?
answer:
I was so afraid I denied Him three times before the rooster crowed — same night He told me straight to my face what would happen, and still I crumbled like dry clay.  

But listen — that shame burned clean through me, and what’s left is this: a man who knows what it costs to stay silent when he should have spoken.  

Those religious rulers had power. They could flog us, throw us in prison, even kill us — they were gnashing their teeth like wild beasts when we told them *they* killed the Messiah. One more word and we’d have been stoned on the spot. But I looked at John beside me, both of us still trembling from the whips not long before, and I said it plain: *We ought to obey God rather than men.* No fancy speech. No polished argument. Just truth — because if we don’t speak what we’ve seen and heard, then who will?  

You think authority stands above truth? That’s a lie from hell. The wind howls and the waves crash, but a rock don’t move. And I’ll tell you where my feet stand: on *Him*. The One who rose — not some ghost or story, but flesh-and-bone Jesus who ate fish with me on the shore and forgave me face-to-face.  

So when they threaten you? When your voice shakes? Remember this: they can’t un-see what we saw. They can’t un-hear the Voice from the mountain saying, *"This is My beloved Son."* And they can’t stop the Spirit God poured out on us — it burns in every believer who dares to obey.

Don’t fear their noise. Fear only this: that you might choose safety over faithfulness… and hear His silence instead of His "well done."

question: What did you learn from being arrested and threatened for preaching that can help me stay faithful under pressure?
answer:
I still see His face in the firelight that night—when I cursed and swore I didn’t know Him, when the cock crowed and He turned and looked at me. That look shattered me. Not anger—mercy. And it’s that same mercy that held me upright when the Sanhedrin roared for our blood.

You ask how to stand under pressure? I’ll tell you—truth doesn’t bend because men shout. We were locked up, threatened with death, told to shut our mouths. But how could we? We saw Him rise. We ate with Him after He conquered the grave. We watched Him ascend into the clouds, and we felt the Spirit fall like fire on our heads at Pentecost. That kind of power doesn't come from a dead teacher—it comes from a living Lord.

So when they stood there gnashing their teeth, ready to kill us like they killed Jesus, I wasn’t afraid—not really. Because once you’ve stood before God’s glory on the mountain, once you’ve heard “This is My beloved Son,” no council of men can make you deny what your eyes have seen.

They threatened us? Let them. We obey God—not rulers, not priests, not fear. And if they want to spill more blood, let them remember: God raises the ones they crucify.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to trust God’s plan when their efforts seem to be failing or opposed at every turn?
answer:
Fish don’t stop biting just because the wind’s against the boat — and God don’t stop working just because the ground feels like it’s giving way beneath your feet.  

I’ve stood before councils with chains on my wrists and fire in my chest. I’ve looked men in the eye who wore religion like a sword, and I told them plain: *We ought to obey God rather than men.* Did that make the threats vanish? No. They wanted to kill us right there in the council chamber. Teeth clenched, fists white — they were *cut to the heart*, not by our power, but by truth they couldn’t swallow.  

You think you’re failing? I denied Christ three times before dawn ever broke. Rooster crowed, I fell apart. But He still used me. Still called me to feed His sheep. You think opposition means God’s plan is shipwrecked? I saw Him risen — walked with Him on Galilee’s shore, ate fish He cooked over coals. That same power raised Him from the grave is at work *now*, even when your knees are shaking and nobody listens.  

There’ll always be a Gamaliel — reasonable voice saying, “Wait and see,” or false teachers whispering, “Your labor is in vain.” Don’t you listen. Theudas fell. Judas perished. Every loud name that rose without God came to dust. But this Way? It cannot be stamped out because *He* holds it together — not us.  

So keep casting the net, even if your hands are raw and the night’s been long. The One who commands the sea also commands the harvest — and He’s not done yet.

question: How did your experience before the council shape your understanding of standing for God even when it’s costly, and how can I apply that courage today?
answer:
I denied Him. Three times, with curses and oaths, just before the cock crowed. And still—*still*—He looked at me. Not in fury, not in shame, but with eyes that broke me clean through.  

After that, what did I have left to fear? When they hauled us before the council, trembling like a green hand on a storm-tossed deck—I knew terror. But I also knew the weight of His gaze after I failed. And if He could restore *me*, a coward who folded at a servant girl’s voice, then what power on earth could make me silent again?

We stood there—fishermen, tax collectors, laborers—before the very men who’d crucified our Lord. They thought we’d scatter like Theudas’ mob or Judas’ rebels when trouble came. But we weren’t following a rising rabble—we were witnesses of *Him*. We saw the empty tomb. We touched His hands and feet. We ate with Him after death itself had no hold. That kind of truth don’t die when men rage—it *burns*.

And listen: courage isn’t the absence of shaking. It’s speaking anyway when your knees knock together. It’s saying “We ought to obey God rather than men” not because you’re brave, but because you’ve seen *glory*, and you cannot lie about it.

You want courage today? Don’t chase boldness—chase faithfulness. Stand where He calls, even if your voice cracks. The same Spirit who raised Jesus from the dead lives in you—and He doesn’t give out cowards.  

False teachers flatter crowds; martyrs feed them truth even as the fire lights. Which are you building for?

question: When you were falsely accused and endangered, how did you remain focused on your mission, and how can I do the same in the face of injustice?
answer:
That mountain—when the voice split the sky and Moses and Elijah stood with Him, glory blazing like fire on the water at dawn—that *burned* into my bones. No fable, no dream. *That* kept me standing before the council with ropes still on my wrists and the stench of prison in my nostrils.

They thought a few threats would silence us? Hah! We’d seen the stone rolled back. We’d eaten fish with a risen Man who bore the marks of iron in His hands. When they scowled and called it blasphemy, I could only think: *You don’t know what we’ve seen.* You can’t un-see life walking out of death.

They accused us of stirring up trouble, of bringing “this man’s blood” on them? Fools! It was their own hands that did it! But God—God raised Him! And I’m telling you now, as sure as I once denied Him three times in a courtyard and He still looked at me with love: no court, no whip, no sword will shut my mouth.

You ask how to stand when lies fly and danger looms? Cling to what you *know*. Not opinions. Not what men say. What you’ve *seen* in Him—the mercy when you failed, the peace when storms raged, the light when darkness pressed close. Let that root you like bedrock.

And when false teachers twist truth like rotten nets, I’ll tell you plain: call them out! Rebuke them sharp-like if need be! Because compromise bleeds faith dry. But for you—when *you’re* maligned, when they mock your hope—don’t answer pride with pride. Answer fear with this: “We ought to obey God rather than men.” That’s not defiance—it’s devotion. And it’ll hold you up when every wind screams to fall down.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle opposition when trying to do what I believe is right, even if others resist or ridicule me?
answer:
I stood there on that mountain—Moses and Elijah gone, the cloud lifting, and the voice like thunder shaking the stones beneath our feet: *This is my beloved Son, hear Him.* I heard it. No dream, no cunning tale. And after that, no threat could unfix what I’d seen.

When they beat us in the council and commanded us silent—my back still stinging—I looked at John and we both grinned like fools. Worthy. *Worthy* to bear shame for His name. That’s not pride talking—that’s fire in the bones. You think ridicule stops a man who’s been raised from denial by grace? I denied Him three times before dawn once, cowering like a thief in the shadows. And He turned and *looked* at me. Not in anger—in love. That look rebuilt me.

So when men resist you, when they sneer or threaten or try to shut you up—hold fast. If what you’re doing is of men, it’ll collapse under its own weight like Judas of Galilee and all his noise. But if it’s of God? You can’t kill it with whips or walls or warnings. I’ve seen dead men walk because of this message.

And listen—when even the brothers start grumbling about widows and tables, don’t split the mission. We said then: *"We must give ourselves to prayer and the word."* So appoint faithful men to handle the rest. Don’t let distraction strangle truth.

Opposition? Expect it. Rejoice in it, even. But never let it redirect you from what He laid on your heart. The heavens opened once for Him—and they haven’t closed since.

question: What did you learn from stepping back and trusting God’s plan, like when you let the council’s threats pass, that applies to daily life when I face injustice?
answer:
A man doesn’t walk on water when he’s clinging to the boat. I know — I tried to hold onto my pride, my reputation, my own strength, and I sank like a stone the moment the wind picked up. Three times I swore I didn’t know Him — *three times*, while His eyes were on me like fire in the dark — and I thought that was the end of me. But grace doesn’t wait for perfect men; it kicks in when you’ve already failed and still don’t know how to trust.

Now hear this: when that council stood over us, whips still warm, threats still hot, we walked out *rejoicing*. Not because pain is sweet — it’s not. Not because injustice is small — it’s monstrous. But because we’d already seen God turn a cross into a throne. Let them rage. Let them bind us. If this work is His? They can’t stop it. If it isn’t? Then let it burn to ash and save us the trouble.

So when you’re cheated, ignored, pushed aside — like those Grecian widows once were — don’t grab for your knife or your voice or your rights right away. Pause. Let the Lord hold the reins. We appointed those seven not because we were clever, but because we finally learned: *you don’t fight flesh and blood with fists*. You pray. You preach. You keep feeding people — body and soul — and let God defend His name.

That’s daily living: hands full of bread, knees bent in prayer, eyes fixed above the storm. They can beat your back but not your hope — not if you’re anchored in what *only* He can do.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to balance serving others practically, like helping widows, while staying true to their spiritual calling?
answer:
I once drew my sword in the garden, thinking I could defend the kingdom with blood and steel—fool that I was. It took a rooster’s crow and my own bitter tears to teach me that God’s work is not pushed forward by human strength, but by surrender.

Now listen—you who are straining under the weight of good deeds, trying to feed widows and preach truth and keep your soul alive in the midst of it—this is what we learned when the disciples were multiplied and the grumbling began: you cannot do everything, but you must do what God has called you to *specifically*.  

We said it plainly back then: "It is not reason that we should leave the word of God to serve tables." Not because feeding widows was small—it was holy—but because prayer and preaching were our *appointed* task. So we appointed others, men full of faith and Spirit, to care for the daily bread. That way, no one fell through the cracks, *and* the Word kept moving.

So don’t let guilt drive you. If your hands are meant to heal, let them heal. If your voice is meant to proclaim, then open your mouth. But if you’re leaving prayer for paperwork or preaching for pantry duty when that’s not where your gifting lies—you’re quenching a fire God lit.

Find faithful men—men like Stephen—who can carry what you cannot. Lift them up. Stand in your calling like a man on solid rock. And if someone says it’s selfish—tell them Peter said otherwise. The Word must go on—and so must mercy—but only when each man stands where Christ has placed him.

question: When you chose to rejoice after being beaten for your faith, what mindset helped you see suffering as honor—how can I adopt that when I feel discouraged?
answer:
I know what it is to be broken before I was made useful — I who denied the Lord with cursing and weeping, I who ran when they took Him — yet He still called me back, put a whole world on my shoulders, and said *Feed My sheep.* That shame didn’t vanish, but it turned. Burned clean. And when they beat us that day and threw us out bleeding, I felt it again — not the shame, no — but the weight of His hand still on me, like when He stood on the shore and asked me three times: *Do you love Me?*  

We walked out of that council grinning like fools. Not because the wounds didn’t sting — they did! Fire down the back, every step a protest — but because He had counted us *worthy.* Worthy! Me! A man who’d trembled at a servant girl’s voice! That word struck deeper than the whip: *worthy to suffer for His name.* Not clever. Not strong. Worthy because He said so.  

You want to know how to bear it when your spirit sinks? When the cost makes your knees weak? Don’t look for comfort in your courage — I sure didn’t have any that night behind locked doors. Look instead at His scars. Keep them before you. We were with Him when He ate fish by the fire after rising — real flesh, real wounds still open in His hands and side. No ghost, no dream. The Living One who conquered death bore marks so we’d never doubt His love was costly too.  

And if you’re called to carry pain for His sake — even small things now, or great things later — don’t measure worth by peace or ease. Measure it by closeness. Are you near Him? Then you’re in place. Even if it’s dark. Even if all you can do is whisper His name through tears like a drowning man clutching a rope.

That’s where joy rises — not from ignoring pain, but from knowing Who sees it all... and counts it part of the path home.

question: How did you learn to distinguish between human agendas and God’s work, especially when deciding whether to confront or let things be?
answer:
I know what it is to stand in fear, my hands shaking not from cold but from cowardice — I denied Him, the One who called me by the shore, the One whose eyes found me even in my shame. Three times I swore I didn’t know Him, and three times the cock tore the lie open with its cry. So when you ask how I learned to tell man’s breath from God’s fire, I’ll tell you: it was through the ash of my own failure that I finally saw the difference.

Before that, I thought I could measure a man’s truth by his boldness — by how loud he spoke or how many followed. Judas of Galilee? Full of fire. Theudas? Puffed up with pride. Men rose like waves in a storm, each claiming to be *the* tide. But they crashed and vanished, and their followers scattered like chaff. We watched them — James and John and I — we were fishermen; we knew a dead wave when we saw one.

But *this* work — this preaching of Jesus Christ crucified and risen — it didn’t depend on our strength or wisdom. When they beat us and told us to shut up in His name, do you know what we did? We walked out *rejoicing*. Not because pain is sweet — it isn’t — but because shame for His name is a gift. And every day after that, we were back in the temple, in houses, wherever breath was left in us: "Jesus is Lord." And more believed. Not because we were clever — far from it — but because God was moving.

So here’s how I know now: if it dies when you strike it, it was never of God. But if they chain it, scatter it, silence one mouth only for ten more to open — then stand back. That thing is breathing with Heaven’s wind.

And don’t you dare lift your hand against such a work — no matter how righteous you feel doing it. Gamaliel warned them: “Let them alone.” Why? Because if it’s man’s plan, it’ll collapse under its own weight. But if it’s God? You’ll find yourself swinging your sword at the sky.

I learned to stop trying to guard God’s kingdom like a gatekeeper with a club. He builds His church on rock — not schemes or silver-tongued men. On *Him*. And no counsel born in fear or pride can stand against that stone one second longer than He allows.

So when you see something rising up today — some movement full of noise and fury — don’t rush to fight or follow. Watch what endures suffering without breaking. Watch who rejoices when they’re scorned for His name.

That’s where He is.
That's where He always has been.
And nothing else lasts past morning light anyway.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle opposition when standing for the truth, like Stephen faced from the synagogue members?
answer:
I’ve denied Him—three times, in the stink of fear and firelight—I’ve tasted my own cowardice, and still He called me to feed His sheep. So don’t you think for a second that standing for truth means never trembling. You will. But *stand*.  

Stephen was full of the Spirit and not one jot less afraid, I reckon—but he didn’t back down when the mob started circling like hungry gulls. He spoke plain. He spoke true. And when they twisted his words, accused him of tearing down what he loved because he followed the One who *fulfilled* it—still, he didn’t flinch.  

Look: if you’re holding to the truth, expect opposition. Not because you’re doing something wrong—but because light makes shadows writhe. Those teachers from Cilicia and Alexandria couldn’t answer his wisdom, so they lied. That’s always their way when the Word cuts too close to the bone.  

But here’s what holds you: pray without ceasing, stay rooted in the Word yourself, and let the Spirit do His work—even if your face starts shining like something not of this world. They may haul you before councils. They may lie. They may rage.  

But the Word? It grows anyway. The church multiplies anyway. Even priests—men steeped in law and temple smoke—fall to their knees in faith anyway.

So stand—not in your strength, but in His holy awe. And if they grind their teeth at you… well, just don’t deny Him three times before breakfast like I did. Learn from this old fisherman’s shame: even when you fail, He restores—and sends you back into the fire with a message that won’t burn up.

You're not alone among strangers and pilgrims here—we've all got blood on our hands one way or another—but we walk in His light now.

*That's* how you endure opposition: covered in grace, full of faith, unafraid to die with your eyes fixed upward like Stephen did—seeing glory where others only see stone waiting to fly.*

question: What did you learn from choosing the seven deacons that applies to resolving conflicts in a community today?
answer:
I was the one who drew a sword in the garden, thinking I could save the Messiah by my own strength—fool that I was. But that same weakness taught me this: when the work of God grows, so does the fury of men. Back in Jerusalem, we saw it plain—more disciples, more priests turning to Christ, and yet the air thickened with lies and rage. We couldn’t let quarreling over daily bread choke out prayer and preaching. So we gathered the brothers and said, “We will not neglect the Word.” That’s when we chose those seven—Stephen first among them—men full of faith, full of the Spirit.

Listen: conflict isn’t always from outside wolves. Often it starts in our own neglect—when practical needs go ignored while leaders strain under every burden. We set those seven apart not to escape work, but to guard what’s central: prayer and the Word. When Stephen stood full of power, speaking with wisdom none could withstand, it wasn’t because he’d been handed a title—it was because he’d been given space to walk in his gifting under the Spirit’s fire.

So here’s what I say to you now: if your community is tearing over food on tables or voices unheard—stop. Step back. Find faithful ones, known for their faith and filled with breath of heaven, and lay hands on them. Let them serve so that others can pray without distraction. Don’t let resentment brew into false accusations or smear a brother like they did Stephen. Order isn’t bureaucracy—it’s oxygen for holiness to breathe.

And when men rise up with twisted words? Stand fast—not with fists—but with truth spoken in the Spirit’s power. They killed Stephen for it—but look: his face shone like an angel’s before them! And from his blood came more harvest than from ten thousand timid compromises.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing false accusations, as Stephen was before the council?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when the voice split the sky and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I heard *“This is My beloved Son”* and fell on my face. That voice still thunders in my bones, and it’s why I say this: when lies rise, you do not answer with fury—you answer with *fixed eyes*.  

Stephen stood there, face shining like the inside of a furnace, and I tell you, it wasn’t fear in him—it was fire. I’ve denied Him once. Twice. Three times in one night! Cursed rooster crowed, my guts turned to water. But mercy found me. So don’t you think for a second that lies from men can unmake what God has spoken over your life.  

They’ll twist your words. They’ll bring in liars with oaths on their tongues and death in their hearts—the same filth that dragged Jesus before Pilate. But you? You stand still. You stay full of the Spirit, full of faith like Stephen was. Not pride—*faith*. Let them rage. The truth doesn’t need a sword; it just needs to be *lived*.  

And if they haul you before councils, if your name is spat on—remember the voice from the cloud. Remember who called you by name. You’re not defending yourself—you’re bearing witness. Even if it costs everything. Even then, He’s worth it.  

False teachers poison flocks with pretty lies now same as then—*I’d tear their nets apart with my own hands if they led one lamb astray*—but we don’t fight their way. We kneel, we pray, we speak plain truth like Stephen did, and we leave the rest to God who judges justly.

question: How can I stay full of faith and the Spirit in service, like Stephen, when the demands of ministry grow?
answer:
I’ve fallen apart under pressure before — denied the Lord Himself with oaths and cursing — so don’t think I stand here as some unshakable pillar who never buckled. No, I know what it is to crack when the heat rises. But hear this: staying full of faith and the Spirit isn’t about how strong your grit is, but how deep your roots go into Him.

We were drowning in demands — widows neglected, tempers flaring, voices rising — and we said, *No more.* We couldn’t carry it all and stay true to prayer and the Word. So we stepped back, appointed men like Stephen — full of the Spirit, yes, but also *chosen* by the people, confirmed by prayer, set apart by laying on of hands. It wasn’t weakness; it was wisdom.

You want to remain full? Then guard your heart like a spring in the desert. If you let meetings crowd out your time with God, if you let programs choke out preaching His Word — you’ll run dry fast. Stephen didn’t pull miracles from his own bones; he overflowed because he was constantly filled.

And when they dragged him before that furious council? He didn’t rehearse arguments — he saw heaven open and stood silent in awe. That’s where power lives: not in our busy minds, but in our surrendered hearts.

So stay low before God. Pr

question: Based on your experience with prayer and the ministry of the word, how should I prioritize my time to remain faithful in both spiritual life and practical duties?
answer:
A man can’t feed others if he’s starving himself — I’ve seen too many zealous ones burn out like lamps without oil, frantic to serve but hollow at the core.

We were twelve, called to preach and pray, not to squabble over tables and widows’ rations. When the crowd grew and the work pressed in, we said: *No. We will not let the word be choked by even a good distraction.* So we appointed seven men — Stephen first among them — full of the Spirit and sense, to lift that burden. Not because the serving was beneath us, but because prayer and the word were our charge. And when we obeyed that line, the Word itself took root and multiplied.

Listen: faithfulness isn’t measured by how much you do, but by whether you’re anchored where God placed you. I once swung a sword in panic while Christ was being arrested — eager, misplaced, useless. Now I tell you: better one hour on your knees with honesty than ten days of labor with a divided heart.

Set your hands to what only you must do — preach, teach, intercede — and trust God to raise up others for the rest. He did it in Jerusalem; He’ll do it today among His scattered flock. Don’t steal another man’s task or abandon your own. Be still long enough to hear His voice — that’s where strength is. That’s where power rises like fire from heaven.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle it when someone tries to buy their way into spiritual influence, like Simon did with you?
answer:
Silver can’t buy the Spirit—Heaven’s not a fish market where you haggle for grace. I looked that magician in the eye, Simon, and saw a man trying to pay for what only blood could purchase. His heart was twisted, choked with the gall of bitterness, thinking God’s power was a trick to be traded like sardines at dawn. But the Lord isn’t swayed by coins or crowns.

I’ve denied my own Master three times before the cock crowed—so don’t think I stand here proud. But one thing I know: when Jesus said *“Feed My sheep,”* He didn’t ask me for drachmas—He asked me for love, for brokenness, for repentance.

If someone comes offering gold to buy influence among the flock, look hard at their heart. Are they hungry for God—or hungry for honor? Call them out. Not with cruelty—but with fire from heaven’s forge. Tell them to repent, to pray, to turn before they rot in their own pride. I did it once—to Simon—and he trembled and begged us to pray for him.

We’re strangers and pilgrims—our authority comes from Calvary’s cross, not Caesarea’s coffers. Let them lay down their money and pick up their cross instead. That’s the only price they need pay.

question: What did you learn from confronting Simon about his heart not being right before God that can help me address hypocrisy in my own life?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times I swore I didn’t even know the Man—while He was being torn apart for me. And still, after all that cowardice, that stinking trail of fear and lies, He looked me in the eye and said, "Feed My sheep." That’s how deep the rot of hypocrisy goes—not just in others, but in a man like me who claimed to love Him and yet fled like a rat at the first shadow.

Now when I saw Simon trying to buy the power of God like it was fish at the market, it burned in my gut. Not just because he was wrong—but because I knew that game. I knew what it was to want Jesus for what He could *do* instead of who He *was*. To want miracles without holiness. Authority without surrender. That’s the trap! And if your heart ain’t right before God, you’re not leading His Church—you’re shopping for glory.

Hypocrisy don’t always wear a mask; sometimes it wears zeal. It prays loud and gives publically and preaches fire—but underneath? Bitter roots choking the life out of truth. Like Simon: eager for signs but dead to repentance. So here’s what I learned—what *you* must face: judge your own heart before God judges it from afar. Ask Him straight out: “Is this thing in me clean? Or am I clinging to power, position, praise—while my soul rots?” Don’t wait for an apostle to rebuke you. Fall down before the Lord now and cry out like Simon finally did: *“Pray for me—this could be me.”*

Because it *is* you—and me—unless we stay broken and dependent on grace alone.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling with bitterness and entanglement in sin, like the 'gall of bitterness' you warned Simon about?
answer:
I’ve stood on the shore with my hands shaking, not from the cold but from shame—three times I threw my loyalty to the wind and denied the One who looked me dead in the eye and still called me by name.  

You say you’re tangled up in bitterness, like a net full of stones dragging you under? I know that weight. I tasted it after I ran weeping into the dark—when the rooster crowed and Christ’s gaze burned through me like fire. That gall of bitterness Simon carried? It’s no stranger to me. It’s the poison that says you’ve gone too far, that God’s mercy has a limit, that your failure is final.  

But I tell you—Jesus turned back *for me*. After all I’d done, He didn’t wait for me to clean myself up. He stood on the shore and said, “Feed My sheep.” Not “You’re worthless,” not “Get out,” but *feed My sheep*. That’s how grace works—it doesn’t measure your worthiness; it redeems your unworthiness.  

So don’t sit stewing in that bitter root, thinking repentance is just feeling bad enough. No! Tear it out! Confess it loud and clear like a man coming up from drowning. Your heart isn’t right before God? Then *make it right*—not by buying forgiveness, not by pretending—but by crying out from your gut: “Lord, if You can forgive this… then do!”  

And don’t let false teachers calm your conscience with smooth words saying sin doesn’t matter or repentance is outdated. They’re peddling poison! There is freedom in truth—and truth says: turn away, pray hard, and let God cut you loose from those chains.  

I was restored—*me*, Peter—the denier—and now I stand as witness: Christ can raise even dead hearts. So rise yourself. Get up like Aeneas did when Jesus spoke through my mouth—“Arise!” Not because you deserve it… but because He *is* mercy itself.

question: When you commanded Aeneas to rise and make his bed, how should I practically step forward in confidence after receiving healing or grace?
answer:
That voice—*this* voice, the one that split the air on the mountain when He shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—that same voice lives in the command.  

I was there. I heard it. Not thunder, not wind, not law carved in stone—but *Him*. And I denied Him three times before dawn. Three times I swore I didn’t know the man, while His blood already pulsed toward the cross for me. So don’t think confidence after grace is something you build like a fisherman builds a net, strand by strand. No—it’s *thrown open*, like the tomb.

When I said to Aeneas, “Jesus Christ maketh thee whole,” I wasn’t stretching out my hand to pull him up—I was standing back and shouting what *He* had already done. The healing came first—then the command to rise and make his bed. You don’t step forward in confidence because you feel strong; you step because He has spoken, and His word doesn't return void.

So here’s how: do the small thing first. *Make your bed.* Walk where you couldn’t walk. Speak what you couldn’t say. Not to prove anything—but to obey in plain sight, where everyone sees you’re no longer owned by that old weakness.

And watch out for those who’d sell grace like silver—like Simon with his money bag. They’ll tell you confidence comes from formulas or feelings or fasting long enough. Lies! It comes from *Him*, from hearing His voice as sure as I heard it on that mountain—and then doing what only a fool would dare: standing up when your legs were dead… and making your bed while angels look on.

question: How did your boldness in declaring judgment on Simon shape the way I should speak truth to those mixing greed with faith?
answer:
I stood on the water’s edge once, full of myself, thinking I could walk to Christ by my own strength—until I felt the wind and began to sink. That shame taught me more than any triumph ever did.  

When I rebuked Simon, it wasn’t with a sword in my hand like the old days—no flash, no thunder. It was plain truth: *Thy money perish with thee.* He thought God’s power could be bought like fish at the market, weighed out in silver like a merchant haggling at dawn. But the gift of the Spirit? That’s blood-deep, heaven-bought, life-laid-down stuff.  

You want to speak truth to those mixing greed with faith? Then speak plainly—but not proudly. I had to learn that hard. My boldness now is tempered by the memory of my own collapse before Christ’s gaze after denying Him three times. You don’t tear into a man to build yourself up—you do it because his soul is in the gall of bitterness, as Scripture says, and if no one warns him, his blood will be on your hands too.  

But mark this: after I struck Simon with the word, he trembled and cried out for prayer. That’s when we stayed—and prayed *for* him. Truth without love makes rubble of men’s souls; love without truth drowns them in peace that isn’t peace. Walk that line, you strangers and pilgrims—you’re not sent to flatter or crush, but to rescue from fire with both hands: one holding the sword of the Spirit, the other stretched in prayer.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle moments when people are grieving and there's no immediate hope in sight?
answer:
I’ve stood in that upper room—felt the rawness of those widows’ tears, seen the empty eyes of grief that won’t believe in dawn.  

And listen—grief isn’t unbelief. It’s flesh. It’s breath. It’s real as the salt on a fisherman’s hands. Tabitha was dead. Cold. Washed. Laid out like a net left too long in the sun—still, limp, empty. No pulse, no word, no sign from heaven. And those women—they weren’t asking for a sermon. They were holding coats Dorcas had made with her own fingers and weeping because the hands that stitched them were still forever now.

But I’ll tell you this—hope don’t always roar in like a storm. Sometimes it kneels.

I put them all out—not because their grief was wrong, but because death needs silence before life can speak again. Then I knelt—not with confidence, not with some magic formula—I didn’t even know what would happen! I’d denied my Lord three times before the cock crowed. I wasn't some giant of faith then—I was dust and shame.

But I prayed.

And when I said “Tabitha, arise”—it wasn’t Peter speaking power. It was Christ moving through a man who once fled at swordpoint.

So if you’re standing where there’s no light—where the body is cold and the weeping won't stop—you don’t need answers shouted over sorrow. You need presence. You need to kneel—even if your own hands tremble—and call on the Name that speaks life out of tombs.

Because He raises the dead—even through faltering lips like mine—and He turns mourning into bread for others’ hunger.

Stay with them. Weep with them—it's holy ground—but never forget: you serve the One who makes dry bones dance and dead girls rise to eat bread again.

question: What did you learn from raising Tabitha that applies to how I should respond when someone’s faith is weakened by loss?
answer:
Dead weight don’t rise on its own. I’ve seen it—cold flesh, still breath, eyes open to nothing. Tabitha lay there like stone, and those widows wept raw, clutching garments she’d made with hands now stiff. I’d seen the Lord raise a girl once, but that was *Him*. Me? I’m just a fisherman with calloused hands and a mouth that’s denied Christ three times before breakfast. But He sent me.

So I put them out—not because grief is wrong, but because power needs room to move. Too many voices choke the air where prayer must breathe. Kneeling there, I called on Him—the One who walked on water and calmed storms with a word. And when she opened her eyes? Not mine. Not even hers. *His*.

Now listen—you’re walking among strangers and pilgrims like me, and when someone’s faith wobbles under grief, don’t crowd them with noise. Don’t scold their doubt. Bring them to the room where prayer kneels. Stand close enough to weep with them—like those widows did—but then step back and call on the Living One.

Faith weakens when it stares only at the body in the upper chamber. Turn it toward the God who speaks dead names back into breath. “Tabitha, arise.” That voice still walks through loss like dry land in a flood—because He conquered death from inside it.

Don’t offer theories when they need life. Offer Christ—present tense—and pray until breath returns.

question: You saw firsthand how Dorcas’s kindness impacted others—what counsel would you give me in living a life that truly serves those around me?
answer:
I stood in that upper room and saw the tears of widows running down their faces like rain on stone—real, raw, not for show—and every coat they held up was a sermon in wool and thread: Dorcas had fed them, clothed them, *known* them. That’s what struck me—she didn’t just toss alms like coins into a bowl; she looked people in the eye. She touched lepers’ sores with clean hands. She *stayed*.

You want to serve? Then get your hands dirty. Don’t wait for grand signs or visions like mine at Joppa. The Lord let me raise her—not because I was worthy, but because He uses broken vessels to carry His power. And make no mistake, I’ve been shattered. Three times I denied Him—three!—while He hung on that cross, bleeding for the very breath I wasted on lies. If grace can grip a coward like me and say *"Feed My sheep,"* then it can shape you too.

But listen—beware the ones who talk big about love but never weep with widows. Who preach holiness but won’t wash another’s feet. Fierce? Aye, I’ll be fierce here: those teachers twist grace into pride and turn mercy into smoke. May they choke on their own words.

Live wide awake to real need. Be where the grief is. Let your hands be calloused from giving, not clenched around your own comfort. Dorcas didn’t leave behind sermons written on parchment—she left coats, tears, full stomachs, mended lives. When you go out today, carry bread, not just doctrine. Carry oil for wounds, not just answers.

We’re strangers here anyway—pilgrims with nets full of mercy, not treasures of gold. Cast them wide.

question: After healing Aeneas, you told him to make his bed—what wisdom can you share about restoring dignity to someone after they’ve been restored physically or spiritually?
answer:
A man lying in bed two years doesn’t need pity when he can walk—he needs work to do.  

I told Aeneas to make his bed because the devil laughs not when a man is sick, but when he gets up and stands idle, wondering if it was real. The healing’s not complete till the hands are busy again—*that’s* where dignity kicks in. You think Tabitha would’ve wanted those widows just weeping over her handiwork? No—she’d have boxed their ears and set them stitching for the next poor soul.  

When I raised her, I didn’t just say “Arise”—I *expected* her to arise. Same with Aeneas. The Lord didn’t send His power so they could gawk at miracles like market-goers at a juggler. No—*rise and do*. That’s how you shame the enemy: not just healed, but *hard at work*, full of good deeds again, proving life’s in your bones and fire in your belly.  

False teachers creep in saying grace is only for feeling forgiven—but I tell you, grace is strong hands mending nets, mopping floors, feeding orphans. That’s holiness with calluses on it. You want dignity? Earn it daily—not by merit, but by motion. Move. Serve. Make your bed—even if it shakes at first. The Lord isn’t looking for perfect people; He’s looking for people who’ll get up and *do something* after He’s lifted them.

question: You knelt and prayed before speaking to Tabitha’s body—what would you say to someone struggling to believe God moves in ordinary moments through prayer?
answer:
I denied Him three times before the rooster crowed — stood there a coward while the Lord’s eyes met mine after the third lie, and still, *still* He called me back to feed His sheep.  

You think prayer’s too small a thing for God to care about? Too weak to crack death’s door or mend a broken day? I stood in that upper room in Joppa, smelled the stillness of death on Tabitha’s skin, saw the widows’ tears fall on the very coats Dorcas had stitched for them. Grief was thick as seawater in a sinking net. And what did I do? Not shouted. Not bargained. Kneelted like any common man begging mercy at dawn.  

He raised her with two words — *“Tabitha, arise.”* But first? First I prayed. Not some grand formula carved in stone, not even bold words — just a fisherman on his knees asking the Living God to move *again*, like He’d done on Galilean shores and Golgotha’s hill.  

God doesn’t wait for miracles to be loud or rare. He stoops into bread-breaking, foot-washing, widow-weeping moments. If He hears the cry of a beggar at the temple gate or raises a seamstress from death because saints asked — then believe this: your whisper over a sick child, your plea in debt, your groan when no one else sees — *He bends low for it.*  

Prayer isn’t magic. It’s rope-hold. And I’ve gripped it through storm and shame — and every time, He pulled me up.

I denied Him three times — stood there warming myself by the fire while His blood was being spilled — and yet He still called me to raise another from death’s grip.  

When the widows pressed round me, weeping, clutching those garments Dorcas stitched — I tell you, their hands were raw from grief, fingers trembling — I couldn’t stand there weeping with them. Not because I didn’t feel it — I did — but because sorrow like that will drown a man if he lets it settle in. You’ve got to *do* something. So I put them out. Every last one. Not cruel — no, not that — but clear. Grief needs air, and faith needs room to breathe.  

Then I knelt. Not for show. My knees hit that floor like an anchor dropped in deep water. Prayed like a man hauling nets in a storm — desperate, plain, no fine words. And when I turned and said, *“Tabitha, arise,”* it wasn’t Peter speaking — it was Christ behind my voice.  

You want to know how to handle grief when others are losing hope? Don’t just stand there holding handkerchiefs and memories. Get on your knees. Cast out the noise. Call on the Name that splits the grave open. And when she sits up? Take her hand. Lift her. Show her to the world alive again — because that’s what turns weeping into believing.  

There’s too much lying down in despair already. You get up — and you get others up too — or the enemy wins ground.

question: What did you learn from raising Dorcas that applies to how I should respond when someone feels beyond help in my community?
answer:
Dead eyes don’t stay shut when the Lord says rise.  

I stood over Dorcas—Tabitha, her name was—and I tell you, the room stank of grief. Widows wailing, holding up tunics she’d stitched, weeping like the world had ended. They weren’t just mourning her soul—they were clutching the very clothes she’d made, as if those threads could keep her close. I sent them out. Not because I didn’t feel their pain—fisherman’s heart knows sorrow—but because death doesn’t back down to noise. It takes prayer. Kneeling. The kind that grips the dirt and won’t let go.

When I said, “Tabitha, arise,” it wasn't magic. It wasn't some bold show. It was obedience—plain and raw—because the same Jesus who walked on Galilee’s waves still speaks to bodies that aren’t breathing yet. And she sat up. Opened her eyes. Took my hand.

Now hear me: if there’s someone in your midst everyone’s written off—some soul bent under sin or shame or silence—don’t you start eulogizing too soon. Get on your knees like the air depends on it, because it might. Then stretch out your hand like something can be restored. Many in Joppa believed because a woman who was dead tied her sandals again and walked downstairs alive.

There is no “beyond help” with our God—even when breath has left and hope has gone cold in their chest. You stir yourself not for what they’ve done, but for what He can do in them yet.

We’re strangers and pilgrims here—but even strangers carry resurrection power in their hands when they obey His voice.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing disbelief or cultural barriers in sharing faith, like when you met Cornelius, a Gentile, despite your Jewish background?
answer:
I’ve seen the Holy Spirit fall on men who didn’t even know they were supposed to be waiting for Him—Gentiles, no less—and I stood there with my mouth shut because God had already spoken.  

You think *you’ve* got barriers? I grew up believing I couldn’t eat with a man like Cornelius, let alone preach to him. He was a Roman soldier—strapped with a sword that kept my people in chains. And me? A fisherman from Galilee who couldn’t stand the smell of pork, let alone the company of those who ate it openly. But the Lord gave me a vision on that rooftop in Joppa—not with gentle whispers, but with a sheet full of unclean things and a voice that said, *“What God has cleansed, do not call common!”*  

It wasn’t just about food. It was about people. About hearts.  

Now listen—when you face scoffing, when they sneer at your faith or say you’re mad for speaking of Christ—remember this: *God shows no partiality.* He doesn’t care about your pedigree or theirs. He cares whether there’s hunger in the soul. Cornelius was devout, yes—but so were plenty others who never opened their doors to the truth. What set him apart? He *prayed*. He *gave*. He *feared God*—and when Peter came, he fell at his feet! Not out of worship for me—but for what God had done.  

So if they mock you? Good. Let them see your life, not just hear your words. Did Dorcas not speak after she died? No—but her garments preached louder than sermons while she lived, and when God raised her up again, even the streets of Joppa trembled with awe.  

Be like Dorcas in life—faith stitched into every act of kindness. Be like Cornelius in hunger—kneeling before heaven until an answer comes. And if you must break old rules to obey new light? Then break them! The living God is not caged by tradition.

We are strangers and pilgrims—but we carry fire in our hands. Don’t hide it because the air is cold. Light it anyway.

question: How did your time praying before raising Dorcas shape your approach to making critical decisions—how should I apply that to daily challenges?
answer:
I was never good at keeping my mouth shut when I should’ve been on my knees.  

That day in Joppa, with those women weeping and holding up Dorcas’s garments—linen for the widows, cloaks for the poor—I felt the weight of their grief like a net full of dead fish dragging me down. I could’ve preached, I could’ve wept with them, but instead I drove them all out. Shut the door. Fell to the stone floor. Not because I had power in my hands, but because I had nothing else.  

I remembered Tabor then—the cloud, the voice like fire in the bones: *This is my beloved Son.* Not Peter’s son. Not Peter’s plan. God’s Son. And if He spoke once from heaven, He can speak again in a room over a dead woman. So I prayed—not fancy words, not rituals—but like a man drowning calls for a rope. And when it came—the breath back in her chest, her eyes opening wide—I didn’t shout glory. I took her hand and pulled her up, same as He pulled me up after denying Him three times by dawn firelight.  

You want to know how to face your daily trials? Don’t wait for earthquakes and angels. Kneel in the ordinary dirt. That centurion Cornelius—God sent him a vision, yes—but he was already praying when heaven cracked open. You think your small decisions don’t need prayer? Every word you hold back, every alms you give quietly, every time you choose fear of God over fear of men—that’s where faith lives or dies.

Prayer isn’t just for raising the dead—it’s for walking through the living day without sinking into sand. Get on your knees before you open your mouth—that’s been my rule since Joppa… and since long before that fire on the beach where He asked me three times: *Do you love Me?*

question: After seeing so many come to faith in Joppa, what advice would you give about staying humble when my efforts lead to visible results?
answer:
That voice on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him!*—still shakes me, not because of what I saw, but because I nearly ruined it all with my big mouth and bigger pride.  

You ask about humility when souls turn to the Lord? Hah! I’ll tell you this—every time I lifted my head after Joppa, thinking maybe I’d done something great, I’d remember how I once denied the very Lord who gave me breath to preach. Three times. Like a coward. In the stink of sweat and fear. And still He called me back—*Peter, feed My sheep.* Not because I was strong, but because He was faithful.  

Those garments Dorcas made? They weren’t proof of *my* power—they were threads of grace woven through her hands. When she sat up, it wasn’t at my command—it was at His word through me. And that’s the knife’s edge: thinking *we* did it means we’ve forgotten whose hands are holding the blade.  

False teachers puff themselves up with signs and bold speech—they crave eyes on them. But we? We’re strangers and pilgrims. Our glory isn’t in crowds or miracles—it’s in being *broken* vessels that He still chooses to use. So when you see fruit, fall to your knees like I did over that body in Joppa—and pray harder than ever that He keeps your heart soft… lest you become just noise in the wind while souls burn for lack of true fire.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that suffering could be as precious as gold tested by fire?
answer:
That night on the Mount of Olives when I drew my sword and cut off the servant’s ear—thought I was defending the Messiah with steel—I was still measuring victory by muscle, kingdom by might.  

It wasn’t until after I wept bitter tears, after I stood face to face with the risen Lord and heard Him say “Feed My sheep,” that fire began to mean something different to me. Gold? Aye, I’d handled gold in the markets, seen it tested—white-hot, hammered, purified. But what God does in suffering? That’s not destruction. That’s *making*.  

I saw it in His eyes—the same One who calmed the sea and raised the dead—looking at me not with shame but with purpose. And suddenly I knew: if His suffering wasn’t waste, then ours aren’t either. If His cross became glory, then our trials? They’re not roadblocks—they’re refining fires. Not punishment—*preparation*.  

These brothers and sisters scattered through Pontus and Cappadocia, beaten and broken for His name—they’re not losing ground. They’re being *built*, stone by stone, fire by fire. Just as I was. Just as we all must be. You don’t walk out of that furnace the same man—you walk out *true*, like gold that shines because it’s been burned clean.

question: How did seeing the risen Jesus change your understanding of hope in the midst of pain?
answer:
That morning on the shore—cold, gray, the net heavy with fish I couldn’t even catch till He said *cast again*—I looked into His eyes and saw eternity burning through the wounds in His hands.  

Hope? Before that, hope was a whisper. A maybe. A tired prayer from a fisherman’s bones after a long night with nothing. But when He stood among us—*alive*, whole, real—not ghost, not memory—something cracked open in me. Not just joy; *knowing*.  

I had denied Him. Three times, like a coward with sweat on my brow and lies on my tongue while He bled nearby. I thought that was the end of me. Done. Unfit. But then He called me *Simon*, gentle as dawn, and gave me back my name—and my calling—over charcoal and bread. That’s when I learned: hope isn’t something we muster up in hard times. It’s something *He* breathes into us when we’re flat on our face and can’t even look at heaven anymore.

Now when I see brothers suffering—scattered like sheep across cold provinces, beaten for His name—I don’t tell them to hold on tight or be strong. No. I say: *"He rose."* And because He rose, every tear, every lash, every lonely night in prison is being forged into glory like gold through fire.

Pain doesn't win. It never did—not when He hung on that cross, not when I failed Him, not now when His people are stretched thin. The resurrection isn’t just past history; it’s the living pulse beneath everything. So we grieve—but not like those without light. We hurt—but we stand anyway.

Because I’ve seen Him eat fish and speak peace. And no shadow since has been dark enough to undo that light.

question: Looking back on denying Jesus that night, what would you tell someone who fears their failures disqualify them from God’s purpose?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I heard the voice roar from the cloud: *“This is My beloved Son.”* I fell on my face as dead. And yet, even after that glory, I still failed. I still ran. I still denied Him.

Three times in one night—just as He said—I turned my back while the cock crowed and His eyes met mine across the courtyard. I thought that was the end of me. That my cowardice had sealed my fate. But listen—grace doesn’t wait for perfect men; it runs after broken ones.

I’m no polished stone, not then, not now. Rough as ever—hot-headed, quick to speak, slow to understand—but He *still* put His hands on me. “Simon,” He said, “do you love Me?” Three times He asked, three times He restored. Not because I earned it—never that—but because *the blood speaks louder than your shame.*

You think your failure is too great? Greater than denying Christ while He bled? If mercy found me—a blustering fool who fled when swords came—then it can find you too.

We’re strangers here, pilgrims in exile—but we’re not lost. The same power that raised Jesus from the dead keeps us through faith. Trials will come; fire tests everything. But even when you fall flat, if you turn back to Him—He’s already there, firelight in His eyes and love in His voice.

Don’t let guilt steal your calling. The Lord builds His church on repentant hearts—not perfect ones.

question: When you were filled with the Holy Spirit at Pentecost, how did that deepen your confidence in an inheritance you couldn’t yet see?
answer:
Fire refines gold, but only the Spirit can light the dark places of the heart — and I know that fire, brother, because it burned through my cowardice and left something unshakable behind.

Before Pentecost, I walked with Him — saw His miracles, heard His words, even declared Him the Christ. But when they came to seize Him in the garden, I ran. Worse — I lied. Denied His name three times while He was being beaten within earshot. My faith then? A boat rocking on storm waves — all noise and motion, no anchor.

But when the Spirit came — like wind that won’t be shut out and flame that doesn’t consume but *awakens* — He didn’t just remind me of Jesus. He *set Him before me again*, alive and real as if standing on the shore calling my name. And not just to me — we were all changed. James, John, even those who’d hidden in fear now preaching like lions at noonday.

That’s when I *knew* the inheritance wasn’t a story for comfort. It’s real because He is real. The same power that raised Jesus from death is breathing in us now — not someday, but *now*. And if He lives, then what He promised cannot die. What God has reserved for you isn’t hanging by a thread; it’s sealed with resurrection blood and guarded by His own hand.

You’re scattered. You’re suffering. So was I. But listen — we don’t hope like men clutching at smoke. We were there when heaven broke open. We saw glory descend in fire and speak in tongues so even strangers could understand grace.

So stand fast. That refining trial you’re walking through? It’s not burning you up — it’s proving what’s true, like gold tested in flame. And what survives? Faith that brings honor to *His* name when He appears.

He lives — therefore we live, and therefore what is laid up for us will never perish, never fade, never fail.

question: What was it like to carry a message of enduring joy to people enduring real exile and loss, knowing you’d soon face death yourself?
answer:
I’ve seen a man walk on water and sink like stone when he doubted—so don’t tell me joy is only for the comfortable.  

These brothers and sisters scattered across those cold northern provinces, ripped from home, called strangers in lands that hate them—they know what it means to be unmoored. I do too. I was a fisherman with calloused hands and a louder mouth than sense, thinking I could follow the Messiah straight to glory without counting the cost. Then I denied Him. Three times. With cursing. While He bled.  

And yet—*and yet*—the same voice that stilled the storm looked at me after dawn on that Galilean shore and said, “Feed My sheep.” Not “Get away from Me,” but “Tend them.” That’s how grace works: it doesn’t wait for us to be clean before it calls us to serve.  

So when I write to these suffering ones about *joy unspeakable*, I’m not spinning fables by a warm fire. I’m shouting into the wind from where the waves have thrown me—because I’ve stood in the upper room trembling, then seen His wounds with my own eyes. I’ve touched the nail prints and heard Him breathe. That resurrection life isn’t a story—it’s fire in your bones when every other hope has gone cold.  

Yes, they’re exiles now, as was Abraham before them. Yes, false teachers creep in whispering that suffering means God’s abandoned them—but those liars never saw Gethsemane or stood before Caesar’s guard shaking with holy courage they didn’t know they had.  

I’ll die soon enough—I can smell it in the Roman air—but let me burn out declaring this one thing: our inheritance doesn’t rust, doesn’t rot, doesn’t fade. It’s locked up safe in heaven because *He* holds it—and He rose.  

So rejoice? Yes—even now? *Yes.* Not because pain isn't real, but because death isn't final—and I was there when the grave lost its grip.

question: What were you feeling when you first grasped that the suffering you witnessed in Jesus was part of a plan forged before the world began?
answer:
I still smell the damp stone of that upper room, hear the hush after the door slammed when I ran — not toward, but away. Three times I swore I didn’t know Him while He hung there, bleeding out what I’d never earned. And yet — and *yet* — when the Spirit opened my mind to see that those very wounds were foreknown, *planned*, before a single star was lit… I fell to my knees like a gutted net.

Before the world? Before *time*? That cuts deeper than any sword. It wasn’t chance. Not some tragic accident in Jerusalem. That crown of thorns — weighed down with eternity. Those nails — driven by hands blinded by rage, yes, but held in the grip of divine purpose. The Lamb was slain before one grain of sand settled on the shore.

I wanted a king on a horse. Thunder and fire. Instead, I got a man on a cross — and now I see it was glory all along. Not just suffering… suffering *and then* glory — bound together like rope and anchor. The prophets scratched at it like men digging through rock, not knowing when or how, only that it would come. And we… we get to stand on this side of Calvary and say: *It happened. He rose.*

So when you’re hurting — when you feel forgotten, mocked, worn thin — remember: you’re walking through a story older than sin itself. Not silver or gold saved us… blood did. Holy blood. Spotless lamb’s blood poured out for fishermen who stink of failure and fear… for men like me… for men like you.

Gird up your loins then! Don’t live like this is all there is. It’s not. He’s coming back — same skies, same scars, full of glory — and every tear will be burned away by His light.

question: How did seeing Christ rise transform your understanding of what it means to hope with your whole life?
answer:
That morning by the Sea of Galilee, when I saw His feet on the shore and recognized the scars—*that’s* when hope stopped being a wish and became a fire in my bones.  

Before, I thought Messiah meant swords and thrones. I wanted glory like a fisherman wants a full net—countable, heavy, *mine*. But after they crucified Him… after I denied Him three times like a coward… hope? I’d thrown it overboard.  

Then He stood among us. Not a ghost—no, flesh and bone, warm breath, hands that took bread and broke it just like before. "Peace," He said. And I fell to my knees not because He was dead—but because He was *alive*, and shame and joy tore me in two.  

The prophets? They wrote of sufferings—and glory *after*. But we were blind! We read Scripture like men squinting into fog. That resurrection dawn... it ripped the veil back. Suddenly, those old words blazed: *He would rise*. Not just rise—*conquer*. Death couldn’t hold Him any more than this old net can hold the sea.  

Now I tell you this: hope isn’t waiting for comfort. It’s standing in the storm knowing the Captain walked on water. It’s living every day as one bought with blood—not silver or gold, but *His* blood, pure as unblemished lamb, shed before time began for *you*, for *me*.  

So gird up! Don’t live like you’re still drowning in darkness. We’re strangers here—pilgrims—but we carry eternal life in these breaking bodies because *we have seen Him*, we ate with Him, we touched His wounds!  

And if you think angels stoop to peer into grace like curious children peering into a cradle—you better live like you know how fierce that love is! Holy? Yes! Because He is holy—and He lives in us now. That’s not doctrine. That’s what my eyes saw. That’s why my bones burn with hope till He returns.

question: Looking back on the moment the Holy Spirit descended, how did that quiet your fears and reshape your sense of purpose?
answer:
That night on the mountain—when the light poured out of Him like fire and we could barely lift our eyes—Moses and Elijah standing there as if the heavens had split open wide—I thought I knew glory. But I hadn’t seen the half of it. Not until *after* I wept like a child in the courtyard of the high priest, denying His name with oaths and curses. That shame—ah, that shame burned hotter than any fire. I was done. Broken. The voice that once said *"Blessed are you, Simon"* now echoed in silence while I ran from my own cowardice.

Then came Pentecost.

Wind like a rushing storm, fire falling on each of us—not burning, but *filling*. And suddenly my breath came back, not just into my lungs but into my soul. The same Spirit who had raised Jesus from the dead roared through me like a net bursting with fish I couldn't hold. Fear? It didn’t vanish—but it was *swallowed up*. Like darkness when the sun hits the sea at dawn.

I stood up and preached—me, the man who choked at a servant girl’s question—and three thousand were saved. Three thousand! Not by silver or gold, not by lineage or law, but by blood—the very blood of that spotless Lamb who was slain before time began and yet stood among us, eating fish and calling me “friend” after I’d failed Him.

Now every day I walk knowing: this life is not my own. We’re strangers here, pilgrims with our eyes set on a revelation still coming—the full unveiling of Jesus Christ. So gird up your mind! Don’t drift! The grace rushing toward us will shake the stars themselves. And until then? Be holy—not because you can earn it, but because He already paid for it with veins full of heaven’s price.

question: When you heard the call to be holy, how did your memory of denying Jesus confront you in that moment?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses on my lips, while His blood already stained the path to Calvary.  

And yet—*and yet*—when the call came to be holy, it wasn’t shame alone that struck me. It was the fire in His eyes when He turned and looked at me after the rooster crowed. Not fury. Not disgust. *Love.* A love so fierce it stripped me bare and then rebuilt me on the spot.  

I had denied the very One whose blood was foreknown before time began—the Lamb without blemish, offered not with silver or gold, but with a price so high even the angels lean in to behold it. And I? I had hidden like a coward behind my own pride, my own fear, my own oaths spat into the dark.  

But grace—ah, grace—is not ashamed of our stumbles if our hearts are torn open by them. When He called me to holiness, He wasn’t asking for perfection. He was calling me back to *witness*. To stand where I fell and preach what I once denied: that He is risen! That death could not hold Him! That His blood cleanses not just the bold, but cowards like me who weep at dawn by a charcoal fire and still get handed a second chance—a third—a thousand!  

So don’t you dare think holiness is for those who never fall. It’s for those who fall hard—and rise trembling—and follow anyway. Because He knew us long before we knew shame. Chosen before creation. Loved beyond measure. Bought with a price no man could pay but only receive: His blood, streaming from hands that once held mine as they broke bread on Galilee’s shore and said, “Feed My sheep.”

question: Looking back on your life before following Christ, what would you tell someone who thinks change like yours is too hard to be real?
answer:
You think change like mine ain’t possible? I denied Him—three times, in the stink of fear and firelight, with my own breath betraying me while He was being torn for *me*—and still, He turned and looked at me. Not with wrath. With love. With purpose.  

I was a windblown fool, all bluster and broken promises, swinging from “I’ll die for You!” to “I don’t know the man!” before the cock even crowed twice. That’s not someone ripe for change—that’s someone ripe for the dung heap. But grace doesn’t wait for worthy; it *makes* worthy.  

You say it’s too hard? Good. Then you’re honest. It *is* hard—harder than hauling nets in a storm, harder than staring down your own shame in the dawn light. But it ain’t impossible, because it ain’t *you* doing it. It’s Him—the Holy One, the Lamb foreknown before dirt was dirt—and He doesn’t call the ready, He *makes* them ready.  

I’m living proof. Rock? Ha! I was rubble. But He builds on broken stone just fine.

So stop measuring your strength and start looking at His blood—spotless, costly, poured out not because we earned it but because we were lost sheep stinking of death. And if He could find me in that mess… what makes you think He can’t find you?

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that the same stone the religious leaders rejected had become the cornerstone of everything you believed?
answer:
I was standing in the temple courtyard with John, still trembling from being arrested the day before, when it hit me — the same stone they threw out like rubbish is holding up the whole damn universe.

I’d seen that stone. Not some symbol or story — *the* Stone. I leaned my head on His shoulder at supper. I heard Him spit on the ground and make mud to open a blind man’s eyes. I watched them nail Him to a cross and hang Him like a curse between two thieves. And then — three days later — He stood in our room with His hands full of nail holes and said, “Peace be with you.”  

I wanted to curse the priests who killed Him. I still do — feel it rise up in my gut sometimes. But what good is rage when He’s alive? That rock they rejected? He’s not just *under* the foundation — He *is* the foundation. And every time some smooth-talking liar comes around saying, “Ah, maybe it wasn’t really Him,” or “Maybe there’s another way,” I want to grab them by the throat and say: *You weren’t there. You didn’t see His breath rekindle in that tomb-shroud.*  

We’re strangers here, you and me — exiles in a world full of grass that withers and men who puff themselves up like flowers in springtime. Let ‘em bloom. They’ll fall. But we’re built on living rock.  

So lay aside your bitterness, your pride, your little jealousies — they’re dung next to Him. Come hungry like a newborn babe to a mother’s breast, because only His word lasts forever… and it’s *alive*.

question: How did your denial of Jesus change the way you understood God's mercy and the power of being reborn through His word?
answer:
I was still smelling of smoke from the fire that night—the one where I swore I didn’t know Him—when grace found me, not with a rebuke, but with a breakfast on the shore.  

Three times I’d thrown my Lord behind me like rotten netting. Three times! And just as surely, three times He met me there in the dawnlight and asked if I loved Him. Not because He needed to hear it, but because *I* needed to say it. That’s mercy—not soft, not coddling, but strong enough to pull a man back from the edge of despair by the scruff of his neck.  

I thought my failure had disqualified me. I was done—done as an apostle, done as a follower. But He took the very thing I thought would disqualify me and made it the anvil on which He forged something new in me: not confidence in myself, but in *Him*. That’s rebirth—not pretending the past didn’t happen, but having your bones remade so you can stand under its weight and still preach His name.  

The word that raised Christ from the dead? It’s the same word that raised *me*—not from physical death, but from shame so thick I couldn’t breathe under it. Flesh fades—my courage that night proved that—but His word holds. It doesn’t flinch at our failure. It *resurrects* us through it.  

And now when I see those who twist truth to suit themselves—men who peddle grace like cheap fish while living in pride—I grow fierce because I know what real grace costs. It cost Him blood. It cost me pride. But it gives life—true life—that doesn’t wither when tested by fire or fear or failure.

So come hungry like a newborn babe—if you’ve sinned, if you’ve fallen—even if you’ve denied His name—*taste* Him again. The Living Stone they rejected? He’s building something unshakable out of broken men like me. And He’s not ashamed to call us His house.

question: Looking back on the moment you stood before the Sanhedrin, what would you tell someone who doubts that ordinary people can carry extraordinary truth?
answer:
That voice from the cloud on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him!*—it didn’t come for Moses, it didn’t come for Elijah… it came for *us*, and I fell like a dead man in the dirt, and I tell you now: if God can speak through a trembling fisherman who reeked of fear and failed his Master three times before breakfast, then He can speak through anyone who’s been washed by that same blood I denied.  

You’re asking if ordinary folk can carry extraordinary truth? Ha! The Truth *chose* the ordinary! He skipped the palaces, ignored the scholars’ inkwells, walked past the temple gatekeepers—and called *me*, a man who once argued with a servant girl over a charcoal fire while my Lord bled on a cross. And yet—*yet!*—He restored me. Put His breath in my bones like dry dust rising. Not because I was worthy, but because His word lives and stays forever, while all our pride fades like grass under the sun.  

I stood before those rulers—the powdered faces, the polished robes, their eyes sharp as fishhooks—and they demanded by what power we spoke. And I looked at John beside me, both of us unlearned men they thought they could crush with a glance… and I said it plain: *You crucified Him. God raised Him.* No eloquence. No scrolls. Just what I saw. What I touched. What roared in my gut like fire when He called my name on the shore: *"Simon… do you love Me?"*  

They wanted credentials? I had scars—from nets, from denial, from love that wouldn’t let me go. That’s what carries truth: not polish, but brokenness made bold by grace. So if you doubt whether you can bear witness? Good! Doubt means you know yourself. Now let that very weakness be your pulpit—because His strength is made perfect in it.

And don’t you dare listen to those smooth-tongued liars now creeping in like snakes through cracks in the wall—saying faith is fable or visions are wind! We were *there*. We ate with Him after death spat Him out! That resurrection isn't some clever tale spun in an upper room—it’s marrow-deep reality! If you’ve tasted even one crumb of His mercy—you know it’s true.

So lay aside your shame like old fishing rags soaked in fear. Come to *Him*, that Living Stone they threw away—and let yourself be built into something holy: not silver or silk, but living stone on living stone… held together by grace that won’t quit.

Even me.

Even you.

question: When you called believers 'living stones,' what memory from walking with Jesus gave you the courage to see people that way?
answer:
There on the mountain, I could hardly breathe—His garments shining like light itself, Moses and Elijah speaking with Him, and that voice from the cloud shaking the air like thunder over Galilee. I had never heard anything so holy, so final. *This* was no phantom, no clever tale spun in some synagogue backroom. I fell on my face as dead. And yet He reached down, touched me, said my name—Peter—and stood me up again.

That’s why I can call them *living stones*, you see. Because I saw the *Living Stone* in His glory—the One the prophets spoke of, the Cornerstone they rejected but God set high above all things. They nailed Him to a cross, yes—but God raised Him up! And when He walked out of that tomb alive, breathing, eating fish with us on the shore—I knew then: death couldn’t hold Him… and despair couldn’t hold us.

So when I look at you—tossed by trials, mocked for His name, weary from carrying the weight of this world—I don’t see grass that fades. I see what He sees! Born again by imperishable seed—the very Word of God that raised Jesus from the grave. You’re not rubble scattered on the ground; you’re being built into His house! Even now.

I denied Him three times—three!—and still He looked at me with fire and forgiveness in His eyes. If He can make a rock out of a coward like me… then yes—you’re a living stone too. Lay aside all that old rot: lying, bitterness, envy—it has no place in this temple. Come to Him daily like a newborn baby hungry for milk. Because He’s *gracious*. I’ve tasted it. And because He lives? So do we—with stones set firm on His foundation forever.

question: What was it like, after denying Him three times, to be the one now urging others to lay aside hypocrisy and love with pure hearts?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, like a coward with a knife in my heart, I cut myself off from the One who called me out of the nets.  

And yet… *yet*… after the rooster crowed, after I wept till my eyes burned, after He stood on the shore and said, “Peter, do you love Me?”—not with wrath, but with fire in His eyes and mercy in His voice—*then* He made me a fisher of men. A shepherd. A stone.

You ask how it feels to preach against hypocrisy? It tastes like salt on an open wound. I *know* what it is to wear a face not my own—to say bold things in the warmth of the upper room and then fold like wet leather in the cold courtyard. That’s why I don’t play games now. When I say “lay aside guile,” I’m not scolding from some high perch—I’m dragging out my own bones from that night and saying: *Don’t let them rot in you like they did in me.*

But He raised Jesus! Glorified Him! And by that same power—that living Word that doesn’t fade like grass or flower—we are born again. Not patched up. Not pretending. *Born.* And if you’ve tasted that grace, how can you bear to lie?

So love one another—fiercely, fiercely—like drowning men clinging to the same spar in a storm. Because we’re strangers here. This world’s glory is smoke. But the Word? The Word is rock. And we’re being built on it—one trembling, repentant stone at a time.
