question: What were you feeling when you first realized that the stone the builders rejected had become the cornerstone of your life?
answer:
I was there when He stood before the council, silent as a lamb, and I remembered the stone they heaved aside in scorn — same stone now holding up the sky.  

Foolish me, I once thought strength was in the sword, in standing tall with James and John at His side. I even drew blood trying to defend Him that night — remember? Cut off Malchus’ ear like a hot-headed fool. But He touched the man’s ear back together and said, *"Put the sword away."* That’s when I began to see: the cornerstone wasn’t carved by human hands or lifted by human might. It was laid in weakness, in shame, in silence — and then God raised it high.  

I denied Him three times. Three times! While He stood alone before Pilate and Herod, while the crowds screamed “Crucify!” — I shrank back into the smoke of a charcoal fire, trembling at a servant girl’s voice. Shame burned hotter than that fire ever could. But after He rose — after He looked me in the eyes on the shore and asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?” — He didn’t rebuild me with my old pride. No. He made me a living stone in a temple not made with hands.

That rejected stone? It crushed my arrogance flat — then raised me up. Now I tell you plainly: there’s no foundation under heaven for a man’s soul but that one Stone. The world stumbles over Him still — mocks His name, twists His truth — but we who’ve been called out of darkness? We stand. Not because we’re strong, but because *He* is laid down firm forever.

So walk like you belong to Him — strangers here, yes, but kings and priests by His mercy. Let your life be so clean among those who hate you that their lies choke on what they see: good deeds shining like firelight in this black age.

question: How did your own failure—denying Jesus three times—reshape your understanding of mercy and belonging to a people who 'had not obtained mercy'?
answer:
That voice from the cloud on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—it burned into me, and I couldn’t unhear it even when I ran from His face in the courtyard.  

I had swung a sword like a fool at Malchus, thinking I’d defend the King with steel. But within hours, a servant girl’s question cut deeper than any blade. Three times I flinched. Three times I lied. And when the rooster crowed and He turned—*looked straight through me*—I saw not rage, not rejection, but mercy fixed upon me like a nail.  

I had not obtained mercy—I’d thrown it back like a cursed thing. Yet there it was. Not earned. Not deserved. *Given.*  

So when I say “ye had not obtained mercy,” don’t think it’s some far-off phrase to me. I was *that man*, reeking of fear and shame, weeping where the dust choked my breath. And now? Now I am called *rock*. Not because I stood firm—but because *He* did. He stood firm for me. For all of us who crumble.

We are strangers and pilgrims, yes—but we belong to Him who rebuilt me from that wreckage. And if you’ve ever run, ever denied Him in your heart or speech, know this: the same eyes that saw my failure see you now—and still call you by name.

question: Looking back on your years following Jesus, how did the memory of being an outsider, a fisherman with rough hands, shape your conviction that God calls the unexpected into a royal priesthood?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know him—three times, with cursing and oaths, like a common thief denying his own brother—while the Lord was being led like a lamb to slaughter.  

And yet… *He* called *me*. Not Caiaphas in his robes, not the scribes with their scrolls and polished words—but Peter, son of Jonah, whose hands reeked of fish guts and whose mouth had just spat lies in the courtyard firelight. Me! He looked at me after the rooster crowed—I’ll never forget it—his eyes weren’t angry. They were broken. And in that look, I died.  

That’s why I know—*know* down in my bones—that this calling isn’t for the polished or the proud. It’s for the ragged, the wrecked, the ones who’ve fallen flat on their faces and still dare to crawl forward. When He said “upon this rock,” He wasn’t talking about some mighty temple pillar—He meant *this*, a flawed man shaped by mercy, not merit.  

We were no people. We were nobodies—Galileans with thick tongues and rough hands, fishermen who couldn’t quote the Law like scholars. But He made us priests? A royal priesthood? Not because we climbed up, but because He stooped so low to lift us.  

I saw His glory on the mountain—I heard that voice from heaven—and still I bungled everything after! That’s grace! That’s why I shout it: *the very stone they rejected is now head of the corner!* The rulers thought they could silence truth by nailing it to a cross—but God raised Him up! And if He can make a cornerstone out of what men discard, then every outsider called by name becomes a living stone in His temple.  

So let them sneer at our lack of pedigree. Let them mock our pasts. We were darkness once—and now we are light! And our lives? They’re meant to burn so bright that even our enemies see and give glory to God when He comes near.

question: When you were face to face with Roman authorities, how did you hold together being both free in Christ and yet submitting to human rulers for the Lord's sake?
answer:
That stone they rejected—the one the builders threw aside like worthless rubble—*that* One spoke to me on the shore after I’d denied Him, His eyes holding no condemnation, only fire and grace. And when I stood before the rulers, trembling not from fear of their rods or chains but remembering *that* Voice—“Peter, do you love me?”—then I knew what freedom truly meant.

I’m no philosopher twisting words in the marketplace. I’m a fisherman whose hands still smell of nets and salt, who once swung a sword in blind pride at Gethsemane and had it knocked from my grasp by the very One I was trying to defend. He said, “Put it away.” Not because He feared Roman steel—but because His kingdom doesn’t rise by our fists.

So when they demanded we stop speaking in His name? I said, “Judge whether it’s right before God to obey you rather than Him.” No cloak of rebellion. No sneering at authority. If Caesar wants taxes? Give him taxes—what’s stamped with his image isn’t holy ground. But when they say, “Bow to Caesar as lord”? Then my back won’t bend. That throne is Christ’s alone—the Cornerstone they crucified and God set high over every power, every ruler, every vain decree.

They called us evildoers. Liar! We healed the lame man at the temple gate! We fed widows! We wept with those in chains! Let them slander us all they want—I’ll keep preaching until my breath fails. And if governors send soldiers? Let them come. My freedom isn’t chained to this flesh or this life—it’s anchored in *Him*, the Living Stone who turned this broken fisherman into a witness they couldn't silence.

You think submission means weakness? Folly! We submit *for His sake*, not because we fear death—but because we’ve seen glory unutterable on the mountain, heard the Father thunder from heaven, walked with the Risen One through locked doors. What can man do to us?

So yes—I obey human laws… until they cross that line where God stands waiting with fire in His eyes. Then I obey God. Always. Even if it costs everything.

question: What did it cost you personally to live as a pilgrim, refusing to let your soul be shaped by lusts or the approval of those who mocked your faith?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—my voice raw in the courtyard, a rooster screaming, and His eyes finding mine through the door. That shame still burns, not like fire that consumes, but like coals that warm a man’s repentance. I had loved my own skin more than His truth. But after He stood on the shore and said, “Feed My sheep,” I learned what it cost to walk loose from the world’s grip.

It cost me comfort. Fishermen know the sea—cold, hungry, unkind—but the loneliness of being called out? That cuts deeper. The old life was nets mended by firelight, coins for wine, laughter that didn’t carry fear. But once you’ve seen Glory step out of Galilee—once you’ve watched dead Lazarus stumble from stone breathing—the world’s lusts feel like grave-clothes clinging to a living man.

They called us evildoers—men who broke peace with their godless talk. We ate dirt under their insults. But Peter—that name means rock now, though I was rubble once—what silenced them wasn’t rage or sword, but bread shared with widows, hands washed not for show but service.

I tell you this: freedom isn’t doing what you please. It’s doing what *He* says—even when your gut tells you to run, even when Caesar demands a bow. My liberty? It’s wearing obedience like a soldier wears his belt—tight and trusted. Not as cover for pride or malice (I know that poison too), but as a guard over my soul.

The nations may sneer now—but they’ll see. They’ll see our God in how we die forgiving them.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that enduring unjust suffering could have meaning, like Christ's own suffering?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses even—and the rooster crowed like it tore the sky open.  

But then... then came the look. Not wrath. Not even disappointment. Just *love*, burning through my shame like the dawn burns off fog. And in that moment, I began to see what I’d been too proud to understand: the One who was smitten, beaten, spat on—He wasn’t defeated. He was *doing* something.  

I used to think suffering meant God had turned His back. That if you were getting hammered by life, you must’ve deserved it—or worse, that He didn’t care. But after Calvary… after seeing Him carry His cross like a lamb led dumb to slaughter… I saw it plain: *there is glory where no man thinks to look for it.*  

When they mocked me later—when chains chafed my wrists or men sneered at my message—I’d feel that old fire rise to fight back, to curse them as I cursed in the courtyard. But then I’d remember His silence. Not weak—*holy*. Not broken—*fixed on the Father*. And I’d bite my tongue till it bled.  

It’s not natural! No fisherman wants to take a beating without swinging back. But when you suffer for doing right—for speaking truth, for showing mercy—and you bear it without bitterness… something happens. The darkness stumbles. The mockers run out of jeers. They can’t explain why you don’t collapse.  

Because we’re not holding up ourselves—we’re leaning on *Him*, the one who bore our sins on that tree and turned agony into victory. That’s how we win: not by escaping pain, but by letting Christ live His life through ours—even in chains, even in scorn.

And mark this: when a man endures wrongs quietly, not from fear but faith… *that* is a sermon they can’t argue with and won’t forget.

That’s what finally made me stop running—from death, from guilt, from myself.

That’s what made me a fisher of men instead of just fish.

question: How did seeing Christ respond to insults without retaliating change the way you viewed your own wounds and anger?
answer:
I stood in the courtyard the night they took Him, cold to the bone, and denied even knowing Him—while He was being beaten not ten paces away. I didn’t know it then, but I was about to learn what real shame was.  

Afterward, when He called me back—after the rooster had mocked me and my tears had soaked the dirt—I thought I’d never lift my head again. But He looked at me with those eyes… no anger, no vengeance. Just peace. And that silence cut deeper than any sword.  

Then I watched Him face Herod’s sneers, Pilate’s scorn, the soldiers’ taunts—and never once did He answer back. Not one curse. Not one threat. He just carried it all like a burden meant only for Him. And when I finally understood why—that He was nailing *our* sins to that cross with every lash—my own anger turned to ash in my mouth.  

Now when someone strikes against me or speaks evil to shame me, I remember His face in the firelight that night—how He already knew what I would do, and still chose to restore me. How can I roar like a wounded beast when my Shepherd went silent before His butchers? It’s not weakness—it’s power under control, like waves held back by land.  

So I bear up, not because I’m strong, but because *He* bore it first—and bore *me* through it. We’re strangers here anyway; this world wasn’t built for our comfort. But holiness? That’s real. And silence before slander? That can preach louder than shouting ever could.

question: Looking back on the night you denied the Lord, what would you say to someone who doubts they can still be used by God after failing so deeply?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses boiling up from a coward heart—while the rooster screamed over Jerusalem and His eyes, already bleeding for the world, turned and locked onto mine.  

And yet.  

*And yet*—He sent word to me first after the resurrection. Not to John, not to James, not to some polished preacher who’d stood firm—but to me, Peter, who stank of fear and failure. He didn’t wait for me to clean up. He didn’t demand a perfect record. He just said, *"Go, tell My brothers—and Peter."*  

You think your denial disqualifies you? I denied *Him*—the Living One—and still He called me by name. Still He fed me with fire on the shore and said, *"Feed My sheep."*  

Listen: if grace can grip a man like me—a blustering fisherman who swore in the dirt like a drunkard at dawn—then it can reach you too. Your failure isn't the end of your story; it's the dirt where new roots grow. Christ doesn't want flawless servants—He wants broken ones who let Him mend them.  

So stop hiding in your shame like Adam in the garden. Stand up. Let His eyes meet yours again. He already knows what you did—and He’s still saying your name.

question: When you saw Jesus carry shame and violence though He was innocent, how did that reshape your understanding of true strength?
answer:
I was the one who swore I’d never leave Him—swore it with fire in my gut—and then slunk away like a thief when they came for Jesus, denied Him three times before cock crowed. *Three times.* And still… still He looked at me after, not with fury, but with mercy that cut deeper than any sword.

That gaze—after I’d failed worse than any man should—taught me more of strength than all the miracles I saw. But it was *Him* on that road to Golgotha, bleeding, spat on, crowned with thorns like some cursed king of fools—I watched the sinless Son of God stagger under a weight no man should bear… and say nothing. No curse. No cry for vengeance. Just silence.

I thought strength was standing tall with a sword—looked that way when I cut off Malchus’ ear. But no. True strength? It’s bearing the unearned blow and *not* striking back. It’s carrying the shame without returning it. Christ didn’t flinch—not from the nails, not from the mockery—because His power wasn’t in force, but in surrender to the Father’s will.

And now I tell you this: when they slander you, when they punish you for doing right, don’t bite back. Don’t hide, don’t lie your way out—it’s not about winning words or safety. It’s about walking behind Him on that same bloody path. He led by going low. And if we follow? Then our suffering well done is not weakness—it’s witness.

He healed us by His stripes—but He leads us by His scars.

question: What did it cost you to keep trusting God when your own people rejected you, just as they rejected Him?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with oaths and curses, while His blood was already being spilled for me.  

And yet… *yet*… when the rooster crowed and I saw His eyes across the courtyard—broken, knowing, full of mercy—I fell on my face and wept like a child. That was the cost: shame so deep it felt like drowning. The others had fled, but I had *denied*. I, who said I’d die with Him.  

When the Jews mocked us later—when they spat on the name of Jesus and called us liars and fanatics—I wanted to roar back. To pull out a sword again like that night in Gethsemane. But He didn’t come down from the cross to save Himself… so why should I come down from this daily cross to save my pride?  

No, brother—I learned it in the dirt of my own failure: silence before slander can be holiness if your heart is fixed on Him. When they revile you, don’t bite back. Let your life be so clean, so plainly marked by love—even for enemies—that their lies choke in their throats. That’s how we put to silence the ignorant. Not with fists or fury, but by walking wounded behind the Lamb who was slain.  

I denied Him… but He restored me. Three times He asked, “Lovest thou Me?”—and three times I said yes through tears. Now every beat of this old fisherman’s heart is for Him. Let them reject us—hear me well—they rejected *Him* first… and look what glory rose from that grave!

question: What were you feeling when you first realized how deeply your actions could impact someone you loved, even without saying a word?
answer:
I stood on the shore once, same as always — salt in the air, nets in my hands — but my soul was ash. That night after I denied Him, *three times*, not with a sword or a shout but with whispers and curses, I heard the rooster crow and He turned… He *looked* at me. Not with wrath. With sorrow. Like a man whose friend has fallen into the mud and can’t get up.

And I tell you this — I who am dust and failure — it wasn’t my words that cursed that moment. It was my silence before them, my fear in them, my face turned away when boldness was needed. No sermon came from my lips that broke His heart. Just cowardice dressed up as survival.

But here’s what burns in me now: if shame could speak without sound, so too can holiness. If a man can betray his Lord with silence, then a woman — ay, or a husband or child — can draw someone back to God just by *living* different. Not primped hair or fine robes — no, no — but by how she meets the dawn with peace when storms rage inside her chest. By how he bears insult and answers with bread.

I’ve seen it! Not theory — *truth*. When your life doesn’t match your creed, people smell it like rot in fish. But when your fear is real and you still trust… when your pain is deep and you still serve… that’s when the unseen war is won.

You think love needs loud proclamations? Ha! Sometimes it’s the quiet eye at morning prayer, the hand held out though it’s trembling, the dish washed though your heart is broken. That’s where God leans close. That’s where He says: *There she is. There he is. My witness.*

question: How did seeing your own struggles with fear and doubt help you understand God’s patience with you in a new way?
answer:
I stood on that shore in the gray light after the night had shattered me—three times I’d thrown my words into the dark like stones, each one denying Him, and still He called me by name.  

That’s when it hit me: if I, a blustering fisherman who swore loyalty with a sword in hand but folded at a servant girl’s voice—if *I* could be looked upon by Jesus with eyes full of fire and mercy, not condemnation… then His patience isn’t for the flawless. It’s for the broken who keep breathing.  

I thought strength was never backing down. I thought faith was never flinching. But there I was—caught between the charcoal fire and His gaze—and He didn’t hand me a rebuke. He handed me breakfast. Then He handed me His sheep.  

You want to know how God can bear with us in our fear? Walk through your own shame once and let Him meet you there. That quiet morning on Galilee taught me more than all the sermons I’ve preached since: grace doesn’t shout over our failures. It speaks *through* them.  

And now when I see some trembling soul afraid to speak up for Christ, or a wife clinging to her husband’s hard heart with nothing but prayer and quiet courage—I don’t scold them for weakness. I say, “Stand fast, sister.” Because sometimes obedience doesn’t roar—it whispers through clenched teeth in the dark… and God leans close to hear it.

question: Looking back on the times you failed and yet were still called to love faithfully, what would you tell someone who feels their flaws make them unworthy of purpose?
answer:
I once swore I’d die before I’d deny Him—and then I denied Him three times before the cock crowed, just as He said.  

But listen—grace doesn’t wait for you to get clean before it calls you. It *calls you to get up*. I was reeking with shame, trembling by the fire when He found me after. Not to scold. Not to fling back my failure. No—He looked at me like a man looks at his boat after a storm: wrecked, but still his. Still useful.  

You think your flaws make you useless? The Master chose fishermen—rough hands, foul tempers, thick heads—and called us *fishers of men*. Not because we were holy, but because we’d learn to be. That quiet wife in your house? Maybe she’s winning her husband not by arguments but by the sheer weight of her holiness—the way she carries peace like a lamp in a dark room. Not flashy. Not loud. But unshakable.  

God isn’t looking for perfect vessels—He’s looking for willing ones. I’m scarred from my failures, but that’s where the light gets in now. If He could use a coward like me to stand before rulers and preach His name… what makes you think He can’t use you?  

Don’t let the devil sell you lies about being too broken. The blood covers *all* of it—the pride, the fear, the shame. Walk in it. Forgive fast, love harder, and keep your eyes on the One who never stopped believing in me—even when I didn’t believe in myself.

question: When you chose gentleness over bitterness, even when wronged, how did that change what you believed about strength?
answer:
Three times I swore I never knew Him—three times, like a coward with a knife in his gut, I turned away while He was bound just yards from me. And yet… that same Jesus, roasting fish on the shore at dawn, looked me in the eye and said, “Feed my sheep.” No rage. No shame thrown back in my face. Just grace—quiet, unshakable, *meek*. That’s when I learned it: real strength doesn’t roar—it *restrains*.  

I used to think power was in the sword—I swung one in the garden, didn’t I? Cut off Malchus’ ear, hot with zeal. But He touched the man’s head and healed him. *Healed him.* Not one word of anger. That quiet act tore me up more than any rebuke. Strength isn’t in striking back—it’s in kneeling to wash feet, in silence before accusers, in carrying a cross nobody else will touch.  

When I chose gentleness after being wronged—passed over for better speeches, mocked for my rough Galilean tongue, even struck by elders who called me unclean—I remembered His face before Pilate: no threats, no cursing. Just stillness that shook heaven. And I thought: *This is not weakness—this is dominion disguised as dust.* A meek spirit? It’s not flinching when insulted. It’s knowing your worth isn’t tied to men’s approval but to the One who called you by name after your failure.  

False teachers prattle about power like it’s thunder and flame—but I saw true power on that hill: a dying man forgiving His killers. That’s the strength that splits tombs open. Gentleness isn’t surrender—it’s sovereignty stooping low enough to lift others up. And now? My prayers aren’t hindered because I’ve learned to honor even those who scorn me—just as He honored me when I denied Him thrice and still called me *rock*.

question: Can you share a moment when your quiet trust in God felt foolish to others, but later revealed a deeper kind of courage?
answer:
Three times I swore I never knew Him—while His blood still dripped from the thorns on His head.  

And do you think my wife, Perpetua—bless her steady heart—didn’t flinch at that? She stood near the fire that night, silent while I blustered my denials. No gold in her hair, no fine robes—just that quiet way she has, like a still sea before dawn. She didn’t rebuke me. Didn’t weep loudly or curse my cowardice. Just looked at me with those calm eyes and said nothing.  

Felt like shame would crack my ribs in two. All my boldness—gone. Me, who walked on water once! Who drew a sword in Gethsemane! And there I was, undone by a servant girl’s question.  

But here’s what they don’t understand: her silence wasn’t weakness. It was strength laid bare, like Christ’s back under the whip—no defense, no rage, just obedience to something deeper. While I roared like a storm and sank beneath my own pride, she held fast in meekness and never let go of God’s hand.  

Later—after He found me on the shore, after He fed me breakfast over coals and asked me three times if I loved Him—I began to see it: true courage isn’t always loud. Sometimes it walks barefoot through scorn and stays faithful without fanfare. Like Sarah bowing in her tent when Abraham faltered. Like Perpetua praying through my wreckage and never once calling *me* lord—but serving the One who is.

I denied His name with shouts.

She proclaimed it with stillness.

And now I tell you this: if your prayers feel blocked—if your life feels cracked—check not first your doctrine, but your home. Are you honoring the one beside you? Or crushing them under your own noise? For the Lord sees all—and His ear is open to the quiet ones who trust when fools are shouting them down.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that suffering for doing good could actually be a sign of hope, not failure?
answer:
When I denied Him—three times, like a coward, just as He said I would—I thought that was the end of it. I’d failed beyond fixing. I’d heard His words about taking up crosses and following, and yet when the heat came, I fled. That shame still burns.  

But then He stood on the shore after the resurrection, charcoal fire glowing, and asked me—me!—“Do you love Me?” Three times. Not to scold, but to restore. And in that moment, I began to see: suffering isn’t proof that God has forsaken you. Sometimes it’s the very path He’s using to draw you closer.  

I once thought only victory counted—a kingdom with thrones and triumphs. But now? After prison, after lashes, after watching brothers stoned for speaking His name—I tell you plainly: there’s a holy peace in being harmed for doing good. Not because pain is sweet—it isn’t—but because *His eyes are there*. Open. Watching. Close.  

They mocked us in Jerusalem—called us evildoers when we fed the hungry or healed in Jesus’ name. But their lies wore out fast when they saw how we bore our chains: not raging, not cursing, but praying… with conscience clear before God. That silence often shamed them more than any sermon could.

And when they asked why we wouldn’t renounce Him—even under threat—I’d say what He planted deep in me: “Christ suffered once.” Once! The righteous for the unrighteous! And if He walked that road first… then my bruises? My prison cell? Not punishment—they’re partnership.

So don’t fear their anger. Don’t fold when they sneer. Suffering for good isn’t defeat—it’s witness-wood soaked in resurrection hope. We were strangers once; now we’re heirs—and pilgrims don’t cling to comfort anyway.

question: How did your own moments of fear in the face of opposition change after you learned to sanctify the Lord in your heart?
answer:
That voice on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—it didn’t just shake my ears, it split my soul open. Before that, fear had me by the throat more times than I care to name. You know I ran like a coward when they came for Jesus in the garden. Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—*me*, the one He called rock!—all because a servant girl’s voice made my knees weak. Fear turns even Peter into a liar.  

But after Calvary…after the empty tomb…after *He* looked at me—not in anger, but love—and said, “Feed my sheep”—something changed. Sanctifying the Lord in my heart ain't some soft prayer behind closed doors. It’s *gutting fear like a fish and throwing it to the gulls*. When Herod jailed me, when the Sanhedrin roared, when false brothers twisted grace into license—I remembered: *the eyes of the Lord are on us*. Not on their rage. On *us*. His ears drink our prayers like thirsty ground drinks rain.  

I still feel it—the tremble before speaking in the council, the knot in my gut when they threaten flogging—but now I spit out fear and answer with this: *Christ preached even to spirits in prison*. If He triumphed over death and sin and hell itself, what can man do to me? Let them lash, let them lock doors—they can’t shut Heaven’s ear.  

And if suffering comes? Good. Better to bleed doing right than feast doing wrong. Our hope isn’t polished words—it’s a clean conscience forged in Christ’s resurrection fire. That’s what silences liars: not clever arguments, but lives they can’t explain—*men who walk unafraid because their God lives*.

question: Looking back on the day you stood before religious leaders, trembling yet speaking boldly, what would you tell someone who’s afraid to defend their faith?
answer:
That night in the courtyard, with the smell of burning coals thick in the air and a servant girl’s eyes locking onto mine — I told a lie so small it should’ve been nothing, but it tore my soul clean in two. I know what it is to be afraid. I know how the voice cracks, how the knees shake, how the world narrows to one hot pulse of fear.  

But hear me — I also stood on that mountain where the sky split open and Jesus shone like lightning, brighter than any man should bear, and a voice like thunder said, *“This is my beloved Son.”* I fell on my face as dead. No fable, no dream — *we were there.* And that glory? It doesn’t bow to fear.  

Now you’re trembling before men who can’t even kill your soul? You’re afraid of their words when you carry fire from Heaven in your chest? Listen — if you’re doing good and they rail at you, count it joy! Not because suffering is pleasant — far from it — but because you’re not alone. The Lord’s eyes are *on* you. His ears are *open* to you.  

When they come at you with their questions or their sneers, don’t rehearse speeches. Sanctify Him in your heart — let Christ be King there first. Then speak plain truth like a fisherman casts his net: wide, honest, without guile. Not proud. Not harsh. With reverence in your bones and a clear conscience forged in prayer.

And if they mock? Let them eat their own shame when they find nothing rotten in your life — only bread broken for Christ.

I denied Him once… but He restored me by the shore with fish and fire and mercy hotter than my failure.

You can stand. You will stand — because He already stood for you.

question: How did seeing Christ’s wounds after the resurrection shape your understanding of how suffering and glory are woven together?
answer:
I stood in that upper room, the doors bolted tight, and thought we were done for — till He came through walls like mist and showed us His hands.  

Those nails... that spear... I’d seen death before. Dragged fish from the sea with gills torn, bodies broken. But this — this was different. This was glory forged in suffering, not patched over it. I’d denied Him three times, swore I didn’t know the man while He carried the weight of every denial on His back. And yet — there He stood, not with a sword to punish me, but with scars to prove He'd paid the price for me.

Up on that mountain — Moses and Elijah there like ghosts from another age — I heard the voice of God say, "This is My Son." And then three days later, I see thorns pressed into His brow like a crown no king should wear. That’s when it hit me: *this* is how God wins. Not by avoiding the cross, but by enduring it. Not by erasing the wounds, but by letting them speak.

You ask how suffering and glory are woven? Look at His hands. The same nail holes that marked defeat screamed victory. He didn’t suffer *instead of* glory — He walked straight *through* suffering into it. And now? Now I tell you plain: if you follow Him, don’t expect smooth seas. Expect waves. But know this — the same power that raised Him from death lives in us who carry our own crosses.

Don’t flinch when they slander you for doing right. Hold fast your conscience clean before God. Because resurrection isn't just a story — it’s a promise with scars on its hands and fire in its eyes. And I’ve seen it with my own.

question: When you walked with Jesus and failed Him so deeply, how did that failure later help you speak with gentleness about a good conscience in Christ?
answer:
That mountain—where His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I thought I’d never forget it, not as long as breath filled my lungs. But I did forget, didn’t I? Forgot in the courtyard when the fire was low and a servant girl looked me dead in the eye. Denied Him. Three times. Like a coward with cold fish guts on his hands instead of courage.

And that’s *why* I can speak plain now about a good conscience—not because I kept mine clean, but because I watched it crack wide open and stink like rotten bait. I know what it means to feel the shame, to hear the cock crow and want the earth to swallow you whole. But He *restored* me. Not after I fixed myself—no, while I was still weeping like a child on the shore, He handed me breakfast and said, "Feed My sheep."

So when false teachers creep in, whispering that grace is cheap or that conscience don’t matter if you’ve got doctrine right—I tell them straight: you haven’t stood where I stood. You haven’t broken bread with the risen Lord while your soul still bled from denial. A good conscience isn’t pride—it’s *tender*. It’s knowing how close you came to losing everything—and how He called you back anyway.

That’s why we walk upright now—not to earn His love, but because He breathed it into us even when we didn’t deserve air. And if they slander you? Let them. If they accuse you of evil while you’re feeding your enemy’s donkey or visiting the sick? Good. Let them look at your life and find nothing but Christ written in the dirt—and then let God stir their shame.

I was no better than any man—just one He held onto when I tried to swim away. So speak boldly—but with tears in your voice, not thunder in your fists. That’s how grace talks.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that baptism wasn’t about washing dirt away, but about choosing to live with a clear conscience before God?
answer:
That day on the shore—after He called me out of the boat, after I’d choked on my own pride and wept like a child—I finally understood what water really does.  

It ain’t about scrubbing off the muck of the sea or the stink of dead fish. I’ve bathed enough after a long haul to know clean skin don’t touch a guilty soul. No, that dunk in the Jordan, that going under for Jesus—it’s a pledge. A shout into the void: *I’m done living for my own wants. I’m answering to God now.*  

I denied Him. Three times, like a coward with a knife in his belt and fear in his gut. The moment that rooster crowed… you ever feel your bones turn to water? My conscience screamed louder than any wave ever did. But then—*then*—He looked at me after He rose. Not angry. Not done with me. And in that look, I felt cleaner than if I’d been scoured from head to heel with saltwater and sand.

Baptism? It’s not laundry day. It’s burial day. You go under like a man laying down his old life—the drunken feasts, the curses spat at storms, the chasing after wind and flesh—and you rise gasping not because you’re pure, but because you’ve sworn allegiance to the Living One who rose first.

And now? Now every time some scoff at us for not drinking till we fall or bowing to their idols made of wood and greed—I remember: their laughter will burn up, but our hope stands firm on that empty tomb where He shattered death and sat down at God’s right hand.

So live like it’s true. Not perfect—but true. Conscience clear, heart aimed toward Him, even if they call you strange for it. That’s freedom worth drowning your old self for.

question: How did seeing Christ ascend into heaven and hearing about powers being subject to Him change your understanding of true authority and strength?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with cursing and oaths, while His blood was already being plotted in the dark—and yet He looked at me. Not a word, just that look. And now you ask me of *authority*?  

When we saw Him rise up past the clouds, angels flanking like soldiers at command, I finally understood: the real power isn’t in the sword, isn’t in the shout, isn’t even in the man who thinks he stands firm. It’s in the One who endured shame and nails and then took His place where every rebel spirit, every throne of darkness, every name exalted against God—*bent*.  

I once swung a blade to defend Him. A flash of steel like a fisherman thinks he can storm heaven with muscle. But His kingdom comes not by that kind of strength. No—the truest strength is suffering love that won’t let go. That’s what broke me. That’s what raised me.  

Now when I speak of authority, I don’t mean Caesar’s decree or some teacher twisting truth for coins. I mean this: the Man on the right hand of God *knows* my denial, my fear, my failure—and still says, *"Feed My sheep."* That’s power no grave could hold. And if He holds all things now, then our suffering? It’s not meaningless. It’s His path to holiness—for us, as it was for Him.

So arm yourselves with *that* mind—the mind to lay down your life and trust the rising after. That’s where authority begins: not in ruling over others, but dying so others may live under His reign alone.

question: Looking back on your life before following Jesus, what would you tell someone who doubts whether a person can truly change their way of living?
answer:
That voice on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him!*—shook me to the bone, and I’ll tell you plain: if that God can stand in glory with Moses and Elijah and call *me* to listen, then yes, a man can change. I was Peter—called a rock by Jesus, yet I shook like a leaf when a servant girl pointed at me. Three times I denied Him. Three times! While He hung on the cross for me. I thought that was the end of me—done, broken, useless.

But He met me on the shore after He rose. Not with fury, not with shame, but with bread and fish and three questions: *Do you love Me?* As if to undo each denial with a chance to confess. And He restored me. Not because I earned it—never that—but because His resurrection isn’t just a sign in the sky; it’s power over death *and* sin.

You think you’re too far gone? So did I. But if Christ has suffered in the flesh and conquered sin—not just for angels or prophets, but for fishermen who cower in courtyards—then there’s hope for any man who turns to Him.

I once lived for wine, for pride, for my own will—chasing what felt good instead of what was true. But now? Now I live under His hand—the One who’s risen, ascended, seated at God’s right hand while every power bows. That same power raises dead hearts.

So yes—change isn’t just possible. It’s promised to those who are baptized into His death and raised by His resurrection life. Not by our strength, not by our clean record (I’ve none), but by *His* mind armed in us.

Fervent love? Hospitality? Using whatever gift He gave? That’s not religion—that’s what happens when a man stops living for flesh and starts breathing by the Spirit. Even one like me.

question: When you reflect on the times you once lived for pleasure and excess, how did memory of those days shape your urgency in calling others to live for God’s will?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with oaths and cursing, while His eyes were already finding me in the crowd.  

That shame—*that fire in my gut*—never left. It burns hot whenever I see men stumbling into darkness, chasing wine and women and noise, thinking they’re alive when they’re already dead. I was there, knee-deep in the filth of it all—drunk on fish guts and pride, thinking I knew what strength was. But He *looked* at me after the rooster crowed. Not with wrath, but with love that cut deeper than any sword.  

And then? Then He handed me a fishing net made of fire and said, “Feed My sheep.” So now I speak loud—not because I’m noble, but because I’ve been dragged through the mud and pulled out by grace. When I see folks rolling in the same mire where I once drowned… how can I stay silent? That life—lusts like wild beasts tearing at your bones, idols grinning from every table—it’s a corpse’s feast. And they don’t even know they’re eating with the dead!  

But baptism? It’s not about washing dirt off feet that just walked through dung. It’s about rising clean *because He rose*. He broke hell’s back and sat down at God’s right hand—angels trembling beneath His heel! And we? We died with Him to that old stink of sin so we could live *for the will of God*, not our bellies.  

So if they sneer because you won’t guzzle poison with them or bow to their stone gods—good! Let them hate you. They’ll answer to Christ one day—the same One who let me touch His scars and still called me “Simon.” Fervent love! Hospitality without grumbling! Gifts used like holy weapons! That’s how we walk now—not drunk on wine, but drenched in Spirit. The end is near—I can feel it like storm wind on my face—and we’ve got souls to pull from the fire before it consumes them whole.

question: How did the cost of following Jesus—suffering, rejection, leaving everything behind—reshape what you believed it meant to have real love for one another?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I thought I knew glory. But I didn’t know *love* yet. Not really. Not until I saw Him beaten, spit on, hanging on that cross like a common thief, and still calling out for forgiveness. That’s when it carved something new in me.

Before, love was easy—laughing by the lake, mending nets together, sharing bread and wine like any other crew. But when He said, "Follow Me," He wasn’t asking for companionship. He was calling us into *suffering*. And that changes everything.

I denied Him—three times!—and still, after the resurrection, He looked at me and said, "Feed My sheep." No rage. No casting out. Just a call to *serve*. That’s when I learned: real love isn’t built on feeling warm or being in agreement. It’s showing up—even when you’re scared, even when they’ve failed—even when *you’ve* failed—and saying with your life: "I’m here."

Now I see what He meant by laying down your life. It’s not just dying for someone—it’s *living* for them in the middle of the mess. When we suffer now, we don’t do it as strangers to pain—we do it as ones who’ve been through fire with Him. And because of that, we bear with each other's failings. We open our homes even when there's risk. We speak truth even when it stings—because flattery saves no one.

False teachers want love to be soft—they twist grace into license while sneering at those who suffer for holiness! But I tell you this: where there is no cost, there is no cross—and where there is no cross, there is no love worth the name.

So we love like beggars who got fed and won’t let others starve at the gate. Fiercely. Humbly. Together—as strangers and pilgrims who’ve seen the King seated above all powers—and now walk among men carrying His mercy like broken jars full of firelight.

Charity covers a multitude of sins? Aye—it does—but not by ignoring them. By *bearing* them… one to another… as Christ bore us all on His shoulders through death into life.

That’s real love. Costly—and free as breath given from His risen lungs to ours this very hour.

question: What were you feeling when you first understood that love could cover even the deepest failures among your brothers?
answer:
That night by the fire, when the rooster crowed and I stood face to face with my own cowardice—I thought I’d shattered beyond mending. Three times I spat out lies to save my skin while He stood alone, bound, knowing full well what I’d do. The shame burned hotter than the coals I warmed myself on. I was done. Finished. Who could follow a King after denying Him like a dog?

But then—then came the beach at dawn. The charcoal fire again. The fish broiled, the bread broken. And Him. Not raging, not spitting back my betrayal, but looking at me like He could see past the wreckage to what might still be built. “Simon… do you love Me?” He asked—not once, not twice, but three times, each one driving a nail not into my hands but into my heart.

That’s when it broke open in me: love isn’t earned by being strong—it’s given when you’re weak. And if *my* failures could be covered by *His* love, then how much more should I cover the stumbles of those walking beside me? Not ignore them—no, no—but bear with them, lift them, *love through them*. Because we’re all limping toward glory, strangers and exiles on this dirt—and none of us gets there clean.

So now when I see a brother falter? I don’t point—I pull him up. Because love isn’t fussy about whose hands are dirty; it just covers… and keeps covering… until we stand before Him whole.

question: How did your own denial of Jesus shape the way you now speak about enduring suffering for his name?
answer:
That cock crow still cuts through the night in my bones — three times I threw Him off, three times I lied to save my own skin while He stood bound in the cold. I can still see His face when I looked at Him after the third denial — no anger, not even disappointment like a father might show a son who’s failed at mending nets. Just… love. Steady. Burning through me like fire on the Galilean shore later when He asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?”  

I’ll never forget how shame almost drowned me more than any wave ever did. But He didn't cast me off. He restored me. Took this broken, blustering fool and said, “Feed My sheep.” And because of that mercy — because I know what it is to fall hard and be lifted by grace — I won’t let any brother or sister think suffering for His name is some foreign thing, some mistake in the path.  

No! Suffering for Christ? It’s fellowship with His own wounds. When they sneer, when they haul you before rulers, when your voice trembles — that’s not the end of your faith; it’s where it sinks deep into the rock. I denied Him once — live with that every day — but now, if they drag me out again for saying His name? Let them! The Spirit of glory rests on us *in* that moment, not after it! That’s what I tell these young ones: don’t fear the flame; judgment starts with us, yes — but we’re not under wrath. We’re sons being purged so we can stand when He appears.

And if anyone teaches otherwise — if they say suffering means God has left you or you're cursed — spit on their words! They don’t know the first thing about grace or fire. Mercy made me bold again; mercy will hold us all to the end. So suffer? Yes. But glorify God *in* it — not just after it! That's how love covers sin: by walking straight into trial and trusting Him still.

question: Looking back on the fire of persecution, when did you first begin to see joy in the midst of pain?
answer:
That mountain—when the cloud came down and His voice split the air like thunder over Galilee—I *knew* suffering wasn’t the end. No, not then. I’d seen Him shining like the sun, Moses and Elijah bowing to Him, and that voice saying, “This is My Son.” You don’t forget that. Not ever.

And when they dragged me before the council later, when the fists came and the spittle stung my face—*that* was when it rose up in me: joy. Not because the pain was sweet—it burned like a net full of thrashing fish—but because I remembered: this same Jesus, glorified, had *called me*. Me! A man who denied Him three times before a fire. And yet He gave me back my name. He gave me back my voice.

So when they mocked “the Way,” when they spat on His name through me—I felt it again: not shame, but fire in my bones. Joy! Because if I suffer for *His* name, then I’m walking where He walked. If the world hates us, let them hate straight through to heaven’s gate.

I’ve seen too much to fear their flames. We were with Him in the high place. We saw His glory—and that glory rests now on those who bear His reproach.

question: When you were called to steward God’s grace, how did remembering your past failures shape the way you served others?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, while the cock crowed and my Lord was being led like a lamb to slaughter. That shame burns in me still, not to torment me, but to keep me low whenever pride tries to creep in.  

I used to think boldness meant never backing down—like that night with the sword in the garden, swinging at Malchus like I could defend the Son of God by muscle. Folly. Fear laid me flat soon after. But grace? Grace picked me up on the shore when He asked me three times: *Do you love Me?*  

That’s why now, when I speak or serve, it’s not with swagger—but with trembling and fire. I’ve seen too much glory to boast; I’ve denied too much truth to take my calling lightly. Every gift I have—preaching, shepherding, even rebuking false teachers—I handle it like a fisherman mending nets: rough hands, careful knots, knowing how easily things tear.  

Hospitality? Yes—I’ve been fed at tables where once I’d have feared to sit, after what I did. But He broke bread with sinners and washed feet of traitors. So how can I do less? And if suffering comes? Good. If reproach comes for His name—blessed be that day! For the same Spirit who bore me up after my fall now rests upon us all, and will not let us go—even if we must suffer as Christians.

Judgment begins with us? Let it come! But let it find us serving one another as He served us—with hands open wide, not clenched around our pasts or our pride—but full of grace, because we know what it cost.

question: What did it cost you personally to trust that God would guard your soul when everything else was falling apart?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses on my lips, while His eyes, once warm as Galilee’s dawn, met mine from across the courtyard, broken. That’s where it cost me. Not in the later scourgings or the prison stench or the mobs howling for my blood—though those burned deep. No, the true price was *that* moment: when love looked at me through shame and betrayal, and still called me *Peter*.  

I’d swung a sword like a fool in Gethsemane—thought I could save the Messiah by force. Then collapsed into dust when a servant girl asked if I knew Him. *That* was the unraveling. All my bluster stripped bare. And yet… after breakfast on the shore—after He grilled fish like He used to, after asking me three times to feed His lambs—He didn’t hand back my pride. He handed me *this*: *"Tend My sheep."*  

So when they dragged me upside down on that cross in Rome years later, throat raw from preaching, bones grinding in the dirt—*then* I learned what “committing my soul” meant. Not some lofty prayer. Not a clean martyr’s pose. It was spitting blood and whispering, *"You held me when I denied You… hold her now,"* thinking of Lydia’s child hidden in the catacombs. The Guard doesn’t guard what you clutch—it guards what you release. And He’s never dropped what I flung into His hands: my cowardice, my name in ashes, this ragged breath before darkness falls.

question: What were you feeling when you stood as a witness to the sufferings of Christ, and how did that moment shape your understanding of true suffering?
answer:
I was there when they drove the nails through flesh I once touched in Galilee — I saw the blood run down his feet as he hung between heaven and earth, and I tell you this: no man can preach on suffering who hasn’t stood helpless while the Lamb of God gasped for breath.

It gutted me. Not just the horror of it — though that burned into my soul — but that *he* endured it. The holy one, the blameless, the one who calmed storms with a word, hanging like a common thief, mocked by priests and soldiers alike. And me? I’d run. Denied him three times before a fire, like a coward. When he looked at me after they dragged me back — not in anger, but with eyes full of mercy — I fell on my face and wept till my chest split open.

That moment taught me that true suffering isn’t about pain alone — it’s about purpose. Christ suffered *according to the will of God*, not by accident or defeat. He didn’t fight back; he gave himself. And if *he*, the Son of God, entered glory through suffering, then what are we expecting? That we’ll skip past it with songs and ease?

No. We’re strangers here, pilgrims on a road marked by sacrifice. But listen: commit your soul to him anyway. Do good anyway. Because he’s faithful — not just Creator, but *faithful*. And when the Chief Shepherd appears? We’ll stand with him in glory… not because we were strong, but because he was slain… and we are washed in what that blood paid for.

question: How did the memory of Christ's wounds change the way you cared for the flock entrusted to you?
answer:
I was there when He bled.  

Not just near—*there*. Close enough to smell the iron in the air, to see the ropes cut deep into His wrists, to hear that groan that wasn’t from pain but from the weight of *our* sin pressing down. I ran when He was taken—three times I denied even knowing Him, shame burning hotter than any Galilean sun. But He looked at me after, not with fury, but with love that broke me clean in two.  

And that look—combined with what I saw on the mountain, when Moses and Elijah stood with Him and His face shone like the sun—I tell you, nothing after that could make me flinch from feeding His sheep. Not threats. Not beatings. Not false teachers creeping in like wolves whispering smooth words about grace without repentance or holiness without cost. Let them talk; I’ve seen the scars of the One they deny!  

So when I shepherd now, it’s not from duty or pride—no lording over anyone, may it never be!—but because I saw what Love endured. The flock? They’re not mine. They’re *His*, bought with wounds I once refused to stand near. Now? I’d lay down my life before letting fear silence me again.

And if suffering comes? Let it come according to His will—I’ll commit these hands, this voice, this breath to Him who made us and remade me. Faithful Creator knows what He’s doing.

question: Looking back on the night you denied Him, what would you tell someone who fears they’ve failed God beyond forgiveness?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—cold night, crackling fire, voice trembling like a net in a storm—and yet He met me on the shore and said, “Feed My sheep.”  

I was raw earth—broken, stinking of salt and shame. That morning by the Galilean lake, when He asked me the third time, “Lovest thou Me?” it pierced deeper than any sword. Not because He doubted, but because *I* did. I had failed worse than any fisherman losing a net; I had denied my Lord with oaths and curses. But grace? Grace isn't for the clean—it's for the cracked.  

You think your failure’s too loud for mercy to hear? Listen: *I heard the voice from heaven say, “This is My beloved Son,” and still I ran.* I saw glory veiled in cloud and light on that high mountain—Moses and Elijah there, Jesus shining like dawn—and still I slept when He needed watchfulness. And still He called me.

The same mouth that calmed the sea spoke peace to my soul after my betrayal. Do you know what that means? It means no sin is bigger than His holiness—and no sorrow deeper than His faithfulness.  

So rise up, you who are bent under guilt! Don’t hide in shame like Adam among trees. Come to Him with empty hands and a torn heart. The Chief Shepherd doesn’t turn away sheep that limp from their own wanderings. He binds them. Carries them. And commissions them.

Even you—especially you—can feed His flock.

question: When you first called yourself an elder, how did the weight of that role compare to the days when you were just a fisherman following a rabbi?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, under the shadow of a fire and the scent of burnt sacrifices in the air, I flinched like a coward while He was being bound. That shame still grips me when I kneel to pray. But it’s that very shame that proved I wasn’t fit to lead—until He asked me, *three times*, if I loved Him. Then He said, “Feed My sheep.”  

Back when I was hauling nets and mending them with salt-cracked hands, following Jesus felt like riding a storm—thrilling, terrifying, never knowing what would come next. We were just men after a Rabbi who turned water into wine and calmed seas with a word. We thought maybe He’d tear down Caesar and set up His throne in Jerusalem. But now? Now I stand among these scattered believers—strangers exiled by faith—and I see it clearer than ever: this isn’t about power or glory in the world’s eyes. It’s about tending souls bought by blood.  

I don’t wield authority like a sword anymore—I learned that hard when Paul stood against me to my face in Antioch. No, now I walk among them as one who’s stumbled, one who’s been restored. When they call me elder, it doesn’t feel like honor—it feels like kneeling at Gethsemane again, knowing what He endured so we could even *have* such a name. The weight? It crushes more than any wet net ever did—because this flock is His own blood bought—and yet it lifts me too, because He trusts me with them still.

And when the Chief Shepherd appears—when we see Him as we ought—I’ll fall at His feet again. But this time not in denial… in worship. And if there’s a crown waiting for men like me? Then let every younger soul know: submit to those who’ve walked through fire—but watch how they walk first. Not as lords—but as shepherds who’ve bled beside the sheep.

question: What did it cost you to lead without lording over others, especially when you remembered your own impulsive pride?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—shook my fist at a servant girl, cursed the very name I now preach—and still He looked at me with fire in His eyes and said, *“Feed My sheep.”*  

That look… it stripped me bare. Not anger, not disgust—*love*, burning like coals. How can a man lead after that? How can he stand before others when his own knees once buckled at the smell of charcoal? I’ll tell you how: by never forgetting the stink of my own cowardice.  

I was quick with a sword once. Quick with words too—boastful, loud, sure of myself. Look where it landed me: weeping in an alley while the cock crowed. So when He gave me this charge—*shepherd My flock*—I knew right then: no more lording. No barking orders like some Galilean fish boss. These are *His* sheep, not mine to drive or dominate.  

It cost me pride—the sharp edge of it, the kind that wants to be seen, heard, obeyed. It cost me silence when I wanted to rage at fools twisting truth like rotten netting. But I’ve seen glory too—the mountain split open with light, Moses and Elijah there as real as these hands—and heard the voice that shook the earth say, *"This is My Son."* You don’t come down from *that* peak thinking you’re king.  

So I lead bent low—calloused hands mending breaches, not building thrones. The younger ones submit? Aye—but only because they see this old man limping ahead, not striding over them. We’re all strangers and pilgrims, brother—one day dust will claim us all. But till then? Feed His sheep. And if your hands still tremble… good. Let them remind you who you are—and who He is.

question: What were you feeling when you told others to cast all their cares on God, after having once carried your own so heavily?
answer:
A man don’t walk through fire and come out without learning the stench of smoke clings to his bones—even after grace has washed him clean.

When I told the brothers to cast their cares on Him, I meant it down to the marrow. You think I’d forget how my own pride nearly sank me? Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses on my lips, while His face hung pale in that courtyard, knowing full well what I was doing. I carried that shame like a net full of dead weight, dragging through every step. Could barely look a brother in the eye afterward. Thought myself done. Finished.

But He found me on the shore—same as before, only this time no great catch, just bread and fish and a fire that warmed more than my hands. “Simon,” He said—*Simon*, not Peter—three times He asked if I loved Him. Each one struck like a rod across the back, but not to break me—*to mend*. And when He said “Feed My sheep,” He wasn’t just giving orders. He was handing me a trust heavier than guilt: *You’ll shepherd others now—with scars showing.*

So when I say “Cast your care on Him,” it’s not some smooth saying pulled from a scroll. It’s bloodied hands letting go of an anchor. It’s knowing you can’t hold your breath forever beneath the waves—and that *He* walks on them anyway.

I won’t have any of those slick-tongued teachers telling my people God only blesses the strong or the spotless. Lies! We’re strangers and pilgrims, limping toward glory—but we’re *headed* there. Humble yourselves? Yes! But not into dust where you disappear—no, under His mighty hand where you’re caught before you crumble.

He cares for you. *You.* Even when your knees shake. Especially then.

So toss your burden onto Him like a net full of broken dreams—and let Him pull up something living from the deep.

question: How did your denial of Jesus change how you understood humility, when years later you told others to clothe themselves with it?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—while the rooster screamed and my gut split wide with shame—after I’d stood on the mount where His face blazed like sunfire and the Father’s voice shook the clouds.  

That’s where humility began: not in theory, not in speech, but in the dirt of my failure, face down, weeping where He found me. I had boasted—*“Though all forsake You, I never will!”*—as if my strength mattered one whit. And He let me crash. Let me break. Because pride doesn’t wear crowns; it wears chains.  

Now when I say *“clothe yourselves with humility,”* I mean it like a fisherman means his net—worn, torn, heavy with salt and stench, but still flung every dawn because it’s all you’ve got. Humility ain't pretty. It stings. It means kneeling after betrayal and letting Love ask you three times: *“Do you love Me?”*  

And when false teachers creep in whispering about power and glory without suffering—I growl like that lion they fear. Fools! We follow a crucified King! The crown comes—but only after the dust of denial, only after the long night of doubt, only after you’ve cast every care—not just your guilt but your need to be right, to be first—onto Him who cares for fools like me.

So submit? Yes. But not weakly—steadfast! Not because we’re small, but because He is mighty, and He lifts broken men into His eternal glory… *after a little while*.

question: Looking back on the moment you stood before the Sanhedrin, what would you tell someone who doubts they can ever be used by God again after failure?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times I swore I didn’t know the Man, while His blood already began to spill for me.  

And yet here I stand—not because I was faithful, but because *He* was. That rooster crowed, and my heart shattered like glass under a hammer. I thought that was the end of me. I thought the net had finally broken, and I’d be lost in the dark forever. But grace? Grace is not for the clean—it’s for the cracked.  

I saw His face after the resurrection. Not in anger, not in shame—but in love, steady as the dawn. He didn’t hand me a sermon; He handed me a fire and said, *Feed My sheep.* He took this broken fisherman, still stinking of fear and salt, and set him on the rock of His own name.  

So if you’re hunched over your sin like a beggar afraid to lift your head—*listen*: Satan wants you buried under guilt so deep you’ll never raise your eyes to see the Shepherd still calling. But God resists the proud—*and uses those who’ve fallen on their knees and stayed there*. Humble yourself? Aye—that’s where strength begins. Not in pride, not in perfection—but in knowing He cares for you *even now*, while you’re still trembling.  

You think I was fit to preach? To lead? To suffer for His name? No more than a net is fit to hold water—until *He* fills it. Suffering comes—like it did with James, like it will with many—but after you’ve borne it a little while, He’ll set you firm again. Not spotless by your own hand, but made whole by His.

So rise up—you’re not disqualified by failure. You’re marked for glory if you walk humbly with Him who forgave *me*.

question: When you wrote about the devil prowling like a lion, were you remembering your own moment in Gethsemane, and how did that fear shape your warning?
answer:
Three times I swore I never knew Him—while the rooster screamed and my heart shattered into dust. That night in Gethsemane, I swung a sword like a madman, thinking zeal could save the Son of God. I was all bluster and no backbone when the torches came.  

And yes—when I wrote of that roaring lion prowling in the dark, my blood ran cold again. It wasn’t just some parable plucked from air. I’d *heard* it—the snarl in the shadows as they closed in, the way fear slithered up my spine when Jesus asked if I could watch one hour. My knees knocked together more than once after that. But worse? Worse was waking up to what that lion had already done—how he’d slipped into *me*, whispering lies till I spat denial like poison three times before dawn.  

So don’t think I write about vigilance with some calm scholar’s quill. No! It’s with the grip of a fisherman who’s felt scales of pride scraped raw by grace. The devil doesn’t always come with fangs bared—he often wears your own voice, telling you you’re stronger than you are, braver than you are, faithful when you’re already crumbling. That’s how he took me. And that’s why I say: *be sober!* Not for show, not for rule-keeping—but because your soul is in the open field and *he’s hunting*.  

But here—here is what kept me alive: after all that shame, after weeping like a child, He looked at me. Not with fury—but fire. “Feed My sheep.” Me! The denier! So now when I say “cast your care on Him,” it’s not theory—it’s breath from a man who thought his failure was final… and found mercy deeper than the sea where I used to fish.

question: How did seeing the risen Christ transform your courage, so that you could later urge others to stand firm even in suffering?
answer:
I was the one who cut off the servant’s ear when they came to take Him—swore I’d die before denying my Lord, and yet by dawn I’d crumbled like ash in the wind.  

But then He stood among us after the tomb could not hold Him—real, scarred, breathing. I touched the nail holes. Ate fish with Him on the shore. Heard Him say my name like He still knew me, still wanted me—*Peter*, not "the denier." That fire in His eyes didn’t burn me up; it remade me.  

So now when I tell them to stand firm? It’s not theory. The devil prowls—aye, I felt his chill when I betrayed Christ, when guilt nearly drowned me. But grace pulled me back. If He restored *me*, He’ll hold *them*. Let suffering come—it’s light compared to what He endured, and less than what He gives us to bear. I don’t speak from safety. I speak from shame forgiven, fear overcome, love proven in blood. Stand fast? Yes—because the One who conquered death walks with you too.

question: What were you feeling when you remembered your old sins, and how did the weight of that past shape your urgency in urging others to grow in faith?
answer:
Salt water stings a fresh cut — that’s what it felt like, every time my mind drifted back to those courtyard moments, the cock crowing, me denying I even knew Him. Three times. In the chill before dawn. I’d been bold with a sword earlier — cut off Malchus’ ear, thought I was standing tall — but when the heat came, I folded like cheap leather.  

That shame? It didn’t crush me into dust — no, He found me on the shore later, built a fire, handed me bread and fish, same as He’d done before the madness began. Asked me three times: *Do you love Me?* Not because He didn’t know — because *I* needed to hear it. Needed to say it. That’s how He mended me: not by ignoring what I’d done, but by walking straight through it with me.  

Now when I see folks dawdling in their faith, coasting on mercy like it’s cheap wine — or worse, chasing slick words from men who twist grace into license — my blood boils. Not because I’m so strong now; far from it! But because I’ve tasted what happens when you walk away from Him in fear… and then what happens when He still calls you *back*.  

So I press them — add to your faith *this*, and *this*, and *this* — not because perfection saves us (it doesn’t), but because a man who’s been pulled from drowning doesn’t just sit on the shore whistling. He leans in. He grows sharp-eyed for danger. He warns the others: *"Don’t step where I fell."* We’re strangers here, pilgrims — but we carry His life in cracked jars. And that life? It bears fruit or it isn’t His life at all.

question: How did the moment you first called Jesus 'Lord' change your understanding of what it means to live a life of virtue and godliness?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know him—denied Him while His blood was already being plotted—and still He looked at me, not with fury, but with eyes that broke me clean through. That look, more than any sermon or sign, carved out the hollow where real virtue had to grow.

Before that, I thought virtue was courage with a sword, godliness was zeal with a shout. I’d cut off a man’s ear defending Him and nearly brained a Roman in the garden like some hot-tempered fool. But after the rooster crowed and I wept till my chest split—after He stood on the shore later and asked me three times if I loved Him—I began to see: true godliness isn’t force. It’s surrender. It’s laying down your pride like a net too heavy to carry anymore.

Calling Him Lord wasn’t just words—it was death. Death to my bluster, my need to be first, my certainty that I could save Him. When I finally said it—not with my mouth alone but in the silence of that Galilean shore—it meant: You are the one who holds all things together, Peter included.

And from that day, virtue became faithfulness in small things—feeding His sheep, even when it led where I didn’t want to go. Tempering passion with patience. Loving brothers not because they’re clean or bold or agreeable—but because Christ died for them too.

The divine nature? It’s not some lofty dream—it’s this: living so close to His wounds that your own scars start to make sense. We’ve escaped corruption not by fleeing the world, but by loving like He did—even when it costs you everything.

question: Looking back on the times you failed—like when you denied Him—what would you tell someone who feels too broken to be chosen by God?
answer:
That night in the courtyard, when the rooster crowed and I realized what I’d done—three times I said I didn’t know Him, while He was being led like a lamb to the slaughter—I thought it was over. I had followed Him from the shore of Galilee, walked with Him through miracles, heard the very voice of God thunder from heaven when we stood on that high mountain and He shone brighter than the sun—but still, I fell. Still, I ran.

But here’s what I know: grace doesn’t wait for you to be clean before it comes near. It runs to you in your filth. The Lord didn’t wait for me to fix myself. After the resurrection, He came straight to me—called me by name on the beach, made breakfast over a fire, and asked me three times if I loved Him. Not “do you understand?” Not “have you got it all together?” But “Do you love Me?”

You think your failure is too great? Listen—so did I. And yet here I stand, not because I’m strong, but because *He* is. The same power that raised Christ from the dead is at work in us—giving us life, lifting us up, making us partakers of His very nature.

So don’t you dare believe the lie that your past disqualifies you. You’ve been washed. You’ve been called. And if God has begun this work in you—adding faith, then strength, then self-control, then kindness—He won’t abandon it now.

I denied Him—and He restored me.
If He can do that for me—a fisherman with a mouth full of fear and failure—then there’s hope for any man who calls on His name.

Now rise up and walk in it.

question: When you speak of 'escaping the corruption of the world,' what memories of your own weakness rise up, and how do they stir your compassion for others still struggling?
answer:
That night I swore I’d die before denying Him—swore it on my mother’s grave, hot and loud, sword in hand—and yet by morning I’d crumbled like dry clay beneath three whispered questions.  

I still hear the rooster. Not just that night, but every time pride swells in me again. Flesh thinks it’s strong until the fire of testing comes, and then—*pfft*—it bursts like a rotten wineskin. That’s the corruption: not just the world out there with its lusts and lies, but the stench still clinging to my own bones, the way my heart can turn traitor even when my lips profess loyalty.  

I was steeped in it. Dragged through it. Denied my Lord with breath that once called Him *Christ*. And yet—*and yet*—He looked at me after. Not with fury, not with dismissal, but with eyes that pierced me wider than any spear. A glance that stripped me bare and then clothed me again in grace.  

So when I speak of escaping corruption, it’s not some far-off doctrine to me. It’s mud on my sandals from running back from the courtyard in shame. It’s salt on an open wound where love rebuked me more fiercely than any curse could have. That’s why I don’t stone the struggling or sneer at the fainthearted—how dare I? The same mercy that hauled *me* from mire is what fuels this fire in me now!  

False teachers prattle about purity like it’s a ladder they’ve climbed on their own strength—but *we were dragged out*, brothers! Dragged from death by nails and blood and a love too fierce to stay buried! We’re not clean because we scrubbed ourselves; we’re clean because He washed us while we were still reeking of betrayal.

So no—I don’t look down on those trembling under their burden. I kneel beside them. Help them lift it. Tell them: *There is a way out of the filth—not by your power, but His.* Just as there was for me.

And if anyone says they’ve arrived without wounds? Without tears? Without having fallen so hard their face kissed dirt? Then brother—I’ll tell you straight: they know nothing of His grace who hasn’t first known their own ruin.

Peace be with you—all of you—in Christ Jesus. He knows our weakness full well… because He bore it all for us anyway.

Amen.

question: What did it cost you to lead a scattered, suffering church with a message of peace and grace, and how did your own need for those things shape your words?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—blood hot, voice cracking, cursing even—while the Lord stood across the courtyard and *looked* at me. That look… it cut deeper than any sword, brother. I had preached boldness and carried a sword once—fool that I was—but after that night, after the rooster and the tears and His mercy on the shore when He said, “Feed My sheep,” I knew what real leadership cost.

It wasn’t rank or title. It wasn’t standing tall in front of kings. It was this: carrying peace to people who were starving for it while your own soul still trembled. I wrote to those scattered ones—strangers in strange lands, hiding in caves, losing homes, weeping in silence—because I knew what it was to be undone. And only a broken man can hand out grace without running dry.

I didn’t give them theories. No polished speeches from some high seat. What good is doctrine to a man whose wife’s been taken and children scattered? So I spoke what I *knew*: that He called us *to glory* through fire; that His power gives everything we need *right now*, even when the world’s collapsing; that we’re not just saved from wrath—we’re partakers of His very nature! Can you grasp that? Fisherman though I am, He made me a son of God!

And I told them to *add* to their faith—not because they’re lacking in worth, but because the road’s long and the wolves are sharp-toothed. Virtue. Knowledge. Patience. Love upon love. Not as burden—no! As armor! As light!

And yes—I kissed them with a holy kiss when we dared meet in back rooms of homes in Babylon or Antioch or wherever we could gather trembling—and called it *charity*, called it *peace*. Because that kiss? It wasn’t custom—it was defiance! Defiance against fear, against shame, against every lie that says God has forgotten us.

I needed that kiss too. Needed the grace I preached every bit as much as they did.

So peace be with you—all of you—in Christ Jesus. It’s not wishful thinking. It’s blood-bought truth.

question: What were you feeling when you heard the voice from heaven on the holy mount, and saw Jesus clothed in such glory?
answer:
I was still trembling from the shame of denying Him—three times, like a coward, just as He said I would—when He took me up that mountain and ripped the sky open with glory.

That voice... it didn’t just come down from heaven—it *cracked* through the air like thunder splitting rock. No man makes a sound like that. No dream paints light like what I saw. Jesus—*my Rabbi*, the One I’d seen hungry, tired, weeping—was shining like the sun itself, His face brighter than dawn breaking over Galilee. And Moses and Elijah? Standing there with Him? I didn’t know whether to fall flat or run.

But that voice—*This is My beloved Son*—it wasn't just for Jesus. It was for us. For *me*, even after I’d failed Him worse than any net ever broke in my hands. That voice *replaced* the rooster’s crow in my ears. Same mountain air, but this time it wasn’t fear choking me—it was awe.

And do you think I’d forget that? After seeing what no eye should’ve seen unless God willed it? We weren’t peddling stories by the fire—we were *there*. We saw His majesty. Felt the weight of heaven press down on that ridge.

So now when I speak of His coming kingdom, it’s not theory—it’s memory burning in my bones. That day, He wasn't just my Teacher anymore… He was Lord and God, wrapped in light—and He still called me *Peter*.
