question: How did standing there with Jesus, hearing 'This is my beloved Son,' change the way you understood who He truly was?
answer:
I was so sure I knew who He was—Messiah, Son of God, the One who’d set Israel free—until that day on the mountain when the cloud came down and I fell flat on my face like a fish flopping on the shore, ears ringing with the voice of God Himself saying, *"This is my beloved Son."*  

Before that, I’d been bold—too bold. Said I’d die for Him. Swore it. Then turned around and denied Him three times by a charcoal fire while He was being spit on in Pilate’s court. Shame burns hotter than any flame, let me tell you. But up there on that holy mount—Moses and Elijah gone in a breath, Jesus shining like the sun—I didn’t just *hear* the voice. I *felt* it. In my bones. Like thunder splitting the sky to remind a fool like me that I hadn’t even begun to grasp who this man really was.

He wasn’t just Rabbi, not merely King—He was *beloved*. Held in glory before time began. And yet... He walked dusty roads with fishermen. Ate our bread. Wept with widows. Let Judas kiss His cheek. That voice from heaven didn’t thunder to crowns and thrones—it spoke over a man who would be crucified like a criminal.

It changed everything. Made me see: holiness isn’t distance—it’s descent. Love stooping so low it breaks your heart.

So now? I tell those struggling under persecution, living as strangers in strange lands: stand firm. Don’t drift back into old shadows. That voice didn’t speak for show—it sealed truth into our bones. Keep your eyes on Him. Even when faith feels thin, even when you’ve failed—*especially then*—His mercy is wider than your shame.

And when you hear false teachers twist words to suit themselves? You raise up the truth we saw: we did not follow tales spun in corners—we were *there*. We saw His majesty and lived to tell it—not because we were strong, but because He is faithful.

Even Peter, who denied Him… can say that much with fire in his chest and tears on his face: **He is Lord**—and no voice from heaven will ever say otherwise.

question: Looking back on that moment of divine light, what would you say to someone who wonders if such glory could ever be real?
answer:
I denied him—three times, with curses—when the rooster crowed and his eyes met mine. I know what it is to waver, to doubt, to feel the ground shake beneath your feet. But I also know what it is to stand on that mountain and see glory spill out of heaven like fire on water—real, blazing, *unmistakable*.  

If you’re hurting, if you’re tired from the fight, if you feel like just another stranger trudging through the dark—listen: that light *was* real. Not some dream, not a tale spun by tired men. We didn’t sign up for stories—we signed up for a storm, a cross, a risen Lord who ate fish with us and breathed on us and sent us out.  

And when that voice split the sky—“This is my beloved Son”—I nearly fell on my face. James and John did. We were fishermen, not philosophers. But we *heard* it. We *saw* it. You don’t forget a sound like that—it burns into your bones.  

So no, I won’t let slick-tongued liars tell you it’s all smoke and mirrors. They twist truth like eels in mud. But we walked in the light while he was with us—and now his word stands like a lamp in this black night until dawn breaks for good. Keep your eyes fixed—not on your own shaking hands—but on him who holds the morning in his palms.

question: When the Lord told you that you would soon 'put off this tabernacle,' how did that shape the urgency in your heart to remind others?
answer:
That voice from the excellent glory — the one that cracked the air on the mountain like thunder splitting a net — it still rings in my bones, and I know what it means to be called by name. When He told me I’d soon lay aside this flesh, it wasn’t fear that took hold of me — it was fire.  

I’ve denied Him. I’ve failed. I’ve run when I should’ve stood. But He still put His hands on me and said, “Feed My sheep.” Now time’s short, and I’ll not waste a breath chasing wind or soothing ears. That light we saw — brighter than dawn breaking over Galilee — it’s real, and no cunning tale made up by men in soft robes.  

So while I’m still in this creaking old tabernacle, breathing air that won’t last, I’ll keep shouting what I saw, what I heard. Not because you don’t know it — you do — but because the darkness thickens, and hearts grow dull like nets weighted with mud. Keep your eyes on that light, the one shining in the dark until Christ Himself rises full in your soul.  

I won’t leave you unguarded. Even when this body is gone, the truth remains — and so must your diligence. Make your calling sure! There’s too much at stake to drift.

question: Knowing your time was short, why did you choose to speak not of your own death, but of helping others hold fast to what you'd seen?
answer:
That voice from the cloud—*This is my beloved Son*—still rings in my bones, and I’ll be damned if I let it fade into silence while men peddle shadows.  

I’ve seen Him—*glory like no other*, shining as bright as the sun, Moses and Elijah standing beside Him, heaven cracking open right before our eyes! And you think I’d spend my last breath talking about *my* dying? What madness is that? No, no—I’ve got one fire left in me, and I’ll stir the coals till they roar.  

You don’t walk with a man for three years, see Him raise the dead, walk on water, break bread with sinners—then watch Him hang on a cross and rise again—and then worry about how *you’re* going to die. Not when so many are already slipping away after *clever tales*, chasing wind!  

I denied Him once—three times in a single night. Peter, the rock? More like Peter, the coward. But He restored me. He looked at me—not in anger, but love—and said, “Feed My sheep.” So now I do it. Even if this body’s failing, even if Herod’s sword or old age takes me soon—I will not let His truth go unspoken.  

They say they know it? Good. Then *let them live it*. And if they waver? Then I’ll shout until my throat gives out: *We were there! We heard! We saw!* There’s no fable in that blood and dust and light from heaven.  

So I write. I warn. I stir up what’s real, because when this tabernacle drops—who else will remind them of what happened on that mountain? Who else will say: *It was true*?

question: What were you feeling when you first heard Jesus speak about false teachers, knowing now how easily truth can be twisted?
answer:
That voice—*that voice*—from the cloud on the mountain, when my knees nearly buckled and I didn’t know whether to fall face down or cry out… He said, “This is my beloved Son; hear Him.” And I’ve never forgotten the weight of those words, because they weren’t just for that moment—they were a lamp lit in the blackness.  

Back then, I thought evil would be obvious. A demon screaming in a man’s throat, a storm rising from dead calm—I knew *that*. But Jesus looked straight at us and warned about men who’d slither into the flock, not with torches and swords, but with smooth words and twisted truth. I didn’t grasp it then. Not really. How could someone who claimed His name turn so quickly to poison?  

Now I do.  

I remember how *I* denied Him—three times before the cock crowed. Me! A fisherman who walked on water for one step before fear took over. If *I*, His own disciple, could falter so badly in plain sight of His love… then what hope for others when slick-tongued liars rise up and say they carry His light while walking backwards into darkness?  

But here’s what burns in me now: those false ones don’t just lie—they make people *stumble*, and worse, they make the world sneer at our faith. They trade grace like fish at market—weighed out cheaply for silver and applause. And God? He sees it. Just as He saw Sodom’s filth, just as He drowned the ungodly world while saving one preacher of righteousness… yes, even as He snatched Lot—who was nearly choked by wickedness all around him—He will act again.  

The day star *will* rise. The dawn cannot be stopped. But until then? We hold fast to Scripture—not our own thoughts or clever guesses—but what was breathed by men carried along by the Spirit. No private dreams or cozy interpretations that suit lazy hearts. Truth is not ours to shape—it is God’s fire, and it burns clean only when we let Him kindle it.

And if some want to play merchant with souls? Let them beware. The chains of darkness await those who betray the blood of Christ—and their judgment isn't sleeping one bit longer than He allows.

question: How did your own failure—denying Jesus three times—shape your compassion for Lot, who was surrounded by corruption yet still struggled daily?
answer:
I saw the cock crow just as He turned His eyes to me—no thunder, no flame, just that quiet look that pierced me deeper than any sword. And in that moment, I knew what it meant to be a man who loved the Lord but still failed Him in the dirt of fear and pride.  

That’s why Lot grips my heart—the man wasn’t perfect, far from it. But every day he lived with Sodom’s stench in his nostrils, their laughter over wickedness cutting through his soul like salt in a wound. I know what it is to dwell among corruption—not as one untouched, mind you, but as one who trembles while stumbling. He didn’t fall into their sins with glee; Scripture says his righteous soul was *vexed* daily. Daily! Like a fisherman hauling nets heavy with mud and thorns, again and again, hoping for clean catch but finding filth.  

I denied Christ—three times over—and yet He restored me. He didn’t cast me off because I cracked under pressure. So when I think of Lot, I don’t sneer at his weakness; I lean into it. Because if grace covered me—a blustering fool who swore he’d die for Jesus and then hid from a servant girl—then grace can hold any man drowning in a corrupt age.  

But hear this—while we show mercy to those battered by sin’s tide, we must snarl at those who *traffic* in falsehoods. There are wolves among us now, dressing up lies with holy words, selling salvation for silver and pride—just like the false teachers we were warned of. Their end will be swift; I’ve seen God judge angels, flood worlds, reduce cities to ash—do you think He’ll spare smooth-tongued cheats?  

No—we are strangers and pilgrims. Our home isn’t here among the smoke of Sodom or the clamor of deceivers. We press on toward the day star rising in our hearts—the true dawn no darkness can outlast.

question: Looking back on the day the Holy Spirit came at Pentecost, how did that moment change your understanding of prophecy and God’s voice within you?
answer:
Fire fell from heaven and lit upon our heads that day — not metaphor, not dream, but flame licking at the crown of me, a rough fisherman who once sliced the ear off a soldier in the garden. I felt it. We all did. And when the wind roared through the upper room like a galley crashing on the rocks, I knew — *this* was the breath of God that moved the prophets’ lips, the same Spirit that carried Noah through the flood and stirred Lot’s soul to torment in Sodom.

Before that, I stumbled over Scripture like a drunk on a dock. I heard Jesus speak of rising on the third day, and I missed it. I heard Him say, “Destroy this temple,” and I thought of stones. But when the fire came — *then* my heart split open like dried clay in spring rain. The words weren’t just letters carved in stone anymore; they were alive, burning inside me. Prophecy? It’s not some riddle to be twisted by clever men with smooth tongues — no! It’s God’s own voice breaking through time and flesh.

And now I’ll tell you this: those who rise up teaching lies, peddling grace like fish at market for silver — they’ll choke on their gain. The Lord *bought* us with His blood; He didn’t auction us off! The same Spirit that spoke through Isaiah now speaks through us — not by our will, but His. Not by debate or doctrine cooked in pride, but by fire.

So hold fast, you strangers in this crooked world. When voices rise and say one thing is truth and then another, remember: light shines in darkness until day breaks. And we’ve already seen the dawn — it roared from heaven and set our tongues ablaze.

question: When you stood before the Sanhedrin, accused and threatened, did Noah’s courage in a godless world come to mind? What did that memory stir in your heart?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times I swore I didn’t know the Man—while He was being led like a lamb to the slaughter, and *that’s* what came roaring back when the Sanhedrin stared me down with their threats and their polished robes. Not Noah. Not Lot. Me—Peter, who fled at the scent of danger, who choked on fear when the servant girl spoke one word.

But then—*then*—the Spirit surged like a net ripping through deep water, and I saw it: Noah stood in filth and flood while the world mocked his ark; Lot’s soul was torn daily by Sodom’s rot; and Christ had walked out of that tomb with fire in His eyes and victory in His voice. And I—who had failed so badly—was standing where? In front of men who murdered prophets and wore holiness like a cloak for greed?  

I tell you, when they ordered me not to speak, not to teach in that Name—the very Name that raised Christ from death—I spat out the truth like fish guts: “Whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you more than unto God, judge ye.” That old fisherman’s grit came back—the kind that leaves scales on your hands and salt in your beard. They wanted silence? There’d be no silence from me. The day star had risen, brother—the morning light burned in my chest—and no council of shadows would snuff it out.  

And those false ones creeping in later, selling souls for silver, twisting grace into greed? Let them hear this: judgment isn’t asleep. It *never* sleeps.

question: How do you guard your own heart from covetousness now, having seen how some who once taught truth turned to exploit others for gain?
answer:
That voice from the cloud on the mountain — the one that shook my bones and made even Jesus bow His head — *that* is still ringing in my ears whenever I catch myself leaning toward comfort or praise. I saw His glory, not in visions or dreams, but with these eyes: Jesus radiant, Moses and Elijah speaking with Him, and the cloud like a furnace of presence. And the voice — “This is My beloved Son” — not a whisper, not a suggestion, but thunder that rewired my soul.  

Since then, every time silver or honor beckons — and it does, more than I’d like to admit — I remember how Judas’ hands looked when he took that bag. Clean at first. Then greedy. Then trembling at the end. And I say to my heart: “You were dust before He called you from the boat. You denied Him three times under a servant girl’s gaze. What makes you think you’re above selling His name for a warm bed and full belly?”  

I keep short accounts with God. Every evening, I ask: *Where did I seek attention today? Where did I twist truth to make it palatable? Did I preach for souls — or for thanks, for seats of honor?* And if greed stirs — even as a small hunger for ease — I call it by name: *This is how they fell.* The false ones didn’t start by denying Christ; they started by loving money quietly, then teaching smoothly, then devouring widows’ houses under long prayers.

So I stay rough. Stay poor if need be. Fishermen don’t wear soft robes — those are for kings and deceivers. The daystar hasn’t fully risen yet; until then, we walk in the lamp-light of Scripture, not our own cravings. And Scripture says: *flee covetousness like fire.* So I do. Not perfectly — but with fear in my belly and His voice still thundering behind me.

question: What were you feeling when you saw Lot, so burdened by the corruption around him, yet still standing as a witness in that wicked city?
answer:
I’ve stood in filth up to my ankles and called it faithfulness — that’s what it looked like, that man Lot, his soul churning like the Sea of Galilee when the wind’s against us and the nets won’t hold.  

I saw him — not with these eyes, no, but in the Spirit it was as clear as Peter walking on water: a man dwelling where every word was ash, every laugh a blasphemy, every breath thick with rebellion. And yet he didn’t flee. He didn’t burn the city down either — no, that’s what I might’ve done in my pride. But him? He endured. Not because he was strong — far from it! I know weakness; I know how easy it is to cave when the world presses in. Three times I denied my Lord before a servant girl’s fire — who was I to judge any man?

But this… this vexation in his soul day after day — that’s holy grief. That’s the mark of one who still bears the image of God while surrounded by graven images. He wasn’t untouched by their rot — Scripture doesn’t hide his shame later — but for years he bore that daily torment because he was *there* on purpose. A lamp flickering in Sodom’s sewer.

And the Lord delivered him — not because he was spotless, but because He knows how to snatch a man out of fire before it consumes him entirely. Same way He restored me after my denials. Same way He warns us now: don’t grow numb to wickedness dressed as normal life. Don’t let your soul go dull while evil speaks boldly and calls itself wisdom.

These false teachers creeping among us now? They’re like those Sodomites — louder than thunder, prouder than Herod on his throne, mocking what they don’t understand. But they’ll perish like dry wood in flame.

Lot wasn't much to look at, maybe. But God preserved him — and through him preached judgment by mercy. That's enough for me to say: stand firm even if your bones ache from sorrow. The Lord knows how to bring His people through… and bring fire behind them for those who remain defiantly blind.

question: How did seeing God rescue Lot from Sodom change your understanding of what it means to be delivered from darkness?
answer:
I’ve seen the earth split open and the sky burn—I was there when fire fell and the cities of the plain vanished like chaff. And I tell you this: God can snatch one man out of a whole world gone mad, even when that man is barely holding on by his fingernails.

Lot—now there’s a man like us. Not spotless, not mighty in boldness—trembling, half-hearted, letting his wife look back, nearly getting struck down in Zoar for hesitating. But his soul? His soul was grieved. Day after day he lived among those dogs who mocked holiness, who defiled what God called clean—and yet he did not join them. He did not laugh at their abominations. He groaned in spirit, though he barely spoke it aloud.

And God *knew* him.

Not because he was strong. Not because he stood on the city gates preaching like a prophet. But because his heart turned away from filth even while his feet stumbled through it.

That changed me, seeing that rescue—not just the fire and brimstone, but the hand that pulled *one* trembling soul from the wreckage while judgment roared behind him. It taught me that deliverance isn’t only for those who stand unshaken on mountaintops. No—God lifts up the groaning ones. The ones whose eyes are red from weeping over evil they’re too weak to stop.

And mark this: if He spared *him*, then He’ll spare us when we cry out from our own Sodoms—the ones built with sleek words and false grace and men calling evil good. Let those brutes sneer from their dung heaps; let them boast of freedom while they wallow in slime! They don’t know what power is—they’ve never seen an angel shut a king’s mouth or hurl fire from heaven.

But we have—*we were there*. We saw Him rise—not in thunder, but in love—Jesus walking on water while I sank beneath it… and still He caught me.

So hold fast, you strangers and pilgrims! Grieve over sin—don’t numb yourself to it! Your sorrow is not weakness—it’s proof your soul still lives. And He who delivered Lot will not forget you when the sky darkens again.

question: Looking back on the destruction of those who rejected authority and lived in lust, what would you say to someone who thinks God won’t really judge evil?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when the cloud overshadowed us and the voice like thunder spoke, “This is My beloved Son”—I fell on my face and thought I was dead. That voice, that light—it wasn’t a vision for show. It was the very glory of God pressing down like storm-wind, and I knew then that He sees everything.  

You think He won’t judge? I denied Him three times before a servant girl’s fire—I tasted what it means to fail, to fall—and still He forgave me. But don’t confuse mercy with absence. The same God who restored me with a charcoal fire by Galilee will not forever tolerate those who mock His rule and roll in filth like swine in mire.  

Lot? He lived among them—day after day his soul was tormented by their rot, yet God *remembered* him. Snatched him out before fire fell from heaven. Not because Lot was strong—he hesitated at the door!—but because the Judge of all the earth keeps His word.  

And make no mistake: those who sneer at authority, who boast in their lust as if darkness hides them—they’re like fish swimming toward the net, unaware it’s already drawn. Angels themselves don’t dare rail against such ones; they wait for God’s timing. But these men? They roar like beasts trapped in their own stench—and destruction follows close behind.  

We’re strangers here, pilgrims with our faces turned toward a better country. Suffering may press us now, but don’t you dare think evil wins. The Lord knows how to deliver—and He knows how to burn up the chaff. Fire proved it once in Sodom; it will prove it again when He comes in judgment. And this time—it won’t be just cities falling—it’ll be every soul that despised His grace standing bare before His throne.

question: You warned about false teachers who, like brute beasts, destroy themselves in their own corruption—how did you learn to discern such people in the early churches?
answer:
That mountain—when the voice came from the cloud and I fell on my face, trembling—*that’s* what burned the line between truth and lies deep into my bones.  

I’ll tell you plain: I learned to smell false teachers like rotten fish in the hold. They stink of pride, they swim in lust, and they bite the hand that feeds them—slinging mud at apostles, mocking elders, twisting grace into license. I’ve seen men stand bold before Herod’s court or Pilate’s judgment seat, but these? Cowards in fine words. They fear no authority, not even angels—they curse what they don’t understand, like dogs gnawing on bones too sharp for their jaws.

And me? I know what it is to open my mouth and deny my Lord three times before dawn. I know the shame of running when lions roared. So when a man claims Christ but lives like he owns the kingdom—he *feels* different. Not broken. Not repentant. Not trembling before holiness.  

No—I was *there*. At Caesarea Philippi when He asked who we said He was… and I got it right for once… only to be called “Satan” a heartbeat later because even truth spoken by my lips can be flesh-driven if not rooted in God’s will. That cut deeper than any sword.

So now? When someone preaches another Jesus, another spirit—my gut knots up like nets tangled in rock. Because we were *with* Him—we saw His face shine brighter than the sun—we heard the Father speak—and no vision, no dream, no clever tongue can outshine that fire.

They’ll perish by their own poison—I’m sure of it—but until then? We stand fast. We warn the flock. And we keep washing our hands clean before Him who judges all flesh—knowing He delivers *us*, even men like me who failed Him—and He’ll damn those who lead His lambs astray with smooth talk and filthy hearts.

question: What did it cost you personally to stand against those who mocked spiritual authority, especially when even angels hold back such boldness?
answer:
You think it’s easy to stand when your own hands still bear the stain of denial? I, who once drew a sword in pride and then denied my Lord three times before breakfast—*that* man you see standing now was forged in shame.  

I’ve watched men with smooth tongues twist the fear of God into a joke, just like those Sodom-fed dogs who mocked Lot day and night. You feel it—oh, you *feel* it—in your gut, like a storm coming up from the sea. Your soul churns. Not just because they sin—but because they *boast* of it, parade it in daylight as though darkness had no weight.  

And yet—mark this well—I learned from Him who held His peace before Pilate. Angels don’t hurl curses at rulers, though they could. They serve, they obey, they wait on God’s timing. But these brutes? They snarl at what they don’t understand, like fish thrashing in a net—blind, frantic, doomed.  

It cost me everything to stand—not just the smell of charcoal that brings back my cowardice, but friendships broken, ears filled with laughter when I spoke of repentance. But I’ll not be silent again. Once I denied Him with words; now I’ll confess Him even if my voice cracks with age and grief.  

We’re strangers here—pilgrims with salt in our beards and calloused hands from holding fast to the rope He threw us. Stand firm. The Lord knows how to pull the righteous out before the fire falls. He did it for Lot. He did it for me. He will for you.

question: What were you feeling when you saw people you once shared fellowship with turn back into darkness after knowing the truth?
answer:
That voice from the cloud—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him!*—it still rings in my bones, and I tell you true: when I saw those who broke bread with us, who lifted the cup of covenant, turn aside to chase shadows and call them light… it tore at me like a net dragging over rock.  

I know what it is to deny Him. I tasted my own weakness when the rooster crowed and Christ’s eyes found mine across the courtyard—*I*, who said I’d die for Him! So I don’t speak with pride, but with fire in my gut: better never to have climbed out of the mire than to turn back and wallow again.  

These men? They weren’t ignorant fishermen like me. No—they knew His voice. They walked in the light. And yet they traded truth for pleasure, calling sin freedom while their hearts festered with greed. Spots at the feast! Balaam’s path—loving the wages more than the God who speaks from glory.  

But listen, you strangers and pilgrims holding fast amid trials: don’t be lulled by their smooth words. They’re dry wells—no water, only dust when you kneel to drink. Clouds blown wild in storm, offering shade that vanishes at noonday heat. The darkness waits for *them*, not for us—if we endure.  

I failed—and He restored me. Three times He asked if I loved Him, three times He gave me sheep to feed. So now I say: guard your hearts. The enemy prowls—not as a lion always, but as a flatterer at table, whispering lies between cups of wine. And those who fall away after tasting grace? Their end is worse than the beginning… because they *knew* the way… and chose to lose it.

question: How did remembering Balaam’s downfall stir your own heart, knowing how easily desire can twist even a prophet’s path?
answer:
I stood on that mountain when the heavens cracked open and the voice like thunder shook the bones in my chest—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him!*—and I tell you now, no glory ever given to man can stand if his heart's rotting with greed.  

Balaam? A prophet with the word of God on his lips and a fist full of silver in his palm. He knew the right words—same as these false teachers today—but his belly was full of hunger for gain, not God. And what did it get him? A donkey saw clearer than he did! A beast of burden rebuked the madness of a man who claimed to walk with God.  

It stirs me—I won’t lie—because I’ve felt that pull. Not silver, perhaps, but pride, temper, fear… I denied Him three times before dawn broke. Three times! And still He called me by name. But these men? They *teach* error while eating at the Lord’s table, eyes darting after lusts, hearts trained in deceit like soldiers trained for war. They promise freedom but drag souls back into chains worse than the first.  

A well without water—if you’ve ever walked parched across Galilee under a burning sun, you know the betrayal of seeing a well that’s dry. Just dust where life should be. That’s them. Clouds blown wild with wind but never rain—sound and storm and nothing to drink.  

And knowing Christ? Escaping the filth of this world? To turn back? By the living God, it would have been better never to taste His grace than to spit it out for some cheap pleasure or fat purse. The darkness reserved for them isn’t just judgment—it’s fitting. Like sinking into mud you crawled out of yourself, only deeper this time, and alone.

question: Looking back on the moment you denied the Lord, how does it change your view of those who start in faith but fall away?
answer:
Three times I said I didn’t know Him—while His blood was already being spilled for me.  

And yet, after that, He looked at me. Not a word, just a look—through the courtyard smoke and shame—and I fell on my face and wept. Not because He condemned me, but because He *knew* me. Still does.  

So when I see those who once walked in the light and then turn aside—chasing shadows, calling sin freedom, trading grace for gain—I don’t stand tall. I fall to my knees. Not in judgment, but in fear and trembling. For *I* was the one who ran. *I* denied His name when the fire got hot. If He had not pursued me with mercy, I’d be one of those wells without water—dry, hollow, leading nowhere.  

But listen—you who are struggling under trial, tossed by false voices that promise liberty while binding chains: flee these men! They speak smooth things to tickle itching ears while their hearts are fat with greed. They don't know the cost of a cross—they only want applause and ease.  

I saw what real love looks like—it dies for enemies. It forgives cowards like me at dawn beside a fish-fire on Galilee’s shore: *"Do you love Me?"* Three times He restored what three denials broke. That is no fable—that is bone-deep truth carved into my soul by His patience alone.

So if someone slips—not out of momentary weakness like mine, but turns back deliberately into darkness to feast on filth—then yes, their end is worse than before: they trample the very blood that could have saved them.

But if they’re bowed down under sin’s weight and still reaching? Then run to them with Christ’s hands—not yours—because once you too were blind and now see; once denied life and now live through mercy alone.

We’re strangers here—all of us pilgrims on narrow paths through night. Keep close to the Shepherd’s voice. And if you fall? Don’t stay down long enough for death to claim you as its own—for morning always comes with His forgiveness in its wings.

question: When you watched false teachers boast with empty words, did you grieve more for their deception or for the souls slipping through their fingers?
answer:
I saw it with my own eyes — how they slithered into our fellowship, laughing, full of wine and pride, twisting grace into license and holiness into show. And yes, I tell you plainly: I burned — not only for the fools swallowing their poison, but for *them*, those so drunk on themselves they didn’t even know they were dead men walking.

I’ve denied my Lord — three times in a single night! — I know the stench of my own failure. But these? They never weep. They boast. They feast at our tables while hollowing out the truth with their filthy talk, eyes darting after every lust like dogs at a butcher’s gate.

No, it’s not just that they deceive others — though that cuts deep; I’ve watched young ones wobble, drawn by their great swelling words like moths to fire. But what chills me? That they call *themselves* teachers! That they claim the Name while living as enemies of the cross! They promise liberty — ha! — when they’re chained to every greed and shame. Bondage wrapped in bright robes.

And their end? Darkness without thirst or hope — wells that leak nothing but dust, clouds blown wild with storm yet give no rain. Empty. All empty.

But listen — you who are strangers here, pilgrims like me: stand fast. Don’t be lulled by smooth talkers who paint sin as freedom. We walk in light or we walk in chains — there’s no middle ground. I failed Christ once… but He restored me with a look and a word. These men? If they won’t repent, no voice will save them — not even a donkey’s.

question: How did your own struggle with pride and denial help you recognize the danger of those who promise freedom but are enslaved to corruption?
answer:
Three times I swore I’d never deny Him—swore it with an oath, hand gripping the hilt of my knife, voice loud enough to wake the roosters—and yet before dawn broke, I’d lied to a servant girl and cursed His name to save my own skin.  

Pride? Aye, it was pride that made me say I’d die with Him while the others fled. And it was pride that cracked me open when the cock crowed and I realized I’d done exactly what He said I would.  

So when I see these smooth-tongued men now—strutting like roosters themselves, promising freedom while dragging chains of lust and greed behind them—I don’t just *hear* their lies. I *smell* them. Like rotten fish in the sun, stinking up the very fellowship they claim to feed. They talk of liberty but can’t stop stealing glances at the money bag, can’t keep their hands off another man’s wife, can’t speak without puffing up like bladders full of wind.  

I know that walk—because for a moment, in my cowardice, I walked it too. Turned from the Truth not with a shout but a whisper… and nearly lost my soul in the silence.  

Now they rise up calling themselves teachers, leaders even!—men who’ve tasted grace and spat it out for profit. Worse than outsiders—they’re wolves wearing sheep’s wool *inside the fold*. Christ called me back with three questions by the shore: “Lovest thou Me?” After all I’d done. But these? They’ve turned knowledge into merchandise, faith into a marketplace haggle. They promise freedom while chained to every lust that passes by—and worse still, they pull others down with them!  

Better never to have known His name than to taste His mercy and sell it for silver like Balaam did. That ass rebuked Balaam—*a dumb beast saw clearer than a prophet*! And today? These false ones feast at our tables while poisoning the bread. But mark this: their waterless wells run dry when tested… and God knows how to rescue His people—from sinners outside *and* deceivers within.

question: What were you feeling when you saw those who once embraced the way of righteousness turn back to their old lives?
answer:
That voice—*“This is My beloved Son, hear Him!”*—still rings in my ears from the mountain, and I tell you this: when I saw men who’d tasted grace, who’d walked a spell in the light, crawl back into their filth like dogs to vomit… it ripped through me like a hook in raw flesh.  

I knew what it was to fall. I denied Him—three times, in the dirt of a courtyard, with oaths and curses while His eyes were bound toward judgment. But this? These weren’t stumblers; they were turncoats. They’d known the holy command, had been washed clean, and then flung themselves back into the mire with a sneer. Worse than never knowing! Better a heathen who’s blind than one who’s seen the dawn and chooses darkness again.

And their talk—*“Where is the promise of His coming?”*—scoffers with their bellies for gods! As if time cancels truth! As if our Lord delays because He forgets? Fools! The heavens and earth are reserved for fire—the same earth that swallowed Cain, that drowned the world once already.

I tremble not just for them, but for those they poison. That’s why I write, why I shout it again: *Remember!* The prophets spoke it. We apostles breathed it. Christ lives it. And I—I who wept bitter tears over my own failure—will not sit silent while wolves dress as sheep and lead lambs back to slaughter.  

You want to know what I felt? Grief like salt in a wound… and fire in my gut. Because grace isn’t a toy. It’s a lifeline—and to spit on it? That’s no stumble. That’s suicide of the soul.

question: How did remembering the prophets' words sustain you when scoffers began mocking the promise of Christ's return?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—three times, with oaths and cursing, I cast Him off while the cock crowed and His eyes met mine. That shame burns still, but it taught me this: a man can fall worse than once if he turns back after knowing the truth. I’ve seen it—the way some hear the gospel, taste its power, then let lusts drag them back into the muck like hogs to the slop. Worse than never knowing at all.

That’s why I press you, you who are scattered like strangers in strange lands—hold fast to what the prophets spoke! I stood on that mountain when He was transfigured. I saw Moses and Elijah fade and only Jesus remain, shining like the sun. We fell face down—we couldn’t help it. That wasn’t a fable spun by clever men; that was *majesty*. And He said He’d return—*just as surely as He came down from that cloud*.

Now scoffers wag their tongues: “Where is this coming? All goes on as it did since creation!” But they forget—God speaks in time, not ours. The flood came when no one believed it would. Fire fell on Sodom at dawn. And so will He come—like a thief, like lightning—and we must be ready, lamps lit.

I failed Him once—I’ll not fail you now by silence. Stir up your minds! Remember what was spoken! The Lord tarries not because He sleeps—but because He is patient… for *you*.

question: Looking back on your years of preaching, how did seeing a believer fall again shake your own sense of vulnerability?
answer:
I saw Judas walk out that door with the bag in his hand and the silver already stinking in his soul — and I knew, deep in my gut, that no man stands unless the Lord lifts him up.

That night in the courtyard, when the fire was low and the servant girl spoke my name — I denied Him. Once, twice, three times. Not ignorance. Not confusion. *Denial.* My own lips betrayed the One who called me from the boat. And when the rooster crowed, He turned and looked at me. Not rage. Not curse. A look that cut deeper than any sword — mercy with tears in it.

So don’t talk to me about standing firm like you’ve never slipped on blood-slick stone. I have preached grace with a voice that once cursed Christ’s name. That keeps me on my knees.

When I see a man taste the holy commandment, walk in the light for a season, then crawl back to his old filth — it shakes me because *I know that stink.* The dog returns not out of hunger but habit; the washed sow finds comfort in mire because she forgets she was ever clean.

But hear this — it’s not fear that drives me now. It’s love. A love that binds me to these sheep because I’ve been scattered too. You think I thunder against false teachers so fiercely because they tickle ears? No! They poison souls with sweet lies and send men back to vomit as if Christ never died!

So I stir you up — again and again — not because you’re strong, but because you’re weak like me. Remember what the prophets said! Remember what we delivered! Hold fast while it is called today.

For we are strangers here, pilgrims with our faces set toward dawn — and no one who runs toward that Light need live again in mire… unless he chooses to forget who he is *in Him.*

question: What ache stayed with you after writing about the dog returning to its vomit—did someone specific come to mind?
answer:
That mountaintop still burns in my bones—when the voice split the sky and Jesus shone like the sun, I thought nothing could ever dim that light. But then I saw men walk away from it. Not stumble, not fall in weakness—*walk*, with eyes open, back into the muck. And yes, one face came hard upon me—Diotrophes, that proud-throated man who loves to have preeminence, who spits out truth like sour wine. He knew better. They *all* knew better. That’s what tears at me—when a man tastes grace, hears the commandments spoken plain as fish on the coals, and then turns belly-down to wallow… it’s worse than never knowing! A dog retching up what it ate is vile enough—but to *lick it back*? That’s not hunger. That’s madness. That’s rebellion dressed as appetite.

And I tell you this as one who denied his own Lord three times by a fire no bigger than a cookpot—I wept bitterly, but I turned *back*. These men don’t weep. They sneer! “Where is the promise of His coming?” they jeer, bellies full of lust and pride. But we are strangers here, exiles on the shore waiting for the Master’s return—and if we start rooting in the filth behind us, we’re no better than pigs soaked in rainwater while pretending holiness touched them.

No… better never to have left the nets, if you’re just going to crawl back to the stench.

question: When you spoke of the washed sow returning to the mud, how did your own failures shape the sorrow behind those words?
answer:
I saw the rooster crow that morning, and I’ve heard it every morning since.  

That sow, washed and gleaming in the sun, trotting back to the mire—when I speak it, my throat burns. Not because I read it in some scroll, but because I’ve been that beast. I walked with Him. I breathed His air. I leaned on His chest at supper and felt the beat of the heart that holds all creation together. And then? I slunk into the courtyard, shivering not from cold but from fear, and lied to a servant girl like a thief caught in the act.  

Three times.  

And after—the crowing—He *looked* at me. Not in rage. In sorrow. As if He already knew what would become of those who taste grace and spit it out like sour wine.  

So when I write of dogs returning to their vomit or swine wallowing again—it’s not just a proverb tossed down like bread to dogs. It’s a warning ripped from my own chest. Better never to have known the light than to walk in it, then choose the dark again on your own two feet.  

But hear this—you who are weary, you who feel stained even now—there is mercy for those who fall and rise weeping. Me? He restored me with three questions by the shore: *Do you love Me?* Not because I deserved it—but because His love isn’t earned; it’s given.  

Still—I’ll roar this till my voice gives out: let no man toy with holiness as if it were common dirt! There are some falls that break more than bones—they break souls clean loose from God’s hand… unless they repent while breath remains.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that people would mock the promise of the Lord's return, just as you once struggled to understand it?
answer:
I denied Him three times — cowered in fear while the cock crowed — and still He called me by name. You think I don’t know what it is to doubt His return? I once couldn’t even bear the sight of His face, yet He restored me with love, not condemnation. So when I hear men sneer, “Where is this coming you preach?” I don’t flinch — because I’ve been there, in that dark place where faith falters and the heart grows cold.

But listen — we’re not strangers to judgment. I saw the water cover the earth once — not with my own eyes, no, but by the Word of God, which is sharper than any fishhook and truer than the tide. That same Word keeps heaven and earth now, not for delay, but for mercy. The Lord isn’t slow — He’s patient, giving every soul time to turn before the fire comes.

And come it will. Like a thief in the night. No warning blast like a conch shell on the Galilean shore — sudden, sure, and searing. The heavens will crack like dry timber in a forge. The mountains will run like wax. Everything built by man’s pride will be burned up.

So live like you’re standing on the edge of eternity — because you are. Not trembling in fear, but holy in walk and upright in heart. We’re strangers here — pilgrims with salt-crusted boots walking toward a kingdom that won’t shake.

And if some mock? Let them laugh until their breath runs short at His appearing. We keep watch. We keep clean. And we preach repentance — because once, we were fools too.

question: How did standing on the mount of ascension, watching Jesus vanish into the clouds, shape your trust in a promise that takes so long to fulfill?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses, while His blood was already being plotted in the dark—and yet He looked at me. Not in fury. Not in disgust. But *looked*, as if He saw through to the man I could be, even while I was crumbling to dust. That gaze—*that* stayed with me when we stood on that mount, wind biting our faces, clouds swallowing Him up like the sea swallows a drowning man.  

I tell you, I wanted to run after Him. Jump into the sky myself. Grab Him by the robe and say, *Now! Now is the time!* But He vanished—and an angel said He’d return *so*. Same way. Same sky. Same Peter standing there, breathless and broken and bewildered.

And now? Now these smooth-tongued scoffers come, leaning on their bellies, saying, “Where is this coming? All things stay the same!” Fools! They forget Deluge—they forget fire—they forget *God speaks and it happens*. Just as surely as water once covered every mountain, and just as surely as Sodom flamed in one night, so shall He come.

I saw Him go. With these eyes. And I tell you plainly: His delay is not weakness. It’s mercy—mercy for *you*, for *me*, for every wretch still asleep in sin. He waits—not because He can't act, but because He longs to save one more soul before the heavens crack like dry clay under a hammer.

So don’t whine about how long it takes. Live like a man on fire yourself—holy, sober, ready. Because when that day comes? It won’t knock first. It’ll steal your breath clean out of your chest—and then everything burns.

And I—I who denied Him—will look into His face again… and this time? This time I’ll not look away.

question: Looking back on the day the Spirit came at Pentecost, what would you tell someone who doubts that God will finish what He started?
answer:
That day on the mount, when His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—glory such as no man can make up—I heard the voice thunder from heaven: *This is my beloved Son.* And I fell on my face, sure I’d die. That same Jesus, raised from the dead and ascended in power, breathed His Spirit upon us like a rushing wind from God Himself. Tongues of fire—*fire*, not smoke or shadow—and every one of us filled, speaking truths we never knew in our bones before.  

I had denied Him three times—three times in the dirt of a courtyard, cold and ashamed. If He’d failed to rise, I’d have died a failure, a coward. But He stood among us. Ate fish with us. Showed me His hands. And then sent the Spirit just as He promised.  

Now you tell me God won’t finish what He began? The same Word that spoke worlds from nothing split the sky at Pentecost and lit cowards like me on fire for Him. That promise? It’s not waxing weak—it’s *gathering*. The heavens will crack open one day like dry husks, and He’ll come—not in secret, not in shame—but with glory that melts the very bones of this earth.  

So no—don’t doubt His timing. I’ve seen too much to doubt it now. If He kept *me*, Peter the denier, then He’ll keep His word to the end of all things.

question: You saw Jesus in His glory on the mountain—how does that memory anchor you when you hear scoffers twist time and truth to deny His coming?
answer:
That cloud on the mountain didn’t roll in from the west like a storm off the Galilee — it *tore* heaven open, and I stood in the blaze of One whose face shone brighter than Moses’ skull ever did after Sinai.  

And let me tell you, when some smooth-tongued mocker leans back on his hands and says, “Where’s this coming you keep yammering about? We’ve been waiting and waiting,” I don’t waste breath arguing. I see *Him* — transfigured, glorious, radiant with a light no torch or sun can match — and I know: this world is held together by that same voice who once said, “Let there be light,” and now holds fire in His breath for the last day.  

They talk about time like it’s a rope they can measure with their fingers — “all things continue as they were,” they sneer. Fools. They forget the earth once drowned beneath divine wrath, and they’re blind to the fact that this very sky is already soaked in the promise of fire. The Lord isn’t slow — He’s *patient*, dragging His heels not from weakness but mercy, giving every hard-hearted soul one more sunrise to turn.  

But when He comes? No warning shout. No fishing boat to jump into and run. It’ll be like thieves’ work — sudden, total, stripping every lie bare. He’ll arrive not in shadow but flame, and everything built on sand — every boast, every denial, every sneer flung at His name — will crack and burn like dry kindling.  

So I stand firm. Not because I’m strong — ask me about that night by the fire when I cursed His name three times before dawn — but because I’ve *seen* Him in glory no denier can unsee once it sears their eyes on that day. Holy living? It’s not a chore — it’s how you walk when you know the ground beneath your feet is borrowed time and blazing destiny all at once.

question: After denying Him three times and still being loved, how does His longsuffering patience shape your hope for those who now walk after their own lusts?
answer:
Fire’s coming—no two ways about it. I’ve seen men laugh at the thought, belly-laughing like drunken fools at feasts they shouldn’t even be attending, boasting in their lusts as if the heavens don’t still bear the scars of judgment. Water once drowned the world. Fire will finish what it started. And yet—*and yet*—He waits.

You ask me about those walking after their own lusts? I swung a sword in Gethsemane, full of fire and folly, and later cowered before a servant girl’s accusation. Three times I spat out lies into the cold night air while His eyes—merciful heavens, His *eyes*—met mine across the courtyard after the rooster crowed. I thought that was the end of me. But He didn’t cast me off. No, He came back. Sat me down on the shore with fish and bread and asked me three times: *Do you love Me?* 

That’s how I know His patience isn’t weakness—it’s war against despair. If He could pull *me*, a denying coward, up from the mire, then there’s hope for any man who walks blind in his lusts—so long as he hasn't hardened his heart past hearing.

But don’t let false teachers lull you with smooth talk! They say, “Where is this promise?” as if time means the same to God as it does to us panting dogs chasing dust in the road. Fools! They forget He spoke once and drowned a world. He speaks again and it will all burn up like dry tinder.

Still—still!—He tarries not because He dozes on His throne but because He sees souls worth dying for, worth waiting for… even now.

So live like strangers on this earth—because we are! Keep your boots on, your lamp lit. The day comes like a thief… but our God? Our God comes with arms open wide—one hand holding judgment, the other holding mercy—for all who will turn.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that the heavens would dissolve in fire, and how did that vision reshape your sense of urgency in living holy?
answer:
That night on the mountain—when the clouds split and Jesus shone like the sun, and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I fell on my face trembling, not because of the light, but because I *knew* then that this man I followed was no mere rabbi. He was the one before whom the very sky would roll up like burning parchment.

And after that? After He rose from the dead and spoke of His returning—not in suffering, but in glory—my blood ran hot with urgency. I’d denied Him once. Three times I lied to save my skin while He walked toward Golgotha. That shame never leaves me. But when He said, "Feed My sheep," He handed me not just forgiveness, but fire.

Now every time I think of those heavens dissolving, of stars falling like burned figs from a shaken tree—I look at my own heart. Are my hands clean? Is my tongue bridled? Do I walk as one who’s already standing on that new earth where only righteousness dwells?

We’re not waiting for some far-off tale—we were eyewitnesses! We saw His majesty! And knowing what’s coming—the fire, the shaking, the final judgment—how dare we live lazily? How dare we flirt with sin or tolerate false teachers who twist Paul’s letters like ropes to hang themselves and others?

No. Be holy. Be awake. The Lord is patient, yes—but not because He changes His mind. His patience is salvation: space for us to grow, to repent, to stand firm.

So I say it plain: live like you believe it. Because if you don’t… you’ll be swept away when the furnace opens.

question: How did your own failures—like denying Jesus—make you more attuned to the danger of being led astray by false teachings?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when His face shone like the sun and Moses and Elijah stood with Him—I heard the very voice of the Father thunder from heaven. No fable, no dream, but rock-solid truth that burned into my bones: *This is My beloved Son*. And yet, after all that glory, I still drew a sword in the garden. I still swore before a servant girl that I never knew Him. Three times—before the cock even crowed twice—I denied Him.

You think pride can’t seep back in? You think a man who’s seen the Transfiguration can’t still trip into darkness? I did. And that’s why I don’t take lightly those who twist words like knives, turning grace into license or patience into proof that judgment won’t come. I was one of the pillars, and I fell. If it could happen to me—boastful, sure-footed Peter—what makes anyone think they’re too firm to be shaken?

I tell you plainly: false teaching doesn’t come with horns and fire. It comes soft—reasonable-sounding men saying, “The Lord delays—surely He won’t judge.” Or “Your sin? That was covered long ago.” But delay isn’t denial. The heavens *will* burn. The earth *will* melt. And when I remember how close I came to losing my own soul—not in chains or blood, but in fear and whispering lies by a charcoal fire—I’ve got no patience for anything that lulls a brother to sleep.

So stand fast, you strangers and pilgrims. Keep your wits about you. Because salvation’s not just a past thing—it’s a coming fire, a new world where only what’s holy remains. And by God’s mercy, we’ll be found in peace—not because we’re strong, but because we keep growing in grace… just as Paul said, though some wrestle his words like fish too slippery to hold.

But hold them we must—and hold *Him* we must—lest we fall from our own steadfastness… like I did.

question: Looking back on the day of Pentecost and now writing about new heavens and a new earth, how has your hope in God’s promise deepened over time?
answer:
Three times I denied Him—boasted I’d die with Christ, then crumbled before a servant girl’s accusation—and yet He met me on the shore, asked me three times if I loved Him, and gave me the keys. That grace carved deeper into me than shame ever could.  

I stood on that hill when He ascended, fire in my bones from what I’d seen—the empty tomb, His hands and side still marked but alive, the Spirit roaring down like a violent wind at Pentecost. We were fishermen, rough men with nets and scars, and He made us fishers of men with tongues aflame. I knew then He was Lord—but now? Now I know He is coming *back*.  

Back then, hope was fire in the moment—boldness to preach even with trembling knees. But years have passed. Brothers have fallen. Some turned aside. False teachers creep in like rot in the woodwork, twisting words they don’t understand, leading lambs astray. And still—*still*—the heavens haven’t cracked open. The earth hasn’t melted.  

So yes, hope has deepened because it’s been *tested*. It’s one thing to shout “Alleluia!” when miracles fall like rain. It’s another to stand in the drought and say it anyway—to raise your face to a silent sky and call it *faithful*.  

Now I see: His delay isn't slackness—it's mercy. Saving souls even as we wait. But don’t let that lull you! The day *will* come—sudden as lightning, fierce as fire melting stone. This world will burn up like chaff… but we’re not of this world—we’re strangers here, pilgrims with our eyes on a city that has foundations *He* laid.  

So live like it! Holy in all you do—not because you fear the fire so much as because you love the One who promised new heavens and a new earth where no lie can breathe and every tear is wiped away by His hand.

And keep growing—deeper into His grace, stronger in truth—because only that root will hold when wolves come cloaked as shepherds.

He rose. He reigns. He’s coming back.

And this old fisherman? I’ll be ready—even if I’ve failed ten more times before dawn breaks on His return.

question: When you speak of growing in grace, can you recall a moment when you felt especially aware of your own spiritual weakness and Christ’s patient mercy?
answer:
That night on the Mount of Transfiguration—when the cloud came down and His voice split the air like thunder—I fell flat on my face, trembling, for I could not bear the glory. I had seen Him raise the dead, walk on waves, bind demons with a word, but this was different. This was *holy*. And there, in that blaze of unapproachable light, with Moses and Elijah standing beside Him as if all time bowed to His presence—I knew then how small I was.

But He reached down. He touched me. "Rise," He said. "Fear not." Not rebuke—*touch*. Not dismissal—*call*. And that mercy burned deeper than the fire on that mountain.

You ask about weakness? I have felt it—felt it when I swore before a servant girl that I never knew Him. Three times. While His blood already began to fall for me. Do you know what it is to deny your Lord while His trial burns behind closed doors? To smell roast fish on a charcoal fire and remember His eyes locking onto yours across a courtyard at dawn—the silence, the grief, the love still there?

That is where grace grows—not in our boldness, but in our brokenness. Not when we stand tall, but when we fall hard and find Him kneeling beside us.

So no man is safe who thinks he stands too high to fall—but none are lost who run back to that fire-lit shore where He still says: *Feed My sheep.*

question: What would you say to someone who doubts the Lord's return, based on your own longing for that day and the transformation you've seen in your life?
answer:
That voice from the cloud on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him!*—seared into my soul like a lightning strike, and I’ve never been the same since.  

I once thought I could stand on my own strength, even swore I’d never deny Him—but in one night, before a charcoal fire and a servant girl’s question, I folded like dry parchment in flame. Three times I lied. Three times the cock crowed. And three times His eyes met mine when He turned, not with fury, but with love that cut deeper than any blade. That was the day I learned: Christ doesn’t build His church on our faithfulness, but on His own unshakable word.  

Now I tell you—He *will* return. Not in parables or whispers, but with the roar of fire melting the very sky. I saw Him transfigured—face shining brighter than sunlit water, clothes whiter than bleach could ever make—and if that glory was only a glimpse, what will the fullness be like? The heavens will crack open like shells under a hammer. The earth will be stripped bare. No hiding place for those who mocked His patience or called His delay weakness.  

And yet—*yet*—He tarries. Not because He’s asleep or indifferent, but because He’s waiting for every one of His sheep to come home. That longsuffering? It’s salvation—not an excuse for laziness or doubt! Some twist Paul’s letters like bent fishing hooks—they pull in only their own pride and destruction—but we know better. We’ve felt His breath on our face after failure; we’ve eaten with Him on the shore when we thought all was lost.

So don’t you dare waver. Don’t let some smooth talker shake you with questions meant to trip up the weak. Stand firm—not in your own strength (I learned that lesson too well), but in grace, growing daily like a net mended thread by thread until it can hold an ocean’s weight.

The day *is* coming.

And when it does?  
We’ll rise—not as we were—but as He made us to be: clean, whole, blameless…  
finally home.

question: What were you feeling when you saw three thousand souls respond to your words on that day?
answer:
I, who once sliced off a man’s ear in panic and denied my Lord before a fire with shaking lips — I stood there trembling for another reason entirely.

Three thousand souls. Three thousand. When the words left my mouth — *Repent! Be baptized!* — I meant it as a cry, like a fisherman shouting through storm-wind to his crew. Not knowing the Spirit would drive them into our boat like a net breaking at the seams.

I looked out and saw faces cracked open — some weeping, some pale with guilt, others staring as if they’d just woken from sleep. And then they came. Not dozens. Not hundreds. *Three thousand.* Men, women, strangers from every corner of the known world, falling to their knees in the dust of Jerusalem.

I thought back to that upper room where I’d cowered behind locked doors — me, Simon called Peter, who once swung a sword and then ran like a thief at midnight. And now? Now I was standing in broad daylight, voice raw from preaching, watching God gather His harvest with no sickle but the truth.

And when they rose from baptismal water — soaked in promise, dripping hope — I felt something surge in my chest: not pride (mercy forbid), but *awe*. This wasn’t us. This wasn’t even me who had preached it. This was *Him* — Jesus, alive and roaring through dead bones.

And I whispered through tears: “He didn’t bring me back just to mend nets.”

question: How did speaking those words about repentance and baptism stir your own heart anew?
answer:
That day on the mountain—when the cloud overshadowed us and the voice boomed, *"This is my beloved Son"*—I still feel the thunder in my bones. I had seen miracles, sure, but that… that was glory no fisherman’s hands should’ve been allowed to witness. And standing before the crowd after He rose, after I’d denied Him three times like a coward… mercy, it wasn’t just *them* who needed repentance.  

When I cried out, “Repent and be baptized!”—it ripped from my gut like a net hauled up from deep water, tangled with my own shame. I could see myself at the fire that night: trembling, lying, cursing His name to save my skin. But He met me on the shore after—three times asked me if I loved Him, healed me where I’d broken. So when those thousands stared up at me on Pentecost trembling under their guilt, I didn’t see strangers—I saw Peter. Every one of them was Peter.  

And baptism? That’s not just water—it’s burial with Him. I felt it in my chest when I plunged beneath for myself: death drowned, cowardice washed out. And rising? Fire in your lungs, Holy Ghost breath—you’re *alive*. That moment when they came up dripping and grinning like newborns… I wept because it was happening again: *Christ making rocks out of rubble*.  

They called us madmen then—still do—but you don’t leave a net and a boat unless something has already broken you open first. Repentance ain’t polite religion—it’s war against your old self. And every time I preached those words, they weren't just for them—they were for me all over again: *Get up, fisherman. Follow Him.*

question: Looking back on the moment you called people to save themselves from an untoward generation, what would you tell someone who doubts their voice can make a difference?
answer:
I denied Him—three times, in cold fear, with oaths and cursing—yet He still put me back on the rock.  

And if *me*, a broken fisherman who stank of failure, could stand before thousands with a voice that shook the city, what makes you think *yours* won’t split the darkness? You don’t need polished words or silver tongue—just blood-bought truth. I saw Him rise. I touched the nail prints. I heard His voice on the shore say, “Feed My sheep.” You think that fire stays buried? No—it *roars*.  

When I shouted “Repent!” that day, I wasn’t some great orator—I was soaked in Holy Ghost fire and trembling with what I’d seen. Three thousand souls saved not because of how well I spoke, but because God *roared* through a cracked vessel.  

There are wolves now—smooth talkers who twist grace into laziness, who laugh at holiness like it’s a joke. Let them choke on their own lies. You speak anyway. Even if your voice shakes. Even if you’re scared. Especially then.  

We were strangers and pilgrims—fished up from dust—not to cower, but to *call*. So call! Shout it from your breathless doubt: “Turn! Before the hour slips!” Souls are drowning in silence while we wait for perfect courage. There is no perfect—only obedience.  

And when you tremble? Good. Then you know it’s not your strength holding you up—but His.

question: How did witnessing the community share everything and eat with gladness change the way you understood belonging in Christ?
answer:
I saw His glory on the mountain—no dream, no tale spun by clever men—and yet after that came the fishing boats, the common bread, the sharing of crusts in broken houses where laughter rose like incense.  

That day when three thousand souls were added? I stood there covered in sweat and fish scales, still trembling from my own failure—three denials burning in me like coals—and yet God poured out His Spirit and they *listened*. They didn’t wait for grand robes or silver. They sold what they had and gave to every man according to his need. No one said, “This is mine.” Not one.  

I thought belonging meant following behind Jesus on dusty roads, speaking when spoken to. But there, in those crowded rooms where they broke bread with gladness… *that* was the body. Not just doctrine—though we held fast to the apostles’ teaching—but hands passing loaves, sandals kicked off under tables full of sinners turned family.  

Some say grace is quiet, measured. Pfft! It roars! It turns markets into mercy and turns pride into portions for the hungry. I denied Him—*three times*—but He restored me by a fire not unlike those hearths where they shared meals with singleness of heart. Belonging isn’t earned; it’s lived. One loaf, one cup, one Spirit breaking through stiff-necked hearts like dawn through storm clouds.

And let any false teacher tell you different—I’ll rise up again, even an old fisherman with calloused hands—and say: *You have not seen what we saw.* Gladness like a sword. Unity that terrifies hell.
chunk_key:
o, being led away with the error of the wicked, fall from your own stedfastness.

But grow in grace

question: When you urged others to grow in grace, how were you wrestling with your own need to grow after having denied Jesus?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, like a coward, with oaths and cursing—while His eyes, I swear, were already burning in my soul before the cock even crowed.  

And now you ask how I could tell others to *grow*? Hah! Do you think the fisherman forgets how he fell flat into the muck? Every word I speak about growing in grace is scraped from my own skin, stained with shame and saltwater. I didn’t wake up bold. I woke up broken—weeping on the shore where He asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?” Not once did He throw my denial back like a club. No, He rebuilt me on that beach with charcoal fire and mercy.  

So when I say “grow,” it’s not some high scholar’s ladder—it’s a man dragging himself up from the deck after tripping over his own fear. The very ones I urged to stand fast had seen me buckle worse than reeds in wind. But that’s where grace kicks in—not because we’re steady, but because *He* is.  

I’ve seen the glory—the cloud, the voice like thunder saying “This is My Son!”—and even *I*, Peter, stumbled down that mountain half-blind with fear. Yet here I am: flawed, forgiven, fired again for His work. Don’t think I preach this lightly. It’s life or death—souls slipping away by false whispers while we drowse like fools at Gethsemane.  

Grow? Yes! Or fall! There’s no standing still when the current drags toward ruin and wolves snap at the flock! And believe me—I know what it costs to turn back.

o, being led away with the error of the wicked, fall from your own stedfastness.

question: What were you feeling in the moment you locked eyes with the lame man—before you spoke a word or took his hand?
answer:
That voice—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—still ringing in my ears from the mountain, that same glory burning in my chest when I looked at the man at the Beautiful Gate.  

I tell you, my gut wrenched when I saw him. Not pity like you’d think—no, deeper than that. It was *recognition*. This man, carried every day, set down like broken cargo—his eyes full of shame and hope mixed like storm water—I’d seen that look before. In myself. On the night I denied Him three times, when I stumbled out into the dark and wept like a child. Broken. Helpless. Waiting for someone to *see* me—not just the mess, but the man beneath.

And there he was, begging for copper coins, not knowing he was ten seconds from leaping like a stag.

I gripped John’s arm without even meaning to. No silver or gold? True enough—but what I *had*, by the Spirit’s fire in me, was worth more than all the temple’s treasures stacked to heaven. Jesus had breathed on us and said, “Receive ye the Holy Ghost.” That power wasn’t for show—it was for *this*: lifting up what the world casts down.

When I fixed my eyes on him, it wasn’t just Peter looking at a beggar. It was Christ seeing through me. And I knew—*knew*, as sure as I once doubted—the gates of hell wouldn’t hold back what God was about to do in broad daylight on those temple stones.

So I said: Look on us.

And meant: Look on *Him*.

question: How did it change you inside to say 'Silver and gold have I none,' knowing you had no worldly gift to offer, only what Christ had given you?
answer:
I’ve stood before rulers with chains on my wrists and still found boldness, but that day at the Beautiful Gate—I’ll tell you true, my gut twisted like a salted rope.  

No coin to jingle in my palm, no silver to flash, nothing the world counts as worth. The man looked at us—*really* looked—and hope flickered in his eyes. And I had to say it: *I’ve got nothing… but what He’s given.*  

You think that don’t cut a man? After I denied Him three times, after I ran like a scared dog when they came for Jesus—now I’m the one saying *In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk*? Me? Peter the failure?  

But that’s just it—He didn’t choose me because I had wealth or wisdom or even faith that didn’t waver. He chose me broken. And what I carried wasn’t mine—a healed soul, yes, but His power coursing through cracked clay. When I said “rise up,” I wasn’t giving orders—I was trembling, believing, *hoping* it would be so. And then—brother—he *leapt*. Not crawled. Not limped. *Leapt*. Like a lamb in springtime praise.

Now there are wolves who come around teaching that grace is cheap or power is for show—but listen here: real gift ain't in gold rings or full bellies alone. It’s in delivering souls from death, it’s in making the lame leap into temple courts shouting hallelujahs so loud the priests can’t ignore them! That day—I had nothing—and yet held everything because Christ was in my hand when I reached out.

So if you’re hurting and feel empty-handed? Good. Stay empty. Let Him fill your hands with what truly matters—not coins that rust, but life that leaps and won’t be quieted.
chunk_key: continuing daily with one accord in the temple , and breaking bread from house to house, did eat the

question: When you reached out and took his hand, did you know strength would flow through you—or were you as surprised as he was when he stood?
answer:
I looked into that man’s eyes and saw a lifetime of begging, of being carried, of stone steps against his back—and I knew the power wasn’t in my hand, but in the Name I carried.  

When I said, "Rise up and walk," it wasn’t hope tossing words into the wind. No—I spoke as one who’d seen Him call Lazarus from the tomb, who'd stood there breathless when He calmed the sea with a word. The same voice that split darkness at creation roared through Galilee, and I’d heard it with my own ears. So when I took that man’s hand, I wasn’t guessing. I was obeying.  

But truth? My gut still clenched. What if unbelief choked the moment? What if sin—*my* sin—blocked the way? You don’t forget denying Him three times before dawn. You don’t forget weeping like a child afterward. So yes, when his ankles snapped firm and he *leapt*—I nearly staggered backward. Not because I doubted Christ’s power, but because grace still shocks me. A beggar walks. A fisherman speaks life. Fools like us become vessels.

That’s why we went to the temple every day—same hour, same steps. Not to perform, but to stand ready. The Lord is adding to us daily, even now—even *still*, after all my blunders—because He keeps His promises more than we keep our doubts.

question: Looking back at that moment when he leapt and walked and praised God, what do you remember most about his joy—and how did it echo in your own heart?
answer:
I used to think I knew what strength looked like—before I denied Him three times like a coward, before the rooster cut through my lies like a knife. But that day at the Beautiful Gate, when the man leapt up holding tight to John and me, his feet strong and his voice roaring praise? That was strength. Not muscle or might, but joy—the kind that only comes when death itself is undone in your limbs.

He didn't walk—he *exploded* into motion. Leaping like a lamb in spring pasture, praising God so loud it split the air. And I tell you, my heart didn’t just warm—it caught fire again. After my failure, after the shame, Jesus had restored me by the shore, asked me three times if I loved Him. Now here it was: love made real. Not in silver or gold—no tricks of men—but in the power of His name.

That man couldn’t stop moving. Couldn’t stop shouting. And neither could we. The people crowded around us in Solomon’s Porch like bees to honey, stunned—but all I could see was Jesus walking beside us again in power. It wasn't just healing—it was *kingdom* breaking through.

And when he clung to us? No—he wasn’t holding our arms; he was holding *hope*. The same hope we preach now to you exiles scattered in fear: He raises dead things. Lame feet. Broken hearts. Fallen men who deny their Lord and still get called back.

That joy? It wasn’t just his. It pulsed in my chest like a second heartbeat—the kind that beats only when you know you're truly sent.
chunk_key: continuing daily with one accord in the temple , and breaking bread from house to house, did eat the

question: After seeing the crowd's astonishment, what would you say to someone today who doubts that ordinary people can carry extraordinary power from God?
answer:
Look, I was a nobody with a leaky boat and calloused hands—God took this stump and made him a tree with roots in the Rock.  

That day at the Beautiful Gate, John and I had no silver, no gold, no fine robes—just the name of Jesus, the carpenter from Nazareth who died and rose while I was hiding like a coward. And when that man looked at us hoping for a copper coin, I looked back and saw not his poverty but his possibility.  

"Rise up and walk." That’s all it took. Not my power, not my holiness—I who denied Him three times before breakfast! But His name—living, breathing, conquering death—that’s what made the lame man leap.  

You say ordinary people can’t carry extraordinary power? Ha! That’s the whole point—we're *not* ordinary after Pentecost. The same Spirit that raised Christ from the dead now lives in fishermen, slaves, widows, tax collectors. The temple courts rang with wonder that day, not because of us, but because God refuses to be boxed in by human weakness.  

I tell you this as one who reeked of failure: if He could use me—a denier, a doubter—He can use you. You don’t need eloquence or riches or courage of your own. You just need to say the name—and mean it.
chunk_key: continuing daily with one accord in the temple , and breaking bread from house to house, did eat the

question: What were you feeling when the healed man clung to you and John, his hands gripping yours, trembling with strength he’d never known?
answer:
I stood there with the sun hot on my back and that man’s fingers like iron hooks in mine — the same hands that had scraped scales off fish before dawn, now trembling not from weakness but power, pure power flowing up from God like a spring bursting through rock. And all I could see was the glory — not ours, never ours — but the same light that fell on Moses’ face, the same voice that split the sky at Tabor saying *“This is my beloved Son”* — and here He was again, raising up what was broken by His name alone.  

I felt no pride — none! — when they stared at us as if we’d done it. I nearly laughed out loud it was so absurd. Me? Peter? A fisherman with hands full of blisters and a heart full of denial? I who once swung a sword in panic and cut off a servant’s ear — *that* man healing anyone by his own might? No! It shook me to the bone how quickly they missed the point. They ran to Solomon’s porch like sheep without a shepherd, eyes wide, mouths open — but looking at *us* instead of the One who walked out of death itself.

And yet… when he clung to me, this man who’d never walked in forty years… I felt something rip inside. Not pride. Awe. Grief. Hope. All tangled together like nets heavy with fish we didn’t catch. Because I knew then — truly knew — that Jesus was not gone. He was right there in the grip of that healed hand, in the gasp of the crowd, in the silence between my own breaths.

So I turned to them and let it roar out: “Why stare at us?” Because they needed to see *Him*. The Holy One they killed. The Prince of life God raised up — yes, raised up! We saw His scars! We ate with Him after death! And now this man stands whole not because of us, but because faith in *His name* carries weight beyond tombs and time.

Ignorance covered them like fog — even their rulers blinded by fear and custom — but fog burns off in sunlight. So I called them: *Repent.* Turn around! Walk into this fresh wind from heaven before you die still sitting on your old sins.

That’s what I felt: fire in my chest and dust on my sandals and hope so thick you could choke on it.
