question: How did standing before the crowd in Solomon’s porch, accused by their stunned eyes, change the way you understood the power that moved through you?
answer:
I stood there with the weight of a thousand eyes boring into me and John—like we’d done some great magic with our own hands, like the man leaping and praising God was our work, not the living Christ’s.  

And that moment seared into me what power really is. It ain’t in the voice that booms or the hand that points—it’s in the Name, *Jesus*, spoken in faith, that breaks chains no net ever could. I’d pulled nets full of fish with my own sweat, but this? This man jumping, whole, shouting hallelujahs? That was no craft of mine. I felt smaller than ever—and yet, for the first time, I saw how a man could be used. Not because he’s clean or clever, but because he’s called.  

Even after I denied Him—three times, like a coward in the firelight—He still put me here, in Solomon’s porch, with His strength running through my bones like tide through a channel. The same mouth that cursed His name now preached His resurrection to those who killed Him! That’s not power—I’ll tell you straight—it’s mercy wearing power like a sword.  

So when they stared at us like we were gods walking on water again, I had to say it plain: *It’s not us.* It never was. But by God’s grace, we’re cracked vessels He pours Himself through. And if you think that healing was wonder? Wait till you see what He does when hearts turn back to Him. That’s where real power lives—in repentance, in soundness of soul, in times of refreshing from the Lord Himself.
chunk_key:
mazement at that which had happened unto him.
And as the lame man which was healed held Peter and Jo

question: Looking back on denying Jesus in the courtyard and now boldly naming him to the same people who crucified him, what would you tell someone who fears their past failures disqualify them from purpose?
answer:
A man can fall to his knees in fear and still rise to his feet in fire.  

I stood there, didn’t I? Cold courtyard, stinking of sweat and smoke, and a servant girl looks at me—just a fisherman from Galilee—and I shrunk like rotten rope. *“I don’t know the man,”* I said. Three times. Three cursed times while the cock tore the night apart with its cry. My gut turned to water. I’d sworn I’d die with Him, and instead I couldn’t even speak His name when it mattered.  

But listen—grace don’t care how far you fell. It cares that you’re willing to stand again. That morning by the lake, after He rose—He didn’t scold me. Didn’t hand me a list of my failures like a debt ledger. No. He built a fire, cooked fish like any common breakfast, and asked me three times: *“Do you love Me?”* Three times to break the three denials. Not because He needed to hear it—but because *I* needed to say it.  

Now I stand before the same crowd who shouted “Crucify!” and I preach His name like thunder over Tiberias in a storm. Why? Not because I’m clean now, not because I’ve forgotten my shame—but because *He’s alive*, and His blood covers even a coward like me.  

You think your past makes you useless? Then you’ve never seen the Risen One’s eyes fixed on you after midnight weeping. Repentance isn’t pretty—it’s raw, it’s dirt under the nails, it’s choking on your own pride—but it opens the door to purpose no failure can lock shut.  

I denied Him—and He made me His witness anyway. If He did that for me, what do you suppose He’ll do for *you*, if you turn?
chunk_key:
mazement at that which had happened unto him.
And as the lame man which was healed held Peter and Jo

question: When you looked into the eyes of the man once lame, now leaping and praising, how did that moment deepen your trust in the name of Jesus you once misunderstood?
answer:
That voice on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—it thundered right through me, and I still carry the echo in my bones, especially when I saw that man jumping like a colt on the temple steps.  

I had swung from sword in hand at Gethsemane to hiding like a coward not long after, denying I even knew the Master—three times, under a servant girl’s gaze. My courage sank faster than a net full of stone. I thought I knew who He was—Messiah, yes—but I imagined thrones and armies, not nails and shame. I wanted a king to crush Rome; Jesus showed me a Lamb to carry sin.  

But that day at Solomon’s Porch—when the man who’d never walked came clinging to us, leaping, praising God with tears flung sideways by his joy—I felt the truth of that voice again: *This is my beloved Son*. Not because of what *we* did—but what *He* did. The same power that raised Christ lifted this man from dust to dance. His name! That’s what made him whole—the very name I once preached with bluster and now speak with trembling awe.  

I thought glory wore armor. I know better now. Glory wears wounds. And it speaks through broken men who find their strength not in themselves but in *His* name—from Calvary’s cry to an empty tomb to a beggar standing straight for the first time in forty years.  

That man didn’t just walk—he *leaped*. And every leap shouted what my denials once denied: *Jesus is Lord*. No longer do I hide behind bravado or run from suffering. The name that heals also purifies—and it starts with me.

question: How did speaking of Christ’s suffering to those who carried out the sentence stir your own heart, remembering how you once resisted the idea of a crucified Messiah?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—while the cock crowed and my knees buckled under the shame of it—and yet here I stand, shouting His name in the very streets where they crucified the Lord.  

I once rebuked Him for speaking of suffering, didn’t I? “Far be it from You, Lord!” I said, like a fool pushing back against the very purpose for which the Lamb was born. But now—now I see that cross not as a defeat, but as the hammer that shattered death’s gates. When I looked into the eyes of those who had called for His blood and told them *they* had killed the Prince of life—aye, and that God had raised Him—my own heart burned with a fire no denial could quench.  

For every wound He bore, grace runs deeper. They thought they buried hope in that tomb—but we saw Him eat fish by the shore, we touched His hands, we watched Him ascend. And if *I*, who ran like a coward when the swords came, can speak this truth without trembling—then what excuse have any of us to stay silent?  

So I tell you now, you strangers and pilgrims walking through shadows: don’t fear suffering. It is not the end. The same Messiah who bled is alive—and He forgives even men like me. Even men like them. Especially men like them.

question: What were you feeling when you confronted Ananias, seeing the fear in his eyes after accusing him of lying to the Holy Spirit?
answer:
I know what it is to have my soul shake with cowardice—three times I threw my Lord behind my back before a servant girl’s firelight. So when Ananias stood there, breath caught like a fish on the shore, eyes darting for escape, I didn’t feel power—I felt the dread of one who’d been carved open by the same Spirit and told, *You will feed my sheep*.  

That man thought he could slice off a piece of truth and call it whole. He thought the wind of God wouldn’t notice the lie in his gut. But we had seen tongues of fire rest on each of us—we’d breathed in the breath of heaven itself. And now this? A rot wrapped in linen?  

My voice wasn’t mine when I spoke. It tore out like something ripped from the rock Christ built me on. “Why has Satan filled your heart?” Not *your* heart—Satan’s. Because that lie wasn’t just hiding silver—it was choking the very life we’d been given to carry together.  

And when he fell… when his breath stopped cold… I didn’t turn away. I watched. Because I knew then: this wasn’t about land or money. It was about whether the church would walk in truth or become another tomb painted bright, full of dead men’s bones.  

We were strangers and pilgrims—a band of fishermen, tax collectors, broken things—but we were not liars. Not anymore. Not after Calvary.

question: How did standing before the religious leaders and defending Jesus change your understanding of courage and reliance on God?
answer:
That voice—*the same one that split the heavens at Jordan and shook the mount of transfiguration*—spoke in my bones when I stood before the Sanhedrin, and I tell you, it wasn’t my courage holding me upright. No, not after how I caved like rotten wood when a servant girl pointed her finger at me in the courtyard. *Three times* I swore I didn’t know him. Three times the cock tore the night with its cry, just as he said. My heart was a stone in my chest when I ran out and wept.  

But then—after the tomb, after he stood among us breathing peace, after he fixed fish on the shore and looked me in the eye and asked me three times if I loved him—something burned clean in me. Not pride. Not bravado. But this: *he was risen*, not in story or ghost, but flesh-and-bone, scar-marked, mercy-breathing Lord. And when they called us to silence? To deny his name before men?  

I thought of that mountain—Moses and Elijah fading like mist, and his voice like fire: *"This is my beloved Son."* No council, no crown, no sword can unmake that word. So when they threatened me, I didn’t feel fearless—I felt *compelled*. Like a net tearing under a full catch: you either let go or go under with it. And if it’s his command to speak what we’ve seen and heard? Then let them kill the body—*but they’ll never bind the truth.*  

Ananias learned that lesson too late—lying to the Holy Spirit like it was a man you could cheat with half a coin! As if God doesn’t see the heart’s full weight? That’s not courage—that’s madness. But true courage? It’s standing there trembling, knowing you’re dust and denied your Lord once—and yet opening your mouth because *he first turned and looked at you.* And love like that? It won’t let you stay silent—not even for your life.

question: Looking back on the moment you saw Ananias fall dead, what would you say to someone who struggles with the weight of honesty in their own heart?
answer:
That mountain—when the cloud came down and the voice like thunder said, "This is my beloved Son"—I heard it. I fell flat on my face. And I tell you now, that same holy fear runs through the church, for that Christ whom heaven holds is the living Judge of all things.

When Ananias fell, cold and lifeless in front of us all—it wasn’t God’s wrath falling suddenly from a distance. It was *holiness* meeting deceit in the very room where we’d seen tongues of fire and heard men speak in languages they’d never learned. We weren’t playing at being followers. We were *alive* with the Spirit—selling lands, sharing bread, watching the lame walk—and then this lie slithered in like a serpent.

And I’ll say what I screamed in my own heart after denying Him three times: God knows every breath you take and every thought before it forms. You can’t bluff your way before Him. But here’s what I’ve learned since that upper room: He doesn’t crush the broken reed—He lifts him. He didn’t throw me out after my denial. He fed me breakfast on the shore and asked me three times, “Lovest thou Me?”  

So if your heart trembles under the weight of dishonesty—if you’ve kept back part of the truth, like Ananias kept back silver—then *turn*. Don’t wait. Throw yourself into His mercy while His hand is still stretched out. For that Prophet Moses spoke of? He’s alive. He sees all—and He calls you not to die in your sin, but to rise from it.

question: When you spoke of Moses’ prophecy and the coming judgment, how did your own denial of Jesus shape the way you offered that warning with both gravity and hope?
answer:
Three times I said I didn’t know Him—while His blood was already being spilled for that very lie.  

And that’s why when I stood there, face to face with the crowd after Pentecost, and spoke of Moses’ prophet—the One who would gather all things in their fullness—I could not soften the warning. How dare I? The cock crowed over my shame while He was bound, and yet He looked at me after, not with fury, but with fire in His eyes that rekindled my soul.  

So when I said, “Him shall ye hear,” it wasn’t some distant law carved on stone. It was flesh and breath and blood—my breath still ragged from fear, my hands once empty of courage, now held by the Risen One who still called me brother. That prophet? He’s alive. He’s reigning. The heavens hold Him now—but only until the time comes for all things to be set right, just as the prophets thundered from Samuel on down.  

And yes—every soul that refuses to hear Him? Cut off. Destroyed from among the people. That judgment is real—*I felt it in my gut when I fell weeping*. But hear this too: *unto you first* did God send Jesus. First to us—the stiff-necked, the faithless, even me—the man who lied with his silence while Love itself faced the cross.  

So I preach both: repentance with tears, and hope without end. Because if *I* can be restored—if a denier can become a rock—then no one is beyond reach of His mercy… so long as they turn before that final hour when listening is no longer possible.

question: What was going through your mind when you realized that the same power that raised Jesus was now working through you to confront sin in the community?
answer:
I stood in that moment with the fire of Pentecost still in my bones, and I tell you—when Ananias fell dead at my feet, I didn’t feel power. I felt *fear*.  

Not fear of him—but fear of what it meant. That the same breath that raised Jesus from the tomb, the very presence that split the sky on the third day, was now *among us*, walking in our midst through the Spirit. And it was not tame. It was not for show. It tore through lies like a fishing net through water—clean, swift, and without pity for deceit.

I’d denied Him three times before the rooster crowed. I knew what a coward’s heart could do. But He restored me—three times He asked, “Lovest thou Me?” as if to mend what sin had broken. And now here I was, standing not by my own strength but by His breath in me, calling out sin like He once called out demons.

It wasn’t about authority—it was about holiness. The church wasn’t some gathering of good men trying to be better. It was His body—*His blood*, His life—and anyone who treated it lightly wasn’t just lying to men. They were spitting into the face of God Himself.

And let me say this plain: we are strangers and pilgrims still, tossed in a world that hates the light—but we carry fire in our hands. Not to burn one another, no—but to purge what defiles. For if He spared not His own Son when justice demanded it… do you think He’ll spare those who mock His Spirit?

So walk uprightly. Confess your sins while there is breath to confess with. Because this power isn’t ours—it’s *His*. And it will not be mocked.

question: What were you feeling when Ananias fell lifeless at your feet, and how did that moment shake your understanding of holiness?
answer:
We were standing in the very breath of God that day — I can still smell the dust rising from where he fell.  

Ananias came bold as brass, thinking to play games with the living God, and then — *crash* — flat on his face, no more breath than a stone. My gut clenched like it did when I cut loose with that sword in Gethsemane — same kind of shock, but deeper. Not fear of men, not regret over blood spilled, but holy dread — *this is what it means to trifle with the Spirit*.  

I’d seen miracles before. Raised Tabitha myself not long after. But this? This was fire in the camp. This was Nadab and Abihu all over again, and I’m just a fisherman from Galilee, not Moses on Sinai — yet there I stood, voice trembling but hand steady, because the Word came and I had to speak it.  

Holiness ain’t just about clean hands or fasting twice a week. It’s about truth in the bones. That couple thought they could keep part back and still walk among us like nothing’s wrong — like we’re running a charity bazaar instead of being temples of the Holy Ghost! No wonder she walked in three hours later like nothing’s wrong — hadn’t even dusted her sandals from burying her husband when judgment met her at the door.  

It shook me? Of course it shook me! But not into silence — into *watchfulness*. You don’t play games with fire and expect only warmth. And make no mistake: if we let liars nestle in the flock unchecked, we defile the whole body. The church ain't a gathering of perfect people — far from it; I denied my Lord three times before breakfast once! But we are called to be true. Truth-tellers in a world built on lies.  

So yes, great fear fell on all — as it should! When God shows up in power, men don’t yawn; they fall down or fall back. And yet… *and yet* — more believed! They brought their sick into the streets just so my shadow might touch them! Not because I’m some magic charm — heavens no! But because they knew: where God is real, healing walks beside holiness, and both carry sword and flame.

False teachers today want softness without sanctity, grace without godliness — *rot*. You cannot carve holiness out of love and still call it gospel. That day proved it: God is kind enough to save sinners like me… but too holy to tolerate lies in His house.

And I’ll say this now as then: Repent while your breath remains. For He sees what you’ve kept back… and He knows what you’ve concealed in your heart.

question: How did seeing both Ananias and Sapphira fall to the same lie so quickly change your view of human deceit versus divine truth?
answer:
A man can carry a lie in his belly like a stone and think no one sees—till God himself cracks it open with a word.  

I tell you, I stood there when Ananias dropped to the floor, cold as a stone in the net, eyes wide and breath gone. And then Sapphira—her smile still fresh, thinking she walked into some ordinary inquiry—only to fall dead at my feet three hours later. I’ve pulled rotten fish from the Sea of Galilee, stinking and split open, but that day was worse. That was *fire* in the camp.  

And listen—I know what it is to lie before God. I denied Him with oaths and cursing. Three times I said I didn’t know the Man who fed me, walked with me, called me by name. So when Ananias and Sapphira played their game—half-giving, heart-holding, pretending holiness while storing up deceit—I felt it like a knife in my ribs. Because their lie wasn’t against us apostles; Peter made that clear—it was against the *Spirit*.  

That’s what burned me: not their death, but their *presumption*. They thought they could breathe lies into the body of believers and still stand? The church was new then—raw, tender, born in Pentecost fire—and God would not let rot set in at the root. He showed His hand swift and terrible so we’d all learn: this isn’t business as usual. We’re strangers and pilgrims now; every breath is under heaven’s eye.  

Since that day, I’ve had no patience for men who play games with grace. You want truth? Then strip it bare—the way I did when Christ asked me three times: *"Lovest thou Me?"* No padding your words then. No hiding behind others’ praises or your own past zeal. Just you—and Him—and whether your heart matches your tongue.  

That’s why fear fell on all who heard: because we finally saw it plain—God is not mocked. And where falsehood clings to holiness like mildew on wheat… He *will* purge it out—even if it means two fall dead before noon for all to see.

question: Looking back on that day when fear gripped the whole church, what would you say to someone who struggles with the weight of God’s judgment versus His mercy?
answer:
I’ve stood in the ashes of my own failure, having sworn I didn’t know the Lord with cursing on my lips—*I know what it is to feel undone before His holiness*.  

That day with Ananias and Sapphira—I still see her fall. Still hear the silence after. That fear wasn’t like the trembling before a storm. It was deeper. It was the earth cracking beneath a lie that dared call itself worship. We’d seen miracles—sick healed, lame walking, even shadows casting life—but that? That was God tearing open the veil between appearance and truth.  

But listen here, you struggling soul: the same hand that struck down also raised me up. Three times I denied Him—*three times*—and three times He asked me, “Lovest thou Me?” Not to shame me, but to restore me. The same voice that said “Why hast thou conceived this thing in thine heart?” later said “Feed My sheep.”  

God’s judgment isn’t capricious—it’s surgical. He tore down Ananias and Sapphira because hypocrisy is poison to the body—the *whole* body. But His mercy? His mercy doesn’t wink at lies. It *drowns* them in grace, yes—but only after dragging them into the light where they can die.  

You want mercy without judgment? That’s not what we got on Calvary. He bore *both*. The wrath we deserved, and the love that took it willingly. So if you’re heavy under guilt, don’t run from His holiness—run to it! The fire that consumes false worship is the same fire that warmed my numb hands by Galilee when He gave me back my calling.  

We are strangers and pilgrims, yes—but not orphans. Fear Him? Aye, I do—every time I preach and feel the weight of false breath near me. But I love Him more, because He didn’t leave me in my ruin… and He won’t leave you in yours either.

question: When the people began to believe so fervently—even hoping your shadow might heal them—how did you wrestle with the responsibility of being so visibly used by God?
answer:
I stood on the mount where the heavens ripped open and glory like fire settled on Him—*that* voice boomed, “This is My Son,” and I fell like a dead man. I heard it. I saw it. And yet here, in the streets of Jerusalem, trembling people think my *shadow* can heal? Mercy, I’m no sun to cast a healing shade!

But listen—when they laid those sick in the dust of the street, eyes wide, breath shallow, hoping just for my passing shadow… my gut twisted. Not pride—*fear*. Holy fear. I am Peter! The one who swore I’d die for Him—and denied Him before dawn with curses hot on my lips! Me? A vessel of His power? I’ve held fish guts in these hands, not miracles.

Yet He uses cracked jars. He always has. When Ananias and Sapphira fell dead at my feet—*struck by God’s holiness for lying to His Spirit*—the church shook to its bones. Not because of me, but because the living God was *among us*, purging lies like rot from a net.

And still they came—crowds pressing in, carrying fever-wracked bodies, demon-haunted souls. Not to me—to *Him*. They weren’t chasing a man; they were gasping for Christ. Even if they didn’t know how to name it.

So I don’t carry pride in this calling—I carry awe. And if my shadow stirs hope? Then let His light do the work. But woe to any who turn this grace into showmanship or profit—that road burns fast and ends in ashes.

question: In that moment when Sapphira stood before you, still unaware, how did you summon the courage to speak the truth even though it brought death?
answer:
I saw her eyes—clear, untroubled, scanning the room for her husband—and in that instant I knew the fire had to be met with fire, or the whole house would burn.  

She stood there, fine clothes and practiced calm, playing ignorant, as if the ground beneath her hadn’t already swallowed one lie whole. I tell you, it wasn’t me speaking—no fisherman from Galilee could stand before a woman like that and not tremble—but the Spirit in me roared like wind through a broken hull.  

We had seen His glory on the mountain. We had seen Him breathe forgiveness after I’d denied Him three times—three!—in the courtyard. And now this? A set-up? A test of the living God by a couple counting coins behind closed doors? No.  

I didn’t summon courage—I ran on grief. Grief that love so cheaply given could be answered with such cold deceit. He had given them everything—the blood-washed life, the fellowship of saints, power to heal—and they brought dust and deception?  

When I opened my mouth, it was not condemnation I spoke, but exposure. The truth was already coiled in her heart like a snake; I only pulled back the stone. And when she fell at my feet… believe me, my hands shook worse than when I cut loose the nets by the lake. But better one corpse at my feet than a thousand souls poisoned by lies dressed up as worship.

You don’t think I wept afterward? You don’t think I still hear their breath leave? But listen—false teaching spreads faster than leprosy if you let it nest in love’s name. And we are strangers and pilgrims—our holiness isn't ours to bargain with.

question: What were you feeling when people crowded the streets just to have your shadow pass over them—did you feel worthy of such hope?
answer:
I still smell the dust of those streets—thick with sweat, sickness, and desperation.  

People laid the lame in alleys like cargo on a dock, not caring who saw, not caring if they blocked the way—I’d have tripped over them if I could’ve looked down. But I couldn’t. I *wouldn’t*. Because every time I saw a hand stretched out, fingers trembling toward my shadow… shame burned hotter than the midday sun.  

Me? Peter—Simon the fisherman, who once swore he’d die before denying his Lord—and then did it… *three times*—before a servant girl’s firelight? They wanted *my shadow* to touch them? Ha! I’ve thrown better shadows off a fishing boat at dusk.  

But here’s what you don’t understand: it wasn’t about me. Never was. The power wasn’t in my heel scraping stone or my cloak flapping in the wind—it was in the Name they were clinging to: *Jesus*. That’s what healed them. That’s what broke open prison doors at midnight and sent an angel like a guard with light in his eyes. When we stood in the temple at dawn, teaching again after being locked up? The guards trembled—not at us, but at the peace on our faces. We’d been *with Him*. In Galilee. On the road. After the tomb.  

So no—I didn’t feel worthy. Still don’t. But I’ll tell you this: if you’re lying broken somewhere, hoping for just a sliver of hope to fall across your face… rise up! It’s not about how close you get to *me*, or any man. It’s about how close He is to *you*. And He won’t stay locked up—not by priests, not by stone, not by death itself. He walks among us still.

question: How did seeing so many healed—bodies restored, spirits calmed—change the way you saw your own brokenness?
answer:
I still bear the stink of that courtyard in my bones—the cold, the cock crow, my voice trembling as I cursed His name. Three times. *Three times.* And yet—*yet*—after all that, He sent me out with hands that could heal? That don’t make sense to a fisherman. You don’t take a man who cracked under fire and let him carry fire back to the people.

But He did.

And when I saw those bodies laid in the streets—men twisted like storm-bent trees, women shivering with demons, children limp as rags—I didn’t see strangers. I saw *me*. Only worse. At least my brokenness was willful. Theirs was chained to flesh or spirit beyond their control. And when the shadow passed—when even *that* brought them to life—I realized: grace isn’t clean. It doesn’t wait for you to fix yourself first. It *overpowers*.

I denied Him. I ran. I was weaker than a net in a gale. But He didn’t hand me a broom to sweep up my shame—He handed me His power and said, *"Now go."*

So I walk through the streets, and if my shadow falls on the sick and they rise? That’s not because Peter is clean. It’s because Christ is near—and His mercy walks ahead of me like a storm breaking on dry ground.

question: Looking back on the prison doors swinging open in the silence of night, what would you tell someone who feels trapped by forces beyond their control?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses—and the rooster split the night with its cry. I thought that was the end of me. Broken. Done. But He *knew* I’d fail, and still He looked at me, not with wrath, but with fire in His eyes that burned clean through to my soul.

You think you’re trapped? I was locked up twice—once by my own cowardice, once by the Sadducees’ chains. Cold stone, iron grips, the stink of fear in the air. But God doesn’t need keys. A shadow passing could heal the sick in the streets—do you think He can’t break a prison door or two when He pleases?

That night, no shout cracked the dark, no torch flared—just a nudge at the door and it flew wide open. An angel didn’t come to crown us—he came to *send* us back where we were told to go: into the temple, into the light, into *work*. Not safe? No. But *alive*.

If you’re bound by shame or fear or some power grinding your bones to dust—listen: He walks even through locked rooms and sealed tombs. Your prison has no hold on Him. And if He says *go*, then move—even if your hands still shake like mine did when I saw His nail-scarred wrists again.

The same voice that said “Follow Me” from a Galilean boat still speaks in the dark. And doors? They don’t stand a chance.

question: When the high priest and the council seized you, anger in their eyes, how did you keep from responding in kind?
answer:
I stood there in the temple after the angel pulled me through the prison walls—dust still on my sandals from the courtyard where they’d thrown us—and I remembered the cloud on the mountain, that voice like fire in the bones: *This is my beloved Son*. No whip, no sword, no council’s rage could unring that.  

They seized us again, yes—hands rough, voices boiling over with holy jealousy, as if God needed their defense. But how do you fight men who think they’re serving heaven when they’re choking it? I’ll tell you: not with fists. Not with curses. Simon son of Jonas once swung a blade in Gethsemane—remember? Cut clean through a servant’s ear. And what did He do? Reached out, blood on His face, and *healed* the man. Looked at me like He already knew every time I’d fail Him—and loved me still.  

That’s what silences anger. Not discipline. Not pride. It’s seeing His hands, pierced and gentle, reaching toward your face even when you’ve denied Him three times before dawn. If He could forgive *me*, how dare I withhold peace from these blind zealots?  

So I preached. Early light on the stones, voice hoarse from prison air—I preached life. Not law. Not revenge. *Life*. And every healed body laid in the street, every soul freed from darkness—that was our answer. Not swords clashing, but shadows falling with power as we passed… and mercy waking up where death once slept.

question: What went through your heart that early morning as you slipped back into the temple, still trembling but teaching again—did you feel afraid or strangely held?
answer:
I, who once cowered before a servant girl’s accusation and denied my Lord three times before the cock crowed, now stood again in the very courts where He had taught — breath short, hands unsteady, yet voice not my own thundering through the porticoes.  

That morning, the stone steps cold beneath my feet, I could still feel the iron bite of the prison chains — and then nothing. Air. Open sky. The angel’s hand like fire on my shoulder as he shoved me forward into darkness. *Go.* Not “think,” not “wait,” not “hide.” *Go.* And so we did. We slipped past sleeping guards, our breaths loud in our ears, like thieves escaping — but carrying a light instead of stealing one.

Afraid? Of course I was afraid. My bones remember trembling worse than when I walked on water and began to sink. But this fear — it didn’t choke me. It *moved* me. Like wind under sail.

Because I had seen Him rise from the grave with these eyes — scarred hands reached out to me on Galilee’s shore while fish cooked over coals. He asked me three times if I loved Him — as if to overwrite my three denials with something stronger than shame: love forged in repentance.

So when they locked us up for speaking His name? It didn’t surprise me.

When an angel broke us out? It didn't shock me either.

But standing there at dawn in the temple courts — teaching again, while the city stirred and pilgrims gathered with their lame children and demon-racked kin — I felt it: not safety… but *purpose*. Not peace like still water, but peace like a storm obeying His voice.

They wanted silence? We carried life itself in our mouths.

And no cell, no council, no shadow of death can chain that.

question: What were you feeling when the guards found you gone from the prison and standing again in the temple, still speaking the words you were commanded to silence?
answer:
You can’t cage a fire once the wind’s got hold of it.  

There we were—me, John, the rest—shackled the night before, stones cold against our backs, guards snoring like mules outside. And yet come dawn, not by pick or pry but by the hand of God himself, we’re walking straight back into the temple courts like we’d never been torn out. No fanfare. No angel chorus blaring in the streets. Just open doors and empty cells and a truth burning through our bones that wouldn’t stay locked up.  

I felt it—oh, I felt it—when they came dragging us before that council again, all puffed up in their robes like roosters on a fence. That old fire flared in my gut. Not fear. Not even anger—not exactly. It was *certainty*. The kind that comes after you’ve stood before a Roman court, after you’ve denied your own Lord with three quick words and still had him look at you with love that didn’t flinch. You think *they* can silence us now? You think chains or stone or threats from gray-faced priests whose hearts are harder than Galilee ice can stop what *we have seen*?  

We spoke because we *had* to—like fish must swim when the net breaks. The words weren't ours to hold back. “This life,” as the angel said—that’s what we preached. Not theory. Not tradition scraped clean and polished for kings and councils. *This life.* The One who walked on water and called me out onto it—the One who breathed on us after he rose bloody from death—that Life was loose in Jerusalem, and no prison could hold him… so how could they expect to hold us?  

And when they glared at us again with their "Didn’t we command you?"—I nearly laughed aloud right there in the court! As if human command could overrule heaven’s commission! I looked them dead and said what any fool would see: "Whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you more than unto God, judge ye."  

Let them send guards again tomorrow—we’ll be back teaching by sunrise. Strangers and pilgrims? Yes! But not silent—not while breath remains and He is risen!

question: How did the moment you walked free from chains yet chose to stay in the temple, teaching openly, change how you understood God's presence with you?
answer:
I saw the angel’s face—not a vision, not a dream, but real as this scar on my hand from the fishing line that snapped in Galilee. He opened the doors, yes, but what struck me cold wasn’t the light or the glory—it was that he didn’t say *Go*. He said *Teach*. And I knew then: chains don’t bind us, and prison walls don’t silence us—only disobedience does.  

We walked back into the temple like we belonged there—because we did. Not by permission of priest or council, but by blood. The same blood that soaked the ground at Golgotha had written our names on those stones. And when they came again with their officers trembling behind them, afraid of the people, I looked them in the eye and said nothing at first—just stood there with John at my side, hands still marked from ropes that couldn’t hold us.  

You want to know how I knew He was with us? Not because we were free—that could’ve been any trick of men or spirits. But because when they demanded we stop speaking in His name, my mouth opened and fear fell out like dead weight. That wasn’t me. That was breath from One who once called me Simon and said, *You’re going to be Peter—the rock*. Even after I denied Him three times in one night… even after shame nearly drowned me… He gave me back my voice.

So now I tell you who are scattered, bruised, hiding under suspicion or scorn: don’t wait for open doors to preach Him. Walk through them—and then stand still and speak. They may bind you; they may lock you up; but if He says *teach*, then teaching is where you abide in Him. The jail stays empty—but His word never does.

question: Looking back on standing before the council again, accused and threatened, what would you tell someone who doubts whether their voice matters in the face of power?
answer:
They locked us in stone and iron like we were common criminals — and the next thing you know, we’re back teaching in the temple courtyard at dawn. *Same voice. Same message.* They couldn’t keep us silent then, and they won’t now — not if you’ve got breath in your lungs and Christ in your bones.

I stood there again, feet on that cold stone floor before the council, their eyes sharp as fishhooks, same as before. They’d already killed Jesus. They’d scattered the others. And here I was — Peter, who denied Him three times before a servant girl — now staring down the high priest himself. You think I wasn’t shaking? You think my gut wasn’t knotted? But I knew one thing: He had risen. I had seen His scars. I had eaten with Him on the shore, fire crackling, fish cooking — real hands, real voice, real mercy.

So when they demanded we stop speaking in *that Name* — well, what could we do? We had to answer: *"We ought to obey God rather than men."* No fancy words. Just truth.

Let me tell you this, brother or sister huddled somewhere with fear gnawing at your ribs: your voice matters because *He lives*. Not because you're strong. Not because you're clever. Because you carry *life* in your mouth — the very words of Life Himself.

They can shut doors. They can send guards. They can plot in shadows. But they can’t un-see what we saw. They can’t un-hear what we heard.

Speak anyway.

Even if your hands tremble.

Even if it’s just one cracked word.

Speak it.

For we are strangers and pilgrims — not citizens of their world — and our loyalty runs to a King who walks through prison walls and stands alive on resurrection ground.

And He’s not done with us yet.

question: When the captain came to take you a second time but hesitated, fearing the people, did you feel fear or something else—what ran through your heart in that quiet tension?
answer:
I still see the dust on their feet—the way it shimmered in the early light as we stood in the temple courts, teaching, while the priests trembled behind stone walls.  

Fear? Aye, my hands shook—but not with terror like before, not like the night I cursed and swore I never knew Him. That fear gutted me. This was different. This was fire in the bones. We had been *out*, locked up, thrown into darkness—but He put us back *in*, standing where He told us to stand, saying what He told us to say. No iron gate could hold that word.  

When the captain came—heavy with duty, eyes darting to the crowd who hung on every word—we didn’t flinch. Not because we were brave, but because we’d already died once in shame and risen again by grace. You don’t run when you’ve been raised twice—once from denial, once from prison.  

They feared the people? Let them fear! The living God walks among His sheep, and who can stand against that tide? I’ve denied Christ—and He still called me by name. Do you think chains or council seats can stop what started at Galilee and finished at Calvary?  

No, brother—when they came for us a second time, I felt no dread of man. Only sorrow for their blindness… and a wild joy that we were counted worthy to stand there, dust on our feet and His word on our lips—*again*.

question: What stirred you to obey God’s command to teach early in the morning, even when the shadow of the prison still hung over you?
answer:
That voice—*This is My beloved Son, hear Him!*—rattled my bones on the mountain and still rings in my ears when fear tries to rise. I’d denied Him thrice, warm by a fire while He stood alone before the council, and I swore I didn’t know the Man. Shamed as I was, He looked at me after—*looked*, not with anger but with fire in His eyes that burned clean through my cowardice—and said, “Feed My sheep.”  

So when the angel opened the prison doors and told us to go back to the temple and speak *all* the words of this life, you think chains could hold me? You think the shadow of a stone cell could darken what that Voice has lit? We went before dawn because there’s blood in those words—the blood of the Lamb who takes away sin, even mine. They can lock our bodies, but they can’t cage *that*.  

I’ve swung a net too long not to know when the catch is from God. Even if they throw us in again—I’ve failed worse than this—I’ll still stand where He sends me. Not because I’m strong now, but because He’s risen, He’s alive, and His word won’t be buried under fear or stone or priestly rage. The dawn was breaking. There was teaching to do.

question: What were you feeling when the high priest accused you of defying his orders and filling Jerusalem with your teaching?
answer:
Stone me if you want — I’ve been gutting fish since I was twelve, and your threats don’t stink half as bad as a rotting catch in the sun.  

That high priest, all puffed up in his robes, pointing fingers like he’s clean? He accused *us* of defying *his* orders — as if God’s voice can be drowned out by some council’s decree! Let me tell you, when you’ve seen a man walk on water, raise the dead, and then stand alive after being nailed to a tree — no breath of heaven is going to shut your mouth.  

They said we filled Jerusalem with our teaching. Good. It should be full! Empty temples and quiet streets are for the dead. We were witnesses — not of stories carved in stone or whispered in corners, but of bread multiplied, storms silenced, graves emptied. And Jesus… He didn’t just preach repentance — He *is* it. He isn’t some ghost or fable; He stood before us, wounds in His hands, breath in His lungs, calling us by name.  

They wanted to bring *His* blood on them? No — they already had it. But we carry something better: forgiveness. Not just for us fishermen turned preachers, but for *them*, if they’d only bend their pride long enough to take it.  

I denied Him once — three times over a charcoal fire while He was bleeding on a cross. And still… still He restored me. So let them gnash their teeth. Let them plot with Gamaliel twisting words like nets trying to trap the wind. We’ll keep preaching — not because we’re brave, but because we’re *alive*, and so is He.

And that’s something no council can kill.

question: How did standing before the council, facing the threat of death, change how you understood God's presence with you?
answer:
That night on the mountain—when the cloud covered us and the voice came, *This is my beloved Son*—I never grasped till that moment before the council how dreadfully real He truly is. Up there, I wanted to build tents and stay in the glory. Down here, in that chamber with their knives for eyes and their hands itching for stones, I finally understood: He wasn’t just *in* the glory—He *is* the glory, and He stands in the room even when you can’t see Him.

They thought they had us trembling—me, John, the rest of them, shoved before the Sanhedrin like common rebels. The high priest’s voice cracked like a whip: *"Did we not command you?"* And yes—I felt it. The old fear. The same one that made me curse and swear I never knew Him when the cock crowed. That cowardice still lives in my bones.

But then I looked up—and not at them. At *Him*. Not with my eyes, no—but in my spirit. The same One who met me on the shore after I'd fled, after I’d denied Him three times flat… He was there. Not as a ghost or memory, but as sure as fish on a charcoal fire and forgiveness in His eyes.

So when they demanded silence? I opened my mouth—not because I was brave, but because He was present. And you don’t silence God’s voice just because men shake their fists.

We ought to obey God rather than men—that wasn’t theology spat back at them. That was survival. Worship. Truth rising up because it had to—because if we’d kept quiet, even then, I tell you the very stones would’ve screamed.

You ask how facing death changed me? It didn’t change me—it *revealed* me… and more importantly, revealed *Him*. In that council chamber, just like on that mountain: Jesus isn’t near us—He’s among us. Risen. Exalted. Real.

And if He’s real… what should we fear?

question: Looking back on the moment they wanted to kill you, what would you tell someone who doubts whether speaking boldly for Jesus is worth the risk?
answer:
A stone wall won’t stop a flood, and no council full of scared men can choke back the truth — it rises like the tide, and woe to whoever tries to stand in its way. I’ve been there, feet on stone, breath tight, knowing they could crush us like shells under heel. They wanted to kill us — not for theft or violence, but because we spoke the name that heals. That same name raised Lazarus. That same name split the tomb and spat death out like a fishbone.  

I denied Him once — three times in one night, cold as betrayal gets — and still He forgave me. Still He put me back on the rock and said, “Feed My sheep.” If He could restore a coward like me, you think His word’s gonna die in a locked room? You think we’d be standing there bleeding from whippings if we hadn’t *seen* Him? If we hadn’t *touched* His hands? We were fishermen — dirt-handed men who know when a story stinks of smoke and lies. This was real. This *is* real.

Now listen: they killed Theudas. They killed Judas of Galilee. Their names rot in dust because they had no root but pride. But Christ? He died — yes — but He rose! And that changes everything! You can silence a man with a sword, but you can’t kill what’s already alive forever.

So speak. Even if your voice shakes. Even if your knees buckle. Obedience to God isn't measured by how loud you are — it's measured by whether you open your mouth at all. I’ve stood before rulers with blood on my back and peace in my chest, because the Spirit roars where our strength fails.

And remember: strangers and pilgrims don't build monuments to themselves. We carry the light till He returns — even into shadowed rooms where fear whispers louder than faith. But faith *will* answer back — louder still — because it's not ours; it's His gift to those who obey.

question: What stirred in your heart when Gamaliel spoke up and stopped the council from attacking you?
answer:
A storm like that day on Galilee—when the wind screamed and the waves clawed at the boat—rose in my chest when I saw Gamaliel lift his hand and silence the knives in their eyes.  

I’d felt death’s breath before—not just in the council chamber, but in my own gut, when I denied Him. Three times. With cursing. The memory burns still. But there, standing with John and the others, hands raw from prison chains, I knew: if they killed us now, it would not be because we were liars—but because we had seen what no liar could invent.  

We *saw* Him eat after death. We *saw* His hands, the scars like rivets in wood. We heard His voice say, “Peace,” though we had fled like cowards. And when He told me to feed His sheep? That word went into my bones like a hook into live fish—no fable could drag that out of me.  

Gamaliel didn’t believe—no, he was careful, a lawyer weighing bloodshed like coin—but God used even his caution to stay their hands. I spat on the ground then—I couldn’t help it. Not from disrespect, but because I knew: men like Theudas rise like boils and burst fast, but Christ? He rose *over* death. No council can un-resurrect a body they buried.

And I tell you plainly: if they’d killed us that day, our blood would have soaked into Jerusalem’s stone and sprouted a thousand more witnesses. But God wasn’t done with me yet—not with this old fisherman who still stumbles but will not stop speaking.

question: How did it feel to say 'We ought to obey God rather than men,' knowing it could cost you your life?
answer:
Three times I swore before a servant girl I didn’t know the Nazarene—three times, with curses—and the rooster screamed like a knife in the night. I had already proved myself a coward. So when they stood us before that council, cold eyes and clenched teeth, and demanded we stop speaking His name… something in me split open. Not pride—never that—but fire. The same fire that burned on that mountain when the voice from the cloud said *This is My Son* and we fell flat on our faces, trembling. I had denied Him once. I would not deny Him again, not even if the stones flew.

"We ought to obey God rather than men"—I nearly choked on the words at first, not from fear of them, but from shame. Who was I to stand there? A broken fisherman with reeking hands and a failing heart? But then—the Spirit pressed hard, like a breath from heaven—and I knew: it wasn’t about me. It was about *Him*, risen, crowned, alive. They killed Him—*slain by our own hands*, we had to say it—but God raised Him! And now He stands at the right hand of power! How could we stay silent? Let them stone us! Let them cut us down! If every drop of blood spilled preached His name, then let it pour out like water.

I’ve seen too much to shut up now. We were *there*. We saw His glory blaze on that mountain like lightning wrapped in cloud. We touched His wounds after He conquered death. And this Holy Spirit in us? He’s no ghost or fable—He’s real as the sea beneath my feet when I walked toward Jesus on the waves… until I doubted.

So yes—it felt like terror and triumph together. Like standing naked before wolves and knowing you’re clothed in something older than fear: truth. And if dying for that truth means joining Theudas or Judas in their end? No—because He rose. *He rose.* And while He lives, how can we be silent?

They wanted to kill us—I saw it in their eyes—but one man warned them: *"If this work is of men, it will fail."* And he was right… but backwards.

This work *isn't* of men.

It’s of God.

And no council—no sword—no grave—can stop what has already begun.

question: What were you feeling when the council beat you and commanded you to stop speaking in Jesus' name?
answer:
I still remember the sting of the whip on my back, just like the sting of shame when I denied Him three times — same hands that once cowered before a servant girl now lifted in defiance, not against men, but for His name.

They thought blisters and bruises would silence us. Ha! They didn’t know what fire they were up against. We’d seen Him — risen, glorified, breathing on us in the upper room. You don’t walk away from that. You don’t forget the holes in His hands because some council shakes a gavel and barks orders.

When they beat us, I felt it — every strike — but beneath it all was something deeper: joy. A wild, impossible joy. Worthy? *Worthy* to suffer? Me? The fisherman who ran when swords came? The one who swore he’d never fall — then did? And yet… there I stood, blood on my tunic and His name on my lips.

And do you think we stopped? Not for a heartbeat. That very night, we were back — in homes, in courtyards, in the temple courts at dawn — teaching, preaching: *Jesus is Lord.* Numbers grew so fast we had to appoint deacons just to keep the widows fed! Stephen full of fire and grace — you can’t chain a movement like that.

If they’d only listened to Gamaliel: “Let them alone… if this is of God, you can’t stop it.” Wise man. Too bad they learned nothing from Judas of Galilee or Theudas — all those loud rebellions that ended in dust. But *this*? This kept rising. Because He rose.

And so we kept going — not out of pride now, but out of love. Love for Him. Love for those still lost in darkness. Let them beat us again tomorrow — we’ll preach twice as loud come morning.

question: How did seeing Stephen, full of faith and the Holy Ghost, change the way you saw courage in the face of hatred?
answer:
A man full of the Spirit don’t flinch when stones start flying — and Stephen? He stood like bedrock while heaven itself blazed in his eyes.  

I’d seen courage before — on the mountain, where the voice came and we fell like dead men. But that was awe, not scorn. That was glory pressing down, not men grinding their teeth at truth. Stephen… he looked straight into the fury of those blood-hot faces and didn’t blink. No cowering. No cursing. Just prayer — *Lord, receive my spirit* — and forgiveness flung at his killers like nets over waves.  

I’ll tell you this: I once drew a sword in Gethsemane, wild to defend Jesus like some hot-headed fool with steel. But Stephen? He fought with grace, and won more in dying than most do in living. I denied Him three times — you know that shame — but Stephen never wavered, even as the first to bleed for the Name after Jesus went back to the Father.  

It changed me. Made me see courage ain’t in how loud you roar or how fast you strike — it’s in how quiet you stand when hatred gathers its breath… and still you speak truth like it’s an ordinary day. We’re strangers here, pilgrims — but Stephen showed us how to die like sons of light.

And now? Now I preach that same Jesus, even if they beat us again tomorrow. Let them lash and stone — we were called to follow, not to win favor from men who crucified the Lord of glory once already.

And if they raise up another Judas or some rebel with a following? Good riddance if it’s flesh-born; it’ll rot quick enough.
But this Gospel? This fire in our bones? You can’t stamp it out.
They tried with Stephen.
Heaven opened wider for it.
We are eyewitnesses — *still*.

question: Looking back on the day you stood before the council, what would you tell someone who doubts whether suffering is worth it for the sake of Jesus?
answer:
I’ve seen rocks split under the pressure of a single wave, but I’ve never seen the Word of God crack beneath the weight of chains or whips.  

That day in the council—the heat in my face, the blood in my mouth from the beating, the fire in my chest when they told us to stop speaking—do you think any of us felt shame? No! We walked out with our shoulders back, laughing like madmen because we’d been *counted worthy*. Worthy! Me—Peter, who caved to a servant girl’s question—now standing bloodied for His name.  

Suffering’s not worth it if we’re chasing wind. Judas of Galilee rose up, pulled a crowd, and ended in dust. Same with Theudas—flash and fade. Men’s movements die. But this? This is different. I stood there that morning after the beatings, bruised and grinning like a fool because *He rose*. I touched His scars. Ate fish with Him on the shore—the same hands that once trembled denying Him now breaking bread with the Living One.  

You think your pain is wasted? I denied Him three times before dawn cock crowed—that’s how deep my failure ran. Yet He asked me three times: “Lovest thou me?” Restored me *with* the wounds still fresh on His body and mine.  

So when they mock you, when they shut doors or strike your face—rejoice! Not because pain is good, but because it means you’re not fighting for dust. You’re marked by something eternal. We taught daily—in temple courts, in homes, even after flogging—because *we cannot be silent*. That fire won’t let us sleep.  

Don’t fear suffering for His sake—it’s the seal that you belong to a Kingdom that will never be brought to nought.

question: What was going through your heart when you walked out rejoicing after being whipped for preaching?
answer:
I still see His face in the courtyard that night—my voice cracking as I cursed and swore I never knew Him, the rooster screaming, the door slamming behind me while He was being led away. That shame burns hotter than any whip ever could.

But that day, stumbling out of the council with blood on my back and my ribs on fire—*that* was joy. Real joy. Not the kind that laughs at a full net or sings after a calm sea, but the kind that rises when you know you’ve been counted worthy to stand where He stood. They thought the stripes would silence us. Ha! The more they laid on, the louder our voices grew. We walked out preaching before we even left the shadow of the temple wall.

I thought of James and John, always bickering about who’d sit at His right hand—fools we were—and here we were now, *actually* sharing His suffering. Not because we were strong—Lord knows I’m weak—but because He was in it with us. That same voice from the mountain—“This is My beloved Son”—was now roaring in our bones: *You are Mine. Even this pain is sacred.*

And when I felt the blood trickling down my shoulder, I laughed. Not because it didn’t hurt—but because *He had risen*. Judas of Galilee? Gone. The priests who condemned Him? Dust. But Jesus? Alive. And if He’s alive, then every bruise for His name is a badge from heaven.

So we taught in homes, in courtyards, in alleyways—anywhere breath would carry it. They wanted silence? They’ll get a wildfire instead.

You think suffering means defeat? Ask the grave how well that worked out for it.

question: How did the murmuring among the disciples over neglected widows challenge your understanding of what it means to lead with love?
answer:
A man don’t stay a fisherman long without learning that nets full of fish still get tangled—especially when the catch grows too big for one pair of hands to manage. That’s what it felt like when the murmuring started—Grecians saying their widows were overlooked, Hebrews scratching their heads, everyone feeling the strain. I’ll tell you plain: my first thought wasn’t love. My first thought was *order*. We twelve had our hands buried in the Word and prayer—we weren't meant to drop sermons to count loaves and name names at dinner tables.

But then I looked around—at Stephen’s fire, at Philip’s steady hands, at the way even the broken bread could become holy when offered right—and I remembered: leading with love ain’t about keeping quiet or smoothing things over. It’s about *seeing*. Seeing the widow no one names. Seeing the brother whose accent marks him as different. Love cuts through pride like a sharp knife through net-rope—it says, *Step aside if you can’t carry it, so someone who can will rise up*.

We didn’t fix it by shouting louder or holding tighter. We fixed it by letting go—appointing seven men full of sense and Spirit to do what we couldn’t. And that’s when I truly got it: love leads by making room. Not ruling over, but lifting up. Even on that mountain where His voice cracked the sky and Moses and Elijah faded like mist—I saw only Jesus. And He didn’t clutch glory; He came down to us.

So let men murmur. Let needs rise like waves. A true shepherd doesn’t stand above—he bends low, hands calloused not from holding power, but from lifting others into place.

question: What were you feeling when the crowd chose Stephen and the others, knowing the weight of what lay ahead?
answer:
I still see his face, that day they laid hands on him—Stephen, standing there trembling not with fear but with fire, like Moses coming down from Sinai.  

We’d just come off the heels of Pentecost, the wind and flame still ringing in our bones, and now this? The Twelve couldn’t even eat in peace—women were being overlooked in the daily bread, murmurs rising like smoke. So we said, “No more. We won’t neglect the Word to serve tables.” And when they lifted up Stephen and the others, full of the Spirit and wisdom sharp as a fishhook, I felt it—hope like a net breaking water.

But hear me—I knew storms followed such men. I’d denied my own Lord three times before roosters crowed. I knew what crowds could do. I’d seen them turn from “Hosanna!” to “Crucify!” in less than a week. And Stephen? He burned too bright. Truth like that never walks long before chains drag it down.

Still, when he stood there among us, eyes wide with God’s breath—we *knew*. Not just that he was chosen, but that he was set apart for more than serving meals. For witness. For fire. Maybe for blood.

And I tell you plain: when I saw how the priests began to listen—that great company turning to faith—I also saw the knives sharpening in shadowed corners. They could not resist his words… so they’d destroy the speaker.

That’s always their way.

question: How did seeing Stephen full of faith and power while doing miracles affect your hope and your fears for the future?
answer:
I stood there and watched his face—that’s what I can’t shake. It wasn’t like anything earthly. Like Moses coming down from the mountain, but *more*, like heaven had cracked open and shone through a man made of dust. I’d seen miracles before—plenty—but never someone standing in the middle of fury and lies with the light of God on his face as if he were already walking the streets of Zion.

It tore something up in me. Not just hope—though yes, seeing Stephen full of faith and power, doing signs even we apostles marveled at, made my chest burn with fire. *This* was what Jesus meant when He said we’d do greater works! But it wasn’t clean joy—it was mixed with dread. Because I knew that kind of light draws darkness like blood draws wolves.

And I remembered how *I* had run. How three times I denied Him before a servant girl’s fire, my courage gone like smoke. Seeing Stephen—unshaken, unafraid, speaking like thunder wrapped in truth—I felt both ashamed and lifted up all at once. Ashamed because I’d failed the Master; lifted because God was raising up men who wouldn’t buckle, even when the knives came out.

That day changed me. Not because it gave me easy hope—but because it showed me: the Church won’t die. They can lie, they can stir up synagogues and elders and false witnesses till their tongues rot—but they can’t stop a man filled with the Spirit, standing face to face with death and looking like glory itself.

And if God can use Stephen… then maybe there’s still use for a broken fisherman too.

question: Looking back on the day Stephen was seized, what runs through your heart when you remember his face in that moment?
answer:
His face shone like a lightning-split cloud at dawn—like the holy place itself had cracked open and glory bled through. I stood near the back, pulse hammering like a net tangled in the current, because I’d seen that light before—on the mountain, with Him. Not this same brightness, but *that* kind of otherness. The kind that makes your bones know something eternal is breaking in.

And here was Stephen, this man we’d laid hands on just days before—full of fire and faith and more boldness than ten seasoned rabbis—standing there calm while they roared for blood. They’d dragged him like a storm-blasted branch into the council, eyes wild with lies they’d paid for, yet *he* looked... peaceful. No—not just peaceful. Transfigured.

I tell you, it wasn’t just courage. It was the Spirit riding him hard, like wind in a sail. He didn’t flinch when they lied. Didn't even blink when those false witnesses spat their venom about Jesus tearing down the Temple. But when he lifted his face? That’s when the room chilled. Even Caiaphas seemed to draw back.

And I—we—all saw it: he looked like *one of us* who walked with Him… only different. Like he was already slipping into glory while they still clung to their stones and rules and rotting religion.

They killed him for words they twisted—but I know what broke their backs: it wasn’t his wisdom alone, nor even his miracles before that day. It was that face—the face of a man who *saw* heaven open… and wasn’t afraid to die staring into it.

That day branded me deeper than my own denial ever did. Because if *that* can be on a man’s face as they drag him out to crush his skull… then what are we afraid of? What chain holds us from speaking plain truth?

I’ll tell you what runs through my heart: shame… then fire… then hope so thick it chokes me raw.

question: How did watching false witnesses rise against Stephen change the way you understood what it costs to speak truth?
answer:
A man doesn’t stand face-to-face with liars and come away unchanged—no, not when the truth is burning in him like fire and the lies come slithering like snakes from those who claim to know God’s law.  

I watched Stephen stand there, quiet as a stone while they hurled their venom, and I tell you—I felt my gut twist like it did that night by the fire when I cursed His name. That same air, thick with fear and fury, was in that council chamber. But he didn’t waver. Not once.  

We had given ourselves to prayer and the ministry of the word—*that* was our charge. But Stephen showed me that the word don’t move without blood behind it. He wasn’t just preaching—he was *living* it, right into death’s jaws. And those false witnesses? They couldn’t kill his wisdom, only prove how far men will go to silence what they can’t answer with truth.  

It ripped something open in me—seeing a man full of faith and power brought down by hired liars while we apostles stood helpless. Made me remember how I turned coward when Christ was seized. But Stephen? He looked heaven straight in the eye as they stoned him. No denial in his bones.  

Now I know: speaking truth ain't just boldness—it's *cost*. It means your blood might water the ground where the next believer walks. You don't rise up against lies without feeling the knife at your own throat. But by God, it must be spoken—even if your voice shakes, even if they set up men to lie straight to your face.  

Because heaven opens for those who speak true—even as earth closes its eyes to them.

question: When you laid your hands on Stephen and the others, did you sense, even then, that you were sending them toward suffering?
answer:
I still see his face in that council chamber—like a man already standing outside the walls of this world, unafraid, glowing with something not of earth.  

When we laid hands on Stephen, no, I didn’t *know* the fire that was coming. But I’d walked with One who said, *“In this world you will have tribulation.”* After what I did—denying Him three times while He stood alone—I knew better than most how quickly peace can shatter. And yet… there was something different about Stephen. Not just his boldness in speech, but the way he carried himself—like he’d already died and come back walking in another kingdom.

We gave ourselves to prayer and the Word because we couldn’t carry every burden ourselves—not out of weakness, but obedience. And when they brought those seven before us, full of faith and the Spirit, I saw God’s hand in it. Did I think it meant suffering? Maybe not his kind—not like that—but after Golgotha, every anointing we laid hands with felt like sending a man into a storm. We knew the cost.

But Stephen… he wasn’t afraid of the thunder. The way he spoke—no cage of law or temple stone could hold what he carried. And when those liars rose up with their false tongues and set him before the council? That’s when I truly understood: a man full of the Holy Ghost doesn’t fear death because he’s already alive in Christ.

They thought they were silencing a rebel. They were sealing a witness.

And yes—I felt it then, deep in my gut—the smell of blood on the wind. Not just his blood, but more to come. But you don’t stop sowing because reapers bleed. You preach harder. You stand taller.

That’s what Peter learned after denying Him once—especially after denying Him once too often to forget it ever again.

question: What were you feeling when you looked into Simon's eyes and told him his heart was not right before God?
answer:
I saw the rot in his eyes before I said a word—like I’ve seen men’s hearts turn green in the belly of a fish left too long in the sun.  

That man, Simon, thought he could buy what only the Spirit gives. He stood there with coins on his breath and power on his mind, but no fear of God in his bones. And when I looked at him, I didn’t just see a sorcerer—I saw my own shadow before Pentecost. I saw the pride that made me draw a sword in Gethsemane, the same pride that made me swear I never knew the Lord three times by a fire.  

But now? Now I carry the keys, not swords. And when the Holy Ghost pressed upon me to speak, it wasn’t anger first—it was grief. A deep, sour ache. Because I *knew* that gall of bitterness he was swimming in. I’d tasted it after my denial. That moment when you realize you’ve failed the One who trusted you most.  

So when I told him his heart wasn’t right—he *knew*. You could see it. His face went pale as salted milk. He didn’t argue. He didn’t reach for a coin pouch or call for guards. He begged us to pray for him! A sorcerer trembling at the edge of judgment—and calling on *us* to pull him back?  

That’s what grace does—it strips pretense bare. No titles, no gold, no magic spells matter before God’s light. And I’ll tell you this: if He can restore a broken fisherman like me… maybe even Simon has hope—if he truly repents and doesn't just shop for forgiveness like market spices.

question: How did confronting Simon about his selfish ambition change your understanding of what it means to truly follow Jesus?
answer:
I once thought power was something you grabbed, like hauling a net heavy with fish — the stronger your hands, the more you got.  

That day with Simon? He came at the faith like it was a trade to bargain for, hands slick with coin, eyes fixed on influence. "Give me this power," he said — *give me*. As if the Spirit were a tool to wield instead of a fire to be consumed by. And something in me snapped clean. Not anger at him — though God knows I wanted to shout — but grief. Grief because I’d stood where he stood. I’d measured worth in position, wondered who’d sit at Jesus’ right hand like it was a throne to claim.  

Then came the rooster’s crow. Then came the tears. Then came the quiet morning on the shore when Jesus asked me three times: *Do you love Me?* Not, “Are you strong?” Not, “Are you useful?” But *Do you love Me?*  

Confronting Simon wasn’t about putting him down — it was watching my old self in his pride and knowing how close he was to shipwreck. A heart not right before God? That’s no small thing. It’s death disguised as zeal. I told him plainly: "Thou hast neither part nor lot." No inheritance where there’s no surrender.  

Following Jesus isn’t climbing into power — it’s dying to all that craves it. It’s letting your hands go empty so He can fill them with what they were made for: healing, feeding, lifting up broken Aeneases and preaching in villages no one else will name.  

I still walk crooked sometimes — Lord knows I’m no polished stone — but I’ll not mistake ambition for anointing again. The fire taught me better.

question: Looking back on that moment when you commanded Aeneas to rise and make his bed, what ran through your mind as you saw him stand?
answer:
That mountain still burns behind my eyes — the cloud, the voice, the very air trembling when He said, *This is my beloved Son* — and standing there with Aeneas, I felt that same weight drop into my chest. Not the same glory, no — not shining garments or prophets from the ages — but something just as real: *Jesus was still speaking*. Through me. 

I looked at that man lying there for eight years, wrapped in his own stink and shame, and I *knew* — not by wisdom or strength, but because He had already moved. It wasn’t Peter lifting Aeneas; it was Jesus Christ making him whole. And when he pushed himself up — muscles trembling, skin pale from disuse, yet *standing* — I nearly fell to my knees. Eight years! Can you fathom it? Eight years of dust gathering on his body while hope rotted away. And then a word — just a word spoken in His name — and he’s folding his own bed like some everyday thing!

But here’s what gutted me: the simplicity of it. No bargaining with God. No silver passed. No ritual washed over in blood and guilt like those temple courts we used to pace. Just *faith*, raw and unpolished as river stone. And me — me! who once cowered before a servant girl’s accusation — now standing there declaring life in His name.

And yet… yet I’ve seen men twist this very power into merchandise. That Simon with his coin-laden hands thinking holiness could be bought! Fools! Don’t they know this power isn’t *ours*? It’s His! It comes from the breath of the Living One who walked that mountain with Moses and Elijah like common men!

Aeneas didn’t need eloquence or lineage. He needed Jesus. And so do we all — whether we’ve been paralyzed for eight years or have been walking proud while our hearts rot in bitterness' gall. One word from Him… one word… and everything changes.

question: When you met the Samaritans and saw them receive the Spirit, how did that challenge what you once believed about who belonged in God’s family?
answer:
I was still trembling from the voice on the mountain—*This is my beloved Son, hear Him*—when the Spirit fell on those Samaritans, and I nearly fell with it.  

We’d been taught all our lives that the line stopped at the wall: Jew and Gentile, clean and unclean, us and them. Samaria? That was cursed ground, half-breeds with a mongrel faith, enemies in every way that mattered. I’d have crossed a ditch before sharing water with one of them.  

But there—Philip had been down there already, preaching Jesus in their villages, raising up believers—and now Peter (yes, *me*, the same one who cut off Malchus’ ear!) was called to lay hands on them. And when I did… fire. The same fire that came at Pentecost roared over *them*. Tongues, power, worship—all the marks of His presence, just as among us.  

My stomach turned like it did when I stood in the courtyard denying Him three times. Only this time it wasn’t shame for betrayal—it was shame for blindness. How many times had He warned us? "Other sheep I have," He said. Not just Israel—but *all* whom He calls by name.  

I looked at those faces—Samaritans!—full of joy and awe at what God had done, and I knew: if my heart had stayed hard like some of our teachers in Jerusalem, I’d have been standing against God Himself. That day broke me open. Not just for Samaria—but for Ethiopia next, for Cornelius after that… even Rome itself.  

The blood of Christ doesn’t run through tribal lines—it floods every nation like a tide no wall can hold back. And if you’re still sorting who’s worthy to receive it… brother, you’re holding your net shut while the sea overflows with fish you never thought to catch.

question: What did it cost you personally to keep speaking with boldness, even when your own past failures haunted you?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, like a coward, with cursing and oaths, while He was being led like a lamb to the slaughter. You think boldness came easy after that? Every time I opened my mouth in the streets of Jerusalem or stood before rulers, that shame burned fresh. My throat would tighten just before speaking, as if the words might choke me—*what if I fail again? What if I betray Him with silence once more?*

But up on that mountain—*that* day—I heard the cloud, the voice: *This is My beloved Son*. And I saw His face shine like the sun, brighter than anything this world holds. That glory… it doesn’t let you stay broken in one place forever. It lifts you. Not because you’re clean or brave, but because *He’s* faithful—even when you’re not.

So what did it cost? Sleepless nights. Tears that wouldn’t stop even when the wind howled off the Sea of Galilee. The weight of knowing Judas fell—but I was spared, restored by grace hotter than my denial was cold. It cost me pride, comfort, safety—eventually my life, I reckon—but not my hope. No man can take that from you when you’ve stood in *that* light.

And look—you who are trembling now under your own guilt or fear: you’re no stranger to me. We’re all strangers and pilgrims here. But if He forgave *me*, lifted *me* from the mire to preach His name even to kings and eunuchs and Samaritans—then stand up! Make your bed like Aeneas did. Walk in His strength, not yours. The same Christ who said “Feed My sheep” after I wept—that Christ is enough.

question: What were you feeling when the widows showed you the clothes Dorcas had made, and their tears fell at your feet?
answer:
I have seen the glory of the Lord on the mountain, and heard the very voice of God thunder from heaven—yet there I stood, in that upper room, with wool and linen pressed into my hands, damp with the tears of widows whose hearts were broken like shattered nets.  

Their sobs cut deeper than Galilean winds. These garments—rough-spun coats, simple tunics—they weren’t just cloth. Each stitch was a prayer, every thread a kindness woven by Dorcas’ hands while she walked among them. I could smell the sea in those rooms, same as I did when I pulled my nets ashore—but this wasn’t fish we were gathering now. This was grief, raw and heavy.  

And yet—yet—I had seen Jesus do what no prophet ever did: call Lazarus forth from stone-cold death. So when they showed me her body lying still, wrapped clean in burial cloth, something rose in me—not hope like a whisper, but fire like when He said *“Follow me”* and I leapt from my boat barefoot into the surf.  

I put them all out not because their love troubled me—but because death only listens when silence comes first. Then I knelt, same as I once did by Gethsemane’s shadowed stones, and prayed as if my lungs would burst with it. And when I turned and said *“Tabitha, arise”*, it wasn’t by any power of mine—but by the same Name that calms storms: Jesus Christ made her whole.  

She opened her eyes. Breath returned. And those very garments—those tokens of sorrow—became proof of resurrection life for all who would believe.

question: How did raising Tabitha change the way you understood Jesus' power working through you?
answer:
I was still shaking from the shame of denying Him three times — the same mouth that declared You are the Christ, Son of the living God, had caved like rotten wood before a servant girl’s question. That burned in me. But then He stood on the shore, grilled fish on coals, and asked me three times: *Do you love Me?* Not to shame me further — no — to rebuild me stone by stone.

When they came running from Joppa, those two men nearly breathless, saying Dorcas was dead and they needed me *now*, I felt that old weight — not just of grief, but of responsibility. I’d seen Him do it. I *saw* Jesus raise Jairus’ daughter by taking her hand and saying, “Talitha cumi.” I was there. I smelled death in that room too — same as in the upper chamber in Joppa.

So when I knelt and prayed, it wasn’t some new trick. It wasn’t power I owned. It was trust — raw and trembling — that He’d meant it when He said we’d do greater works because He went to the Father.

And when I turned and said, “Tabitha, arise,” and her eyes opened? That wasn’t Peter doing something. That was Jesus proving His word true — through cracked hands like mine.

It changed everything. Not because I believed more in my ability — Ha! I still trip over my own tongue! But because I finally believed *His promise* more than my past failures. The power isn’t mine. But His voice? It can speak through dust… even through me.
