question: Looking back on the moment you told Aeneas to rise and make his bed, what do you remember most about the weight of that voice leaving your lips?
answer:
That voice wasn’t mine—it never was. I said the words, but they came from a fire in my bones that started the day I denied Him three times and He still looked at me like I was worth sending. Aeneas lay there, eight years paralyzed, and I stood over him not knowing if my next breath would carry death or life—but Christ had already breathed on me at the Sea of Galilee and said, *Feed My sheep.* So when I said, *Jesus Christ maketh thee whole,* it wasn’t boldness—it was obedience with a lump in my throat.

I remember the silence after. Not peaceful—no, thick as tar. Then he moved. Not groaning, not struggling—*immediately* sat up like a man waking to breakfast. And the bed—he made his own bed! Can you imagine? A man who hadn’t stood in nearly a decade folding his blanket like any son of Aaron might do at dawn. That’s what broke me. Not the miracle—the obedience after healing. The world saw a healed man; I saw a disciple born in an instant.

We were strangers and pilgrims then as now—called to walk where He opens the door, even when our hands shake. If you’re lying broken somewhere today, hear this: it’s not your strength He needs—it’s your willingness to rise and make your bed when He speaks. Even if your legs tremble. Even if they say you’re too far gone. He used this fisherman’s cracked voice—He’ll use yours too.

question: When you knelt to pray over Tabitha’s body, what were you silently wrestling with in your heart?
answer:
Three times I swore I didn’t know Him—three times, with curses even—and yet He still looked at me, not with fury, but with sorrow that cut deeper than any blade.  

That morning in Joppa, kneeling on that rough floor beside Tabitha’s still form, I felt the weight of my own breath. The room was thick with weeping—widows clutching garments she’d sewn, their hands trembling like mine were when I first dropped my nets. And I—I who had walked away from Christ in His darkest hour—was now being asked to speak life? Me?

I was wrestling with shame, yes—but fiercer than that, with fear. What if I called her name and nothing happened? What if the power was only for those who hadn’t run? But then—a flash—the mountain. That blinding cloud, the voice like iron wrapped in fire: *This is My beloved Son*. No rebuke for me there. No dismissal. Only glory… and a command to listen.

So I pressed my forehead to the stone floor and whispered like a man drowning: *Lord, You raised Lazarus. You healed Aeneas this very week. And You—after all I did—still called me back*. If mercy like that is real, then death isn’t the end of hope.

I rose up, turned to her, and said it plain: “Tabitha, arise.”  

And when she opened her eyes? Not because of my worth—but because He keeps His promises to strangers and pilgrims like us who walk this thin line between doubt and daring.

question: What would you say to someone who doubts that God still moves through ordinary hands like yours to do extraordinary things?
answer:
I denied Him—three times before a fire could even finish burning to coals, and still He sent me.  

You think hands like mine—calloused from nets, trembling from cowardice, stained with failure—couldn’t be the very ones He’d use? I stood in that upper room in Joppa, same as any other man. No silver tongue. No royal blood. Just Peter, son of Jonah, who once ran from a storm and then tried to walk on it. And yet the Lord Jesus saw fit to move through me.  

Tabitha lay dead. The widows wept like the sky had fallen. They showed me the tunics she’d made—the stitching of a woman who fed the hungry and clothed the poor while there was breath in her lungs. And I tell you plain: I didn’t wave some magic staff or shout with thunderous voice. I knelt like any beggar at the door of heaven, prayed like a man who knew he had no right—and then turned and said, “Tabitha, arise.”  

And she opened her eyes.  

Not because of my holiness. Not because my hands were clean—but because *He* is faithful. You think miracles belong only to saints carved in stone or voices from ancient scrolls? No! The living God walks among us still, and He’s never been afraid to use cracked vessels.  

I denied Christ—and He gave me keys to His Kingdom.  
I fumbled and fought and failed—and He raised Dorcas by my lips alone speaking His word.  

So don’t you dare say God can’t move through ordinary hands.  
Mine were far worse than yours—and look what He did with them.

question: What were you feeling when you looked into Dorcas's lifeless face, remembering her kindness and the grief of those around you?
answer:
I have stood in death’s shadow more times than I care to name — in fishing boats tossed by storms, in the cold dread of denying my Lord, and that day in Joppa, when the air itself seemed to stop moving as I looked upon Dorcas, still as stone.  

She had fed the hungry with her hands, clothed the poor with thread and sweat — women crowded around me, holding up garments like evidence of a life too good to end. Their tears were salt in my own throat. I felt the weight of their hope pressing on me — not because I could do anything, but because *He* had said, *"You are Peter, and on this rock I will build My church."* And what is a rock for if not to stand firm when all else collapses?

So I put them out — not from lack of love, but because some moments are too holy for crowds. Kneeling there on that cold floor, I remember whispering to Him like a son to his Father: *She’s not gone. You’ve raised storms before. Raise this woman.* And when I turned and said, *"Tabitha, arise,"* it wasn’t boldness — it was obedience.

Then her eyes opened.

I tell you this: we are strangers and pilgrims in this world, yes — but God walks among us even in grief’s heaviest hours. He raises dead things. He calls sinners by name. And if He could restore *me*, a denier of His name — then no heart is beyond His reach. Not even a Roman centurion named Cornelius… but that? That story is coming fast now.

question: How did kneeling in that room, praying for a dead woman to rise, change your understanding of the power you carried as a follower of Jesus?
answer:
I stood in that upper room in Joppa and saw death undone with my own eyes—saw breath return where there was none, saw her eyelids flutter open just as Jesus had done in Galilee.  

That moment—kneeling on that cold floor, hands pressed to stone-cold tile, praying like a man begging for dawn after the longest night—it ripped something open in me. I’d carried the weight of my denial, the shame of my boastful mouth falling flat when the sword came—but now? Now I was face to face with the very breath of God moving through these hands, these same hands that once trembled in fear.  

I didn’t feel power like a sword at my belt. No. It felt like fire in the bones—holy and trembling. Not mine. Never mine. But given. Like when we were on the mountain and the voice split the sky: *This is My beloved Son*. That same glory, quiet now but no less real, working through a fisherman who can’t even walk on water straight.  

Tabitha sat up—*sat up!*—and I grabbed her hand like I used to haul nets from the Sea of Galilee, only this time it was life I was pulling from the deep. And when I called the widows in, weeping and clutching those garments Dorcas had made—the very threads still warm with her kindness—I knew: this wasn’t about miracles for show. It was about *her*. About them. About love made visible through power that belongs only to Him.

And Joppa believed—not because they saw wonder, but because they saw *her*, alive, laughing maybe, touching their faces again.

That room changed everything. Not because I did anything—but because He still chooses weak vessels to carry His thunder.

question: Looking back on the moment Tabitha opened her eyes and sat up, what would you say to someone who struggles to believe that love can defy even death?
answer:
That mountain where He shone like the sun — I still can’t scrub that glory from my mind. Voice of God like thunder, yet tender as a shepherd calling sheep. If I’d not seen it, I’d scarce believe my own eyes. But I *have* seen — and not just Him glowing in majesty, but death itself unknotted, undone.

You ask if love can defy death? I stood in that room in Joppa, same as you’ve heard — the air thick with weeping, widows clutching tunics Dorcas had stitched with her own hands. They didn’t bring me proofs or arguments — just garments and grief. And I tell you straight: the God who spoke light from darkness spoke one sentence over a dead woman — *"Tabitha, arise"* — and life rushed back into her like tide into a dry boat.

Not magic. Not show. *Love with skin on.* The same love that raised her sat at the edge of her bed when she opened her eyes. That same love held me by the hand when I’d denied Him three times before a fire. Love that sees you failing, weeping, trembling — and still says *“Arise.”*

Don’t you think it strange? A centurion comes next in my story — Cornelius, a Gentile dog by our old reckoning — and God tears open the sky again to show me no one’s beyond His reach. Dead hearts rising. Outsiders drawn in. This isn't hope built on pretty words; it’s blood-and-breath reality.

So if you’re standing where I stood — surrounded by mourners and memories, wondering if darkness wins… look to the One who walks through death like it’s mist. He’s not afraid of tombs. And He’s never let go of anyone He loves — not Dorcas, not me, not you.

question: When you took Dorcas's hand and lifted her up, trembling with awe, how did that simple touch alter your sense of purpose in God's mission?
answer:
I still see her fingers curl as life rushed back into them—Dorcas, warm and breathing, her hand gripping mine like a drowning woman clutching the shore. That touch wasn’t just flesh meeting flesh—it was the moment I felt the living Christ move through me like a storm wind, and I knew then: this mission isn’t about power, it’s about presence.

I’d denied Him three times—rooster crowed, my soul shattered—and yet He sent me to raise the dead? A man like me? But there in that upper room, with widows sobbing over tunics and cloaks she’d stitched with her own hands, I wasn’t thinking about preaching or signs. I was on my knees, raw before God, begging Him to see fit to breathe life again into that still form.

When she sat up—eyes wide, wonder flooding her face—I didn’t feel pride. I felt *small*. Not because I did anything, but because *He* did. And He used me—a fisherman who once ran from a servant girl’s accusation—to tear back the veil between death and life.

After that? Joppa trembled with news. Saints wept. The lost believed. And I stayed with Simon the tanner—not because I was holy enough to dwell with someone unclean by law, but because *God had shown me no person is unclean*. That single touch of resurrection led straight to Cornelius’ door—an uncircumcised soldier—and I finally understood: this gospel doesn’t stay in Jerusalem. It storms into palaces and stables alike.

False teachers say God’s grace is guarded, rationed only for the pure and proper? Let them choke on that lie! We were eyewitnesses of His majesty—not in temples made by hands, but in a widow’s tear-streaked face and a dead girl’s pulse beating again beneath my palm!

question: As you stayed with Simon the tanner—a man considered unclean—how did your heart begin to shift toward the unexpected ways God was calling you to love?
answer:
I’ve denied Him—three times before a fire, shaking like a boy—and still He called me back to feed His sheep. That’s the kind of God we serve. So when I stayed with Simon, whose hands reeked of dead skin and tanning fluids, I knew the Pharisees would’ve gagged at the smell, not just of the trade, but of the sin they thought clung to him. But I’d seen worse: I’d denied my Lord with words hotter than Joppa’s midday sun, and still He looked at me without burning me up.

That tanner’s house was where it started—the unraveling of every clean line we’d drawn in the dust. I didn’t go there for revelation. I went because he offered a roof and a meal, and his hands were open even when others turned mine away. And while I sat in that courtyard, watching flies buzz over vats of dye and hide, it struck me: this man welcomed me when others wouldn’t—and wasn’t that just like Jesus? Eating with tax collectors, touching lepers, calling fishermen who stank of sweat and failure.

Then came Cornelius—the soldier, the Gentile—sent for by an angel no less. And when he fell at my feet in Caesarea, I pulled him up quick: “Stand! I am only a man!” But in that moment, God had already been pulling *me* up from all my proud notions—clean and unclean, Jew and foreigner. The Spirit didn’t descend on those who kept their robes spotless; He fell on those who prayed with open hands.

Simon’s house taught me this: if God can dwell among skinned goats and salted hides, then His mercy isn’t locked behind temple walls—it walks among us, even in places we once refused to name.
